topics.title: Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET304 - SURVEYING AND GEOMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Surveying

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> **Surveying** is the art, science, and technology of determining the relative positions of points on, above, or beneath the Earth's surface, and of representing these positions graphically in the form of maps, plans, or numerical data (coordinates).

**Geomatics** is the modern, integrated discipline that encompasses all the traditional surveying methods along with advanced geospatial technologies such as **GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System)**, **GIS (Geographic Information System)**, **Remote Sensing**, **Photogrammetry**, and **Digital Terrain Modelling (DTM)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024:**
> The course explicitly uses the term *Surveying AND Geomatics* to emphasize that students must be fluent in both **conventional chain-and-theodolite methods** and **modern space-based positioning techniques**.

## 1.2 Primary Objectives of Surveying

The three classical objectives of any surveying operation are:

1. **Horizontal Positioning** — Determining the (X, Y) planar coordinates of points.
2. **Vertical Positioning (Levelling)** — Determining the elevation (Z) or reduced level of points.
3. **Cartographic Representation** — Plotting the above information to scale on a map or plan, which is dimensionally accurate and universally interpretable.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of Surveying as "Translating Reality to Paper":**
> Imagine a civil engineer wants to build a **20 km highway** through a forested hilly region. The terrain is full of trees, rivers, and slopes. The engineer cannot just "guess" where to dig. A surveyor is like a **master translator**: they stand on the Earth's curved, irregular, three-dimensional surface, measure distances, angles, and heights using precision instruments, and then "translate" that complex 3D reality into a flat, two-dimensional **engineering drawing** with mathematical precision. Without this translation, no construction project — buildings, dams, tunnels, or highways — can begin with confidence.

## 1.4 Standard Units & Constants (KTU Mandate)

| Parameter | SI Unit | Engineering Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Length | Metre (**m**) | Metre |
| Area | Square metre (**m²**) | Hectare (**ha**) = 10,000 m² |
| Volume | Cubic metre (**m³**) | m³ |
| Angle (Plane) | Radian (**rad**) | Degree / Grad |
| Angle (Sexagesimal) | — | 1° = 60' = 3600" |
| Geographic Position | Degree, Minute, Second (DMS) | DMS / Decimal Degrees |
| Gravity (Reference) | **9.80665 m/s²** (Standard) | 9.81 m/s² (for levelling) |
| Geodetic Reference | **WGS-84 Ellipsoid** (GNSS) | Everest / Kalyanpur (Legacy) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Principles of Surveying

These are the **philosophical pillars** of the discipline and are favorite KTU 2-mark / short-answer questions.

### Principle 1: Working from Whole to Part
- The survey of a large area is first controlled by establishing a **framework of high-precision primary stations** (Triangulation / Traversing stations).
- Once the main framework is accurate, the **minor details** (interior features) are surveyed later using less precise, faster methods.
- **Why?** If a small error creeps in, it remains *localized* and does not distort the whole map.

### Principle 2: Location of a Point by Measurement from Two References
- Any new point's position is fixed by measuring its **distance and direction** from *at least two* already-known points.
- The classical methods are:
  - **Trilateration** (measuring only distances)
  - **Triangulation** (measuring only angles)
  - **Traversing** (measuring both angles and distances)
  - **Resection & Intersection** (angular methods)

### Principle 3: Economy of Accuracy
- The **precision adopted must be appropriate to the importance of the work**.
- A 1 km² cadastral survey for a residential plot does **not** require the sub-millimetre accuracy demanded by a particle accelerator tunnel.
- The principle dictates a hierarchy: *Control > Detail > Checking*.

## 2.2 Classification of Surveys (Highest-Yield KTU Topic)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Alert:** A flowchart-style question on "Classify surveys and explain any two" carries 7 marks almost every year.

**A. Based on Instrument & Method**
- **Chain Surveying** — Linear measurements only (shortest distance, no angles).
- **Compass Surveying** — Linear + Magnetic bearings.
- **Theodolite Surveying** — Linear + Horizontal/Vertical angles.
- **Tacheometric Surveying** — Optical distance + angle measurement.
- **Photogrammetric Surveying** — Aerial / Terrestrial photographs.
- **Remote Sensing Surveying** — Satellite imagery interpretation.
- **GNSS / GPS Surveying** — Satellite-based positioning.

**B. Based on Purpose**
- **Topographic Survey** — Natural and man-made features of a region.
- **Cadastral Survey** — Property boundaries for legal ownership records.
- **City Survey** — Streets, sewers, water supply networks.
- **Engineering Survey** — Route alignment, tunnels, dams, canals.
- **Mine Survey** — Underground galleries and shafts.
- **Military Survey** — Defensive positions and terrain intelligence.
- **Geological Survey** — Rock strata, mineral exploration.
- **Archaeological Survey** — Ancient monuments and excavation sites.

**C. Based on Position of Instrument Station**
- **Terrestrial Survey** — Instruments placed on the ground.
- **Aerial Survey** — Photographs taken from aircraft / drones.
- **Space Survey** — Satellites (GNSS, Remote Sensing).

**D. Based on Shape of the Earth Considered**
- **Plane Surveying** — Curvature of Earth ignored; area < **250 km²**; standard for most civil engineering projects.
- **Geodetic Survey** — Curvature of Earth considered; area > **250 km²**; used for state and national mapping.

## 2.3 Scales — The Mathematical Bridge

A **scale** is the ratio of any distance on the map to the corresponding distance on the ground.

### Types of Scales

1. **Numerical Scale (Representative Fraction, RF)**
   - Expressed as a pure ratio, e.g., **RF = 1 : 50,000**.
   - Unitless; valid in any system of units.

2. **Graphical (Plain) Scale**
   - A bar drawn on the map divided into primary and secondary divisions.
   - Survives shrinking/enlargement of the map.

3. **Time Scale, Diagonal Scale, Vernier Scale** — Specialized scales used in construction and cartography.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Representative Fraction (RF) | $RF = \dfrac{\text{Map Distance (MD)}}{\text{Ground Distance (GD)}}$ | Unitless, dimensionless |
| 2 | Ground Distance from RF | $GD = \dfrac{MD}{RF}$ | Ensure unit consistency |
| 3 | Map Distance from RF | $MD = GD \times RF$ | — |
| 4 | Area Scale vs Linear Scale | $Area \ RF = (Linear \ RF)^2$ | Square of the linear ratio |
| 5 | Volume Scale vs Linear Scale | $Volume \ RF = (Linear \ RF)^3$ | Cube of the linear ratio |
| 6 | Shrunk/Enlarged RF | $Corrected \ RF = \dfrac{RF_{original}}{1 \pm Shrinkage \ Factor}$ | **+ for shrinkage, − for enlargement** |
| 7 | Curvature Threshold | Plane Survey valid if $A \le 250 \ km^2$ | Beyond this → Geodetic Survey |
| 8 | Error of Closure (Linear) | $e = \sqrt{e_x^2 + e_y^2}$ | $e_x, e_y$ = components in X, Y |
| 9 | Relative Precision | $Precision = \dfrac{e}{P}$ | $P$ = perimeter of traverse |
| 10 | Allowable Angular Error | $E = k \sqrt{n}$ (seconds) | $k$ = instrument constant, $n$ = number of angles |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Trap:** In KTU answer sheets and even in this digital table, never write the absolute value of x as `|x|`. Always write it as $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ to avoid table-rendering breakage in Markdown.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Setting out building columns, highway centre lines, and canal beds.
- **Mining:** Stope surveys and shaft plumbing.
- **Defence:** Strategic terrain mapping and missile guidance.
- **Disaster Management:** Inundation mapping for flood-prone rivers using GNSS-RTK.
- **Urban Planning:** Smart city GIS databases integrating cadastral and utility networks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: The Relationship between Linear, Area, and Volume Scales

Let $L_m$ be a length on the map and $L_g$ the corresponding length on the ground.

**Step 1: Define the Representative Fraction (RF).**

$$
RF = \frac{L_m}{L_g}
$$

**Step 2: For a square plot of side $L_g$, the ground area is $A_g = L_g^2$.**

**Step 3: On the map, the same area is $A_m = L_m^2$.**

**Step 4: Form the area ratio (called the Area Scale).**

$$
\frac{A_m}{A_g} = \frac{L_m^2}{L_g^2} = \left(\frac{L_m}{L_g}\right)^2 = (RF)^2
$$

**Step 5: Generalize to volume.** A cubic volume scales as the cube of the linear scale.

$$
\frac{V_m}{V_g} = (RF)^3
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This derivation is the single most-tested KTU 4-mark derivation in Module 1.

## 3.2 Derivation: Scale Conversion under Shrinkage

When a map is printed on paper that later shrinks (or stretches), the apparent RF must be corrected.

**Step 1: Original printed RF.**

$$
RF_{original} = \frac{1}{n}
$$

**Step 2: The shrinkage factor $S$ is defined as:**

$$
S = \frac{\text{Original Paper Length} - \text{Shrunk Paper Length}}{\text{Original Paper Length}}
$$

**Step 3: A known ground distance $L_g$ corresponds to a printed map length $L_m = L_g / n$.**

**Step 4: After shrinking, the *same* $L_g$ is now represented by a *shorter* map length $L_m'$:**

$$
L_m' = L_m (1 - S) = \frac{L_g}{n}(1 - S)
$$

**Step 5: The corrected (true) Representative Fraction is therefore:**

$$
RF_{true} = \frac{L_m'}{L_g} = \frac{1 - S}{n}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This shows that **shrinkage reduces the denominator, making the map effectively larger-scale** (i.e., showing finer detail). KTU examiners love to test whether students remember the *sign* of the shrinkage correction.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Survey Type (Decision Logic)

| Field Condition | Recommended Primary Survey | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Open, fairly level ground; small plot (< 1 ha) | **Chain Surveying** | Cheapest; no angular measurement needed |
| Large open area with hilly, broken terrain | **Triangulation** | Angular methods bypass physical distance barriers |
| Dense urban area with obstacles | **Traversing** | Flexible route; high accuracy with total station |
| Linear project (road, canal, railway) | **Traversing + Cross-sections** | Continuous alignment control |
| Country-scale mapping (> 250 km²) | **Geodetic / GNSS** | Earth's curvature cannot be ignored |
| Cloudy / forested inaccessible zone | **Photogrammetry** | Bypasses ground obstruction |
| Real-time positioning of a moving machine | **GNSS-RTK** | Centimetre-level real-time accuracy |

## 3.4 Error Analysis: True Value vs Measured Value

The classical "True Error" formulation:

$$
E_{true} = M - T
$$

where $M$ is the measured value and $T$ is the true value. For multiple observations $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_n$ of the same quantity, the **Most Probable Value (MPV)** by the method of least squares is:

$$
MPV = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i}{n}
$$

**Residual** of the $i^{th}$ observation:

$$
r_i = M_i - MPV
$$

**Sum of residuals (fundamental check):**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i = 0
$$

**Standard deviation:**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum r_i^2}{n-1}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The condition $\sum r_i = 0$ is a self-check that KTU examiners frequently mention as the "fingerprint" of a correctly solved least-squares adjustment problem.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Hierarchical Classification of Surveying

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Surveying Classification] --> nodeB[Based on Instrument]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Based on Purpose]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Based on Earth Curvature]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Based on Medium]

    nodeB --> nodeB1[Chain Survey]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Compass Survey]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Theodolite Survey]
    nodeB --> nodeB4[Tacheometry]
    nodeB --> nodeB5[Photogrammetry]
    nodeB --> nodeB6[Remote Sensing]
    nodeB --> nodeB7[GNSS Survey]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[Topographic]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Cadastral]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Engineering]
    nodeC --> nodeC4[Mine]
    nodeC --> nodeC5[Military]
    nodeC --> nodeC6[City]
    nodeC --> nodeC7[Geological]

    nodeD --> nodeD1[Plane Survey less than 250 sq km]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Geodetic Survey greater than 250 sq km]

    nodeE --> nodeE1[Terrestrial]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Aerial]
    nodeE --> nodeE3[Space]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Workflow — From Field Observation to Map Output

```mermaid
graph TD
    sA[Reconnaissance of Site] --> sB[Decision on Survey Method]
    sB --> sC[Primary Control Network Establishment]
    sC --> sD[Secondary Control Densification]
    sD --> sE[Detailed Feature Measurement]
    sE --> sF[Field Data Recording and Checks]
    sF --> sG[Office Computation and Adjustment]
    sG --> sH[Plotting and Cartographic Drafting]
    sH --> sI[Quality Assurance and Final Map Output]

    sB -. Choice 1 .-> sB1[Chain for open ground]
    sB -. Choice 2 .-> sB2[Traverse for urban]
    sB -. Choice 3 .-> sB3[Triangulation for large area]
    sB -. Choice 4 .-> sB4[GNSS for geodetic]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: The "Whole-to-Part" Surveying Philosophy

```mermaid
graph LR
    p1[Step 1: Primary Framework] --> p2[Step 2: Secondary Stations]
    p2 --> p3[Step 3: Tertiary Detail Points]
    p3 --> p4[Step 4: Map Production]

    p1 -. High Precision .-> pX[Triangulation Net]
    p2 -. Moderate Precision .-> pY[Traverse Lines]
    p3 -. Low Precision .-> pZ[Offset and Tie Lines]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Whole-to-Part"** principle ensures that the controlling skeleton is established first with the highest possible precision, and subordinate details are filled in later. Any localized error is then contained within a small sub-block and does not propagate to corrupt the entire map.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP, Set B]
**Define surveying. List any four branches of surveying based on purpose.**

**Model Answer:**

Surveying is the art and science of determining the relative positions of points on, above, or beneath the Earth's surface and representing them on a map, plan, or as numerical coordinates.

**Four branches based on purpose:**

1. **Topographic Survey** — Maps the natural and artificial features of a region (rivers, hills, roads).
2. **Cadastral Survey** — Determines property boundaries for ownership and taxation.
3. **Engineering Survey** — Provides data for designing and executing engineering projects (roads, dams).
4. **Mine Survey** — Maps underground mine workings and supports safe extraction.

*(Any other valid branch like City, Military, or Geological Survey accepted; 1 mark for definition + 0.5 × 4 = 2 marks for branches.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, S1 Civil, Q1(a)]
**Differentiate between Plane Surveying and Geodetic Surveying.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Plane Surveying | Geodetic Surveying |
|---|---|---|
| **Earth's Curvature** | Ignored | Considered |
| **Area Limit** | Up to **250 km²** | Greater than 250 km² |
| **Line Considered As** | Straight | Curved arc |
| **Datum** | Mean Sea Level (MSL) for height only | Geoid / Ellipsoid (3D) |
| **Accuracy** | Moderate | Very high |
| **Instruments** | Chain, compass, theodolite | Total station, GNSS, precise levels |
| **Use** | Cadastral, engineering projects | National mapping, boundary surveys |

*(4 points × 0.75 = 3 marks; tabulation preferred.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> In "Principles of Surveying" questions, students often *list* the three principles without explaining *why* each is essential. Examiners specifically deduct marks when the **engineering rationale** is missing. Always accompany each principle with a 2-line "Why this is needed" justification.

---

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — June 2024, S1 Civil, Model QP, Q2 (a+b)]
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the three fundamental principles of surveying with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cadastral map has a scale of 1 cm = 50 m. If the map shrinks by 2%, calculate the new scale. Also, find the area scale and volume scale for both the original and the shrunk map.

#### Model Solution (a):

The three fundamental principles of surveying are:

**1. Working from Whole to Part (Locating New Stations by Measurement from Whole).**
- A primary framework of high-precision stations is first established across the entire region.
- Interior details are then surveyed with respect to this skeleton.
- *Example:* In a national mapping project, first-order triangulation stations are established. Then lower-order stations are filled in, and finally individual plot boundaries are surveyed.
- *Why:* Localizes any error; prevents cumulative distortion.

**2. Location of a Point by Measurement from Two Reference Points.**
- To fix a new point $P$, measurements are taken from at least two known stations $A$ and $B$ (distance or angle, or both).
- *Methods:* Triangulation (angles), Trilateration (distances), Traversing (mixed), Intersection/Resection (angles only).
- *Example:* A theodolite intersection of two arcs from stations $A$ and $B$ locates a chimney top on a hill.

**3. Economy of Accuracy (or "Adaptation of Methods to Required Precision").**
- The surveyor selects instruments and procedures whose precision matches the *purpose* of the survey.
- *Example:* A sub-millimetre-accurate total station is **not** used to draw a school playground map; a steel tape is sufficient.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for naming, 5 marks for explanation and example.)*

#### Model Solution (b):

**Given:**
- Original scale: 1 cm = 50 m  →  $RF_{orig} = \dfrac{1}{50 \times 100} = \dfrac{1}{5000}$
- Shrinkage $S = 2\% = 0.02$

**Step 1: Corrected RF after shrinkage.**

From the derived formula in Section 3.2:

$$
RF_{true} = \frac{1 - S}{n} = \frac{1 - 0.02}{5000} = \frac{0.98}{5000}
$$

$$
RF_{true} = \frac{1}{5000 / 0.98} = \frac{1}{5102.04}
$$

So the **new scale is 1 cm ≈ 51.02 m**.

**Step 2: Area scale (original).**

$$
Area \ RF = (RF)^2 = \left(\frac{1}{5000}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{25{,}000{,}000}
$$

i.e., $1 \ cm^2 = 25 \times 10^6 \ cm^2 = 2500 \ m^2$.

**Step 3: Volume scale (original).**

$$
Volume \ RF = (RF)^3 = \left(\frac{1}{5000}\right)^3 = \frac{1}{1.25 \times 10^{11}}
$$

i.e., $1 \ cm^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{11} \ cm^3 = 125 \ m^3$.

**Step 4: Shrunk map's area and volume scales.**

The new linear RF is $\frac{1}{5102.04}$.

$$
Area \ RF_{shrunk} = \left(\frac{1}{5102.04}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{26{,}030{,}746}
$$

$$
Volume \ RF_{shrunk} = \left(\frac{1}{5102.04}\right)^3 = \frac{1}{1.328 \times 10^{11}}
$$

**Final Answer Summary:**

| Quantity | Original Map | After 2% Shrinkage |
|---|---|---|
| Linear RF | 1 : 5000 | 1 : 5102.04 |
| Area RF | 1 : 2.5 × 10⁷ | 1 : 2.603 × 10⁷ |
| Volume RF | 1 : 1.25 × 10¹¹ | 1 : 1.328 × 10¹¹ |

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks each for deriving the three new RFs, 1 mark for original scales, 1.5 marks for the final table and result.)*

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, S1 Civil, Q1 (a+b) — Alternative Choice]
**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the different types of surveys. Explain in detail the classification based on the purpose of the survey with examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A plot of land 750 m × 600 m is to be represented on a map such that the largest dimension is 25 cm. Find the RF. What area on the map will represent the plot? If the map is enlarged to twice its size, what will be the new RF?

#### Model Solution (a):

Surveys can be classified on the basis of **instrument, purpose, medium, and Earth's curvature**. The detailed classification based on purpose is:

| # | Type | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Topographic** | Maps natural and man-made surface features | Survey of a hill station |
| 2 | **Cadastral** | Property boundary determination | Revenue survey of a village |
| 3 | **Engineering** | Data for design/construction of structures | Alignment survey of NH-66 |
| 4 | **Mine** | Mapping of underground/above-ground mine works | Stope survey of a coal mine |
| 5 | **City** | Municipal planning: streets, drainage, water supply | Smart city GIS base map |
| 6 | **Military** | Strategic terrain intelligence | Topo map for artillery |
| 7 | **Geological** | Subsurface strata, mineral exploration | Mapping a limestone belt |
| 8 | **Archaeological** | Mapping of ancient monuments and sites | Survey of an excavated site |

Each type differs in **scale, accuracy, instruments, and legal status** of the resulting document. Cadastral surveys, for example, are legally binding and therefore demand the highest positional accuracy relative to property corners.

*(7 marks: 1 mark for intro, 5 marks for the table/explanation, 1 mark for any extra context.)*

#### Model Solution (b):

**Given:**
- Ground plot dimensions: 750 m × 600 m
- Largest dimension (750 m) is represented by 25 cm on the map.

**Step 1: Compute the Representative Fraction.**

$$
RF = \frac{MD}{GD} = \frac{25 \ cm}{750 \ m} = \frac{25}{750 \times 100} = \frac{25}{75{,}000} = \frac{1}{3000}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the area on the map.**

Ground area:

$$
A_g = 750 \times 600 = 450{,}000 \ m^2
$$

Map area using Area Scale:

$$
\frac{A_m}{A_g} = (RF)^2 = \left(\frac{1}{3000}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{9{,}000{,}000}
$$

$$
A_m = \frac{450{,}000 \ m^2}{9{,}000{,}000} = 0.05 \ m^2 = 5000 \ cm^2
$$

**Step 3: When the map is enlarged to twice its size.**

Enlargement means every linear dimension on the map is **doubled**. So the new linear RF is:

$$
RF_{new} = 2 \times \frac{1}{3000} = \frac{2}{3000} = \frac{1}{1500}
$$

**Step 4 (Verification):** The largest dimension on the original map was 25 cm; after 2× enlargement it becomes 50 cm, so:

$$
RF_{new} = \frac{50 \ cm}{750 \ m} = \frac{50}{75{,}000} = \frac{1}{1500} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final Answer:**

- $RF = 1 : 3000$
- Map area $= 5000 \ cm^2 = 0.05 \ m^2$
- Enlarged $RF = 1 : 1500$

*(7 marks: 2 marks RF, 2 marks area, 2 marks enlargement, 1 mark final answer.)*

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Surveying = "Whole-to-Part"** — always establish the controlling framework first.
- **Three Principles:** (1) Whole to part, (2) Location from two references, (3) Economy of accuracy.
- **Plane vs Geodetic:** Cut-off is **250 km²**; below → plane, above → geodetic.
- **Branches by Purpose:** Topographic, Cadastral, Engineering, Mine, City, Military, Geological, Archaeological.
- **RF is unitless.** Convert map and ground distances to the *same unit* before forming the ratio.
- **Area scale = (Linear scale)²** and **Volume scale = (Linear scale)³**. These are the most-tested relationships.
- **Shrinkage correction:** Use $RF_{true} = (1 - S)/n$ for shrinkage and $RF_{true} = (1 + S)/n$ for enlargement. *Sign convention is the #1 reason students lose marks.*
- **Modern Geomatics = Conventional Surveying + GNSS + GIS + Remote Sensing + Photogrammetry**.
- **MPVs by Least Squares** require the **sum of residuals = 0** as a self-consistency check.
- **Curvature of the Earth** is neglected in plane surveying because, for small areas, the difference between a chord and an arc is negligible (≈ 0.1 m over 1 km).
- **In KTU answer sheets**, always draw a **schematic or flowchart** when explaining a classification — it is the most efficient way to earn full marks.
- **Geodetic datum for GNSS in India:** WGS-84 is the global standard; Everest spheroid is the historical Indian legacy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET304 - SURVEYING AND GEOMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Linear Measurement

In the discipline of **Surveying and Geomatics**, the term **"Linear"** refers to **Linear Measurements** — the fundamental process of determining the **horizontal (or slope) distances** between two points on the Earth's surface. It is the most basic and primitive form of measurement in surveying, upon which almost all higher-order operations (traversing, triangulation, leveling, and modern GNSS networks) are conceptually founded.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for Module 1, *Linear Measurements* encompasses the study of direct and indirect methods of measuring distances, the instruments employed (chain, tape, ranging rods, arrows, etc.), the techniques of **ranging** (direct and indirect), and the various **corrections** applied to observed measurements to obtain the **true horizontal distance**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Linear measurements form the foundation of **chain surveying**, which is the simplest branch of surveying. Mastery of this topic is essential before progressing to compass surveying, theodolite surveying, and modern total station / GPS operations.

### Mathematical / Functional Statement

A linear measurement operation can be formally expressed as:

$$D_{\text{true}} = L_{\text{measured}} + C_{\text{std}} + C_{\text{slope}} + C_{\text{temp}} + C_{\text{pull}} + C_{\text{sag}}$$

Where the various $C$ terms represent corrections due to standardisation, slope, temperature, pull, and sag respectively. Each correction will be derived explicitly in Section 3.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ruler of the Earth"

Imagine you are trying to measure the length of your classroom with a long rope. You lay the rope from one wall to the other, mark the end, and then physically count how many rope-lengths fit into the room's length. This is exactly how ancient surveyors measured the dimensions of the Egyptian pyramids and the Great Wall of China — using **chains and ropes**.

In modern engineering practice, the *chain* has been replaced by the **steel tape** and **electronic distance measurement (EDM)** devices, but the **philosophy remains identical**: we wish to know the exact horizontal separation between two points so that we may plot it on a map or use it to set out a structure on the ground.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is "Horizontal" distance so important?**
> Plans and maps are always two-dimensional representations. Even if a slope is steep on the ground, what the engineer needs on paper is the *plan (horizontal) projection* of that distance. All chain/tape measurements are therefore **corrected to horizontal equivalent** before being used for plotting or calculation.

### Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Expected)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Chain** | A measuring instrument composed of 100 or 66 links, traditionally used by surveyors. |
| **Tape** | A more modern flexible measuring device (cloth, steel, invar). |
| **Ranging** | Establishing an intermediate point (or points) on a straight line between two terminal stations. |
| **Ranging Rod** | A wooden/iron rod, usually **2 m to 3 m** long, painted in alternating red and white bands, used as a sighting target. |
| **Arrows** | Piece of wire bent into a U-shape, used to mark the end of each chain length on the ground. |
| **Offset** | The perpendicular distance from the chain line to an object whose position is to be located. |
| **Leader** | The person who drags the chain forward. |
| **Follower** | The person who holds the rear end of the chain at the previous station. |

### Standard Metric Units Used in Linear Measurement

- **1 chain (Gunter's)** = **66 feet** = **20.1168 metres** (≈ 20.12 m)
- **1 link** = **0.66 feet** = **0.201168 m** = **20.1168 cm**
- **1 engineering chain** = **100 feet** = **30.48 m** (used in India for cadastral surveys)
- **1 metric chain** = **20 m** or **30 m** (most common in modern Indian practice)
- **1 fathom** = **6 feet** = **1.8288 m** (used for soundings in hydrographic surveying)
- **1 nautical mile** = **1852 m** (used in geodetic and marine work)
- **1 yard** = **0.9144 m**
- **1 foot** = **0.3048 m**

> [!TIP]
> For KTU numerical problems, always remember the conversion **1 m = 3.28084 ft** and **1 ft = 0.3048 m**. Many student errors arise from incorrect unit conversions between metric chains (20 m/30 m) and imperial chains (100 ft).

---

## 1.3 Classification of Linear Measurement Methods

Linear measurements in surveying are broadly classified into **two** main categories:

### (A) Direct Methods
In direct methods, the distance is measured by **physically applying the measuring instrument** along the line. The measurement is taken in a continuous, point-to-point contact manner.

**Examples:**
- Pacing
- Passometer / Pedometer
- Odometer
- Chain (metric, Gunter's, engineer's)
- Tapes (cloth, steel, invar)
- Subtense bar

### (B) Indirect Methods (Optical / Electronic)
In indirect methods, the distance is **computed** from other measured quantities (angles, time, etc.) using mathematical relationships. The instrument does not physically span the line.

**Examples:**
- Optical methods (tacheometry, subtense bar)
- Electronic Distance Measurement (EDM)
- Total Station (combination of angle + EDM)
- GPS / GNSS (uses time-of-flight of radio waves)

The image below (Section 4) provides a hierarchical block-level schematic of this classification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** When asked to "list the methods of linear measurement", students frequently forget the **passometer** and **oddometer** (vehicle-mounted wheel). These are valid, low-precision methods used for reconnaissance and route surveys. Always include them in the list.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Direct Methods of Linear Measurement — Detailed Analysis

### 2.1.1 Pacing
Pacing is the **simplest** and most rudimentary form of distance measurement. The surveyor counts the number of steps required to traverse a line and multiplies by the **average length of his pace** (usually obtained by walking over a known distance multiple times).

- **Accuracy:** About **1 in 100 to 1 in 200**
- **Use:** Reconnaissance surveys, rough estimates of distance in inaccessible terrain.

### 2.1.2 Passometer (Pedometer)
A pocket-sized instrument that mechanically counts each step via a lever mechanism linked to the surveyor's hip. The total count is then multiplied by the **average pace length**.

### 2.1.3 Odometer
A wheel of known circumference (typically **2 m** or **perimeter fixed**) is rolled along the line. The number of revolutions is recorded, and the distance is computed as:

$$D = n \times C$$

where $n$ = number of revolutions and $C$ = circumference of the wheel.

- **Accuracy:** About **1 in 200**
- **Use:** Rough traverse and route alignment surveys.

### 2.1.4 Chain
The chain is composed of **100 (or 66) links** of galvanised mild steel wire, with each link connected by **three rings**. At every **10th link**, a **brass or coloured tally** is attached to help count the tens.

- **Length of Gunter's chain:** **66 ft (20.117 m)** — preferred for cadastral and land surveys because 10 sq. chains = 1 acre.
- **Length of Engineer's chain:** **100 ft (30.48 m)** — used by Indian Railways and PWD for long lines.

> [!NOTE]
> The chain has largely fallen out of use in modern surveying, replaced by steel tapes. However, the terms "chainman", "chainage", and "chainage formula" are still actively used in railway and highway alignment design.

### 2.1.5 Tapes
Tapes are the most widely used instruments for short-to-medium range linear measurement today.

| Type of Tape | Material | Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (per °C) | Accuracy | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cloth / Linen tape** | Linen fabric with brass / copper wire reinforcement | Variable (humidity-sensitive) | Very low | Rough sketches, offset measurement |
| **Steel tape (standard)** | Hardened & tempered steel ribbon | $\alpha \approx 11 \times 10^{-6} / °C$ | High (1 in 1000 to 1 in 5000) | General surveying, building, road |
| **Invar tape** | Alloy of nickel (36%) + steel (64%) | $\alpha \approx 0.5 \times 10^{-6} / °C$ | Very high (1 in 10$^5$ to 1 in 10$^6$) | Base line measurement, geodetic triangulation |

### 2.1.6 Subtense Bar
A horizontal bar of fixed length (usually **2 m**) with two targets at the ends. The horizontal angle $\theta$ subtended at the instrument station is measured, and the distance is computed using the formula:

$$D = \frac{b}{2} \cot \frac{\theta}{2}$$

where $b$ is the length of the bar. This is an *indirect* optical method but is often listed under linear measurement instrumentation.

---

## 2.2 Ranging — The Heart of Linear Measurement

When two stations **A** and **B** are **not inter-visible** (e.g., due to a hill or a tall building in between), or when they are too far apart to be sighted from a single position, intermediate points must be established on the straight line AB. This process is called **Ranging**.

### 2.2.1 Direct Ranging (when A and B are mutually visible)
Both end stations are visible from each other. The surveyor stands a few metres behind A, sights a ranging rod at B, and signals the **ranger** (the person holding the intermediate rod) to move left or right until the intermediate rod lies *exactly* on the line AB.

- The ranger is aligned by **"lining in"** signals — hand gestures or shout.
- This is repeated for as many intermediate points as required.

### 2.2.2 Indirect (Reciprocal) Ranging (when A and B are NOT mutually visible)
Used when there is an obstacle (a hill, a building) between A and B, so neither end is visible from the other. Two intermediate points **M** and **N** are selected on opposite sides of the obstacle but on rough alignment. Two surveyors alternately sight each other to bring M and N onto the true line AB.

**Procedure (Step-by-Step):**
1. Two persons stand at M and N with ranging rods. M is nearer to A, N is nearer to B.
2. Person at M sights to N, signals N to move so that N aligns with B. N moves sideways to satisfy this.
3. Person at N then sights to M, signals M to move so that M aligns with A. M moves.
4. Steps 2 and 3 are repeated alternately. M and N **converge to the true line AB** after a few iterations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** The convergence of indirect ranging is **rapid** — typically, the method converges to within centimetric accuracy in 3 to 4 cycles. A diagram showing this is provided in Section 4.

### 2.2.3 Ranging by Line Ranger (Modern Instrument)
A **line ranger** is a small instrument with two right-angle prisms that allow the observer to see two images simultaneously — one of the back rod and one of the front rod. When the two images *coincide*, the intermediate rod is exactly on the line.

---

## 2.3 Errors in Linear Measurement

Linear measurements are subject to three classes of errors:

### 2.3.1 Instrumental Errors
- Imperfect graduations on the chain/tape.
- Tape not of standard length.
- Joint wear in chain links.
- Bends or kinks in tape.

### 2.3.2 Personal Errors
- Incorrect reading of graduations.
- Chain not held horizontal.
- Plumb-bob not used at sloping ground.
- Inconsistent pull applied to the tape.
- Misalignment during ranging.

### 2.3.3 Natural Errors
- Variation in temperature causing tape expansion/contraction.
- Wind causing tape to sway.
- Ground settlement or sag of tape during measurement.
- Atmospheric refraction (for optical methods).

> [!TIP]
> **Errors are systematic (cumulative) or random (compensating).** Temperature, sag, and pull cause *systematic* errors that must be corrected. Reading and sighting errors are *random* and are minimised by repetition.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table contains every formula the student must memorise for KTU Board examinations on this topic. **Memorise this table** before attempting numericals.

| # | Quantity / Correction | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **True distance** | $L = l + C_{a} + C_{s} + C_{t} + C_{p} + C_{sl}$ | $l$ = measured length (m) |
| 2 | **Absolute correction for tape standardisation** | $C_{a} = \left(\dfrac{l' - l_0}{l_0}\right) L$ | $l' =$ actual length, $l_0 =$ nominal length |
| 3 | **Correction for slope (when angle of slope $\theta$ known)** | $C_{sl} = L (1 - \cos\theta)$ | $\theta$ in radians, $L$ = slope length |
| 4 | **Correction for slope (when difference in level $h$ known)** | $C_{sl} = \dfrac{h^{2}}{2L}$ (approximate) | $h$ in m, $L$ in m |
| 5 | **Correction for temperature** | $C_{t} = \alpha (T_{m} - T_{0}) L$ | $\alpha$ = coefficient of expansion, $T$ in °C |
| 6 | **Correction for pull (sag absent)** | $C_{p} = \dfrac{(P - P_{0}) L}{A E}$ | $P$ = applied pull, $A$ = area, $E$ = Young's modulus |
| 7 | **Correction for sag (tape suspended, zero pull at ends)** | $C_{s} = \dfrac{W^{2} L_{s}^{3}}{24 P^{2}}$ (per span) | $W$ = weight/unit length, $L_s$ = span length, $P$ = pull |
| 8 | **Reduction to horizontal (slope angle method)** | $H = L \cos\theta$ | $H$ = horizontal distance |
| 9 | **Reduction to horizontal (hypotenuse method)** | $H = \sqrt{L^{2} - h^{2}}$ | $h$ = difference in level |
| 10 | **Subtense bar distance** | $D = \dfrac{b}{2} \cot\dfrac{\theta}{2}$ | $b$ = bar length, $\theta$ = subtended angle |
| 11 | **Slope correction** | $C_{sl} = L - H = L(1 - \cos\theta)$ | — |
| 12 | **Mean sea level reduction** | $C_{msl} = -\dfrac{L \cdot H}{R}$ | $H$ = elevation, $R$ = Earth radius $\approx 6.371 \times 10^{6}$ m |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention Pitfall:** *Corrections* (to be **added** to measured length) and *reductions* (to be **subtracted**) have opposite signs. Slope correction is *always subtractive* (since slope > horizontal). Temperature correction has the sign of $(T_m - T_0)$.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Linear measurement is the **starting point** of every project:

- **Civil Engineering:** Setting out building foundations, plot boundaries, road centre-lines.
- **Railway Engineering:** Laying tracks along alignment, computing chainage of stations, turnouts, bridges.
- **Highway Engineering:** Calculation of earthwork volumes requires cross-sectional linear measurements (chainage × offsets).
- **Hydrographic Surveying:** Sounding depths are computed as perpendicular offsets from the shore line measured by chain/tape.
- **Mining & Tunneling:** Cross-measurements of headings, drives, and stopes.
- **Cadastral Surveying:** Demarcation of property boundaries using chains/tapes.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Slope Correction

**Problem Statement:** A slope distance $L$ is measured on ground that is inclined at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. Derive an expression for the horizontal equivalent $H$, and show that the slope correction is $C_{sl} = L(1 - \cos\theta)$.

### Step 1 — Geometric Setup

Consider a line $AB$ on the ground that is inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. The measured (slope) distance is $L = AB$, and the horizontal projection is $H = AC$.

![Geometric Setup — text representation]

The relationship in the right-angled triangle $ABC$ is:

$$H = L \cos\theta$$

### Step 2 — Definition of Slope Correction

The **slope correction** $C_{sl}$ is the quantity that must be **subtracted** from the slope length to obtain the horizontal length:

$$C_{sl} = L - H$$

### Step 3 — Substitution

Substitute $H = L\cos\theta$ into the correction equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{sl} &= L - L\cos\theta \\
C_{sl} &= L(1 - \cos\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

**This is the required expression for slope correction when the angle of slope $\theta$ is known.**

### Step 4 — Approximate Form for Small Slopes

For small angles, $\cos\theta$ can be expanded using the Maclaurin series:

$$\cos\theta = 1 - \frac{\theta^{2}}{2!} + \frac{\theta^{4}}{4!} - \cdots$$

Retaining only the first two terms (valid for $\theta < 5°$):

$$\cos\theta \approx 1 - \frac{\theta^{2}}{2}$$

Substituting into the slope correction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{sl} &= L \left(1 - \left(1 - \frac{\theta^{2}}{2}\right)\right) \\
C_{sl} &= L \cdot \frac{\theta^{2}}{2} \\
C_{sl} &= \frac{L\theta^{2}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\theta$ is in **radians**.

### Step 5 — Alternative Form using Difference in Level

If the **difference in level** $h$ between the two stations is measured, then by Pythagoras:

$$L^{2} = H^{2} + h^{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; H = \sqrt{L^{2} - h^{2}}$$

Therefore:

$$C_{sl} = L - \sqrt{L^{2} - h^{2}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by the conjugate $L + \sqrt{L^{2} - h^{2}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{sl} &= L - \sqrt{L^{2} - h^{2}} \cdot \frac{L + \sqrt{L^{2} - h^{2}}}{L + \sqrt{L^{2} - h^{2}}} \\
&= \frac{L^{2} - (L^{2} - h^{2})}{L + \sqrt{L^{2} - h^{2}}} \\
&= \frac{h^{2}}{L + \sqrt{L^{2} - h^{2}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $h \ll L$, $\sqrt{L^{2} - h^{2}} \approx L$, so:

$$C_{sl} \approx \frac{h^{2}}{2L}$$

**This is the standard KTU formula for slope correction when only the difference in level is known.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Temperature Correction

**Problem Statement:** A steel tape of nominal length $l_0$ is standardised at temperature $T_0$. Derive the temperature correction for a measurement of length $L$ taken at mean temperature $T_m$.

### Step 1 — Fundamental Thermal Expansion

The length of any material changes with temperature according to:

$$l_T = l_0 \left[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)\right]$$

where $\alpha$ is the **coefficient of linear thermal expansion** (per °C).

### Step 2 — Change in Length per Unit Length

The change in length per unit length of tape is:

$$\Delta l = l_0 \cdot \alpha (T_m - T_0)$$

The fractional change in length is:

$$\frac{\Delta l}{l_0} = \alpha (T_m - T_0)$$

### Step 3 — Correction for Total Length L

If the measured length is $L$, the total temperature correction is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_t &= \alpha (T_m - T_0) \cdot L \\
\boxed{C_t &= \alpha (T_m - T_0) L}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Sign Convention:
- If $T_m > T_0$ (tape expands): $C_t$ is **positive** (added to measured length).
- If $T_m < T_0$ (tape contracts): $C_t$ is **negative** (subtracted).

### Standard Values of $\alpha$ for KTU Problems:

| Material | $\alpha$ (per °C) |
|---|---|
| Steel | $11 \times 10^{-6}$ to $12 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Invar | $0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ to $1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Brass | $18 \times 10^{-6}$ to $19 \times 10^{-6}$ |

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Pull Correction (Tape on Ground, No Sag)

**Problem Statement:** A tape is stretched between two supports with a pull $P$ that differs from the standardisation pull $P_0$. Derive the pull correction.

### Step 1 — Young's Modulus Relation

The elongation of a tape of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and Young's modulus $E$ under an additional pull $(P - P_0)$ is:

$$\delta L = \frac{(P - P_0) L}{A E}$$

### Step 2 — Pull Correction

This elongation is the correction to the measured length:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{C_p &= \frac{(P - P_0) L}{A E}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Sign Convention:
- If $P > P_0$: tape is stretched more than at standardisation → $C_p$ is **positive** (added).
- If $P < P_0$: tape is stretched less → $C_p$ is **negative** (subtracted).

> [!NOTE]
> For a **30 m** steel tape with cross-section $A = 6 \text{ mm}^{2}$, $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm$^{2}$, an excess pull of **10 N** produces a correction of $C_p = (10 \times 30000) / (6 \times 2 \times 10^5) = 0.25$ mm — small but measurable in precise work.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Sag Correction (Tape Suspended in Air)

**Problem Statement:** When a tape is suspended in the air between two supports, it forms a **catenary** (a sagging curve). Derive the sag correction.

### Step 1 — Geometry of the Catenary

Consider a tape of weight $W$ per unit length suspended between two supports at the same level, distance $L_s$ apart. The tension $P$ at the supports acts horizontally.

### Step 2 — Elemental Analysis

The shape of a small element $ds$ of the tape is governed by the balance of forces. The standard result from catenary mechanics is:

$$y = \frac{P}{W} \left( \cosh \frac{W x}{P} - 1 \right)$$

### Step 3 — Length of the Tape

The actual arc length of the tape is:

$$L = 2 \cdot \frac{P}{W} \sinh \frac{W L_s}{2P}$$

### Step 4 — Expansion for Small Sags

For small values of $\dfrac{W L_s}{2P}$, the hyperbolic sine can be expanded:

$$\sinh u = u + \frac{u^{3}}{3!} + \frac{u^{5}}{5!} + \cdots$$

Retaining up to the third-order term:

$$\sinh u \approx u + \frac{u^{3}}{6}$$

Substituting $u = \dfrac{W L_s}{2P}$:

$$L \approx 2 \cdot \frac{P}{W} \left[ \frac{W L_s}{2P} + \frac{1}{6} \left(\frac{W L_s}{2P}\right)^{3} \right]$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &\approx L_s + \frac{1}{6} \cdot \frac{P}{W} \cdot \frac{W^{3} L_s^{3}}{8 P^{3}} \\
L &\approx L_s + \frac{W^{2} L_s^{3}}{48 P^{2}} \cdot \frac{2}{2} \\
L &\approx L_s + \frac{W^{2} L_s^{3}}{24 P^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Sag Correction

The excess length due to sag is the **difference** between the curved length $L$ and the straight (chord) length $L_s$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{C_s = L - L_s = \frac{W^{2} L_s^{3}}{24 P^{2}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**The sag correction is always SUBTRACTIVE** because the curved tape is *longer* than the chord.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a tape in **multiple spans** (e.g., a 30 m tape supported at the 10 m and 20 m marks, creating 3 spans of 10 m each), the total sag correction is the **sum** of the corrections for each span:
> $$C_{s,\text{total}} = 3 \cdot \frac{w^{2} (10)^{3}}{24 P^{2}}$$
> where $w$ is the weight per metre.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Pattern)

**Problem:** A steel tape of nominal length 30 m was standardised at 20 °C under a pull of 50 N. It was used to measure a line at a mean temperature of 32 °C. The cross-sectional area of the tape is 6 mm², the coefficient of thermal expansion is $11 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, and Young's modulus is $2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm². The applied pull during measurement was 70 N. The slope of the ground corresponds to an angle of 2° 30' and the difference in level between the ends is 1.2 m. The standardised length of the tape was found to be 30.012 m when tested against a standard. Find the true horizontal length if the field measurement recorded was 425.500 m.

### Solution

#### (i) Correction for Standardisation

$$C_a = \left(\frac{l' - l_0}{l_0}\right) L = \left(\frac{30.012 - 30.000}{30.000}\right) \times 425.500$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_a &= \frac{0.012}{30.000} \times 425.500 \\
C_a &= 0.0004 \times 425.500 \\
C_a &= +0.1702 \text{ m} \;\; (\text{additive})
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (ii) Correction for Temperature

$$C_t = \alpha (T_m - T_0) L = 11 \times 10^{-6} \times (32 - 20) \times 425.500$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_t &= 11 \times 10^{-6} \times 12 \times 425.500 \\
C_t &= 132 \times 10^{-6} \times 425.500 \\
C_t &= +0.0562 \text{ m} \;\; (\text{additive})
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (iii) Correction for Pull

$$C_p = \frac{(P - P_0) L}{A E} = \frac{(70 - 50) \times 425500}{6 \times 2 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{20 \times 425500}{12 \times 10^{5}} \\
C_p &= \frac{8.51 \times 10^{6}}{1.2 \times 10^{6}} \\
C_p &= +0.00709 \text{ m} \;\; (\text{additive})
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (iv) Correction for Slope (using angle $\theta$)

$\theta = 2° 30' = 2.5°$. Convert to radians: $\theta = 2.5 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 0.04363$ rad.

$$C_{sl} = L(1 - \cos\theta) = 425.500 \times (1 - \cos 2.5°)$$

$\cos 2.5° = 0.99905$, so $1 - \cos 2.5° = 0.000952$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{sl} &= 425.500 \times 0.000952 \\
C_{sl} &= 0.4051 \text{ m} \;\; (\text{subtractive})
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (v) Total Correction

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{total}} &= C_a + C_t + C_p - C_{sl} \\
&= +0.1702 + 0.0562 + 0.0071 - 0.4051 \\
&= -0.1716 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (vi) True Horizontal Length

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{true}} &= 425.500 + (-0.1716) \\
\boxed{L_{\text{true}} &= 425.3284 \text{ m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Verification using $h$ directly:** $C_{sl} \approx h^{2}/(2L) = (1.2)^{2}/(2 \times 425.500) = 1.44 / 851 = 0.00169$ m... This is too small! Why? Because the given angle $2.5°$ and $h = 1.2$ m are *inconsistent*. In KTU problems, always use the value of $h$ **or** the value of $\theta$, **not both**. The 2.5° angle is the controlling parameter in this problem.
