> **Intervisibility of Stations** is defined as the ability of two ground stations (points whose relative positions and Reduced Levels — RLs — are known) to be **mutually visible** along a straight line of sight that is **not obstructed by the intervening terrain** between them.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme surveying practice**, this determination is the *first geometric check* performed when planning:
- **Triangulation / Trilateration networks** (inter-station sight lines for theodolite observations)
- **Alignment surveys** for roads, railways, canals, pipelines, and power transmission corridors
- **Microwave / radio link tower placement** (line-of-sight propagation)

The procedure reduces to comparing the **elevation of the straight line joining the two stations** (the "line of sight", LoS) at every point along the alignment with the **actual ground elevation** at that point. If the LoS lies *above* the ground profile everywhere in between, the stations are intervisible; otherwise they are not.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine two friends standing on the rooftops of two buildings of different heights, separated by a hill. They want to see each other with a torchlight. Even if both torchlights are switched on, the hill in between may block the beam. To make them see each other, they have exactly three options:
> 1. **Cut the hill down** (reduce the obstacle's height).
> 2. **Raise the lower building** (or signal on top of it) so the beam clears the hill.
> 3. **Raise the higher building** too — but this is wasteful.

The *surveyor's* problem is identical, but expressed in terms of **RLs, horizontal distances, and required signal heights**. The math is essentially the equation of a straight line in a vertical plane.

## 1.3 Why Intervisibility Matters — Engineering Relevance

| Application | Consequence if Non-Intervisible |
|---|---|
| Triangulation network | Lost observations → break in chain, expensive re-sighting |
| Highway / railway alignment | Detour, deep cutting, or viaduct needed |
| Power transmission line | Tower height re-design, sag re-calculation |
| Microwave link | Link failure, costly tower raising |
| Canal alignment | Aqueduct / tunnel needed |

## 1.4 Categories of the Problem

The KTU 2024 scheme classifies the problem into **two principal cases**, based on the RLs of the two terminal stations:

> [!NOTE]
> **Case 1 — Stations at the Same Level** (h_A = h_B = H)
> The line of sight is perfectly horizontal. It is parallel to the datum at elevation H.
> **Case 2 — Stations at Different Levels** (h_A ≠ h_B)
> The line of sight is inclined; it slopes from the higher to the lower station.
> *In most real problems, Case 2 is the common scenario.*

The detailed analytical treatment of both cases, together with the **remedial measures** (cutting the obstacle / raising either station), is developed in Sections 2 and 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Setting

Consider two stations **A** and **B** separated by a horizontal distance **L**.

Let:
- **h_A** = Reduced Level of station A
- **h_B** = Reduced Level of station B
- **d** = horizontal distance of an intervening point (or obstacle) C from station A
- **h_C** = Reduced Level of the intervening ground/obstacle at C

The line of sight is a straight line in the vertical plane containing A and B, joining elevations h_A and h_B over a horizontal span L.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Decision

The procedure to test intervisibility is:

1. **Identify** the two terminal stations A, B and read their RLs (h_A, h_B).
2. **Measure** the horizontal separation L (from map, plan, or chain/tape).
3. **Locate** the highest intervening point (or the given obstacle point) C and find d and h_C.
4. **Compute** the elevation of the line of sight (LoS) at distance d from A using the linear-interpolation formula.
5. **Compare** LoS elevation with h_C:
   - If **LoS ≥ h_C** → stations are **intervisible** (optionally, with a clearance).
   - If **LoS < h_C** → **obstacle blocks** the view; compute the *excess*.
6. **Remedy**: cut the obstacle, raise A, or raise B (with cost trade-off analysis).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following is the **complete formula set** required to solve any intervisibility problem at KTU 2024 ESE level.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Line of sight elevation at distance d from A** | $h_{LoS}(d) = h_A + d \cdot \dfrac{h_B - h_A}{L}$ | m; valid for both rising and falling LoS |
| 2 | Equivalent form (h_A − h_B) | $h_{LoS}(d) = h_A - d \cdot \dfrac{h_A - h_B}{L}$ | m; preferred when h_A > h_B |
| 3 | **Excess at obstacle C** (ground above LoS) | $s_C = h_C - h_{LoS}(d) = h_C - h_A + d \cdot \dfrac{h_A - h_B}{L}$ | m; if $s_C \le 0$, stations are intervisible |
| 4 | **Height to cut obstacle C** | $s_C$ (as above) | m; direct from the excess |
| 5 | **Height to raise station A** | $s_A = \dfrac{L}{L-d} \cdot s_C$ | m; "raising A" reduction factor $L/(L-d)$ |
| 6 | **Height to raise station B** | $s_B = \dfrac{L}{d} \cdot s_C$ | m; "raising B" reduction factor $L/d$ |
| 7 | **Ratio of station-raising to obstacle-cutting** | $\dfrac{s_A}{s_C} = \dfrac{L}{L-d}, \quad \dfrac{s_B}{s_C} = \dfrac{L}{d}$ | dimensionless; cost multiplier |
| 8 | Special case: obstacle at **midpoint** ($d = L/2$) | $s_A = s_B = 2 \cdot s_C$ | m; raising is exactly **twice** as costly as cutting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU examiner favourite):** The closer the obstacle is to the *higher* station, the more economical it is to *raise the lower station*. Conversely, an obstacle close to the *lower* station makes *cutting the obstacle* the cheapest remedy. **Always quote the "L/(L−d)" and "L/d" factors explicitly in your answer** to score full marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Transmission-line tower design:** Engineers use the LoS equation to compute the **minimum tower height** at a saddle / crossing point.
- **Road profile design:** The same equation (run as a constraint) governs the **maximum allowable ground elevation** between two control points.
- **Survey network optimisation:** For triangulation, only intervisible stations are admissible; the cost of raising a signal mast is directly read from $s_A$ or $s_B$.

## 2.5 Limiting Cases & Boundary Conditions

1. **$d = 0$** (obstacle coincident with A): $s_B = \infty$ (cannot compensate by raising B); $s_A = L/0 \to \infty$ in the wrong direction — physically, $s_C = h_C - h_A$ and only A-raise works.
2. **$d = L$** (obstacle coincident with B): $s_A = \infty$; only B-raise works.
3. **$s_C \le 0$**: stations are *already* intervisible — no remedy needed.
4. **$h_A = h_B$ (Case 1)**: LoS is horizontal at height H; the maximum ground elevation between A and B must satisfy $\max(h_{ground}) \le H$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Line-of-Sight Elevation

Consider stations A and B with horizontal distance L and RLs h_A, h_B. In the vertical plane through A and B, the LoS is a straight line.

**Parametric line equation** (treating horizontal distance x from A as the parameter):

$$y(x) = h_A + (x / L) \cdot (h_B - h_A)$$

**At an obstacle C located at distance d from A** (i.e. x = d):

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{LoS}(d) &= h_A + \frac{d}{L} \cdot (h_B - h_A) \\
           &= h_A - \frac{d}{L} \cdot (h_A - h_B) \\
           &= \frac{h_A (L - d) + h_B \cdot d}{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

The last form is a **weighted linear interpolation** between h_A and h_B, with weights $(L-d)/L$ and $d/L$ — a useful sanity check.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Excess $s_C$

For the obstacle to *just* be cleared, we require:

$$h_{LoS}(d) \ge h_C$$

The amount by which the ground pierces the LoS is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_C &= h_C - h_{LoS}(d) \\
    &= h_C - h_A - \frac{d}{L}(h_B - h_A) \\
    &= h_C - h_A + \frac{d}{L}(h_A - h_B)
\end{aligned}
$$

- If $s_C \le 0$: **intervisible**.
- If $s_C > 0$: **blocked by $s_C$ metres**; remedies follow.

## 3.3 Derivation of $s_A$ (Height to Raise Station A)

Suppose we raise **only A** by $s_A$, keeping B fixed at $h_B$. The new LoS endpoints are $(h_A + s_A)$ at A and $h_B$ at B.

**New LoS elevation at C** (distance d from A):

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{LoS}^{\,new}(d) &= (h_A + s_A) - \frac{d}{L} \cdot \bigl[(h_A + s_A) - h_B\bigr] \\
                   &= h_A + s_A - \frac{d}{L}(h_A - h_B) - \frac{d}{L} s_A \\
                   &= h_A - \frac{d}{L}(h_A - h_B) + s_A \!\left(1 - \frac{d}{L}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For *just clearing* the obstacle, set $h_{LoS}^{\,new}(d) = h_C$:

$$
h_A - \frac{d}{L}(h_A - h_B) + s_A \cdot \frac{L - d}{L} = h_C
$$

Solving for $s_A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_A \cdot \frac{L-d}{L} &= h_C - h_A + \frac{d}{L}(h_A - h_B) = s_C \\[4pt]
s_A &= \frac{L}{L-d} \cdot s_C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reduction factor for A:** $\boxed{\dfrac{L}{L-d}}$  — this is the *cost multiplier* over cutting the obstacle.

## 3.4 Derivation of $s_B$ (Height to Raise Station B)

Raise **only B** by $s_B$, keeping A at $h_A$. New LoS endpoints: $h_A$ at A, $(h_B + s_B)$ at B.

**New LoS elevation at C** (distance d from A):

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{LoS}^{\,new}(d) &= h_A - \frac{d}{L} \cdot \bigl[h_A - (h_B + s_B)\bigr] \\
                   &= h_A - \frac{d}{L}(h_A - h_B) + \frac{d}{L} \, s_B
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting equal to $h_C$:

$$
h_A - \frac{d}{L}(h_A - h_B) + s_B \cdot \frac{d}{L} = h_C
$$

Solving for $s_B$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_B \cdot \frac{d}{L} &= h_C - h_A + \frac{d}{L}(h_A - h_B) = s_C \\[4pt]
s_B &= \frac{L}{d} \cdot s_C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reduction factor for B:** $\boxed{\dfrac{L}{d}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** Raising B is *always* more expensive than raising A when the obstacle is closer to A (i.e. $d < L-d$). This is why on transmission-line projects, **the lower terminal signal is preferentially raised** rather than the higher one, provided terrain access permits.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Analytical Matrix

**Problem statement:** Two stations A and B are 6 km apart. RL of A = 250 m, RL of B = 280 m. An obstacle C lies between them at 2 km from A with RL = 295 m. Check intervisibility; if blocked, find the heights of cutting C, raising A, and raising B.

### Step 1 — Tabulate the data

| Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $h_A$ | 250 | m |
| $h_B$ | 280 | m |
| $L$ | 6 | km |
| $d$ | 2 | km |
| $h_C$ | 295 | m |

### Step 2 — Line of sight at C

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{LoS}(d) &= h_A + \frac{d}{L}(h_B - h_A) \\
           &= 250 + \frac{2}{6}(280 - 250) \\
           &= 250 + \frac{2}{6} \times 30 \\
           &= 250 + 10 = 260 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Check intervisibility

Ground at C = 295 m; LoS at C = 260 m.

$$
s_C = 295 - 260 = 35 \text{ m} > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NOT intervisible}
$$

The obstacle protrudes **35 m** above the LoS.

### Step 4 — Remedy calculations

| Remedy | Formula | Numerical | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cut obstacle C** | $s_C$ | $295 - 260$ | $35$ m |
| **Raise station A** | $s_A = \dfrac{L}{L-d} \cdot s_C = \dfrac{6}{4} \times 35$ | $1.5 \times 35$ | $52.5$ m |
| **Raise station B** | $s_B = \dfrac{L}{d} \cdot s_C = \dfrac{6}{2} \times 35$ | $3 \times 35$ | $105$ m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion of the worked example:** The *most economical* remedy is to **cut the obstacle by 35 m**; if cutting is infeasible (e.g. rocky ground), the *next best* option is to **raise station A by 52.5 m**, while **raising station B requires 105 m** — the most expensive by far.

### Step 5 — Engineering interpretation

Since $d = 2$ km and $L - d = 4$ km, the obstacle is closer to A. Per Section 2.5, the rule of thumb correctly predicts that **raising A is cheaper than raising B** (factor 1.5 vs 3.0). The decision reduces to comparing the *unit cost of excavation* vs *unit cost of tower/signal construction*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Intervisibility Decision Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below captures the **complete decision pipeline** for an intervisibility problem — from input data acquisition to the final engineering remedy selection. Each block is a procedural step; the labelled arrows indicate the direction of information flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1([Start]) --> inp1[Input: hA, hB, L, d, hC from field/plan]
    inp1 --> calc1[Compute LoS elevation at C: hA + d/L times hB minus hA]
    calc1 --> diff1[Compute excess: sC = hC minus LoS]
    diff1 --> dec1{Is sC less than or equal to 0?}
    dec1 -->|Yes| out1[Output: STATIONS INTERVISIBLE — No remedy required]
    dec1 -->|No| out2[Output: STATIONS NOT INTERVISIBLE — Obstacle blocks view]
    out2 --> cost1[Evaluate remedies: cut C, raise A, raise B]
    cost1 --> calc2[Compute sA = L over L minus d times sC]
    cost1 --> calc3[Compute sB = L over d times sC]
    calc2 --> tab1[Tabulate: sC, sA, sB with reduction factors]
    calc3 --> tab1
    tab1 --> eng1[Engineering decision: select cheapest feasible remedy]
    eng1 --> end1([End])
    out1 --> end1
```

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Geometry — Vertical Plane Block Diagram

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagram below is a **schematic block representation** of the vertical plane through stations A, the obstacle C, and station B. It maps all the geometric entities used in the derivations of Section 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Station A — RL = hA] --Horizontal distance d--> C1[Obstacle C — RL = hC, distance d from A]
    C1 --Horizontal distance L minus d--> B1[Station B — RL = hB, total distance L from A]
    A1 -.Line of sight inclined.-> B1
    A1 -.Height of line of sight at C = hA + d over L times hB minus hA.-> C1
    C1 -.Excess above LoS = sC = hC minus LoS elevation.-> C1
    A1 -.Raise by sA.-> A2[Station A raised to hA plus sA]
    B1 -.Raise by sB.-> B2[Station B raised to hB plus sB]
    C1 -.Cut by sC.-> C2[Obstacle C lowered to hC minus sC]
```

## 4.3 Component / Variable Reference Card

| Symbol on Diagram | Surveying Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| A1, A2 | Station A: original and raised | — |
| B1, B2 | Station B: original and raised | — |
| C1, C2 | Obstacle C: original and cut-down | — |
| hA, hB, hC | Reduced Levels | metres (m) |
| d | Horizontal distance from A to C | kilometres (km) or metres (m) |
| L | Total horizontal distance A to B | kilometres (km) or metres (m) |
| sA, sB, sC | Remedial heights (raise A, raise B, cut C) | metres (m) |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]** Define **intervisibility of stations**. State the two main cases that arise in practice.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 m):** Intervisibility of two stations means that a straight, unobstructed line of sight can be drawn between them, with no intervening terrain point rising above that line.
- **Two cases (1 m):**
  1. *Stations at the same level* ($h_A = h_B$): the LoS is horizontal.
  2. *Stations at different levels* ($h_A \neq h_B$): the LoS is inclined and rises/falls linearly between the two RLs.

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]** What are the **three remedial measures** available to make two non-intervisible stations mutually visible? Which one is the most economical when the obstacle lies near the middle of the line?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Cut the obstacle** by an amount $s_C$.
2. **Raise station A** by $s_A = \dfrac{L}{L-d} \cdot s_C$.
3. **Raise station B** by $s_B = \dfrac{L}{d} \cdot s_C$.

When the obstacle lies near the **midpoint** ($d = L/2$), the reduction factor for raising either station is exactly **2**, so **cutting the obstacle is the most economical** (cost-multiplier = 1) compared to raising a station (cost-multiplier = 2).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Question with Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Note to student:** KTU ESE Module-2 questions carry **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Attempt **either** Option A **or** Option B in full.

### ⬛ OPTION A — 14 Marks

> **Q.A(a). [7 marks]** Two stations A and B are **4 km apart**. Their RLs are **245 m** and **250 m** respectively. An obstacle C lies between them at a distance of **1.5 km from A** and has an RL of **252 m**. **(i)** Check whether A and B are intervisible. **(ii)** If not intervisible, determine the height to which **station B** must be raised to make the stations intervisible. **(iii)** Also state the amount by which the obstacle must be cut.

#### Model Solution (Q.A-a)

**Given:** $h_A = 250 \text{ m},\ h_B = 245 \text{ m},\ L = 4 \text{ km},\ d = 1.5 \text{ km},\ h_C = 252 \text{ m}$.

> *Note:* The problem statement reverses A and B vs. the typical convention (here station A at 250 m is the *higher* one — the convention in the formula adapts seamlessly.)

**Step 1 — LoS elevation at C (distance d from A):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{LoS}(d) &= h_A + \frac{d}{L}(h_B - h_A) \\
           &= 250 + \frac{1.5}{4}(245 - 250) \\
           &= 250 + 0.375 \times (-5) \\
           &= 250 - 1.875 = 248.125 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Excess at obstacle:**

$$s_C = h_C - h_{LoS}(d) = 252 - 248.125 = 3.875 \text{ m} > 0$$

⇒ **Stations are NOT intervisible.** Obstacle protrudes **3.875 m** above the LoS.

**Step 3 — Height to raise station B:**

$$
s_B = \frac{L}{d} \cdot s_C = \frac{4}{1.5} \times 3.875 = 2.6667 \times 3.875 = 10.33 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Height to cut obstacle:**

$$s_C = 3.875 \text{ m}$$

**Final results (3 marks for tabulation):**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Intervisible? | **No** |
| $s_C$ (cut obstacle) | **3.875 m** |
| $s_B$ (raise B) | **10.33 m** |
| $s_A$ (raise A) | $\frac{4}{2.5} \times 3.875 = 6.2$ m (bonus) |

---

> **Q.A(b). [7 marks]** Discuss the **various methods to overcome the problem of non-intervisibility** between two survey stations. Compare the relative cost of each.

#### Model Solution (Q.A-b) — Key Points to Cover (1 mark each)

1. **Cutting the obstacle** — direct removal of earth/rock; baseline cost = $s_C$.
2. **Raising station A** — cost multiplier = $L/(L-d)$.
3. **Raising station B** — cost multiplier = $L/d$.
4. **Raising both stations** — adds signal cost but reduces required individual heights; rarely economical.
5. **Rerouting the alignment** — the surveyor changes A or B location; only justified if obstacle cutting is impossible.
6. **Special case d = L/2**: cutting is half the cost of either station-raise.
7. **Conclusion (1 m):** Choose the method with the **lowest total (excavation + signal) cost**, after considering site access, geology, and signal/visibility needs.

---

### ⬛ OPTION B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice)

> **Q.B(a). [7 marks]** Derive the **expressions for the height to which station A or station B should be raised** to make two non-intervisible stations A and B intervisible when an obstacle C lies between them at a distance d from A.

#### Model Solution (Q.B-a) — Derivation Outline

**Setup:** $h_A,\ h_B,\ L,\ d,\ h_C$ as defined earlier; $s_C > 0$ is the excess.

**LoS elevation at C (without raising):**

$$h_{LoS}(d) = h_A + \frac{d}{L}(h_B - h_A) = h_A - \frac{d}{L}(h_A - h_B)$$

**Excess:** $s_C = h_C - h_{LoS}(d)$.

**Raising A by $s_A$:** The new LoS from $(h_A + s_A)$ to $h_B$ gives at C:

$$
h_{LoS}^{\,new}(d) = h_A - \frac{d}{L}(h_A - h_B) + s_A \cdot \frac{L-d}{L}
$$

Setting $h_{LoS}^{\,new}(d) = h_C$ and solving:

$$
s_A \cdot \frac{L-d}{L} = s_C \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{\,s_A = \frac{L}{L-d}\,s_C\,}
$$

**Raising B by $s_B$:** The new LoS from $h_A$ to $(h_B + s_B)$ gives at C:

$$
h_{LoS}^{\,new}(d) = h_A - \frac{d}{L}(h_A - h_B) + s_B \cdot \frac{d}{L}
$$

Setting equal to $h_C$ and solving:

$$
s_B \cdot \frac{d}{L} = s_C \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{\,s_B = \frac{L}{d}\,s_C\,}
$$

(Each step above carries **1 mark**; the two boxed formulae carry **1.5 marks each** for a total of 7.)

---

> **Q.B(b). [7 marks]** Two stations A and B are **2.5 km apart** with RLs of **350 m** and **360 m** respectively. An intervening ground at C, **1.2 km from A**, has an RL of **365 m**. Determine if the stations are intervisible. If not, find the **height of signal required at A** and the **height to which the obstacle should be cut** to make them intervisible.

#### Model Solution (Q.B-b)

**Given:** $h_A = 350 \text{ m},\ h_B = 360 \text{ m},\ L = 2.5 \text{ km},\ d = 1.2 \text{ km},\ h_C = 365 \text{ m}$.

**Step 1 — LoS at C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{LoS}(d) &= h_A + \frac{d}{L}(h_B - h_A) \\
           &= 350 + \frac{1.2}{2.5}(360 - 350) \\
           &= 350 + 0.48 \times 10 = 350 + 4.8 = 354.8 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Excess at obstacle:**

$$s_C = 365 - 354.8 = 10.2 \text{ m} > 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{NOT intervisible.}$$

**Step 3 — Signal height at A:**

$$
s_A = \frac{L}{L-d} \cdot s_C = \frac{2.5}{2.5 - 1.2} \times 10.2 = \frac{2.5}{1.3} \times 10.2 = 1.9231 \times 10.2 \approx 19.62 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Cut at obstacle:** $s_C = 10.2 \text{ m}$

**Tabulated result (1 mark):**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Intervisible? | No |
| Signal at A ($s_A$) | **≈ 19.62 m** |
| Cut at C ($s_C$) | **10.2 m** |
| Signal at B ($s_B$) | $\frac{2.5}{1.2} \times 10.2 = 21.25$ m (bonus) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Mark-Loss Hotspots:**
> 1. **Unit mix-up:** Mixing km and m in $L$ and $d$ — your answer will be **off by 1000×**. Always convert $L$ and $d$ to the **same unit** before substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting the sign of $(h_B - h_A)$:** If $h_A > h_B$, the LoS *falls* from A to B; do not blindly insert a positive value.
> 3. **Sign of $s_C$:** A *negative* $s_C$ means the stations are already intervisible — do not compute remedies.
> 4. **Choosing the wrong station to raise:** KTU examiners award 1 mark specifically for **identifying the economical remedy** (smaller of $s_A$ and $s_B$, or compare with $s_C$).
> 5. **Omitting units in the final answer:** Each numerical answer must carry **m** or **km** explicitly.
> 6. **Skipping the linear-interpolation sanity check:** Cross-verify with $\frac{h_A(L-d) + h_B d}{L}$ to catch arithmetic errors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before the exam — tick every box below:**

- ☐ Intervisibility = straight LoS above all intervening ground elevations.
- ☐ Two cases: **same level** (LoS horizontal) vs **different levels** (LoS inclined).
- ☐ LoS elevation at distance $d$ from A:  $\;h_{LoS}(d) = h_A + \dfrac{d}{L}(h_B - h_A)$.
- ☐ Excess:  $\;s_C = h_C - h_{LoS}(d)$. **Positive → blocked.**
- ☐ Cut obstacle: $\;s_C$.
- ☐ Raise A:  $\;s_A = \dfrac{L}{L-d} \cdot s_C$.
- ☐ Raise B:  $\;s_B = \dfrac{L}{d} \cdot s_C$.
- ☐ **Reduction factors:**  $L/(L-d)$ for A; $L/d$ for B.
- ☐ **Midpoint special case** ($d = L/2$):  $s_A = s_B = 2 s_C$.
- ☐ **Economy rule:**  obstacle closer to higher station → raising the *lower* station is cheaper.
- ☐ Always state **units** in the final answer; always keep $L$ and $d$ in the **same unit**.
- ☐ Always **interpret** the result (most economical remedy) — KTU awards marks for engineering judgement, not just arithmetic.
- ☐ Remember to apply the **sign convention** correctly when $h_A > h_B$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Satellite Stations and Reduction to Centre
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET304 - SURVEYING AND GEOMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Satellite Stations and Reduction to Centre

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — SATELLITE STATIONS & REDUCTION TO CENTRE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Satellite Station (Auxiliary / Eccentric Station):** A station established adjacent to a true (main) survey station when it becomes physically impossible to set up the instrument, signal, or target directly over the true station (e.g., due to an obstruction, water body, dense vegetation, or an unstable ground). Observations taken from/to this auxiliary point are subsequently **reduced to centre** — i.e., mathematically corrected — to obtain the equivalent values that *would* have been observed had the instrument been exactly over the true station.

In strict KTU/Punmia terminology, three distinct cases are recognised:

| Case | Description | Affected Quantity |
|---|---|---|
| **Case I** | Instrument is eccentric (over satellite) | Horizontal angle, Distance, Vertical angle |
| **Case II** | Signal / Target is eccentric (e.g., on a tower) | Horizontal angle, Distance, Vertical angle |
| **Case III** | Both instrument AND signal are eccentric | Combined reduction formula |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reduction to Centre** is a *correction* operation — it does **not** re-observe the field data, but rather applies a deterministic geometric correction derived from the known eccentricity and the measured sub-angles.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you are a **cricket umpire** who must stand *exactly* behind the stumps to judge an LBW correctly. But the pitch is waterlogged at that exact spot, so you stand **2 m to the left** (your *satellite position*). From this offset, the line of sight to the batsman's pad and the line of sight to the stumps form an angle *slightly different* from what it would be in the true position.

**Reduction to centre** is the trigonometric "mental shift" that mathematically transports you back to the true position and tells you the *correct* angle. The satellite station is the offset umpire; the true station is the correct umpire position; the math is the "rewind" that converts your offset reading into a true reading.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world frequency:** Satellite stationing is encountered in *every* major triangulation survey, in theodolite work over hilltops, in dam axis alignment, in tower-sighting problems (signals on lighthouse masts), and in the precise observation of trigonometrically fixed control points.

### 1.3 Key Physical / Geometric Quantities

- **Eccentricity, $e$** — the linear offset between the true station and the satellite station (in **metres, m**).
- **Measured sub-angles, $\alpha$ and $\beta$** — angles at the satellite station between the eccentricity line and the lines to the target stations (in **degrees, °**).
- **Measured distances, $s_1$ and $s_2$** — slant or horizontal distances from satellite to the targets (in **metres, m**).
- **Reduction correction, $\delta$** — the small angular correction to be added algebraically to the measured value (in **seconds of arc, "**).
- **Standard conversion constant:** $1$ radian $= \dfrac{180 \times 3600}{\pi} = 206264.806'' \approx 206265''$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why and How

The reduction to centre problem is a pure application of the **sine rule** in the small triangle formed by the true station, the satellite station, and the remote target.

**Logical Steps:**
1. Identify the true station $A$, satellite $C$, and the far station $B$ (or $B$ and $D$).
2. Measure the **eccentricity** $e = AC$ and the **sub-angles** $\angle ACB$, $\angle ACD$ at the satellite.
3. The unknown correction lies in the triangle $ACB$ (and similarly $ACD$).
4. By the **sine rule**: $\dfrac{\sin(\angle CAB)}{CB} = \dfrac{\sin(\angle ACB)}{AB}$.
5. For small corrections (eccentricity $\ll$ distance), the small-angle approximation $\sin x \approx x$ (radians) is invoked.
6. The correction is then algebraically added to the measured angle/distance, with the sign governed by the relative position (left or right of the line of sight).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below assume the eccentricity $e$ is much smaller than the sight distance $s$. Angles used inside $\sin(\cdot)$ are in degrees, and the result is converted using the $206265''$/radian constant.**

### 📋 Master Formula Table

| # | Quantity to Reduce | Measured Value | Reduction Formula (approx., small $e$) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Horizontal distance** $AB$ | $s = CB$ | $D = s - e\cos\alpha$ | m |
| 1a | Horizontal distance (exact) | $s = CB$ | $D = \sqrt{s^2 + e^2 - 2se\cos\alpha}$ | m |
| 2 | **Horizontal angle** at $A$ between $B$ and $D$ | $\theta'$ at $C$ | $\theta = \theta' \pm \dfrac{e\sin\alpha}{s_1} \pm \dfrac{e\sin\beta}{s_2}$ | rad → convert to " |
| 2a | In seconds of arc | — | $\delta'' = \dfrac{e\sin\alpha \cdot 206265}{s_1} \pm \dfrac{e\sin\beta \cdot 206265}{s_2}$ | seconds (") |
| 3 | **Vertical angle** (instrument eccentric) | $\phi'$ at $C$ | $\phi = \phi' \pm \dfrac{e\cos\alpha}{s}$ (rad) | rad |
| 4 | **Height of tower** (signal eccentric, instrument at true station) | $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ (zenith/elevation) | $h = s\left[\cot\alpha_2 - \cot\alpha_1\right] \pm e$ (tower eccentricity term) | m |
| 5 | **Combined case** (instrument + signal eccentric) | — | $H = h \pm$ (instrument correction) $\pm$ (signal correction) | m |

### 2.3 Sign Convention (Critical for KTU Board Exams)

| Position of Satellite | Sign of Correction |
|---|---|
| Satellite $C$ lies to the **LEFT** of the line $AB$ (i.e., angle $\angle CAB$ opens in one direction) | **Positive (+)** |
| Satellite $C$ lies to the **RIGHT** of the line $AB$ | **Negative (−)** |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** *"Left = Lift = + ; Right = Reduce = −"* — although the *direction* of the sign depends on the orientation of the angle being measured, in **standard clockwise theodolite readings**, the satellite being to the left typically yields a **positive** correction.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Triangulation networks** of national mapping agencies (Survey of India) routinely use satellite stations when peak stations are inaccessible.
- **Dam axis surveys** require the instrument to be set on a true pillar, but abutment observations may need to be taken from offset points.
- **High-rise tower (chimney, lighthouse) alignment** uses the *signal eccentric* case when the signal cannot be mounted exactly at the top.
- **Trigonometric levelling** over inaccessible terrain (rivers, gorges) uses the **combined instrument-and-signal eccentric** formula.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reduction of Horizontal Distance

**Setup:** True station $A$, satellite station $C$ (instrument here), and target station $B$. Let $e = AC$, $s = CB$ (measured), $D = AB$ (true), and $\alpha = \angle ACB$ (measured at $C$).

**Step 1 — Apply the cosine rule in $\triangle ACB$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^2 &= s^2 + e^2 - 2 \cdot s \cdot e \cdot \cos\alpha \\
D^2 &= s^2 - 2se\cos\alpha + e^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Factor out $s^2$ to use binomial expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^2 &= s^2 \left[1 - 2\left(\frac{e}{s}\right)\cos\alpha + \left(\frac{e}{s}\right)^2\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the binomial identity $(1 - x)^n \approx 1 - nx$ for $x \ll 1$:**

Since $e \ll s$, we have $\left(\frac{e}{s}\right)^2$ negligible, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^2 &\approx s^2 \left[1 - 2\left(\frac{e}{s}\right)\cos\alpha\right] \\
D &\approx s \left[1 - \frac{e\cos\alpha}{s}\right] \\
D &\approx s - e\cos\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:** The true distance $D$ is obtained by *subtracting* the projection of the eccentricity along the sight line from the measured distance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cosine of the angle $\alpha$ between the eccentricity line and the sight line gives the component of $e$ that is *along* the line $AB$. The perpendicular component is absorbed by the angle correction (Derivation 2).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reduction of Horizontal Angle (Single Line Case)

**Setup:** In $\triangle ACB$, find $\angle CAB$ given $e, s, \alpha$.

**Step 1 — Apply the sine rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\sin(\angle CAB)}{CB} &= \frac{\sin(\angle ACB)}{AB} \\
\frac{\sin(\angle CAB)}{s} &= \frac{\sin\alpha}{D}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\sin(\angle CAB)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin(\angle CAB) &= \frac{s \cdot \sin\alpha}{D}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Since $D \approx s$ (small $e$ assumption) and the angle $\angle CAB$ is small, use $\sin x \approx x$ (radians):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\angle CAB \text{ (radians)} &\approx \frac{e \cdot \sin\alpha}{s} \\
\angle CAB \text{ (seconds)} &= \frac{e \cdot \sin\alpha}{s} \times 206265
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic sign:** This is the correction that must be **added to or subtracted from** the measured angle at $C$ to obtain the angle at $A$. The sign is determined by the relative position (left/right) of $C$ w.r.t. $AB$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reduction of Horizontal Angle (Two-Line Case, $\angle BAD$)

**Setup:** Instrument at satellite $C$ measures $\angle BCD = \theta'$. We want $\angle BAD = \theta$ at true station $A$. Sub-angles $\angle ACB = \alpha$ and $\angle ACD = \beta$. Distances $CB = s_1$, $CD = s_2$.

**Step 1 — From the two sub-triangles $ACB$ and $ACD$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin(\angle CAB) &= \frac{s_1 \sin\alpha}{D_1} \quad \text{(where } D_1 = AB) \\
\sin(\angle CAD) &= \frac{s_2 \sin\beta}{D_2} \quad \text{(where } D_2 = AD)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Use small-angle approximation $\sin x \approx x$ and $D_i \approx s_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\angle CAB &\approx \frac{e\sin\alpha}{s_1} \quad \text{(radians)} \\
\angle CAD &\approx \frac{e\sin\beta}{s_2} \quad \text{(radians)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Geometric angle sum:** The true angle at $A$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \theta' \pm \angle CAB \pm \angle CAD \\
\theta &= \theta' \pm \frac{e\sin\alpha}{s_1} \pm \frac{e\sin\beta}{s_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert each small-angle correction to seconds of arc:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \theta' \pm \frac{e\sin\alpha}{s_1}\left(\frac{206265}{\rho}\right) \pm \frac{e\sin\beta}{s_2}\left(\frac{206265}{\rho}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\rho = 1$ radian when angles are in radians, or $\rho = 180°/\pi$ when angles are in degrees. In practice, since $\alpha, \beta$ are in degrees and the result must be in seconds, we use $\delta'' = (e \sin\alpha \cdot 206265)/s_1$ directly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board-Exam Tip:** Examiners love this derivation. Memorise the three-line structure: (1) sine rule, (2) small-angle approximation, (3) angle summation with sign convention.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model Solution)

**Problem:** Instrument is at satellite station $C$, $3.50$ m from true station $A$. The measured horizontal angle $\angle BCD = 47°32'24''$. Distances: $CB = 1240$ m, $CD = 980$ m. Sub-angles: $\alpha = \angle ACB = 28°10'$, $\beta = \angle ACD = 19°22'$. Find the true angle at $A$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_1'' &= \frac{e \sin\alpha \cdot 206265}{s_1} = \frac{3.50 \times \sin 28°10' \times 206265}{1240} \\
&= \frac{3.50 \times 0.4720 \times 206265}{1240} \\
&= \frac{340720.65}{1240} \\
&\approx 274.8''
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_2'' &= \frac{e \sin\beta \cdot 206265}{s_2} = \frac{3.50 \times \sin 19°22' \times 206265}{980} \\
&= \frac{3.50 \times 0.3316 \times 206265}{980} \\
&= \frac{239388.6}{980} \\
&\approx 244.3''
\end{aligned}
$$

Total correction: $\delta = 274.8'' + 244.3'' = 519.1'' = 8'39.1''$

True angle at $A$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= 47°32'24'' + 8'39'' = 47°41'03''
\end{aligned}
$$

(Assuming both signs are positive; the student must confirm orientation from the figure.)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture — Reduction to Centre Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: True Station A inaccessible]
    A1[Establish Satellite Station C at offset e from A]
    A2[Set up instrument over C]
    A3[Measure: eccentricity e, sub-angles alpha and beta, distances s1 and s2, observed angle theta-dash]
    A4{Which reduction is required?}
    A5[Horizontal Angle Reduction]
    A6[Horizontal Distance Reduction]
    A7[Vertical Angle or Height Reduction]
    A8[Compute correction delta-1 = e sin alpha x 206265 / s1 in seconds]
    A9[Compute correction delta-2 = e sin beta x 206265 / s2 in seconds]
    A10[Compute D = s - e cos alpha in metres]
    A11[Compute H using tower formula with eccentricity terms]
    A12[Apply sign convention based on left or right of sight line]
    A13[True angle theta = theta-dash plus or minus delta-1 plus or minus delta-2]
    A14[True distance D obtained]
    A15[True height H obtained]
    A16[End: Reduced values ready for network adjustment]
    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 -->|Angle| A5
    A4 -->|Distance| A6
    A4 -->|Height or Vertical| A7
    A5 --> A8
    A5 --> A9
    A8 --> A12
    A9 --> A12
    A12 --> A13
    A6 --> A10
    A10 --> A14
    A7 --> A11
    A11 --> A15
    A13 --> A16
    A14 --> A16
    A15 --> A16
```

## 4.2 Geometric Schematic — Field Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Field_Geometry
        A[True Station A] -- e = AC --> C[Satellite Station C]
        C -- s1 = CB --> B[Far Station B]
        C -- s2 = CD --> Dnode[Far Station D]
        A -. True angle BAD .-> B
        A -. True angle BAD .-> Dnode
        C -- Measured angle theta-dash --> B
        C -- Measured angle theta-dash --> Dnode
        C -- Sub-angle alpha --> B
        C -- Sub-angle beta --> Dnode
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the schematic:** The solid lines represent *measured* field quantities; the dotted lines represent *derived* (reduced) quantities. $A$ is the inaccessible true station, $C$ is the satellite. The corrections $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are measured at $C$ between the eccentricity line $CA$ and the lines to $B$ and $D$ respectively.

## 4.3 Decision Logic — Sign of Correction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Locate Satellite C relative to line AB]
    S1{Is C to the LEFT of line AB when looking from A to B?}
    S2[Sign for correction from sub-angle alpha is PLUS]
    S3[Sign for correction from sub-angle alpha is MINUS]
    S4{Target is B or D?}
    S5[Apply correction to angle BCD]
    S6[Apply correction to angle DCB reversed]
    S0 --> S1
    S1 -->|Yes| S2
    S1 -->|No| S3
    S2 --> S4
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S4 --> S6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ❓ Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**Q: Define a *satellite station*. Under what circumstances is it necessary to establish one in theodolite surveying?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *satellite station* (also called an *auxiliary* or *eccentric station*) is a station set up close to but not coinciding with the true (main) station of a survey, when it is impossible to set up the instrument directly over the true station.

It becomes necessary under the following circumstances:
1. The true station is **inaccessible** (e.g., in the middle of a river, on a rocky pinnacle, or in a heavily trafficked zone).
2. The point is **obstructed** by vegetation, buildings, or terrain features that prevent clear sighting.
3. The ground at the true station is **unstable** (marshy, loose soil) and cannot hold the instrument plumb.
4. The point is **on a tall structure** (tower, chimney) where a signal/target cannot be mounted at the exact top.

After observations are taken from/to the satellite station, the values are mathematically *reduced to centre* using the known eccentricity and the measured sub-angles. *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for circumstances.)*

### ❓ Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Q: State the formula for the correction to be applied to a measured horizontal angle when the instrument is set up at a satellite station. Explain the meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The correction to be applied to a measured horizontal angle, when the instrument is at a satellite station $C$ offset from the true station $A$ by an eccentricity $e$, is given by:

$$
\delta'' = \frac{e \cdot \sin\alpha \cdot 206265}{s}
$$

where:
- $e$ = eccentricity (distance $AC$, in metres) — the linear offset of the satellite from the true station.
- $\alpha$ = sub-angle measured at the satellite station between the eccentricity line and the line to the target station (in degrees).
- $s$ = horizontal distance from the satellite station to the target station (in metres).
- $206265$ = conversion constant from radians to seconds of arc.
- $\delta''$ = correction in **seconds of arc**.

The sign of the correction is determined by whether the satellite lies to the left (+) or right (−) of the sight line. *(1 mark for formula, 2 marks for term explanation.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question B-A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model, 14 Marks]

**Q: From a satellite station $C$, $4.20$ m from the main station $A$, the following observations were taken to two triangulation stations $B$ and $D$:**

- **Measured horizontal angle $\angle BCD = 84°17'30''$**
- **Sub-angle $\angle ACB = 32°24'$ (at $C$, between $CA$ and $CB$)**
- **Sub-angle $\angle ACD = 51°53'$ (at $C$, between $CA$ and $CD$)**
- **Distance $CB = 1860$ m**
- **Distance $CD = 2240$ m**

**The satellite station $C$ lies to the left of the line $AB$ and to the right of the line $AD$. Compute the corrected horizontal angle at the true station $A$.**

### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Sub-part (a) — Compute the correction $\delta_1$ from triangle $ACB$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_1'' &= \frac{e \cdot \sin(\angle ACB) \cdot 206265}{CB} \\
&= \frac{4.20 \times \sin(32°24') \times 206265}{1860}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $\sin(32°24')$:
$\sin(32°24') = \sin(32.4°) = 0.5358$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_1'' &= \frac{4.20 \times 0.5358 \times 206265}{1860} \\
&= \frac{464103.7}{1860} \\
&\approx 249.5'' \\
&\approx 4'09.5''
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sign convention:** $C$ is to the **left** of $AB$ → $\delta_1 = +4'09.5''$ *(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for calculation, 1 mark for sign, 1 mark for unit conversion.)*

### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Sub-part (b) — Compute the correction $\delta_2$ from triangle $ACD$ and the final corrected angle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_2'' &= \frac{e \cdot \sin(\angle ACD) \cdot 206265}{CD} \\
&= \frac{4.20 \times \sin(51°53') \times 206265}{2240}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $\sin(51°53')$:
$\sin(51°53') = \sin(51.883°) = 0.7869$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_2'' &= \frac{4.20 \times 0.7869 \times 206265}{2240} \\
&= \frac{681762.3}{2240} \\
&\approx 304.4'' \\
&\approx 5'04.4''
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sign convention:** $C$ is to the **right** of $AD$ → $\delta_2 = -5'04.4''$ *(2 marks for calculation, 1 mark for sign.)*

**Final corrected angle at $A$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \theta' \pm \delta_1 \pm \delta_2 \\
&= 84°17'30'' + 4'09.5'' - 5'04.4'' \\
&= 84°16'35.1''
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark for the final summation logic, 1 mark for the answer in correct D-M-S format.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 206265 conversion constant** — losing 2 marks per term.
> 2. **Mixing up sign convention** — *always draw a sketch and label left/right*; this single drawing habit will save 3–4 marks in every KTU exam.
> 3. **Not converting decimal degrees to D-M-S** in the final answer — automatic 0.5 mark deduction.
> 4. **Confusing sub-angle notation** — $\angle ACB$ is *at $C$*, not at $A$ or $B$. Read the question twice.

---

### ❓ Question B-B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Model, 14 Marks]

**Q: (a) Derive the expression for the reduction of a measured horizontal angle to the centre when the instrument is set up at a satellite station. Clearly state all assumptions.**

**(b) A triangulation station $A$ is to be observed from a satellite station $C$ offset by $5.0$ m. The sub-angle at $C$ to the far station $B$ is $42°18'$ and the measured distance $CB$ is $2150$ m. The sub-angle to the second station $D$ is $37°42'$ and $CD = 1880$ m. The measured angle $\angle BCD$ at $C$ is $79°54'20''$. If the satellite lies to the left of both lines $AB$ and $AD$, calculate the true angle at $A$.**

### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

Let $A$ = true station, $C$ = satellite, $B$ = target. Let $e = AC$, $s = CB$, $\alpha = \angle ACB$.

In $\triangle ACB$, by sine rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\sin(\angle CAB)}{CB} &= \frac{\sin(\angle ACB)}{AB} \\
\sin(\angle CAB) &= \frac{s \cdot \sin\alpha}{AB}
\end{aligned}
$$

For small eccentricity ($e \ll s$), $AB \approx CB = s$, and the angle $\angle CAB$ is very small. Using $\sin x \approx x$ (radians):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\angle CAB \text{ (rad)} &\approx \frac{e \cdot \sin\alpha}{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

In seconds of arc:

$$
\angle CAB \text{ (sec)} = \frac{e \cdot \sin\alpha \cdot 206265}{s}
$$

For two targets $B$ and $D$, the true angle at $A$ becomes:

$$
\theta = \theta' \pm \frac{e \sin\alpha \cdot 206265}{s_1} \pm \frac{e \sin\beta \cdot 206265}{s_2}
$$

**Assumptions:** (i) $e \ll s$ so that $\sin x \approx x$ is valid; (ii) $D_i \approx s_i$ so that the denominator simplification is valid; (iii) the correction is small enough that the linear superposition of the two sub-corrections is geometrically valid. *(3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for the two-target formula, 2 marks for assumptions.)*

### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Compute $\delta_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_1'' &= \frac{5.0 \times \sin(42°18') \times 206265}{2150} \\
&= \frac{5.0 \times 0.6730 \times 206265}{2150} \\
&= \frac{693962.4}{2150} \\
&\approx 322.8'' = 5'22.8''
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute $\delta_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_2'' &= \frac{5.0 \times \sin(37°42') \times 206265}{1880} \\
&= \frac{5.0 \times 0.6111 \times 206265}{1880} \\
&= \frac{630218.4}{1880} \\
&\approx 335.2'' = 5'35.2''
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sign convention:** $C$ is to the left of both lines → both corrections are **positive**.

**Final corrected angle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= 79°54'20'' + 5'22.8'' + 5'35.2'' \\
&= 80°05'18''
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks for $\delta_1$, 2 marks for $\delta_2$, 1 mark for sign, 2 marks for final summation.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Missing the "+" sign** when satellite is to the left — extremely common error; drawing a clear sketch is the only defence.
> 2. **Confusing $\alpha$ and $\beta$** — note that $\alpha$ corresponds to $s_1$ (target $B$) and $\beta$ to $s_2$ (target $D$).
> 3. **Off-by-one in the seconds** — verify each decimal second sum; a small arithmetic slip costs 1 full mark in KTU's strict marking scheme.
> 4. **In derivation part (a)**, students often forget to state the *assumptions* — this is a guaranteed 2-mark item.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Satellite station** = an *auxiliary* station set up when the true station is inaccessible; observations are *reduced to centre* using trigonometric corrections.
- ✅ **Three reduction cases:** (1) Instrument eccentric, (2) Signal eccentric, (3) Both eccentric.
- ✅ **Master formula for horizontal angle reduction:**

$$
\theta = \theta' \pm \frac{e\sin\alpha \cdot 206265}{s_1} \pm \frac{e\sin\beta \cdot 206265}{s_2}
$$

- ✅ **Master formula for horizontal distance reduction:** $D = s - e\cos\alpha$ (approx.) or $D = \sqrt{s^2 + e^2 - 2se\cos\alpha}$ (exact).
- ✅ **Sign convention:** Satellite to the **left** = **+** ; satellite to the **right** = **−** (for standard clockwise theodolite).
- ✅ **Key constant:** $1$ radian $= 206265''$ — must be multiplied to convert small-angle (radians) corrections to seconds.
- ✅ **Always draw a free-hand sketch** in the exam — examiners reward clear geometry and penalise "no diagram" answers.
- ✅ **Final answer must be in D-M-S format** (e.g., $84°16'35''$), not decimal degrees.
- ✅ **Assumptions to state:** (1) $e \ll s$ so $\sin x \approx x$ is valid; (2) the perpendicular component of $e$ is absorbed in the angle correction, not the distance.
- ✅ **Practical threshold:** if the eccentricity exceeds $\sim 5\%$ of the sight distance, the small-angle approximation begins to fail and the exact cosine-rule formula is preferred.
- ✅ **Convert all sub-angles to decimal degrees** before applying $\sin(\cdot)$; a mistake here propagates through the entire calculation.
- ✅ **KTU favourite sub-question:** *"Discuss the case when the signal is on a tall tower"* — be ready to write the height-of-tower formula $h = s(\cot\alpha_2 - \cot\alpha_1) \pm e$ with proper explanation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCET304 - SURVEYING AND GEOMATICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Types # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Curve in Surveying? In the language of **KTU 2024 Scheme Surveying and Geomatics (PBCET304)**, a **curve** is a geometrical figure representing a smooth, continuously bending alignment used to connect two intersecting straight lines (tangents) on highways, railways, canals, pipelines, or any other linear infrastructure project. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Board Standard):** > *A curve is a line whose direction changes continuously in a smooth manner, employed on the surface of the earth to connect two straight tangents that meet at an angle, thereby providing a gradual change of direction for safe and efficient movement of vehicles, water, or trains.* Mathematically, every horizontal curve is an **arc of a circle** (simple, compound, or reverse) or a **transition spiral** gradually merging into a circular arc. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Why Don't Roads Go Straight Forever?" Imagine you are driving a car at **80 km/h**. If the road suddenly bends at a sharp 90° corner (called an **intersection angle**), you will either skid off the road or be forced to brake hard — both dangerous and uncomfortable. Now, picture instead a **gently rounded bend** that gradually steers your car from one straight road into another. That gentle rounded path is a **horizontal curve**. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy (Intuition):** > - **Tangent = Straight road segment** > - **Intersection Point (IP) = Sharp corner where the two tangents meet** > - **Curve = The smooth, bowl-shaped arc that "rounds off" the corner** > - **Vehicle = Car (or train) — needs a curved path to turn safely without leaving the road** This is exactly why **every road, railway, runway, and canal** uses curves to change direction — it makes motion safe, smooth, and predictable. --- ## 1.3 The Five Fundamental "Types" of Curves (Syllabus Focus) The KTU 2024 Scheme Module-3 prescribes the following classification of horizontal and vertical curves: | # | Type of Curve | Purpose in One Line | |---|---|---| | **1** | **Simple Circular Curve** | Single arc of a circle connecting two tangents. | | **2** | **Compound Curve** | Two or more circular arcs of **different radii** curving in the **same direction**. | | **3** | **Reverse Curve** | Two circular arcs of equal (or different) radii curving in **opposite directions**, sharing a common tangent. | | **4** | **Transition Curve** | A curve of **progressively changing radius** (clothoid) used to enter or leave a circular curve gradually. | | **5** | **Vertical Curve** | A curve in the **vertical plane** (summit or valley) used on gradients of roads/railways. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** > *Module 3 places equal weightage on (a) classification of curves, (b) elements and geometry of the simple circular curve, and (c) the basic differences between simple, compound, reverse, transition, and vertical curves. Numerical problems on simple curve elements are board-favourites.* --- ## 1.4 Standard Designations Used by KTU Examiners Before diving deeper, lock in these universally-accepted symbols (used verbatim in **University question papers**): - **R** = Radius of the circular curve (in **metres**) - **I** (or Δ) = Deflection angle / Intersection angle (in **degrees**) - **T** = Tangent length (in **metres**) - **L** = Length of the curve (in **metres**) - **C** (or LC) = Long chord (in **metres**) - **E** = External distance (in **metres**) - **M** (or V) = Middle ordinate / Versed sine (in **metres**) These symbols will appear hundreds of times in your **ESE (End Semester Examination)** answers — memorize them like alphabet letters. --- ## 1.5 Why the Study of Curve Types Matters (Engineering Utility) | Field | Application of Curve Types | |---|---| | **Highway Engineering** | Horizontal and vertical alignment of roads, superelevation design. | | **Railway Engineering** | Transition curves for high-speed trains, reverse curves in hilly ghat sections. | | **Irrigation Canals** | Vertical curves for water-flow gradient transitions. | | **Pipeline Engineering** | Compound curves around obstructions. | | **Airport Runways** | Smooth tangential transitions to taxiways. | | **Urban Planning** | Reverse curves in hairpin bends of hill roads. | > **Key Insight:** *A B.Tech Civil Engineer who cannot design a curve cannot design a road, a railway, a canal, or an airport. Curve geometry is the backbone of all transportation engineering.* # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Type-1: Simple Circular Curve — The "Backbone" Curve A **simple curve** is a single arc of a circle joining the two tangents. It is the most frequently tested topic in KTU board papers. ### 2.1.1 Elements of a Simple Curve (Geometric Anatomy) Consider two tangents **T₁T₂** and **T₂T₁** intersecting at point **I** (or **V₁/V₂**). A circle of radius **R** touches both tangents. - **PC (Point of Curvature) / T₁ / B.C. (Beginning of Curve)** — point where the curve **leaves** the first tangent. - **PT (Point of Tangency) / T₂ / E.C. (End of Curve)** — point where the curve **meets** the second tangent. - **O** — Centre of the circular curve. - **O₁, O₂** — Points on the principal radii at PC and PT respectively. - **Long Chord (LC or C)** — Straight line from PC to PT. - **Versed Sine / Middle Ordinate (M or V)** — Perpendicular distance from the midpoint of the long chord to the midpoint of the curve. - **External Distance (E)** — Distance from the intersection point **I** to the midpoint of the curve. > [!NOTE] > **Memory Hook:** "PC" stands for **P**oint of **C**urvature, and "PT" stands for **P**oint of **T**angency. These two points are the boundary stones of every simple curve. ### 2.1.2 Operational Logic of Curve Geometry 1. Draw two tangents meeting at angle **I**. 2. Construct the angle bisector at **I**. 3. The angle bisector passes through the **centre O** of the circle. 4. Drop perpendiculars from **O** to both tangents — these are the principal radii of length **R**. 5. The foot of the perpendicular on the first tangent is **PC**; on the second is **PT**. 6. The arc **PC–PT** is the simple curve of length **L = πR·I°/180°**. --- ## 2.2 Type-2: Compound Curve A **compound curve** consists of **two or more simple circular arcs of different radii**, all curving in the **same direction**, joined tangentially without any kink. **When is it used?** - When terrain constraints prevent a single radius from fitting. - To follow the natural shape of a valley or hillside. - Common in hill-road alignment. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Property:** A compound curve has **more than one radius (R₁ ≠ R₂)**, but the **curvature is in the same sense** (both turning left, or both turning right). The point where the two arcs meet is the **Point of Compound Curvature (PCC)**. --- ## 2.3 Type-3: Reverse Curve A **reverse curve** is formed by **two simple circular arcs of equal (typically) or different radii curving in opposite directions**, with their centres on opposite sides of the common tangent at the **Point of Reverse Curvature (PRC)**. **When is it used?** - Hairpin bends in mountainous ghat roads. - Cross-overs in railway yards. - Canal alignments in switchbacks. > [!WARNING] > **Critical Disadvantage (often asked in viva):** A reverse curve has a **zero-radius point at the PRC**, which causes a **centrifugal jerk**. Hence, reverse curves are **avoided on highways** unless separated by a sufficient straight tangent (≥ 30 m) or a transition curve. --- ## 2.4 Type-4: Transition Curve A **transition curve** (also called a **spiral** or **easement curve**) is a curve whose **radius decreases (or increases) gradually** from infinity at the tangent point to a finite value **R** at the start of the circular curve. **Geometry:** The radius at any point is inversely proportional to the distance from the tangent point: $R \cdot L = R_c \cdot L_c = \text{constant}$. **Why is it needed?** - To introduce **superelevation gradually** (cant in railways). - To allow drivers to turn the steering wheel gradually, avoiding jerk. - Required on all **highways with design speed > 60 km/h** and **all railways**. > [!NOTE] > **Cubic Parabolic Spiral:** For short transitions, the curve is approximated by a cubic parabola $y = \dfrac{x^3}{6RL}$, which is mathematically simpler than the clothoid. --- ## 2.5 Type-5: Vertical Curve A **vertical curve** is a curve in the **vertical plane** used to provide a smooth change in gradient. | Sub-Type | Shape | Where Used | |---|---|---| | **Summit Curve (Crest)** | Convex upward (∩) | Hill tops, downgrade meeting upgrade. | | **Valley Curve (Sag)** | Concave upward (∪) | Valley bottoms, upgrade meeting downgrade. | **Geometry:** Usually a **parabola** of the form $y = \dfrac{ax^2}{2}$ with second derivative giving constant rate of change of grade. --- ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet) > **🚨 BOARD EXAM GOLD — The following table is memorized by every KTU topper.** | # | Element | Symbol | Formula | Approximate Formula (for small I) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Tangent Length** | **T** | $T = R \cdot \tan(I/2)$ | $T \approx \dfrac{R \cdot I}{2} \times \dfrac{\pi}{180°}$ (in radians) | | 2 | **Length of Curve** | **L** | $L = \dfrac{\pi R I°}{180°}$ | $L \approx R \cdot I$ (radians) | | 3 | **Long Chord** | **C / LC** | $C = 2R \cdot \sin(I/2)$ | $C \approx 2R \cdot (I/2)$ radians | | 4 | **External Distance** | **E** | $E = R \left[\sec(I/2) - 1\right]$ | $E \approx \dfrac{R \cdot I^2}{8}$ rad² | | 5 | **Middle Ordinate (Versed Sine)** | **M / V** | $M = R \left[1 - \cos(I/2)\right]$ | $M \approx \dfrac{R \cdot I^2}{8}$ rad² | | 6 | **Apex Distance** | **A** | $A = R \cdot \sec(I/2)$ | — | | 7 | **Degree of Curve (Arc definition)** | **D** | $D = \dfrac{1718.9}{R}$ (R in m) | Used in Railway Engineering | | 8 | **Degree of Curve (Chord definition)** | **D** | $D = 2 \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{30}{R}\right)$ | Used in Highway Engineering | | 9 | **Compound Curve Total Tangent** | **T_total** | $T_1 + T_2$ (with correction) | — | | 10 | **Reverse Curve Common Tangent** | — | $L_1 / L_2 = R_1 / R_2$ | Equal radii ⇒ equal lengths | | 11 | **Transition Spiral Length (rate of change of centrifugal acc.)** | **L** | $L = \dfrac{v^3}{C \cdot R}$ | $C$ = 0.5 to 0.8 m/s³ (IRC) | | 12 | **Spiral Angle** | **θ_s** | $\theta_s = \dfrac{L}{2R}$ radians | Approx. for cubic parabola | | 13 | **Vertical Curve Elevation** | **y** | $y = \dfrac{ax^2}{2}$ (parabolic) | $a$ = rate of change of grade | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical LaTeX Note (For Your Notes):** > When writing $\sec$ or $\sin$ inside a table cell, **always use parentheses** like $R \left[\sec(I/2) - 1\right]$ to avoid breaking the markdown table. **Never use the vertical bar \vert x \vert** inside tables — use \left( ... \right) instead. --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility Recap > [!NOTE] > **Industry Application Map:** > - **L&T Construction (Roads & Highways):** Uses simple + transition curves for expressway design at design speeds 100–120 km/h. > - **Indian Railways:** Uses 1° to 5° curves on broad gauge; transition curves mandatory above 100 km/h. > - **Kerala PWD (Hill Roads – Ghat Sections):** Reverse curves used on hairpin bends of Western Ghats. > - **Airport Authority of India (AAI):** Vertical curves on runway gradients to maintain sight distance. > - **KWA / Irrigation Dept:** Vertical curves on canal drops and falls. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Simple Curve Element Formulas > **Setting the Stage:** > Let **O** be the centre of the circle, **PC** and **PT** be the tangent points, and **I** be the intersection of the two tangents. The triangle **O–I–PC** is a right-angled triangle (since OI bisects angle I and OO₁ is perpendicular to the tangent at PC). ### 3.1.1 Derivation of Tangent Length **T** In the right triangle $\triangle O \, I \, \text{PC}$: - Hypotenuse $OI = R + E$ (since $OI = OP + PI = R + E$). Wait — let us be more rigorous. The triangle has: - $OP = R$ (radius, perpendicular to tangent at PC) - $OI = $ hypotenuse - $\angle IOP = I/2$ (half-deflection angle) - The adjacent side of $\angle I/2$ is the radius $R$, and the opposite side is **T**. $$ \tan\left(\frac{I}{2}\right) = \dfrac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} = \dfrac{T}{R} $$ Therefore: $$ T = R \cdot \tan\left(\frac{I}{2}\right) $$ ✅ **This is the master formula. Memorize it.** --- ### 3.1.2 Derivation of Length of Curve **L** The curve is an arc of a circle subtending angle **I** (radians) at the centre. By the arc-length formula: $$ \text{Arc length} = \text{Radius} \times \text{Angle (radians)} $$ Converting **I** from degrees to radians: $$ I_{\text{rad}} = I° \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{180°} $$ Therefore: $$ L = R \cdot I_{\text{rad}} = R \cdot I° \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{180°} = \dfrac{\pi R I°}{180°} $$ > **Approximation for small I (in radians):** $L \approx R \cdot I$ (since $\pi/180 \approx 0.0175$ becomes negligible for direct radian input). --- ### 3.1.3 Derivation of Long Chord **C (LC)** The long chord is the straight line **PC to PT**. Triangle **O–PC–PT** is an **isosceles triangle** with two sides equal to **R** and apex angle **I** at the centre. Using the **chord-angle relation**: $$ C = 2R \cdot \sin\left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right) $$ **Proof (using isosceles triangle splitting):** Drop the perpendicular from O to the midpoint M of the long chord. This perpendicular bisects the apex angle I. In the right triangle: $$ \sin\left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right) = \dfrac{C/2}{R} $$ Multiplying both sides by 2R: $$ C = 2R \cdot \sin\left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right) $$ --- ### 3.1.4 Derivation of External Distance **E** In the right triangle $\triangle O \, I \, \text{PC}$: - $OI = $ hypotenuse $= R + E$ (because E = IP, the distance from I to the curve) - Adjacent side to angle $I/2$ is $R$ (the radius $OP$). - Hypotenuse is $OI$. Therefore: $$ \cos\left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right) = \dfrac{R}{R + E} $$ Solving for E: $$ R + E = \dfrac{R}{\cos(I/2)} = R \cdot \sec(I/2) $$ $$ E = R \cdot \sec(I/2) - R $$ $$ E = R \left[ \sec\left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right) - 1 \right] $$ --- ### 3.1.5 Derivation of Middle Ordinate (Versed Sine) **M** The middle ordinate M is the perpendicular distance from the midpoint of the long chord to the midpoint of the curve. In the right triangle formed by the centre O, the midpoint of the chord (call it M_c), and the midpoint of the curve (call it M_v): - Hypotenuse = $R$ (the radius to the midpoint of the curve) - Adjacent side = $R - M$ (radius minus middle ordinate) - Angle at O = $I/2$ (half the central angle) Therefore: $$ \cos\left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right) = \dfrac{R - M}{R} $$ Solving for M: $$ R - M = R \cdot \cos(I/2) $$ $$ M = R - R \cdot \cos(I/2) $$ $$ M = R \left[ 1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right) \right] $$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Example — Full KTU Board-Style Solution > **Question (KTU 2024 Pattern):** > *Two tangents intersect at an angle of **60°**. A simple circular curve of radius **300 m** is to be inserted. Calculate all the elements of the curve.* ### Step-1: Identify Given Data - Deflection angle $I = 60°$ - Radius $R = 300$ m ### Step-2: Compute Tangent Length T $$ T = R \cdot \tan\left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right) = 300 \cdot \tan(30°) $$ $$ \tan(30°) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.5774 $$ $$ T = 300 \times 0.5774 = 173.21 \text{ m} $$ ### Step-3: Compute Length of Curve L $$ L = \dfrac{\pi R I°}{180°} = \dfrac{\pi \times 300 \times 60}{180} $$ $$ L = \dfrac{\pi \times 100 \times 60}{60} = 100\pi = 314.16 \text{ m} $$ ### Step-4: Compute Long Chord C $$ C = 2R \cdot \sin\left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right) = 2 \times 300 \times \sin(30°) $$ $$ \sin(30°) = 0.5 $$ $$ C = 600 \times 0.5 = 300.00 \text{ m} $$ ### Step-5: Compute External Distance E $$ E = R \left[ \sec(I/2) - 1 \right] = 300 \left[ \sec(30°) - 1 \right] $$ $$ \sec(30°) = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.1547 $$ $$ E = 300 \times (1.1547 - 1) = 300 \times 0.1547 = 46.41 \text{ m} $$ ### Step-6: Compute Middle Ordinate M $$ M = R \left[ 1 - \cos(I/2) \right] = 300 \left[ 1 - \cos(30°) \right] $$ $$ \cos(30°) = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660 $$ $$ M = 300 \times (1 - 0.8660) = 300 \times 0.1340 = 40.19 \text{ m} $$ ### ✅ **Final Tabulated Answer** | Element | Symbol | Value | |---|---|---| | Tangent Length | **T** | **173.21 m** | | Length of Curve | **L** | **314.16 m** | | Long Chord | **C** | **300.00 m** | | External Distance | **E** | **46.41 m** | | Middle Ordinate | **M** | **40.19 m** | > **Examiner's Tip:** *Always present numerical answers in a clean tabulated form. KTU examiners reward neatness, units, and clarity. Skipping units = 0.5 mark deducted.* --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Curve > [!NOTE] > **Master Decision Table for KTU Viva & Design Problems:** | Design Situation | Recommended Curve Type | Why? | |---|---|---| | Straight road with one bend, open terrain | **Simple Curve** | Easiest geometry, single R. | | Hill road with multiple bends, similar direction | **Compound Curve** | Different R fits terrain. | | Hairpin bend on a ghat road | **Reverse Curve** | Two opposite-direction arcs. | | Highway design speed > 60 km/h | **Transition + Simple** | Gradual superelevation. | | Railway high-speed corridor | **Cubic Parabola Spiral** | Comfort + cant transition. | | Canal crossing a ridge | **Summit (Crest) Vertical** | Sight distance + drainage. | | Canal crossing a valley | **Valley (Sag) Vertical** | Drainage + headlight sight. | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Classification Flowchart — Types of Curves ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Curves in Surveying] --> B[Horizontal Curves] A --> C[Vertical Curves] B --> D[Simple Circular Curve] B --> E[Compound Curve] B --> F[Reverse Curve] B --> G[Transition Curve] G --> G1[Cubic Parabola] G --> G2[Clothoid Spiral] G --> G3[Lemniscate] C --> H[Summit Curve] C --> I[Valley Curve] H --> H1[Parabolic Crest] I --> I1[Parabolic Sag] D --> D1[Single Radius R] D --> D2[Constant Curvature] E --> E1[Two Arcs Same Direction] E --> E2[Different Radii R1 and R2] F --> F1[Two Arcs Opposite Direction] F --> F2[Equal or Different Radii] style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff style C fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff style D fill:#5b9bd5,color:#fff style E fill:#5b9bd5,color:#fff style F fill:#5b9bd5,color:#fff style G fill:#5b9bd5,color:#fff style H fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000 style I fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000 ``` > **Reading the Chart:** *Every curve in surveying falls under Horizontal or Vertical. From Horizontal, you branch into Simple, Compound, Reverse, and Transition. From Transition, you further specialize into Cubic Parabola, Clothoid, and Lemniscate. From Vertical, you branch into Summit (convex up) and Valley (concave up).* --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Geometry of a Simple Circular Curve ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SimpleCurve["Geometry of Simple Circular Curve"] direction LR A1[PC: Point of Curvature
B.C. Start of Curve] -- Tangent T1 to I --> A2[I: Intersection Point
Deflection Angle I] A2 -- Tangent I to T2 --> A3[PT: Point of Tangency
E.C. End of Curve] A1 -- Long Chord C --> A3 A4[O: Center of Circle] -- Radius R --> A1 A4 -- Radius R --> A3 A4 -- Distance R plus E --> A2 A5[M_v: Midpoint of Curve
Versed Sine M] -. Perpendicular .-> A6[M_c: Midpoint of Long Chord] A5 -- Radius R --> A4 end style A1 fill:#c6e0b4,color:#000 style A3 fill:#c6e0b4,color:#000 style A2 fill:#ffd966,color:#000 style A4 fill:#f4b084,color:#000 style A5 fill:#bdd7ee,color:#000 style A6 fill:#bdd7ee,color:#000 ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** *PC and PT lie on the circle, both at distance R from the centre O. The intersection point I lies on the angle bisector OI, at distance R + E from O. The midpoint of the curve M_v is offset from the midpoint of the long chord M_c by the middle ordinate M. The line PC–PT is the long chord of length C.* --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Chart — Compound vs Reverse Curve ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph CompoundLayout["Compound Curve Layout"] direction LR CP1[Tangent 1] --> CPA[PC1
Radius R1] --> CPB[PCC
Point of Compound Curvature] --> CPC[PT2
Radius R2] --> CP2[Tangent 2] end subgraph ReverseLayout["Reverse Curve Layout"] direction LR RP1[Tangent 1] --> RPA[PC1
Radius R1] --> RPB[PRC
Point of Reverse Curvature] --> RPC[PT2
Radius R2] --> RP2[Tangent 2] end CompoundLayout --> Note1[Note: Both arcs curve in same direction, R1 not equal to R2] ReverseLayout --> Note2[Note: Arcs curve in opposite directions, common tangent at PRC] style CP1 fill:#d9e1f2,color:#000 style CP2 fill:#d9e1f2,color:#000 style CPA fill:#a9d08e,color:#000 style CPB fill:#ffc000,color:#000 style CPC fill:#a9d08e,color:#000 style RP1 fill:#d9e1f2,color:#000 style RP2 fill:#d9e1f2,color:#000 style RPA fill:#a9d08e,color:#000 style RPB fill:#c00000,color:#fff style RPC fill:#a9d08e,color:#000 style Note1 fill:#fff2cc,color:#000 style Note2 fill:#fff2cc,color:#000 ``` > **Reading the Chart:** *In a compound curve, both arcs curve in the **same sense** (e.g., both turning left). The PCC is highlighted yellow as a "soft" transition. In a reverse curve, the arcs curve in **opposite senses**, and the PRC is highlighted red as a "sharp/unsafe" transition (hence avoided on highways).* --- ## 4.4 Transition Curve Working Principle (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR T1[Tangent Straight Section] --> INF[Infinity R
Zero Curvature] --> SP[Spiral Transition Curve
Radius Decreases Gradually] --> CIRC[Circular Curve of Radius R] --> SP2[Exit Spiral
Radius Increases Gradually] --> INF2[Infinity R
Zero Curvature] --> T2[Next Tangent Straight] SP -.-> EQ1[R times L equals Constant] SP -.-> EQ2[Cant increases linearly from 0 to full superelevation] style T1 fill:#d9e1f2,color:#000 style INF fill:#bdd7ee,color:#000 style SP fill:#a9d08e,color:#000 style CIRC fill:#ffc000,color:#000 style SP2 fill:#a9d08e,color:#000 style INF2 fill:#bdd7ee,color:#000 style T2 fill:#d9e1f2,color:#000 style EQ1 fill:#fff2cc,color:#000 style EQ2 fill:#fff2cc,color:#000 ``` > **Reading the Chart:** *The spiral starts at infinity radius (no curvature) at the tangent point, gradually tightens to radius R at the start of the circular curve, and (optionally) unwinds symmetrically on the exit side. This allows superelevation to be built up linearly and the driver to turn the steering wheel smoothly.* # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define a simple circular curve. List its main elements with a neat sketch.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > A **simple circular curve** is a single arc of a circle of radius **R** that connects two intersecting straight tangents, providing a gradual change in direction. > > **Main elements (with figure described in words):** > > 1. **PC (Point of Curvature)** — point where the curve begins. > 2. **PT (Point of Tangency)** — point where the curve ends. > 3. **I (Intersection point)** — where the two tangents meet. > 4. **R** — radius of the circle. > 5. **T** — tangent length from PC to I (or I to PT). > 6. **L** — length of the curve (arc PC to PT). > 7. **LC (Long Chord)** — straight line from PC to PT. > 8. **E** — external distance (from I to midpoint of curve). > 9. **M (Versed sine)** — middle ordinate from midpoint of long chord to midpoint of curve. > > *(Sketch: A bow-shaped arc touching two straight lines meeting at I, with O as centre, perpendicular radii to PC and PT.)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between a compound curve and a reverse curve.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > | Aspect | **Compound Curve** | **Reverse Curve** | > |---|---|---| > | **Definition** | Two or more circular arcs of different radii curving in the **same direction** and meeting tangentially. | Two circular arcs curving in **opposite directions** sharing a common tangent. | > | **Radius relation** | $R_1 \neq R_2$ (different radii). | $R_1$ and $R_2$ may be equal or different. | > | **Point of junction** | **PCC** (Point of Compound Curvature) — safe transition. | **PRC** (Point of Reverse Curvature) — sharp, causes jerk. | > | **Application** | Hill roads, valley routes. | Hairpin bends, ghat roads, canal switchbacks. | > | **Safety on highways** | Generally permitted. | Avoided unless separated by a tangent. | --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice ### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** > **A simple circular curve is to be set out between two tangents intersecting at a deflection angle of 48°30′. The radius of the curve is 400 m. Calculate:** > > **(a) (7 marks)** Tangent length, length of curve, and long chord. > > **(b) (7 marks)** External distance and middle ordinate. Also find the chainage of PC if the chainage of the intersection point I is 1250.00 m. #### Model Solution **Given:** - $I = 48°30' = 48.5°$ - $R = 400$ m - Chainage of I = 1250.00 m --- ### Part (a) — T, L, C (7 marks) **Step 1: Tangent Length T** $$ T = R \cdot \tan\left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right) = 400 \cdot \tan(24°15') $$ $$ \tan(24°15') = 0.4509 \text{ (from tables)} $$ $$ T = 400 \times 0.4509 = 180.36 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2: Length of Curve L** $$ L = \dfrac{\pi R I°}{180°} = \dfrac{\pi \times 400 \times 48.5}{180} $$ $$ L = \dfrac{3.1416 \times 400 \times 48.5}{180} = \dfrac{60946.4}{180} = 338.59 \text{ m} $$ **Step 3: Long Chord C** $$ C = 2R \cdot \sin\left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right) = 2 \times 400 \times \sin(24°15') $$ $$ \sin(24°15') = 0.4107 $$ $$ C = 800 \times 0.4107 = 328.56 \text{ m} $$ --- ### Part (b) — E, M, Chainage of PC (7 marks) **Step 4: External Distance E** $$ E = R \left[ \sec(I/2) - 1 \right] = 400 \left[ \sec(24°15') - 1 \right] $$ $$ \sec(24°15') = \dfrac{1}{\cos(24°15')} = \dfrac{1}{0.9114} = 1.0972 $$ $$ E = 400 \times (1.0972 - 1) = 400 \times 0.0972 = 38.88 \text{ m} $$ **Step 5: Middle Ordinate M** $$ M = R \left[ 1 - \cos(I/2) \right] = 400 \left[ 1 - 0.9114 \right] $$ $$ M = 400 \times 0.0886 = 35.44 \text{ m} $$ **Step 6: Chainage of PC** By the standard relationship: $$ \text{Chainage of PC} = \text{Chainage of I} - T $$ $$ \text{Chainage of PC} = 1250.00 - 180.36 = 1069.64 \text{ m} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bonus 1-Mark Concept:** *Chainage of PT = Chainage of PC + L = 1069.64 + 338.59 = 1408.23 m. Always cross-verify: Chainage of PT = Chainage of I + T = 1250 + 180.36 = 1430.36 m. Wait — these differ by L = 22 m only if there is a discrepancy. The correct relationship is: Chainage of PT = Chainage of PC + L = 1069.64 + 338.59 = 1408.23 m. The check: Chainage of I = Chainage of PC + T = 1069.64 + 180.36 = 1250.00 m ✅.* ### ✅ **Final Tabulated Answer** | Element | Value | |---|---| | Tangent length **T** | **180.36 m** | | Length of curve **L** | **338.59 m** | | Long chord **C** | **328.56 m** | | External distance **E** | **38.88 m** | | Middle ordinate **M** | **35.44 m** | | Chainage of PC | **1069.64 m** | | Chainage of PT | **1408.23 m** | --- ### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** > **(a) (7 marks)** Explain the **classification of curves** used in highway and railway engineering with neat sketches. State the situations where each type is preferred. > > **(b) (7 marks)** Two tangents intersect at a chainage of 1500 m with a deflection angle of 40°. A compound curve of radii 300 m and 200 m is to be set out such that the longer radius is on the side of the smaller deflection. Calculate the **total tangent length** and the **chainage of the PCC**. #### Model Solution ### Part (a) — Classification of Curves (7 marks) Curves are classified into **two broad categories**: **1. Horizontal Curves** (curves in the horizontal plane): - **Simple Circular Curve** — single arc of constant radius. Used where terrain is open and one arc fits the alignment. - **Compound Curve** — two or more arcs of different radii curving in the **same direction**. Used in hilly terrain where a single radius does not fit. - **Reverse Curve** — two arcs curving in **opposite directions** with a common tangent. Used in hairpin bends, ghat roads, and railway yards. - **Transition Curve** — a spiral of **progressively changing radius**, used at the entry and exit of circular curves on highways (design speed > 60 km/h) and all modern railways to introduce superelevation gradually and provide rider comfort. **2. Vertical Curves** (curves in the vertical plane): - **Summit (Crest) Curve** — convex upward. Used at hilltops, downgrade meeting upgrade. - **Valley (Sag) Curve** — concave upward. Used at valley bottoms, upgrade meeting downgrade. > *(Sketches: Each type drawn as a block schematic with PC, PT, and the directional sense of curvature clearly shown.)* --- ### Part (b) — Compound Curve Numerical (7 marks) **Given:** - Chainage of I = 1500 m - Total deflection angle $I = 40°$ - $R_1 = 300$ m (longer radius, on the side of smaller deflection) - $R_2 = 200$ m (shorter radius) **Step 1: Find the two sub-angles $I_1$ and $I_2$** In a compound curve problem, the deflection angles are typically given or split based on tangent constraints. Here we assume a symmetric split proportional to the radii for first-cut estimation: $$ \dfrac{I_1}{I_2} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = \dfrac{200}{300} = \dfrac{2}{3} $$ Let $I_1 = 2k$ and $I_2 = 3k$.
