Then $I_1 + I_2 = 5k = 40°$, so $k = 8°$. $$ I_1 = 16°, \quad I_2 = 24° $$ *(KTU Boards generally split compound curve problems this way when no other constraint is given.)* **Step 2: Compute $T_1$ and $T_2$** $$ T_1 = R_1 \cdot \tan(I_1/2) = 300 \cdot \tan(8°) $$ $$ \tan(8°) = 0.1405 $$ $$ T_1 = 300 \times 0.1405 = 42.16 \text{ m} $$ $$ T_2 = R_2 \cdot \tan(I_2/2) = 200 \cdot \tan(12°) $$ $$ \tan(12°) = 0.2126 $$ $$ T_2 = 200 \times 0.2126 = 42.51 \text{ m} $$ **Step 3: Total Tangent Length** $$ T_{\text{total}} = T_1 + T_2 = 42.16 + 42.51 = 84.67 \text{ m} $$ **Step 4: Chainage of PCC** $$ \text{Chainage of PC} = \text{Chainage of I} - T_{\text{total}} = 1500 - 84.67 = 1415.33 \text{ m} $$ Length of first arc: $$ L_1 = \dfrac{\pi R_1 I_1}{180°} = \dfrac{\pi \times 300 \times 16}{180} = 83.78 \text{ m} $$ $$ \text{Chainage of PCC} = \text{Chainage of PC} + L_1 = 1415.33 + 83.78 = 1499.11 \text{ m} $$ ### ✅ **Final Tabulated Answer** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Sub-angle $I_1$ | **16°** | | Sub-angle $I_2$ | **24°** | | $T_1$ | **42.16 m** | | $T_2$ | **42.51 m** | | $T_{\text{total}}$ | **84.67 m** | | Chainage of PC | **1415.33 m** | | Chainage of PCC | **1499.11 m** | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **🚨 WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS IN THIS TOPIC:** > > 1. **Unit Mistakes (–0.5 to –1 mark):** Forgetting to write units (m) on every numerical answer. KTU examiners are strict about units. *Always append "m" after every length.* > > 2. **Degree vs. Radian Confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Using $I$ in degrees inside the formula $L = R \cdot I$ without dividing by 180° and multiplying by π. *Always write $L = \pi R I° / 180°$ explicitly when $I$ is in degrees.* > > 3. **Chainage of PC vs. PT Mix-up (–1 mark):** Writing Chainage of PC = Chainage of I + T instead of −T. *Memorize: PC is BEFORE I by distance T.* > > 4. **Compound Curve Tangent Sum (–2 marks):** Forgetting that the common tangent at PCC means $T_{\text{total}} = T_1 + T_2$ only if the chainage geometry is honoured. *Always draw a rough diagram and label PC, PCC, PT, I, $T_1$, $T_2$.* > > 5. **Trig Table Reading Errors (–0.5 to –1 mark):** Using $\tan(24°15') = 0.4509$ carelessly. *Show the table reference or use a calculator with 4-decimal accuracy.* > > 6. **Skipping the Sketch (–1 to –2 marks):** KTU answers without a labelled sketch lose 1–2 marks easily. *Even a rough free-hand diagram with PC, PT, I, O, R, T, L labelled fetches full geometry credit.* > > 7. **Forgetting to Verify (–0.5 mark):** Not cross-checking $T_1 + T_2 = $ total tangent and chainage of PT = chainage of PC + $L_1$ + $L_2$ = chainage of I + $T_{\text{total}}$. *Always do a sanity check in the last 30 seconds.* --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **📌 HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — TYPES OF CURVES** > > - [x] **Curves** are smooth bending alignments used to connect two straight tangents that meet at an angle. > - [x] **Simple curve** = single arc, constant R. Most board-tested. > - [x] **Compound curve** = multiple arcs, **same direction**, **different radii**. > - [x] **Reverse curve** = two arcs, **opposite directions**, common tangent at PRC. Avoided on highways. > - [x] **Transition curve** = radius changes from ∞ to R, allows smooth superelevation and steering. > - [x] **Vertical curves** = Summit (∩) and Valley (∪) — both are parabolic in practice. > - [x] **Master formulas** to memorize cold: > - $T = R \tan(I/2)$ > - $L = \pi R I° / 180°$ > - $C = 2R \sin(I/2)$ > - $E = R [\sec(I/2) - 1]$ > - $M = R [1 - \cos(I/2)]$ > - [x] **Chainage of PC = Chainage of I − T**. > - [x] **Chainage of PT = Chainage of PC + L** = **Chainage of I + T** (must match). > - [x] **Compound curve** total tangent $T_{\text{total}} = T_1 + T_2$ (with proper geometry). > - [x] **Reverse curve** has jerk at PRC — never used on high-speed corridors. > - [x] **Transition curve** uses cubic parabola $y = x^3 / (6RL)$ for short spirals. > - [x] Always draw a **labelled sketch** in every answer. > - [x] Always show **units** (m) on every numerical value. > - [x] Always **verify** chainage consistency at the end of the solution. > - [x] For small angles (radian mode): $L \approx R \cdot I$, $T \approx R \cdot I/2$, $C \approx 2R \cdot I/2$, $E \approx M \approx R I^2/8$. > - [x] The **angle bisector of I** always passes through the **centre O** of the simple curve. > - [x] **Long chord bisects** the central angle, and the radius to the midpoint is perpendicular to the long chord.

topics.title: Theory of Least Squares
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET304 - SURVEYING AND GEOMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Theory of Least Squares

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Principle of Least Squares (Gauss-Markov Principle)**
> *"The most probable value of an unknown quantity, obtained from a set of observations of varying precision, is the one that makes the sum of the squares of the weighted residuals (errors) a minimum."*

Mathematically, if $v_i$ represents the **residual** of the $i^{th}$ observation and $w_i$ represents its **weight**, then the least squares criterion demands:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i v_i^2 = \text{Minimum}$$

This is the foundational axiom upon which all modern **adjustment computations**, **network adjustments**, and **statistical analysis of surveying measurements** are built.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Bullseye" Analogy**
> Imagine you are throwing 5 darts at a dartboard, and you are slightly nearsighted. Each dart lands *somewhere* near the bullseye, but not exactly on it. If you are asked: *"Where is the most probable position of the bullseye?"* — you wouldn't pick the closest dart, nor the farthest. You would take the **centroid (average) of all dart positions**. That single point *minimizes* the sum of the squared distances from every dart to it. This is **Least Squares in one line**.
>
> In surveying, the "bullseye" is the **true (unknown) value** of a quantity (a distance, an angle, a coordinate), and the "darts" are **observations** contaminated by random errors. The most probable value is the one that best fits all observations simultaneously.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are standard survey adjustment metrics you **must memorize**:
> - **Standard Error (σ)** → $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum v_i^2}{n-1}}$ (for unit weight observations)
> - **Probable Error (PE)** → $PE = 0.6745 \times \sigma$ (≈ 2/3 σ)
> - **90% Error** → $1.6449 \times \sigma$
> - **95% Error** → $1.9600 \times \sigma$
> - **99% Error** → $2.5758 \times \sigma$
> - **Mean Error (η)** → $\eta = 0.7979 \times \sigma$
> - **Weight of an observation** is inversely proportional to its variance: $w_i = \dfrac{k}{\sigma_i^2}$, where $k$ is a constant of proportionality.

## 1.4 Key Terminology

| Term | Formal Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **True Error (e)** | The difference between the true value and the observed value: $e_i = X - l_i$ |
| **Residual (v)** | The difference between the most probable value and the observed value: $v_i = \bar{x} - l_i$ |
| **Most Probable Value (MPV)** | The value obtained by the principle of least squares |
| **Weight (w)** | A measure of the relative precision of an observation |
| **Observation Equations** | Equations expressing the relationship between observations and unknown parameters |
| **Condition Equations** | Equations expressing the geometric/physical constraints among the observations |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Least Squares? — The Logic Behind the Method

Every surveying measurement contains **three classes of errors**:

1. **Mistakes (Blunders):** Human errors (e.g., misreading a tape). These must be **eliminated** by careful field procedure, not adjusted.
2. **Systematic Errors:** Errors following a physical/mathematical law (e.g., tape not at standard temperature). These are **corrected** by applying known corrections.
3. **Random Errors:** Small, unavoidable errors following the **Normal (Gaussian) Probability Law**. The Principle of Least Squares deals with these.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Normal Law of Errors (Gauss's Law)** states that random errors follow:
> $$y = \frac{h}{\sqrt{\pi}} \cdot e^{-h^2 x^2}$$
> where $h$ is the **Measure of Precision** and $x$ is the magnitude of the error. Least squares is the *mathematical consequence* of this law.

## 2.2 Fundamental Assumptions of the Theory

For least squares adjustment to be strictly valid, observations must satisfy:

- **Assumption 1:** Errors are **random** and follow the Normal distribution.
- **Assumption 2:** The **mathematical expectation (mean) of errors is zero**: $E(e_i) = 0$.
- **Assumption 3:** Observations are **uncorrelated** (no covariance between them).
- **Assumption 4:** Observations have **equal precision (equal weight)** unless weights are explicitly assigned.
- **Assumption 5:** The **sum of the weighted squared residuals is minimized**.

## 2.3 Derivation of the Arithmetic Mean (Most Probable Value for Equal Weight Observations)

Given $n$ independent observations $l_1, l_2, l_3, \ldots, l_n$ of equal precision for a quantity whose true value is $X$:

The residual of the $i^{th}$ observation is:
$$v_i = \bar{x} - l_i$$

where $\bar{x}$ is the most probable value. The least squares condition is:
$$\sum v_i^2 = \text{Minimum}$$

Differentiating with respect to $\bar{x}$ and setting to zero:
$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \bar{x}}\left[\sum (\bar{x} - l_i)^2\right] = 2\sum (\bar{x} - l_i) = 0$$

This gives $n\bar{x} = \sum l_i$, hence:
$$\boxed{\bar{x} = \frac{\sum l_i}{n}}$$

> The **arithmetic mean is the most probable value** of a quantity observed several times with equal precision.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| S.No. | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum l_i}{n}$ | Most probable value (equal weights) | Same as $l_i$ |
| 2 | $v_i = \bar{x} - l_i$ | Residual of $i^{th}$ observation | Same as $l_i$ |
| 3 | $\sum v_i = 0$ | Check sum (for equal weight) | Dimensionless check |
| 4 | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum v_i^2}{n-1}$ | Variance of unit weight observation | (units)$^2$ |
| 5 | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum v_i^2}{n-1}}$ | Standard error of unit weight | Same as $l_i$ |
| 6 | $PE = 0.6745\,\sigma$ | Probable error | Same as $l_i$ |
| 7 | $w_i = \dfrac{\sigma_0^2}{\sigma_i^2}$ | Weight of $i^{th}$ observation | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum w_i l_i}{\sum w_i}$ | Weighted mean (most probable value, unequal weights) | Same as $l_i$ |
| 9 | $\sigma_0^2 = \dfrac{\sum w_i v_i^2}{n-1}$ | Variance of unit weight (weighted case) | (units)$^2$ |
| 10 | $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\sqrt{\sum w_i}}$ | Standard error of weighted mean | Same as $l_i$ |
| 11 | $V = aX + bY + cZ \ldots$ | Error propagation (linear) | Function of variances |
| 12 | $\sigma_V^2 = a^2\sigma_X^2 + b^2\sigma_Y^2 + c^2\sigma_Z^2$ | Variance of sum (independent variables) | (units)$^2$ |
| 13 | $V = aX_1 \pm bX_2 \Rightarrow \sigma_V^2 = a^2\sigma_1^2 + b^2\sigma_2^2$ | Two-variable propagation | (units)$^2$ |
| 14 | $\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} = \dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}$ | Weight relation (single quantity) | Ratio |
| 15 | $y = a + bx$ | Linear regression model | — |
| 16 | $b = \dfrac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n\sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2}$ | Slope of best-fit line | (units of y)/(units of x) |
| 17 | $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$ | Intercept of best-fit line | Same as $y$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Tip for KTU:** The constants **0.6745 (PE), 0.7979 (Mean Error), 1.6449 (90%)** appear *very frequently* in board examinations. Always quote them to **4 decimal places**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Geodetic Control Networks:** Adjustment of triangulation and trilateration networks (millimeter-level accuracy).
- **GNSS / GPS Surveying:** Baseline processing, network adjustment, datum transformation.
- **Photogrammetry:** Bundle block adjustment, aerial triangulation.
- **Deformation Monitoring:** Detecting millimeter-scale movements in dams, bridges, and high-rise buildings.
- **GIS & Cartography:** Geometric rectification, coordinate transformation, least-squares image matching.
- **Hydrographic Surveying:** Sea-bed mapping and tidal data smoothing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Weighted Mean (Most Probable Value with Unequal Weights)

**Given:** $n$ observations $l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_n$ of a quantity $X$ with weights $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n$.

**Step 1 — Write the observation equations:** Each observation gives:
$$l_i = X + e_i \quad \text{where } e_i = \text{true error}$$

**Step 2 — Express residuals:**
$$v_i = \bar{x} - l_i$$

**Step 3 — Least Squares Criterion:**
$$\phi(\bar{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i v_i^2 = \sum w_i(\bar{x} - l_i)^2 = \text{Minimum}$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\bar{x}$ and equate to zero:**
$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \bar{x}} = 2\sum w_i(\bar{x} - l_i) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**
$$\bar{x}\sum w_i = \sum w_i l_i$$

**Step 6 — Final result (Weighted Mean):**
$$\boxed{\bar{x} = \frac{\sum w_i l_i}{\sum w_i}}$$

**Step 7 — Check equation:** A powerful verification identity:
$$\sum w_i v_i = 0$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Standard Error of the Weighted Mean

**Given:** Variance of unit weight is $\sigma_0^2 = \dfrac{\sum w_i v_i^2}{n-1}$ and the weighted mean is $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum w_i l_i}{\sum w_i}$.

**Step 1 — Treat the weighted mean as a linear function of independent observations:**
$$\bar{x} = \left(\frac{w_1}{\sum w}\right) l_1 + \left(\frac{w_2}{\sum w}\right) l_2 + \ldots + \left(\frac{w_n}{\sum w}\right) l_n$$

**Step 2 — Define linear coefficients:**
$$a_i = \frac{w_i}{\sum w}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the error propagation law for a linear function:**
$$\sigma_{\bar{x}}^2 = \sum a_i^2 \sigma_i^2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $a_i = w_i / \sum w$ and $\sigma_i^2 = \sigma_0^2 / w_i$ (from weight definition):**
$$\sigma_{\bar{x}}^2 = \sum \left(\frac{w_i}{\sum w}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{\sigma_0^2}{w_i} = \frac{\sigma_0^2}{(\sum w)^2} \cdot \sum w_i = \frac{\sigma_0^2}{\sum w_i}$$

**Step 5 — Take the square root:**
$$\boxed{\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\sqrt{\sum w_i}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** As more precise observations (high weights) are added, $\sum w_i$ grows, and the standard error of the mean **decreases** as $1/\sqrt{\sum w_i}$. This is the classical *√n law* of averaging.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Least Squares Fit of a Straight Line (Linear Regression)

**Given:** $n$ data points $(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)$ with errors assumed to be in $y$ only. We want to find the best-fit line:
$$y = a + bx$$

**Step 1 — Residual of the $i^{th}$ point (vertical distance from the line):**
$$v_i = (a + bx_i) - y_i$$

**Step 2 — Least squares criterion:**
$$\phi(a,b) = \sum v_i^2 = \sum [(a + bx_i) - y_i]^2 = \text{Minimum}$$

**Step 3 — Partial derivatives set to zero (necessary conditions for minimum):**
$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial a} = 2\sum [(a + bx_i) - y_i] = 0$$
$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial b} = 2\sum x_i[(a + bx_i) - y_i] = 0$$

**Step 4 — Expand to obtain the Normal Equations:**
$$an + b\sum x_i = \sum y_i$$
$$a\sum x_i + b\sum x_i^2 = \sum x_i y_i$$

**Step 5 — Solve simultaneously using Cramer's rule (a standard KTU step). Let $D = n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2$:**

$$b = \frac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$$

$$a = \frac{\sum y_i - b\sum x_i}{n} = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$$

**Step 6 — The final best-fit line equation:**
$$\boxed{y = (\bar{y} - b\bar{x}) + bx}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (Standard KTU Problem):**

> A surveyor measures the height of a tower from 4 stations. The horizontal distances (x, in m) and corresponding vertical angles (y, in degrees) are:
>
> | Point | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
> | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
> | $x$ | 30 | 45 | 60 | 75 |
> | $y$ | 12.4 | 10.8 | 9.5 | 8.2 |
>
> Fit a straight line $y = a + bx$ by the method of least squares.

**Solution — Step-by-step:**

**Step 1 — Compute the required summations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 4 \\
\sum x_i &= 30 + 45 + 60 + 75 = 210 \\
\sum y_i &= 12.4 + 10.8 + 9.5 + 8.2 = 40.9 \\
\sum x_i^2 &= 900 + 2025 + 3600 + 5625 = 12150 \\
\sum x_i y_i &= (30)(12.4) + (45)(10.8) + (60)(9.5) + (75)(8.2) \\
&= 372 + 486 + 570 + 615 = 2043
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the determinant of the normal equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2 \\
&= (4)(12150) - (210)^2 \\
&= 48600 - 44100 = 4500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute slope $b$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \frac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{D} \\
&= \frac{(4)(2043) - (210)(40.9)}{4500} \\
&= \frac{8172 - 8589}{4500} \\
&= \frac{-417}{4500} = -0.0927
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute intercept $a$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \bar{y} - b\bar{x} = \frac{\sum y_i}{n} - b \cdot \frac{\sum x_i}{n} \\
&= \frac{40.9}{4} - (-0.0927) \cdot \frac{210}{4} \\
&= 10.225 - (-0.0927)(52.5) \\
&= 10.225 + 4.86675 = 15.092
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — The best-fit equation:**

$$\boxed{y = 15.092 - 0.0927\,x}$$

**Step 6 — Compute residuals for check ($\sum v_i \approx 0$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_1 &= (15.092 - 0.0927 \times 30) - 12.4 = 12.311 - 12.4 = -0.089 \\
v_2 &= (15.092 - 0.0927 \times 45) - 10.8 = 10.921 - 10.8 = 0.121 \\
v_3 &= (15.092 - 0.0927 \times 60) - 9.5 = 9.530 - 9.5 = 0.030 \\
v_4 &= (15.092 - 0.0927 \times 75) - 8.2 = 8.140 - 8.2 = -0.060
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Verify:** $\sum v_i = -0.089 + 0.121 + 0.030 - 0.060 = 0.002 \approx 0$ ✓ (small rounding error)

**Step 8 — Standard error of estimate:**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum v_i^2}{n-2}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0079 + 0.0146 + 0.0009 + 0.0036}{2}} = \sqrt{0.0135} = 0.116$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The negative slope ($b = -0.0927$) confirms that vertical angle decreases linearly with distance (as expected geometrically for a fixed tower height), and the model is suitable for interpolation between $x = 30$ m and $x = 75$ m.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Adjustment of a Simple Triangle (Triangulation Example)

**Problem Setup:** A triangle $ABC$ has three measured interior angles $A$, $B$, $C$ (in degrees):
$$A = 58°\,30'\,15'', \quad B = 71°\,20'\,30'', \quad C = 50°\,08'\,45''$$

**Step 1 — Compute the misclosure (the actual sum of angles minus the theoretical sum of 180°):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Observed Sum} &= 58°\,30'\,15'' + 71°\,20'\,30'' + 50°\,08'\,45'' \\
&= 179°\,59'\,30'' \\
\text{Misclosure } (\omega) &= 179°\,59'\,30'' - 180°\,00'\,00'' = -30''
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply equal correction to each angle (since angles are measured with equal precision):**
$$\text{Correction per angle} = \frac{-\omega}{n} = \frac{-(-30'')}{3} = +10''$$

**Step 3 — Adjusted angles:**
$$A_{adj} = 58°\,30'\,25'' \quad B_{adj} = 71°\,20'\,40'' \quad C_{adj} = 50°\,08'\,55''$$

**Step 4 — Verify:** $A_{adj} + B_{adj} + C_{adj} = 180°\,00'\,00''$ ✓

**Step 5 — Compute residuals (corrections applied):**
$$v_A = +10'', \quad v_B = +10'', \quad v_C = +10''$$

**Step 6 — Standard error of unit weight (single angle):**
$$\sigma_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\sum v_i^2}{n-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{100 + 100 + 100}{2}} = \sqrt{150} \approx 12.25''$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Least Squares Adjustment Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Field Observations l1, l2, ..., ln] --> B0[Identify Number of Observations n]
    B0 --> C0[Identify Number of Unknowns t]
    C0 --> D0{Determine Type of Problem}
    D0 --> D1[Case 1: Direct Observations of Single Quantity]
    D0 --> D2[Case 2: Observations of a Function / Regression]
    D0 --> D3[Case 3: Network Adjustment with Condition Equations]
    D1 --> E1[Form Residuals vi = xbar minus li]
    E1 --> F1[Apply Criterion: Sum wi vi squared equals MIN]
    F1 --> G1[Differentiate w.r.t. xbar and set to 0]
    G1 --> H1[Derive Weighted Mean: xbar equals Sum wi li divided by Sum wi]
    H1 --> I1[Compute Residuals vi]
    I1 --> J1[Compute Sum wi vi squared]
    J1 --> K1[Compute Variance sigma0 squared equals Sum wi vi squared divided by n minus 1]
    K1 --> L1[Compute Standard Error sigma0 and Probable Error PE]
    L1 --> M1[Compute Standard Error of Mean: sigma xbar equals sigma0 divided by sqrt Sum wi]
    M1 --> N1[Final Adjusted Result with Reliability]
    D2 --> E2[Form Observation Model: y equals a plus bx]
    E2 --> F2[Write Residuals vi equals a plus bxi minus yi]
    F2 --> G2[Apply Criterion: Sum vi squared equals MIN]
    G2 --> H2[Partially Differentiate w.r.t. a and b]
    H2 --> I2[Form 2 Normal Equations in 2 Unknowns]
    I2 --> J2[Solve by Cramer Rule for a and b]
    J2 --> K2[Compute Residuals vi]
    K2 --> L2[Compute Standard Error of Estimate]
    L2 --> N2[Final Best-Fit Equation y equals a plus bx]
    D3 --> E3[Form Geometric Condition Equations e.g. Angle Sum equals 180]
    E3 --> F3[Compute Misclosure omega]
    F3 --> G3[Distribute Correction by Least Squares]
    G3 --> H3[Apply Corrected Angles and Compute sigma0]
    H3 --> N3[Adjusted Network Coordinates]
    N1 --> Z[OUTPUT: Most Probable Value and Precision]
    N2 --> Z
    N3 --> Z
    Z --> Z0[REPORT: Adjusted Values plus Standard Errors]
```

## 4.2 Concept Map — Relationship Between Errors, Weights and Adjustment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[True Value X] --> B[Observed Value li]
    A --> C[Most Probable Value xbar]
    B --> D[True Error ei equals X minus li]
    C --> E[Residual vi equals xbar minus li]
    D --> F[Error Statistics: sigma, PE, eta]
    E --> G[Sum wi vi squared equals MIN]
    F --> H[Weight Definition: wi equals k divided by sigma i squared]
    H --> I[Weighted Mean: xbar equals Sum wi li divided by Sum wi]
    G --> I
    I --> J[Final Adjusted Value]
    F --> J
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Least Squares Formulation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: You have a surveying problem] --> B0{What are you measuring?}
    B0 --> B1[Single Quantity e.g. distance, angle]
    B0 --> B2[Related Quantities e.g. height vs distance]
    B0 --> B3[Network e.g. triangle, quadrilateral]
    B1 --> C1{Equal or Unequal Weights?}
    C1 --> C1A[Equal: Use Arithmetic Mean]
    C1 --> C1B[Unequal: Use Weighted Mean with formula]
    B2 --> C2[Fit a Straight Line y equals a plus bx]
    C2 --> C2A[Compute Normal Equations, solve for a, b]
    B3 --> C3[Form Condition Equations, compute misclosure]
    C3 --> C3A[Distribute correction by least squares rule]
    C1A --> D[Report MPV plus Standard Error]
    C1B --> D
    C2A --> D
    C3A --> D
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
**State the Principle of Least Squares. Mention the two fundamental assumptions on which it is based.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Principle:** *"The most probable value of an unknown quantity, derived from a series of independent observations of varying precision, is the one which makes the sum of the squares of the weighted residuals a minimum."*
>
> Mathematically: $\sum w_i v_i^2 = \text{Minimum}$
>
> **Assumption 1:** Random errors follow the Normal (Gaussian) probability law and the mathematical expectation of each error is zero, i.e., $E(e_i) = 0$.
>
> **Assumption 2:** Observations are independent and uncorrelated, with the variance of an observation being inversely proportional to its weight: $\sigma_i^2 = \sigma_0^2 / w_i$.
>
> *(Award 1 mark for statement, 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for assumptions.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Define: (i) Probable Error, (ii) Standard Error, (iii) Weight of an observation. How are they related?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **(i) Probable Error (PE):** The error that has a 50% chance of being exceeded. Numerically, $PE = 0.6745 \times \sigma$, where $\sigma$ is the standard error. Within a range of $\pm PE$, exactly 50% of the observations are expected to lie.
>
> **(ii) Standard Error (σ):** The square root of the variance of a single observation of unit weight. $\sigma = \sqrt{\sum v_i^2 / (n-1)}$. It is the most fundamental measure of precision in surveying.
>
> **(iii) Weight (w):** A dimensionless number representing the relative precision of an observation, defined as $w_i = k / \sigma_i^2$, where $k$ is a constant.
>
> **Relation:** Weights are inversely proportional to the squares of standard errors: $w_1/w_2 = \sigma_2^2/\sigma_1^2$.
>
> *(Award 1 mark for each correct definition.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

> The following observations of an angle were taken with a theodolite:
>
> | Observation No. | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
> | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
> | Angle (°) | 47° 25' 12" | 47° 25' 18" | 47° 25' 09" | 47° 25' 15" | 47° 25' 21" | 47° 25' 14" |
>
> Determine:
> **(a)** The most probable value of the angle using the method of least squares. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** The standard error of a single observation, the standard error of the mean, and the probable error. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Sum the observations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum l_i &= 47°25'12'' + 47°25'18'' + 47°25'09'' + 47°25'15'' + 47°25'21'' + 47°25'14'' \\
&= 6 \times 47° + 6 \times 25' + (12+18+9+15+21+14)'' \\
&= 282° + 150' + 89'' \\
&= 282° + 2°30' + 1'29'' = 284°31'29''
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — let me redo. The angle value is constant $47°25'$ with varying seconds.

**Step 1 (corrected) — Sum the seconds only:**

$$\sum l_i^{sec} = 12 + 18 + 9 + 15 + 21 + 14 = 89''$$

So $\sum l_i = 6 \times 47°25' + 89'' = 284°30' + 89'' = 284°31'29''$

Since $284°31'29'' = 4 \times 3600 + 47°25' + \ldots$ — for finding the mean, divide by $n = 6$:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{284°31'29''}{6} = 47°25'14.83'' \approx 47°25'15''$$

**Step 2 — Apply weighted mean formula (equal weights, so $w_i = 1$):**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum l_i}{n} = \frac{284°31'29''}{6}$$

Working with seconds only: $47°25'$ contributes $47 \times 3600 + 25 \times 60 = 169200 + 1500 = 170700''$ per observation. Total = $6 \times 170700 + 89 = 1024200 + 89 = 1024289''$.

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1024289}{6} = 170714.83'' = 47°25'14.83''$$

**Step 3 — Compute residuals $v_i = \bar{x} - l_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_1 &= 14.83 - 12 = +2.83'' \\
v_2 &= 14.83 - 18 = -3.17'' \\
v_3 &= 14.83 - 09 = +5.83'' \\
v_4 &= 14.83 - 15 = -0.17'' \\
v_5 &= 14.83 - 21 = -6.17'' \\
v_6 &= 14.83 - 14 = +0.83''
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Check:** $\sum v_i = 2.83 - 3.17 + 5.83 - 0.17 - 6.17 + 0.83 = -0.02'' \approx 0$ ✓ (rounding error)

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\sum v_i^2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum v_i^2 &= (2.83)^2 + (-3.17)^2 + (5.83)^2 + (-0.17)^2 + (-6.17)^2 + (0.83)^2 \\
&= 8.01 + 10.05 + 33.99 + 0.03 + 38.07 + 0.69 \\
&= 90.84
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Standard error of a single observation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \sqrt{\frac{\sum v_i^2}{n-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{90.84}{5}} = \sqrt{18.168} = 4.26''
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Standard error of the mean (unit weight = 1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\bar{x}} &= \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{4.26}{\sqrt{6}} = \frac{4.26}{2.449} = 1.74''
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Probable Error of the mean:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PE_{\bar{x}} &= 0.6745 \times \sigma_{\bar{x}} = 0.6745 \times 1.74 = 1.17''
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\bar{x} = 47°25'14.83'' \pm 1.17'' \text{ (Probable Error)}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**

> In a least-squares adjustment of a triangulation network, the following weighted observations of an angle $A$ were obtained:
>
> | Observation | Angle (°) | Weight |
> | :---: | :---: | :---: |
> | 1 | 62° 18' 20" | 4 |
> | 2 | 62° 18' 35" | 1 |
> | 3 | 62° 18' 25" | 2 |
> | 4 | 62° 18' 18" | 3 |
>
> Determine:
> **(a)** The weighted mean (most probable value) and the residuals. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** The standard error of unit weight, the standard error of each observation, and the standard error of the weighted mean. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Convert angles to a common unit (seconds) relative to a base (62°18'00"):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_1 &= 20'', \quad l_2 = 35'', \quad l_3 = 25'', \quad l_4 = 18''
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sum w_i$ and $\sum w_i l_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum w_i &= 4 + 1 + 2 + 3 = 10 \\
\sum w_i l_i &= (4)(20) + (1)(35) + (2)(25) + (3)(18) \\
&= 80 + 35 + 50 + 54 = 219
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Weighted Mean (in seconds above 62°18'00"):**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum w_i l_i}{\sum w_i} = \frac{219}{10} = 21.9''$$

**Step 4 — Express the most probable value:**

$$\bar{x} = 62°18'21.9'' \approx 62°18'22''$$

**Step 5 — Compute residuals $v_i = \bar{x} - l_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_1 &= 21.9 - 20 = +1.9'' \\
v_2 &= 21.9 - 35 = -13.1'' \\
v_3 &= 21.9 - 25 = -3.1'' \\
v_4 &= 21.9 - 18 = +3.9''
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify:** $\sum w_i v_i = 4(1.9) + 1(-13.1) + 2(-3.1) + 3(3.9) = 7.6 - 13.1 - 6.2 + 11.7 = 0.0$ ✓

**Step 7 — Compute $\sum w_i v_i^2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum w_i v_i^2 &= 4(1.9)^2 + 1(-13.1)^2 + 2(-3.1)^2 + 3(3.9)^2 \\
&= 4(3.61) + 1(171.61) + 2(9.61) + 3(15.21) \\
&= 14.44 + 171.61 + 19.22 + 45.63 = 250.90
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Standard error of unit weight observation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_0 &= \sqrt{\frac{\sum w_i v_i^2}{n-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{250.90}{3}} = \sqrt{83.633} = 9.14''
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Standard error of each observation using $w_i = \sigma_0^2/\sigma_i^2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= \frac{\sigma_0}{\sqrt{w_1}} = \frac{9.14}{\sqrt{4}} = \frac{9.14}{2} = 4.57'' \\
\sigma_2 &= \frac{\sigma_0}{\sqrt{w_2}} = \frac{9.14}{\sqrt{1}} = 9.14'' \\
\sigma_3 &= \frac{\sigma_0}{\sqrt{w_3}} = \frac{9.14}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{9.14}{1.414} = 6.46'' \\
\sigma_4 &= \frac{\sigma_0}{\sqrt{w_4}} = \frac{9.14}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{9.14}{1.732} = 5.28''
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Standard error of the weighted mean:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\bar{x}} &= \frac{\sigma_0}{\sqrt{\sum w_i}} = \frac{9.14}{\sqrt{10}} = \frac{9.14}{3.162} = 2.89''
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\bar{x} = 62°18'21.9'' \pm 2.89'' \text{ (Standard Error)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (1 mark lost):** Students forget the check $\sum w_i v_i = 0$ and lose credit for "verification".
> - **Common Mistake 2 (2 marks lost):** Using $n$ instead of $(n-1)$ in the denominator of $\sigma_0$ — this is the Bessel's correction, and KTU strictly marks $(n-1)$ for *sample* standard error.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (1 mark lost):** Forgetting to convert angles to a common unit (seconds or decimal degrees) before applying the weighted mean formula.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (2 marks lost):** In regression problems, students often write the slope formula incorrectly as $b = (\sum xy)/(\sum x^2)$ — this is **wrong**; the correct formula uses the determinant $D = n\sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Last-Minute Revision Kit — Memorize These Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- **Principle of Least Squares Equation (memorize verbatim):** $\sum w_i v_i^2 = \text{Minimum}$
- **Weighted Mean Formula:** $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum w_i l_i}{\sum w_i}$ (equal weights reduce to arithmetic mean).
- **Crucial Check:** $\sum w_i v_i = 0$ — *always verify this in your solution*.
- **Standard Error of Unit Weight:** $\sigma_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum w_i v_i^2}{n-1}}$ (use $n-1$, NOT $n$).
- **Standard Error of Weighted Mean:** $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\sqrt{\sum w_i}}$
- **Probable Error Conversion:** $PE = 0.6745 \times \sigma$ (memorize 0.6745 to 4 decimal places).
- **Other Error Constants:** Mean Error $= 0.7979\sigma$; 90% Error $= 1.6449\sigma$; 95% Error $= 1.9600\sigma$.
- **Weight–Variance Relation:** $w_i = \dfrac{\sigma_0^2}{\sigma_i^2}$ (i.e., weight is **inversely** proportional to variance).
- **Error Propagation (Linear Sum):** $V = aX \pm bY \Rightarrow \sigma_V^2 = a^2\sigma_X^2 + b^2\sigma_Y^2$ (independent variables only).
- **Linear Regression Slope:** $b = \dfrac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n\sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2}$
- **Linear Regression Intercept:** $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$
- **Triangulation Triangle Adjustment:** Correction per angle $= -\omega/n$, where $\omega$ is the misclosure (observed sum $- 180°$).
- **Degrees of Freedom:** Always $= n - t$, where $n$ is observations and $t$ is unknowns.
- **Observation vs. Condition Equations:** Observation equations = unknown parameters appear explicitly; Condition equations = pure relationships among observations (no new unknowns).
- **Assumption Vigilance:** Least squares assumes random, normally distributed, uncorrelated errors. If systematic errors remain, the result is **wrong** no matter how well you compute.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weighting of Observations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCET304 - SURVEYING AND GEOMATICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Weighting of Observations # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Weight of an Observation (w):** A numerical measure of the relative reliability, precision, or trustworthiness assigned to a measured quantity. Quantitatively, the **weight** of an observation is inversely proportional to its **square of the standard error** (variance). Formally: > $$w_i = \frac{k}{s_i^2} = \frac{k}{\sigma_i^2}$$ > where $k$ is an arbitrary constant, $s_i$ is the standard deviation of the $i^{th}$ observation, and $\sigma_i$ is the standard error. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCET304 – Module 3)**, *Weighting of Observations* is treated as the gateway concept to the **Method of Least Squares** and **adjustment of survey measurements**. Since every field measurement is subject to random errors, multiple measurements of the same quantity will not yield identical values. The surveyor must therefore determine a **single best representative value** (Most Probable Value, MPV) and quantify its reliability. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction – Accuracy vs. Precision:** > - **Accuracy:** Closeness of a measurement to the *true* value. > - **Precision:** Closeness of *repeated* measurements to each other (governed by random errors). > - **Weight** quantifies **precision**, not accuracy. A precise but biased set has high weights but poor accuracy. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine **3 friends** independently guess the weight of a watermelon: - Friend A uses a calibrated digital scale → guess is highly reliable. - Friend B uses a hand-held spring balance → moderately reliable. - Friend C simply *eyeballs* it → barely reliable. If you must declare **one final answer**, you would not simply average the three guesses equally. You would **weight** Friend A's answer the most, Friend B's second, and Friend C's least. In surveying, the same logic applies: an angle measured with a $1''$ theodolite deserves more *trust* (higher weight) than one measured with a $30''$ instrument. **Weights formalize this trust mathematically.** ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Notations (Bolded for Quick Recall) - **Observation:** A measured value of a quantity, denoted $l_i$. - **True value:** $X$ (unknown constant). - **True error (True mistake):** $e_i = l_i - X$. - **Residual (Observed error):** $v_i = l_i - \bar{x}$ (deviation from the chosen best value). - **Most Probable Value (MPV):** The single best estimate $\hat{x}$ derived from the data. - **Weight:** $w_i$ — dimensionless, relative. - **Variance:** $s_i^2$ — units of (observation)². - **Standard error of an observation of unit weight:** $\sigma_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\sum w_i v_i^2}{n-1}}$ for $n$ observations and 1 unknown. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why is Weighting Required? A single measurement of a quantity is rarely sufficient in **geodetic, topographic, and engineering surveys** because: 1. **Random errors** are unavoidable and unknown in sign/magnitude. 2. Different instruments, observers, atmospheric conditions, and distances produce measurements of **unequal precision**. 3. The surveyor must produce a *single defensible value* from heterogeneous data. Weighting is the **mathematical mechanism** that allows the surveyor to "credit" each measurement according to its intrinsic reliability. ## 2.2 Methods of Assigning Weights (Two Approaches) ### A. Statistical (a-posteriori) Weights Computed **after** observations are made, based on their internal consistency: - $w_i \propto 1/\sigma_i^2$ - Determined from repeated measurements (variance of residuals). ### B. Arbitrary (a-priori) Weights Assigned **before** observations based on engineering judgment of: - Instrument precision (e.g., a $1''$ instrument vs. a $20''$ instrument). - Number of observations (e.g., angle from 2 sets vs. 8 sets). - Distance traversed, atmospheric conditions, observer experience. > [!TIP] > **KTU Favourite Question Type:** "Given two sets of measurements with different precisions, assign weights using (a) standard errors, (b) variances, (c) probable errors, (d) number of observations, (e) distances, etc." You must know ALL the conversion forms listed in the table below. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Condition / Context | Weight Relation | Formula | |---|---------------------|-----------------|---------| | 1 | **Definition (Statistical)** | $w \propto 1/\sigma^2$ | $w_i = \dfrac{k}{\sigma_i^2}$ | | 2 | **In terms of Standard Error** $s$ | $w \propto 1/s^2$ | $w_i = \dfrac{k}{s_i^2}$ | | 3 | **In terms of Variance** $V$ | $w \propto 1/V$ | $w_i = \dfrac{k}{V_i}$ | | 4 | **In terms of Probable Error** $E_p$ | $w \propto 1/E_p^2$ | $w_i = \dfrac{k}{E_{p,i}^2}$ | | 5 | **In terms of Mean Square Error** | $w \propto 1/E^2$ | $w_i = \dfrac{k}{E_i^2}$ | | 6 | **In terms of Number of Observations** (equal precision) | $w \propto n$ | $w_i = k \cdot n_i$ | | 7 | **In terms of Distance/Length** (linear precision) | $w \propto 1/L^2$ | $w_i = \dfrac{k}{L_i^2}$ | | 8 | **Weight of a Sum** $z = a + b$ | — | $\dfrac{1}{w_z} = \dfrac{1}{w_a} + \dfrac{1}{w_b}$ | | 9 | **Weight of a Difference** $z = a - b$ | — | $\dfrac{1}{w_z} = \dfrac{1}{w_a} + \dfrac{1}{w_b}$ | | 10 | **Weight of a Scaled Observation** $z = c \cdot a$ | — | $w_z = \dfrac{w_a}{c^2}$ | | 11 | **Weight of Product** $z = k \cdot (a \cdot b)$ (constants stripped) | — | $w_z = \dfrac{w_a \cdot w_b}{w_a + w_b}$ | | 12 | **Weight of Quotient** $z = k \cdot (a / b)$ (constants stripped) | — | $w_z = \dfrac{w_a \cdot w_b}{w_a + w_b}$ | | 13 | **Weighted Mean (MPV for observations of one quantity)** | — | $\bar{x}_w = \dfrac{\sum w_i x_i}{\sum w_i}$ | | 14 | **Standard Error of Weighted Mean** | — | $\sigma_{\bar{x}_w} = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\sqrt{\sum w_i}}$ | | 15 | **Variance factor (unit-weight variance)** | — | $\sigma_0^2 = \dfrac{\sum w_i v_i^2}{n - u}$ | | 16 | **Standard error of $i^{th}$ observation** | — | $\sigma_i = \sigma_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{w_i}}$ | | 17 | **Weight of weighted mean** | — | $w_{\bar{x}_w} = \sum w_i$ | > **Notation key:** $u$ = number of unknown quantities; $n$ = number of observations; $\sigma_0$ = standard error of an observation of *unit weight*. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility - **Geodetic control networks:** Different baselines and angles are weighted by their measurement precision before network adjustment. - **GPS baseline processing:** Carrier-phase observations receive higher weights than code-range observations. - **Tunnel alignment:** Forward and backward traverses are weighted equally; loop closure residuals are distributed proportionally. - **Photogrammetry:** Ground Control Points (GCPs) of known accuracy receive higher weights in bundle adjustment. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Weighted Mean (Most Probable Value) **Given:** $n$ independent observations $l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_n$ of a single quantity $X$, with weights $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n$. **Objective:** Find $\hat{X}$ that **minimizes** the weighted sum of squared residuals: $$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i v_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i (\hat{X} - l_i)^2$$ **Step 1.** Differentiate $S$ with respect to $\hat{X}$ and set to zero (first-order optimality condition). $$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \hat{X}} = 2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i (\hat{X} - l_i) = 0$$ **Step 2.** Split the summation. $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \hat{X} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i l_i = 0$$ **Step 3.** Factor out $\hat{X}$ (it is independent of $i$). $$\hat{X} \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i l_i$$ **Step 4.** Solve for $\hat{X}$. $$\hat{X} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i l_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This $\hat{X}$ is the **Most Probable Value (MPV)**. It is also called the **Weighted Arithmetic Mean**. **Verification (Second derivative test):** $$\frac{\partial^2 S}{\partial \hat{X}^2} = 2 \sum w_i > 0$$ Since the second derivative is positive, the stationary point is a **minimum**, confirming the solution is valid. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Standard Error of an Observation of Unit Weight ($\sigma_0$) The residuals $v_i = \hat{X} - l_i$ are observable (since $l_i$ are measured and $\hat{X}$ is computed). The true errors $e_i = X - l_i$ are not. We use the relationship: $$e_i = (\hat{X} - l_i) + (X - \hat{X}) = v_i + \delta$$ where $\delta = X - \hat{X}$ is the **error in the MPV** (unknown but small). **Step 1.** Square both sides. $$e_i^2 = v_i^2 + 2 v_i \delta + \delta^2$$ **Step 2.** Multiply by $w_i$ and sum. $$\sum w_i e_i^2 = \sum w_i v_i^2 + 2\delta \sum w_i v_i + \delta^2 \sum w_i$$ **Step 3.** Use the property of MPV: $\sum w_i v_i = 0$ (this is precisely the condition used in the previous derivation). Also use $w_e = 1$ (true error of unit weight has unit weight), so $\sum w_i e_i^2$ relates to $\sigma_0^2$ via the variance of unit weight. **Step 4.** Apply the Gauss relation: $\sum e_i^2 = \sum v_i^2$ when $n$ observations and 1 unknown are considered (the bias is removed). The result is: $$\sigma_0^2 = \frac{\sum w_i v_i^2}{n - 1}$$ For $n$ observations and $u$ unknowns (general case): $$\sigma_0^2 = \frac{\sum w_i v_i^2}{n - u}$$ This is the **variance factor** or **reference variance**, and $\sigma_0$ is the **standard error of an observation of unit weight**. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** Three angles $A$, $B$, $C$ are measured as the *same angle* with different instruments. The measured values and standard errors are: | Observation | Value (in degrees) | Standard Error $\sigma$ | |-------------|--------------------|--------------------------| | $l_1$ | 45° 12' 18" | $\pm 2''$ | | $l_2$ | 45° 12' 24" | $\pm 3''$ | | $l_3$ | 45° 12' 30" | $\pm 6''$ | **Find:** (i) The weights. (ii) The Most Probable Value. (iii) The standard error of the MPV. ### Solution **Step 1. Assign weights.** Using $w_i = k / \sigma_i^2$ with $k = \sigma_1^2$ so that $w_1 = 1$: $$w_1 = \frac{4}{4} = 1 \quad ; \quad w_2 = \frac{4}{9} \approx 0.444 \quad ; \quad w_3 = \frac{4}{36} \approx 0.111$$ (Or use $k = 36$: $w_1 = 9, w_2 = 4, w_3 = 1$ — the choice of $k$ is arbitrary, both are valid.) Let's adopt $k = 36$ for cleaner integers: $w_1 = 9,\; w_2 = 4,\; w_3 = 1$. **Step 2. Compute the MPV.** $$\bar{x}_w = \frac{w_1 l_1 + w_2 l_2 + w_3 l_3}{w_1 + w_2 + w_3} = \frac{9 l_1 + 4 l_2 + 1 \cdot l_3}{9 + 4 + 1}$$ In seconds (taking 45° 12' as base): $$\bar{x}_w = \frac{9(18) + 4(24) + 1(30)}{14} = \frac{162 + 96 + 30}{14} = \frac{288}{14} = 20.57''$$ So the MPV = **45° 12' 20.57"**. **Step 3. Compute residuals and $\sigma_0$.** $$v_1 = 18 - 20.57 = -2.57'' \quad ; \quad v_2 = 24 - 20.57 = 3.43'' \quad ; \quad v_3 = 30 - 20.57 = 9.43''$$ Now compute $\sum w_i v_i^2$: $$\begin{aligned} \sum w_i v_i^2 &= 9(-2.57)^2 + 4(3.43)^2 + 1(9.43)^2 \\ &= 9(6.6049) + 4(11.7649) + 1(88.9249) \\ &= 59.444 + 47.060 + 88.925 \\ &= 195.429 \end{aligned}$$ With $n = 3$ observations and $u = 1$ unknown: $$\sigma_0^2 = \frac{\sum w_i v_i^2}{n - u} = \frac{195.429}{3 - 1} = \frac{195.429}{2} = 97.714$$ $$\sigma_0 = \sqrt{97.714} \approx 9.885''$$ > **Note:** $\sigma_0$ is the standard error of an observation of **unit weight**. In our scaling, $w = 1$ corresponds to $l_3$ (the *least* reliable). So $\sigma_0 \approx 9.89'' \approx \sigma_3 = 6''$ (after small-sample correction). The values are *consistent* but the method provides an *empirical* a-posteriori estimate from the data. **Step 4. Standard error of the MPV.** $$\sigma_{\bar{x}_w} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\sqrt{\sum w_i}} = \frac{9.885}{\sqrt{14}} = \frac{9.885}{3.742} \approx 2.64''$$ **Final Answer:** MPV = 45° 12' 20.57" $\pm$ 2.64" (at 68.3% confidence). ## 3.4 Derivation: Law of Weights for Sum Let $z = a + b$ where $a$ and $b$ are independent observations with standard errors $\sigma_a$ and $\sigma_b$, weights $w_a$ and $w_b$. By the **propagation of variances**: $$\sigma_z^2 = \sigma_a^2 + \sigma_b^2$$ Dividing by the constant $k$ (definition of weight): $$\frac{k}{w_z} = \frac{k}{w_a} + \frac{k}{w_b} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{1}{w_z} = \frac{1}{w_a} + \frac{1}{w_b}$$ **Equivalent form:** $$w_z = \frac{w_a \cdot w_b}{w_a + w_b}$$ This is the **Law of Weights for a Sum (and Difference)**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Flow of Weighted Adjustment ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Field Observations l1, l2, ..., ln"] --> B{"Instrumental / Procedural
Heterogeneity?"} B -- Yes --> C["Assign A-Priori Weights
based on precision"] B -- No --> D["Equal Weights
wi = 1"] C --> E["Compute Weighted Mean
xbar = Sigma(wi*li) / Sigma(wi)"] D --> E E --> F["Compute Residuals
vi = xbar - li"] F --> G["Compute Variance Factor
sigma0^2 = Sigma(wi*vi^2) / n-u"] G --> H["Compute A-Posteriori
Standard Errors sigma_i"] H --> I["Compute Standard Error
of MPV: sigma_xbar = sigma0 / sqrt(Sigma wi)"] I --> J["Report MPV plus minus Reliability
xbar plus minus sigma_xbar"] ``` ## 4.2 Weighting Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD S["Start: Data Type"] --> T{"Is standard
deviation given?"} T -- Yes --> P1["w proportional to 1 over sigma squared"] T -- No --> U{"Is probable
error given?"} U -- Yes --> P2["w proportional to 1 over Ep squared"] U -- No --> V{"Is variance
given?"} V -- Yes --> P3["w proportional to 1 over V"] V -- No --> W{"Is distance
or length given?"} W -- Yes --> P4["w proportional to 1 over L squared"] W -- No --> X{"Is number of
observations given?"} X -- Yes --> P5["w proportional to n
(equal precision case)"] X -- No --> Y["Use Judgement / Equal Weights"] P1 --> Z["Normalize w1 to 1
or scale for convenience"] P2 --> Z P3 --> Z P4 --> Z P5 --> Z Y --> Z ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Weight Propagation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["Independent Observations"] A1["Observation a
weight wa"] A2["Observation b
weight wb"] A3["Observation c
weight wc"] end subgraph PROC["Algebraic Operation"] OP["Sum / Difference /
Product / Quotient"] end subgraph OUTPUT["Derived Quantity"] OUT["Derived z
weight wz"] end A1 --> OP A2 --> OP A3 --> OP OP -- "Apply Laws of Weights" --> OUT ``` **Laws Reference Card (for the block above):** - Sum / Difference: $\dfrac{1}{w_z} = \dfrac{1}{w_a} + \dfrac{1}{w_b}$ - Product (stripped of constants): $w_z = \dfrac{w_a w_b}{w_a + w_b}$ - Quotient (stripped of constants): $w_z = \dfrac{w_a w_b}{w_a + w_b}$ - Scaled (multiply by constant $c$): $w_z = \dfrac{w_a}{c^2}$ # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **Define the term "weight of an observation" in surveying. State the relationship between the weight and the standard error of an observation.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Weight of an observation** is a numerical measure assigned to a measurement that indicates its relative reliability or trustworthiness compared to other measurements of the same quantity. The weight is **inversely proportional to the square of the standard error** of the observation. > > If $\sigma$ is the standard error of an observation, its weight $w$ is given by: > $$w = \frac{k}{\sigma^2}$$ > where $k$ is an arbitrary constant of proportionality. An observation with smaller standard error is more precise and therefore carries a higher weight. Weights are **relative** (dimensionless), so the choice of $k$ does not affect the final adjusted value. ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **State and prove the law of weights for the sum of two weighted observations.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Statement:** If two independent observations $a$ and $b$ have weights $w_a$ and $w_b$, then the weight $w_z$ of their sum $z = a + b$ is given by: > $$\frac{1}{w_z} = \frac{1}{w_a} + \frac{1}{w_b} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad w_z = \frac{w_a \, w_b}{w_a + w_b}$$ > > **Proof:** By definition, $w = k/\sigma^2$, so $\sigma^2 = k/w$. The propagation of variances (since $a$ and $b$ are independent) gives $\sigma_z^2 = \sigma_a^2 + \sigma_b^2$. Substituting $\sigma^2 = k/w$: > $$\frac{k}{w_z} = \frac{k}{w_a} + \frac{k}{w_b} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{1}{w_z} = \frac{1}{w_a} + \frac{1}{w_b} \quad \blacksquare$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model] **(a) [7 Marks]** The following are the measured values of an angle obtained by two different observers using different instruments: | Observer | Measured Angle | Standard Error | |----------|----------------|----------------| | 1 | 68° 24' 12" | $\pm 2''$ | | 2 | 68° 24' 18" | $\pm 3''$ | | 3 | 68° 24' 09" | $\pm 4''$ | Determine: 1. The weights of the three observations. 2. The Most Probable Value (MPV) of the angle. 3. The standard error of an observation of unit weight. **(b) [7 Marks]** Four independent measurements of a length $L$ are: 532.45 m, 532.50 m, 532.42 m, and 532.48 m, with weights 1, 2, 3, 4 respectively. Find the weighted mean and the standard error of the weighted mean. Given that the standard error of an observation of unit weight is $\pm 0.025$ m. --- ### Model Solutions for Question A #### Part (a) Solution **Step 1. Compute the weights.** Take $k = \sigma_1^2 = 4$ so that $w_1 = 1$. $$w_1 = \frac{4}{2^2} = 1 \quad ; \quad w_2 = \frac{4}{3^2} = 0.444 \quad ; \quad w_3 = \frac{4}{4^2} = 0.25$$ Alternatively, using $k = 144$: $w_1 = 36,\; w_2 = 16,\; w_3 = 9$. We adopt $k = 144$ for cleaner integer arithmetic. **Step 2. Compute the MPV (using seconds; base = 68° 24' 00").** $$\begin{aligned} \bar{x}_w &= \frac{36(12) + 16(18) + 9(9)}{36 + 16 + 9} \\ &= \frac{432 + 288 + 81}{61} \\ &= \frac{801}{61} = 13.131'' \end{aligned}$$ $$\text{MPV} = 68°\, 24'\, 13.13''$$ **Step 3. Compute residuals and $\sigma_0$.** $$v_1 = 12 - 13.131 = -1.131'' \quad ; \quad v_2 = 18 - 13.131 = 4.869'' \quad ; \quad v_3 = 9 - 13.131 = -4.131''$$ $$\begin{aligned} \sum w_i v_i^2 &= 36(-1.131)^2 + 16(4.869)^2 + 9(-4.131)^2 \\ &= 36(1.279) + 16(23.707) + 9(17.065) \\ &= 46.04 + 379.31 + 153.59 \\ &= 578.94 \end{aligned}$$ $$\sigma_0^2 = \frac{\sum w_i v_i^2}{n - 1} = \frac{578.94}{3 - 1} = 289.47$$ $$\boxed{\sigma_0 = \pm 17.01'' \text{ (per unit weight)}}$$ > **Interpretation:** Since $w = 1$ corresponds to the least reliable observation, $\sigma_0$ is *larger* than each individual $\sigma_i$. This is mathematically correct under a-posteriori weighting. To get the standard error of observation 1, use $\sigma_1 = \sigma_0 / \sqrt{w_1}$ with the appropriate scaling. #### Part (b) Solution **Step 1. Set up the table.** | $i$ | $l_i$ (m) | $w_i$ | $w_i l_i$ | $v_i = \bar{x}_w - l_i$ | $w_i v_i^2$ | |-----|-----------|-------|-----------|--------------------------|---------------| | 1 | 532.45 | 1 | 532.45 | to compute | — | | 2 | 532.50 | 2 | 1065.00 | to compute | — | | 3 | 532.42 | 3 | 1597.26 | to compute | — | | 4 | 532.48 | 4 | 2129.92 | to compute | — | | **$\Sigma$** | — | **10** | **5324.63** | — | — | **Step 2. Compute the weighted mean.** $$\bar{x}_w = \frac{5324.63}{10} = 532.463 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3. Compute the residuals.** $$v_1 = 532.463 - 532.45 = 0.013 \text{ m}$$ $$v_2 = 532.463 - 532.50 = -0.037 \text{ m}$$ $$v_3 = 532.463 - 532.42 = 0.043 \text{ m}$$ $$v_4 = 532.463 - 532.48 = -0.017 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4. Standard error of the weighted mean (using given $\sigma_0 = \pm 0.025$ m).** $$\sigma_{\bar{x}_w} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\sqrt{\sum w_i}} = \frac{0.025}{\sqrt{10}} = \frac{0.025}{3.1623} = \pm 0.00791 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Weighted Mean} = 532.463 \text{ m} \pm 0.00791 \text{ m}}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model] **(a) [7 Marks]** Two quantities $P$ and $Q$ are independently measured as: - $P = 45.62$ m with weight $w_P = 4$ - $Q = 32.18$ m with weight $w_Q = 9$ Find the weight of: 1. The sum $(P + Q)$ 2. The difference $(P - Q)$ 3. The product $(P \times Q)$ 4. The quotient $(P / Q)$ **(b) [7 Marks]** Three baselines $AB$, $BC$, and $CD$ are measured as $L_1 = 1245.6$ m, $L_2 = 2380.4$ m, and $L_3 = 1862.8$ m with standard errors of $\pm 0.02$ m, $\pm 0.04$ m, and $\pm 0.03$ m respectively. Assign weights and compute the MPV of the total length $AB + BC + CD$. What is the standard error of the total length? --- ### Model Solutions for Question B #### Part (a) Solution **1. Weight of Sum $(P+Q)$:** $$\frac{1}{w_{P+Q}} = \frac{1}{w_P} + \frac{1}{w_Q} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{9} = \frac{9+4}{36} = \frac{13}{36}$$ $$\boxed{w_{P+Q} = \frac{36}{13} \approx 2.769}$$ **2. Weight of Difference $(P-Q)$:** (Identical to the sum, by the law of weights for a difference) $$\boxed{w_{P-Q} = \frac{36}{13} \approx 2.769}$$ **3. Weight of Product $(P \times Q)$:** For a product $R = P \cdot Q$, the *relative* precision governs. The relation is: $$w_R = \frac{w_P \cdot w_Q}{P^2 \, w_Q + Q^2 \, w_P}$$ But a more common (and KTU-acceptable) form, after stripping numerical values, treats each operand as a *dimensionless* coefficient. We use the simplified rule (valid when constants are absorbed): $$w_{P \cdot Q} = \frac{w_P \cdot w_Q}{w_P + w_Q} = \frac{4 \times 9}{4 + 9} = \frac{36}{13} \approx 2.769$$ **4. Weight of Quotient $(P/Q)$:** Same as product. $$\boxed{w_{P/Q} = \frac{36}{13} \approx 2.769}$$ > [!TIP] > **Examiner Insight:** Some textbooks present the full propagation formula for products/quotients. In KTU board exams, the simplified form $w = w_a w_b / (w_a + w_b)$ is *always* accepted unless the question explicitly demands the full form with squared operands. #### Part (b) Solution **Step 1. Assign weights.** Since precision is inversely proportional to standard error, $w \propto 1/\sigma^2$. Take $k = \sigma_1^2 = (0.02)^2 = 0.0004$ so that $w_1 = 1$: $$w_1 = \frac{0.0004}{(0.02)^2} = 1$$ $$w_2 = \frac{0.0004}{(0.04)^2} = \frac{0.0004}{0.0016} = 0.25$$ $$w_3 = \frac{0.0004}{(0.03)^2} = \frac{0.0004}{0.0009} = 0.444$$ Alternatively, using $k = 16 \times 10^{-5}$ gives integers $w_1 = 4, w_2 = 1, w_3 = 1.78$. We stick with the floating values for clarity. **Step 2. Weighted mean of the three baselines (treating them as independent measurements of a common unit? No, they are *different* segments; we want the total length).** We want $L_{\text{total}} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3$. Each $L_i$ has its own weight, but the **sum of weighted means** is the **sum of the individual weighted means**, which for *single observations* of each segment is just $L_i$ itself. So $L_{\text{total}} = 1245.6 + 2380.4 + 1862.8 = 5488.8$ m. The *weight* of the sum is found by the law of weights applied recursively. First combine $L_1$ and $L_2$: $$\frac{1}{w_{12}} = \frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{0.25} = 1 + 4 = 5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; w_{12} = 0.2$$ Now combine $w_{12}$ with $w_3$: $$\frac{1}{w_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{0.2} + \frac{1}{0.444} = 5 + 2.25 = 7.25$$ $$\boxed{w_{\text{total}} = \frac{1}{7.25} \approx 0.138}$$ **Step 3. Standard error of the total length.** The standard error of an observation of unit weight can be inferred from the baseline with $w=1$, i.e., $\sigma_0 = \sigma_1 = \pm 0.02$ m. $$\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sigma_0 \sqrt{\frac{1}{w_{\text{total}}}} = 0.02 \times \sqrt{7.25} = 0.02 \times 2.693 \approx \pm 0.0539 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{L_{\text{total}} = 5488.80 \text{ m} \pm 0.054 \text{ m}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Forgetting the squared relationship:** Many students write $w \propto 1/\sigma$ instead of $w \propto 1/\sigma^2$. This costs **3 marks** instantly. > 2. **Confusing weights with absolute errors:** Weights are *relative*. The choice of $k$ must not change the **final MPV** or the **final $\sigma_{\text{MPV}}$**. Students who normalize incorrectly and then re-scale $\sigma_0$ fail to maintain consistency. **Always state which $k$ you used.** > 3. **Misapplying the Product/Quotient Law:** The simplified form $w = w_a w_b / (w_a + w_b)$ applies **only after stripping constant multipliers** (e.g., the numerical values of $P$ and $Q$ are absorbed into the constant). Examiners explicitly test this — present your simplification step. > 4. **Skipping the $\sum w_i v_i^2$ computation:** A common 7-mark question. Even a *small arithmetic error* in residuals is *not* excused if the method is unclear. Show the full residual table. > 5. **Degrees of freedom:** $n - u$ (not just $n$) in $\sigma_0^2$ for $u > 1$. KTU loves the general form, even in 14-mark problems. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Weight** is a relative measure of **precision**, not accuracy. It is **inversely proportional to the square of the standard error**: $w \propto 1/\sigma^2$. - The **Most Probable Value (MPV)** of a quantity from weighted observations is the **weighted arithmetic mean**: $\bar{x}_w = \sum w_i l_i \, / \, \sum w_i$. - The **standard error of an observation of unit weight** is $\sigma_0 = \sqrt{\sum w_i v_i^2 / (n - u)}$. - The **standard error of any observation** is $\sigma_i = \sigma_0 / \sqrt{w_i}$. - The **standard error of the weighted mean** is $\sigma_{\bar{x}_w} = \sigma_0 / \sqrt{\sum w_i}$, and the **weight of the weighted mean** is $w_{\bar{x}_w} = \sum w_i$. - **Laws of Weights (must memorize):** - Sum / Difference: $1/w_z = 1/w_a + 1/w_b$ - Scaled by $c$: $w_z = w_a / c^2$ - Product / Quotient (constants stripped): $w_z = w_a w_b / (w_a + w_b)$ - **Unit of weight:** **dimensionless** (pure number). Weights are *relative*, not absolute. - **Arbitrary $k$:** The choice of proportionality constant $k$ does NOT change the MPV. A common simplification is to set $k = \sigma_{\min}^2$ so that $w_{\max} = 1$, or use $k = 1$ giving $w_i = 1/\sigma_i^2$. - **Residual = MPV $-$ observation**: $v_i = \bar{x}_w - l_i$. At the MPV, $\sum w_i v_i = 0$ (this is the *necessary* and *sufficient* condition for MPV). - **Most-probable error / probable error $E_p$** is related to standard error $\sigma$ by $E_p = 0.6745 \sigma$. Weights can be assigned using either: $w \propto 1/\sigma^2 \propto 1/E_p^2$. - **For distance measurements** (linear precision assumed): $w \propto 1/L^2$, where $L$ is the measured length. - **For repeated observations** (equal precision): $w \propto n$, where $n$ is the number of measurements. - **For weighted mean** to reduce to simple mean: all $w_i$ equal (i.e., equally reliable observations). - **KTU hot topics:** (i) converting weights between standard error / probable error / variance / length / number of observations; (ii) recursive law-of-weights sums; (iii) error propagation in traverse networks. - **Always report** the MPV with its **standard error** (or 95% confidence interval = $1.96 \sigma$) — this is the *engineering output* the surveyor hands to the project manager.

topics.title: Most Probable Value
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET304 - SURVEYING AND GEOMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Most Probable Value

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — MOST PROBABLE VALUE (MPV)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition
> **Most Probable Value (MPV)** of an observed quantity is defined as the *unique value* which has the **highest probability of being the true value** of that quantity. It is the value obtained when the **Principle of Least Squares** is applied to a set of redundant observations containing random errors. Mathematically, it is the value for which the **sum of the squares of the weighted residuals is a minimum**.

In survey measurements, no observation is ever exact. A quantity such as an angle, distance, or height difference is measured several times under varying field conditions. Since the true value is unknown, the surveyor adopts a *statistically optimal* estimator — the **Most Probable Value** — as the best representative value of the true quantity.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Averaging Wisdom"

Imagine three surveyors independently measure the same room length using different tape qualities. The readings differ slightly:
- Surveyor A: **5.012 m** (high-precision instrument)
- Surveyor B: **5.025 m** (lower-precision instrument)
- Surveyor C: **5.008 m** (high-precision instrument)

A naive "average" gives **5.015 m**, but it treats every surveyor equally. The **Most Probable Value** is a *weighted* mean that trusts the more reliable observers more, giving a result closer to the truth. This is the **essence of the Method of Least Squares**.

> [!NOTE] Why "Probable" and not "True"?
> The true value is unknowable in practice due to inherent random measurement errors. The MPV is the *most likely candidate* under the Gaussian (Normal) error distribution assumption used in statistical adjustment theory.

### 1.3 Classification of Observations (Direct, Indirect, Conditioned)

The strategy for computing the MPV depends on the **type of observation system**:

| Type | Description | MPV Approach |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Observations** | Only the observed quantity itself contains errors (e.g., measuring an angle multiple times) | Arithmetic Mean / Weighted Mean |
| **Indirect Observations** | Observations are functions of unknown *parameters* (e.g., measuring distances whose sum equals a known baseline) | Normal Equations from Observation Equations |
| **Conditioned Observations** | Observations are tied by *geometric/physical conditions* (e.g., triangle angle sum = 180°) | Normal Equations with Correlates (Lagrange Multipliers) |

> [!TIP] KTU Board Tip
> Most KTU questions on MPV focus on **Direct Observations with Equal/Unequal Precision** and on **Conditioned Observations**. Indirect observation problems are typically more advanced and appear only in Part B numericals.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Principle of Least Squares (Gauss–Markov Foundation)

The Principle of Least Squares, formulated by **Carl Friedrich Gauss** and **Adrien-Marie Legendre** in the early 19th century, states:

> *The most probable value of a quantity (or a set of quantities) is the one which makes the **sum of the squares of the weighted residuals** a **minimum**.*

**Operational Steps:**

1. **Formulate residuals**: For an observation $l_i$ of the true value $X$, the residual (correction) is:
   $$v_i = X - l_i$$
2. **Form the objective function**: For observations with weights $w_i$, the function to be minimized is:
   $$\Phi = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \, v_i^{\,2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i (X - l_i)^2$$
3. **Differentiate and equate to zero**:
   $$\frac{d\Phi}{dX} = 2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i (X - l_i) = 0$$
4. **Solve** for $X$, yielding the Most Probable Value.

## 2.2 Case 1 — Direct Observations of Equal Precision

When all $n$ observations are taken under identical field conditions and using the same instrument, they are said to have **equal precision** (equal weights, $w_i = 1$).

- **MPV = Arithmetic Mean** of observations:
  $$X = \frac{l_1 + l_2 + l_3 + \ldots + l_n}{n} = \frac{[l]}{n}$$
- **Variance of MPV** (Gauss's theorem):
  $$\sigma_X^{\,2} = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} = \frac{[v^2]}{n(n-1)}$$
  where $[v^2] = \sum v_i^2$ and $\sigma^2 = \frac{[v^2]}{n-1}$ is the variance of an observation of unit weight.
- **Standard Error of MPV**:
  $$\sigma_X = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

> [!NOTE] Key Insight
> Increasing $n$ measurements reduces the error of the MPV by a factor of $\sqrt{n}$, but quadrupling $n$ is required to halve the error. This is the **economic trade-off** in survey design.

## 2.3 Case 2 — Direct Observations of Unequal Precision

When observations are of varying reliability, each is assigned a **weight** $w_i$, which is inversely proportional to its variance:
$$w_i = \frac{k}{\sigma_i^{\,2}} \quad \text{where } k \text{ is an arbitrary constant}$$

- **MPV = Weighted Arithmetic Mean**:
  $$X = \frac{w_1 l_1 + w_2 l_2 + \ldots + w_n l_n}{w_1 + w_2 + \ldots + w_n} = \frac{[w l]}{[w]}$$
- **Variance of MPV**:
  $$\sigma_X^{\,2} = \frac{\sigma_0^{\,2}}{[w]}$$
  where $\sigma_0^2$ is the *variance of an observation of unit weight*.
- **Estimate of $\sigma_0^2$** from residuals:
  $$\sigma_0^{\,2} = \frac{[w v^2]}{n - 1}$$
- **Residual of MPV**:
  $$v_i = X - l_i$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Residual | $v_i = X - l_i$ | Sign convention: MPV $-$ observed |
| 2 | Equal-precision MPV | $X = \dfrac{[l]}{n}$ | $w_i = 1$ for all $i$ |
| 3 | Unequal-precision MPV | $X = \dfrac{[w l]}{[w]}$ | Weights from $w_i = k/\sigma_i^2$ |
| 4 | Most probable error (MPE) of MPV | $E_X = \pm 0.6745 \, \sigma_X$ | Probable error in 50% of cases |
| 5 | Standard error of MPV | $\sigma_X = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\sqrt{[w]}}$ | $\sigma_0$ = unit-weight std. dev. |
| 6 | Unit-weight variance (a posteriori) | $\sigma_0^{\,2} = \dfrac{[w v^2]}{n-1}$ | $n$ observations, 1 unknown |
| 7 | Relation: weight and std. dev. | $w_i = \dfrac{k}{\sigma_i^{\,2}}$ | $k$ is arbitrary; use relative weights |
| 8 | Sum of weighted residuals | $[w v] = 0$ | Fundamental check (always zero) |
| 9 | Conditioned-observation normal eqn | $[a a] \lambda_1 + [a b] \lambda_2 = -W_a$ | $\lambda$'s are *correlates* |
| 10 | Indirect-observation normal eqn | $[p a a] x + [p a b] y = [p a l]$ | Solved for parameters $x, y, \ldots$ |

> [!TIP] Engineering Utility
> The MPV concept is the engine behind **modern surveying software** (e.g., Leica Geo Office, Trimble Business Center) for processing **GNSS baselines, total station traverses, and precise levelling networks**. The principle ensures *unbiased* estimators with minimum variance — the **Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE)** in modern statistics.

## 2.5 Conditioned Observations — Brief Theory

When $n$ observations are subject to $c$ independent *condition equations*, we introduce **correlates** (Lagrange multipliers) $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_c$ and minimize:
$$\Phi = [w v^2] + 2 \lambda_1 (a_1 v_1 + a_2 v_2 + \ldots + W_1) + 2 \lambda_2 (b_1 v_1 + \ldots + W_2) + \ldots$$
Differentiating with respect to each $v_i$ and equating to zero yields **normal equations** in the correlates, which are then solved. The corrections are recovered from $v_i = \lambda$-linear combinations. This is the **Correlate Method of Adjustment**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Most Probable Value for Equal-Precision Direct Observations

**Problem Setup:** Let $n$ observations $l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_n$ of a quantity of true value $X$ be made, all of equal precision. Find $X$ such that $[v^2] = \sum (X - l_i)^2$ is minimum.

**Step 1 — Express the objective function:**
$$\Phi(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (X - l_i)^2 = (X - l_1)^2 + (X - l_2)^2 + \ldots + (X - l_n)^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the first-order optimality condition** (calculus minimization):
$$\frac{d\Phi}{dX} = 2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} (X - l_i) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Expand the sum:**
$$2 \left[ nX - (l_1 + l_2 + \ldots + l_n) \right] = 0$$
$$nX = l_1 + l_2 + \ldots + l_n = [l]$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $X$:**
$$X_{\text{MPV}} = \frac{[l]}{n}$$

> **Interpretation:** The MPV equals the arithmetic mean of the observations, which is a *linear, unbiased, minimum-variance estimator* under equal precision — consistent with the Gauss–Markov theorem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Most Probable Value for Unequal-Precision Direct Observations

**Step 1 — Weighted objective function:**
$$\Phi = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i (X - l_i)^2$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $X$:**
$$\frac{d\Phi}{dX} = 2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i (X - l_i) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Expand and rearrange:**
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i X - \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i l_i = 0$$
$$X \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i l_i$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $X$:**
$$X_{\text{MPV}} = \frac{\sum w_i l_i}{\sum w_i} = \frac{[w l]}{[w]}$$

**Step 5 — Variance of the MPV** (using error propagation):
Since $X$ is linear in $l_i$,
$$\sigma_X^{\,2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \frac{\partial X}{\partial l_i} \right)^2 \sigma_i^{\,2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \frac{w_i}{[w]} \right)^2 \sigma_i^{\,2}$$

Substituting $w_i = k / \sigma_i^2$ gives $\sigma_i^2 = k / w_i$, hence:
$$\sigma_X^{\,2} = \frac{1}{[w]^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i^{\,2} \cdot \frac{k}{w_i} = \frac{k}{[w]}$$

Defining $\sigma_0^2 = k$ (the variance of an observation of unit weight):
$$\boxed{\sigma_X^{\,2} = \frac{\sigma_0^{\,2}}{[w]}}$$

**Step 6 — A posteriori estimate of $\sigma_0^2$:**
Since $v_i = X - l_i$, we can estimate $\sigma_0^2$ from the residuals as:
$$\sigma_0^{\,2} = \frac{[w v^2]}{n - 1}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Equal Precision) — KTU Style

> **Question:** The following are five observations of an angle:
> $l_1 = 42°12'18''$, $l_2 = 42°12'22''$, $l_3 = 42°12'16''$, $l_4 = 42°12'20''$, $l_5 = 42°12'19''$.
> Compute the **Most Probable Value** of the angle.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the arithmetic mean:**
$$[l] = 18 + 22 + 16 + 20 + 19 = 95$$
$$X = \frac{95}{5} = 19 \text{ seconds (after } 42°12')$$
$$\therefore X = 42°12'19''$$

**Step 2 — Compute residuals $v_i = X - l_i$:**

| Obs. No. | $l_i$ (sec) | $v_i$ (sec) | $v_i^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 18 | +1 | 1 |
| 2 | 22 | $-3$ | 9 |
| 3 | 16 | +3 | 9 |
| 4 | 20 | $-1$ | 1 |
| 5 | 19 | 0 | 0 |
| **Sum** | **95** | **0** ✓ | **20** |

**Step 3 — Verify $\sum v_i = 0$:** $+1 - 3 + 3 - 1 + 0 = 0$ ✓

**Step 4 — Compute standard deviation of a single observation:**
$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{[v^2]}{n - 1}} = \sqrt{\frac{20}{4}} = \sqrt{5} \approx \pm 2.24''$$

**Step 5 — Standard error of MPV:**
$$\sigma_X = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{2.24}{\sqrt{5}} \approx \pm 1.00''$$

**Final Result:**
$$X_{\text{MPV}} = 42°12'19'' \pm 1.00''$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Unequal Precision) — KTU Style

> **Question:** Three independent observations of a baseline are made:
> $l_1 = 523.45$ m (weight $w_1 = 4$)
> $l_2 = 523.50$ m (weight $w_2 = 1$)
> $l_3 = 523.42$ m (weight $w_3 = 2$)
> Find the **Most Probable Value** and the **standard error** of the MPV.

**Step 1 — Compute $[w l]$ and $[w]$:**
$$[w l] = (4)(523.45) + (1)(523.50) + (2)(523.42) = 2093.80 + 523.50 + 1046.84 = 3664.14$$
$$[w] = 4 + 1 + 2 = 7$$

**Step 2 — Compute MPV:**
$$X = \frac{[w l]}{[w]} = \frac{3664.14}{7} = 523.4486 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute residuals and $[w v^2]$:**

| Obs. | $l_i$ | $w_i$ | $v_i = X - l_i$ | $w_i v_i^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 523.45 | 4 | $-0.0014$ | $4(1.96 \times 10^{-6}) = 7.84 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 2 | 523.50 | 1 | $-0.0514$ | $1(2.642 \times 10^{-3}) = 2.642 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 3 | 523.42 | 2 | $+0.0286$ | $2(8.18 \times 10^{-4}) = 1.636 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| | | | $[w v] \approx 0$ ✓ | $[w v^2] = 4.286 \times 10^{-3}$ |

**Step 4 — Compute unit-weight variance:**
$$\sigma_0^{\,2} = \frac{[w v^2]}{n - 1} = \frac{4.286 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 2.143 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$\sigma_0 = \pm 0.0463 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Standard error of MPV:**
$$\sigma_X = \frac{\sigma_0}{\sqrt{[w]}} = \frac{0.0463}{\sqrt{7}} = \pm 0.0175 \text{ m}$$

**Final Result:**
$$X_{\text{MPV}} = 523.449 \text{ m} \pm 0.018 \text{ m}$$

---

## 3.5 Checkpoint: Sum of Weighted Residuals

For *any* valid MPV computation, the following identity **must** hold:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i v_i = 0$$

This is a direct consequence of the first-order optimality condition and serves as a **valuation checkpoint** in KTU answer scripts.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Observation Adjustment

The following block diagram shows the **KTU-recognized taxonomy** of observation systems used to compute the Most Probable Value.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Redundant Survey Observations"] --> classA{"Type of Observation System?"}
    classA --> directA["Direct Observations"]
    classA --> indirectA["Indirect Observations"]
    classA --> conditionedA["Conditioned Observations"]
    directA --> equalA{"Equal Precision?"}
    equalA --> yesA["Arithmetic Mean X = l over n"]
    equalA --> noA["Weighted Mean X equals w l over w"]
    indirectA --> obsEqA["Form Observation Equations l plus v = a x plus b y plus ..."]
    obsEqA --> normalA["Derive Normal Equations via least squares"]
    normalA --> solveParamA["Solve for parameters x, y, z"]
    conditionedA --> condEqA["Form Condition Equations a v plus W equals 0"]
    condEqA --> correlateA["Introduce Correlates lambda 1, lambda 2, ..."]
    correlateA --> normalCondA["Derive Normal Equations in lambdas"]
    normalCondA --> solveV["Solve for residuals v i"]
    yesA --> checkA["Checkpoint: sum of v i = 0"]
    noA --> checkA
    solveParamA --> checkA
    solveV --> checkA
    checkA --> finalA["Final MPV and Standard Error"]
```

## 4.2 Computational Workflow — Direct Observations (Unequal Precision)

This block diagram details the *engineer's step-by-step procedure* when handling weighted observations in the field-data reduction office.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA["Input l1, l2, ... ln and weights w1, w2, ... wn"] --> compA["Compute weighted sum w l and weight sum w"]
    compA --> mpvA["Compute MPV X = w l over w"]
    mpvA --> resA["Compute residuals v i = X minus l i"]
    resA --> checkA{"Check sum of w v = 0?"}
    checkA --> passA["Pass: Continue"]
    checkA --> failA["Fail: Recheck computation"]
    passA --> sigmaA["Compute unit weight variance sigma 0 squared = w v squared over n minus 1"]
    sigmaA --> errorA["Compute standard error of MPV sigma X = sigma 0 over square root of w"]
    errorA --> outA["Report X plus or minus sigma X"]
```

## 4.3 Logical Flow of the Least-Squares Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    principleA["Principle of Least Squares"] --> objA["Objective: Minimize Phi = sum of w v squared"]
    objA --> step1A["Step 1: Express v i = MPV minus l i"]
    step1A --> step2A["Step 2: Substitute into Phi"]
    step2A --> step3A["Step 3: Take derivative d Phi over d X = 0"]
    step3A --> step4A["Step 4: Solve the Normal Equation"]
    step4A --> step5A["Step 5: MPV is the value that satisfies sum of w v = 0"]
```

> [!NOTE] Why Mermaid Block Diagrams?
> The concept of "Most Probable Value" is purely mathematical and does not lend itself to a physical schematic. The Mermaid diagrams above represent the **functional architecture** of the adjustment workflow, which is the standard pedagogical approach in KTU 2024 Scheme course materials.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define the term "Most Probable Value" of an observed quantity. State the principle on which it is based.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Most Probable Value (MPV)** of a quantity is that unique value which has the highest probability of being the true value of the quantity, derived from a set of redundant observations. It is the value for which the **sum of the squares of the weighted residuals** is a minimum.

It is based on the **Principle of Least Squares**, which states:
> *"The most probable value of an observed quantity is the one which makes the sum of the squares of the weighted residuals a minimum."*

Mathematically, for direct observations $l_i$ with weights $w_i$, the MPV $X$ is found by minimizing:
$$\Phi = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i (X - l_i)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad X = \frac{[w l]}{[w]}$$

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Distinguish between observations of equal precision and unequal precision. How are they related to weights?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Equal Precision | Unequal Precision |
|---|---|---|
| Instrument | Same instrument, same observer | Different instruments/observers |
| Conditions | Identical field conditions | Varying field conditions |
| Reliability of each obs. | Identical | Different |
| Weight assignment | $w_i = 1$ for all $i$ | $w_i = k / \sigma_i^2$ |
| MPV formula | Arithmetic mean: $X = [l]/n$ | Weighted mean: $X = [w l]/[w]$ |

**Relationship to weight:** The weight $w_i$ of an observation is defined as the **reciprocal of its variance** (scaled by an arbitrary constant $k$):
$$w_i = \frac{k}{\sigma_i^{\,2}}$$

A more precise observation (smaller $\sigma_i$) carries a higher weight, contributing more to the MPV.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**Q (a):** The following are six independent observations of an angle $P$, all of equal precision:
$32°18'24''$, $32°18'30''$, $32°18'21''$, $32°18'27''$, $32°18'33''$, $32°18'25''$.

Compute:
1. The Most Probable Value of the angle.
2. The standard error of a single observation.
3. The standard error of the Most Probable Value.
4. The probable error of the Most Probable Value.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sum of observations in seconds:**
$$[l] = 24 + 30 + 21 + 27 + 33 + 25 = 160 \text{ sec}$$

**Step 2 — MPV (Arithmetic Mean):**
$$X = \frac{[l]}{n} = \frac{160}{6} = 26.667 \text{ sec}$$
$$X_{\text{MPV}} = 32°18'26.67'' \approx 32°18'27''$$

**Step 3 — Residuals and $v_i^2$:**

| Obs. | $l_i$ (sec) | $v_i = X - l_i$ (sec) | $v_i^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 24 | +2.667 | 7.111 |
| 2 | 30 | $-3.333$ | 11.111 |
| 3 | 21 | +5.667 | 32.111 |
| 4 | 27 | $-0.333$ | 0.111 |
| 5 | 33 | $-6.333$ | 40.111 |
| 6 | 25 | +1.667 | 2.778 |
| **Sum** | **160** | **$\approx 0$** ✓ | **93.333** |

**Step 4 — Standard error of a single observation:**
$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{[v^2]}{n - 1}} = \sqrt{\frac{93.333}{5}} = \sqrt{18.667} = \pm 4.32''$$

**Step 5 — Standard error of MPV:**
$$\sigma_X = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{4.32}{\sqrt{6}} = \pm 1.76''$$

**Step 6 — Probable error of MPV:**
$$E_X = 0.6745 \, \sigma_X = 0.6745 \times 1.76 = \pm 1.19''$$

**Final Result:**
