> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCET304 - SURVEYING AND GEOMATICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Global Positioning Systems - Components and Principles # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Global Positioning System (GPS)** is a satellite-based navigation and positioning system developed and maintained by the **United States Department of Defense (US DoD)** that provides three-dimensional position, velocity, and time information (PVT) to suitably equipped users anywhere on or near the Earth, in all weather conditions, 24 hours a day. GPS is officially designated as **NAVSTAR GPS** (Navigation Satellite Timing And Ranging Global Positioning System) and became fully operational on **17 July 1995** with a constellation of **24 satellites** (Baseline) orbiting at an altitude of approximately **20,200 km** above the Earth's surface in **Medium Earth Orbit (MEO)**. The full operational constellation (as per 2024 standards) consists of **31+ operational satellites** distributed across **6 orbital planes** inclined at **55°** to the equatorial plane, with an orbital period of approximately **11 hours 58 minutes** (half a sidereal day). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** GPS is a space-based radio-positioning and time-transfer system that provides accurate three-dimensional coordinates (X, Y, Z or Latitude, Longitude, Height) and precise time to an unlimited number of users equipped with suitable receivers. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are lost in a dense forest at night. You have three friends stationed at distant, well-known hilltops, each carrying a powerful torch (a satellite). Each friend shouts out the **distance** between themselves and you (the range) and the **exact time** they shouted it. - If you know the distance to **Friend 1**, you are somewhere on a **sphere** of that radius around Friend 1. - If you also know the distance to **Friend 2**, you are at one of **two intersection points** of two spheres — a circle. - Add **Friend 3**, and you narrow it down to **two possible points** in 3D space. - A **fourth** friend is needed to resolve the timing ambiguity in your receiver's clock (since cheap receiver clocks are not atomic-grade), giving you the **single unique 3D position**. This is essentially how GPS works — using **range measurements from at least 4 satellites** to triangulate your position on Earth. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Critical GPS Constants to Memorize:** > - Number of satellites (Baseline): **24** > - Orbital planes: **6** > - Inclination of orbits: **55°** > - Orbital altitude: **20,200 km** above Earth's surface > - Orbital period: **11 hours 58 minutes** (≈ 12 sidereal hours) > - Satellite signal speed: Speed of light **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** > - Carrier frequencies: **L1 = 1575.42 MHz**, **L2 = 1227.60 MHz**, **L5 = 1176.45 MHz** > - Code chipping rate (C/A code): **1.023 MHz** > - Code chipping rate (P code): **10.23 MHz** > - Wavelength of L1 carrier: **≈ 19.05 cm** > - Wavelength of L2 carrier: **≈ 24.45 cm** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Three Major Segments of GPS The entire GPS architecture is divided into three functional segments: ### 2.1.1 Space Segment - The **constellation of satellites** transmitting radio signals. - Each satellite broadcasts on **two (or more) carrier frequencies** modulated with navigation data, coarse/acquisition (C/A) code, and precise (P) code. - Satellites are powered by **solar panels** with **nickel-hydrogen batteries** as backup. - Each satellite carries **4 atomic clocks** — 2 cesium and 2 rubidium — providing timing accuracy of **±1 nanosecond**. - Design life: **10 years**; current satellites are the **Block III/IIIF** series. ### 2.1.2 Control Segment (Ground Segment) - A worldwide network of **monitoring and control stations** that tracks the satellites, computes their precise orbital elements (ephemeris), and uploads updated navigation messages. - Components: - **1 Master Control Station (MCS)** — at **Schriever Air Force Base, Colorado, USA** - **11 Command and Control Antennas** (ground antennas for upload) - **16 Monitoring Stations** distributed globally - Functions: orbit determination, time synchronization, clock error correction, atmospheric model updates, and integrity monitoring. ### 2.1.3 User Segment - Includes all **GPS receivers** (civilian and military) and the user community. - Receivers decode satellite signals to compute **position (x, y, z), velocity (vx, vy, vz), and time (t)**. - Types: hand-held, surveying-grade (geodetic), marine, aviation, automotive. ## 2.2 Working Principle — Ranging and Trilateration GPS uses the principle of **one-way passive ranging** based on **Time of Arrival (TOA)** measurement. The fundamental steps are: - Satellites transmit signals at precisely known instants (defined by their atomic clocks). - The receiver records the time of arrival using its own (less accurate) clock. - The difference multiplied by the speed of light gives the **pseudorange**. - Using pseudoranges from at least **4 satellites**, the receiver solves for **(x, y, z, t)** via a system of linearized equations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why 4 satellites and not 3?** > Three satellites fix the 3D position mathematically, but the receiver's clock has an unknown bias (typically a few milliseconds). The 4th satellite provides the **4th equation** needed to solve for the **4 unknowns (X, Y, Z, dt)**. ## 2.3 Pseudorange Concept The measured range is called **pseudorange** because it contains errors due to the receiver clock bias, atmospheric delays, etc. $$R_{measured} = c \times (t_{received} - t_{transmitted})$$ True range: $$R_{true} = \sqrt{(X_s - X_r)^2 + (Y_s - Y_r)^2 + (Z_s - Z_r)^2}$$ Pseudorange equation: $$PR = R_{true} + c \cdot \Delta t_r + c \cdot \Delta t_{atm} + c \cdot \Delta t_{other}$$ Where: - $PR$ = measured pseudorange - $R_{true}$ = geometric (true) range - $c \cdot \Delta t_r$ = receiver clock error - $c \cdot \Delta t_{atm}$ = atmospheric (ionospheric + tropospheric) delay - $c \cdot \Delta t_{other}$ = other errors (multipath, noise, relativistic) ## 2.4 Sources of Error in GPS | Error Source | Approximate Magnitude | Mitigation | |---|---|---| | Satellite Clock Error | ± 2 m | Dual-frequency correction / broadcast ephemeris correction | | Ephemeris (Orbit) Error | ± 2.5 m | SBAS / precise ephemeris | | Ionospheric Delay | ± 5 m | Dual-frequency receivers / Klobuchar model | | Tropospheric Delay | ± 0.5 m | Tropospheric model (Hopfield / Saastamoinen) | | Multipath | ± 1 m | Antenna design / choke ring / siting | | Receiver Noise | ± 0.3 m | Better electronics / averaging | | Selective Availability (SA) | ± 10–25 m (turned OFF on 1 May 2000) | Discontinued | > [!NOTE] > **Total User Range Error (URE)** without SA ≈ **± 4 m** (1-sigma) which yields typical standalone accuracy of **± 5–10 m** in horizontal position. ## 2.5 Dilution of Precision (DOP) DOP is a dimensionless number that describes the **geometric strength** of the satellite configuration. Better geometry (satellites well spread across the sky) yields a **lower DOP** and thus better accuracy. | DOP Type | Formula Symbol | Represents | |---|---|---| | GDOP | Geometric Dilution of Precision | Position + Time | | PDOP | Position Dilution of Precision | 3D position | | HDOP | Horizontal Dilution of Precision | Latitude + Longitude | | VDOP | Vertical Dilution of Precision | Height | | TDOP | Time Dilution of Precision | Clock bias | | RDOP | Relative Dilution of Precision | Used in RAIM | **Accuracy relation:** $$\sigma_{position} = \sigma_{URE} \times DOP$$ | DOP Value | Quality | Rating | |---|---|---| | 1 – 3 | Excellent | High confidence | | 4 – 6 | Good | Acceptable | | 7 – 8 | Moderate | Marginal | | > 8 | Poor | Reject | ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Term | Expression | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Pseudorange | $PR = c \cdot \Delta t$ | meters | | 2 | True range | $R = \sqrt{(X_s-X_r)^2 + (Y_s-Y_r)^2 + (Z_s-Z_r)^2}$ | meters | | 3 | Position accuracy | $\sigma = \sigma_{URE} \times DOP$ | meters | | 4 | Speed of light | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ | m/s | | 5 | L1 wavelength | $\lambda_1 = c/f_1 = (3 \times 10^8)/(1575.42 \times 10^6)$ | $\approx 0.1905$ m | | 6 | L2 wavelength | $\lambda_2 = c/f_2 = (3 \times 10^8)/(1227.60 \times 10^6)$ | $\approx 0.2445$ m | | 7 | Number of satellites needed | 4 minimum | for 3D fix | | 8 | Orbital altitude | $h = 20,200$ | km | | 9 | Orbital radius | $r = R_E + h$ | $R_E = 6378$ km | | 10 | Orbital period | $T = 11$ h $58$ min | sidereal | | 11 | Code resolution (C/A) | $\Delta L = c \cdot t_{chip} = 3 \times 10^8 \times (1/1.023 \times 10^6)$ | $\approx 293$ m | | 12 | Code resolution (P) | $\Delta L = c \cdot t_{chip}$ | $\approx 29.3$ m | | 13 | Carrier phase resolution | $\lambda/100$ (millimeter level) | mm precision | | 14 | Iono delay (1st order) | $\Delta_{iono} = (40.3 / f^2) \cdot TEC$ | m | | 15 | Free iono combination | $L_C = (f_1^2 \cdot L_1 - f_2^2 \cdot L_2) / (f_1^2 - f_2^2)$ | eliminates iono delay | | 16 | GDOP definition | $GDOP = \sqrt{q_{xx}+q_{yy}+q_{zz}+q_{tt}}$ | from covariance matrix | ## 2.7 Real-World Utility of GPS in Engineering - **Cadastral and land surveying** — boundary demarcation, sub-cm accuracy via RTK/PPK - **Construction layout** — setting out structures, roads, and bridges - **Mining** — equipment tracking, blast monitoring, slope stability - **Hydrography** — bathymetric surveys - **Geodetic control networks** — replacing triangulation/trilateration - **GIS data collection** — asset mapping - **Machine guidance** — autonomous vehicles, precision agriculture - **Deformation monitoring** — dams, landslides, high-rise buildings # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Pseudorange Positioning Equation ### Step 1 — Definition of Pseudorange A satellite transmits a navigation message at a precise epoch $t_s$ (in satellite time). The receiver captures the signal at its local epoch $t_r$ (in receiver time). The elapsed time is: $$\Delta t = t_r - t_s$$ The pseudorange is the apparent distance calculated using this elapsed time and the speed of light: $$PR = c \cdot (t_r - t_s)$$ ### Step 2 — Decomposition of the True Range The true geometric distance between the satellite and the receiver is: $$R_{true} = \sqrt{(X_s - X_r)^2 + (Y_s - Y_r)^2 + (Z_s - Z_r)^2}$$ where: - $(X_s, Y_s, Z_s)$ = satellite coordinates in ECEF (Earth-Centered, Earth-Fixed) frame - $(X_r, Y_r, Z_r)$ = receiver coordinates in ECEF frame ### Step 3 — Inclusion of Receiver Clock Error Since the receiver clock is imperfect, it has a bias $\delta t_r$ relative to GPS time: $$t_r = t_{true} + \delta t_r$$ ### Step 4 — Inclusion of Other Errors Adding all error terms $\varepsilon$: $$PR = R_{true} + c \cdot \delta t_r + \varepsilon_{ion} + \varepsilon_{tropo} + \varepsilon_{multi} + \varepsilon_{eph} + \varepsilon_{clk}$$ ### Step 5 — Rearranged Standard Form (Ignoring Small Terms for First Approximation) $$PR = \sqrt{(X_s - X_r)^2 + (Y_s - Y_r)^2 + (Z_s - Z_r)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_r$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Solving the System Using 4 Satellites Let us write the pseudorange equations for 4 satellites: $$ \begin{aligned} PR_1 &= \sqrt{(X_{s1} - X_r)^2 + (Y_{s1} - Y_r)^2 + (Z_{s1} - Z_r)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_r \\ PR_2 &= \sqrt{(X_{s2} - X_r)^2 + (Y_{s2} - Y_r)^2 + (Z_{s2} - Z_r)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_r \\ PR_3 &= \sqrt{(X_{s3} - X_r)^2 + (Y_{s3} - Y_r)^2 + (Z_{s3} - Z_r)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_r \\ PR_4 &= \sqrt{(X_{s4} - X_r)^2 + (Y_{s4} - Y_r)^2 + (Z_{s4} - Z_r)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_r \end{aligned} $$ **This is a system of 4 nonlinear equations with 4 unknowns**: $X_r, Y_r, Z_r, \delta t_r$. ### Linearization Using an Initial Guess (Taylor Series Expansion) Provide an initial estimate $(X_0, Y_0, Z_0, \delta t_{r,0})$. Expand each equation using first-order Taylor series: $$ \begin{aligned} PR_i &\approx PR_{i,0} + \frac{\partial PR_i}{\partial X_r}\Delta X_r + \frac{\partial PR_i}{\partial Y_r}\Delta Y_r + \frac{\partial PR_i}{\partial Z_r}\Delta Z_r + \frac{\partial PR_i}{\partial \delta t_r}\Delta(\delta t_r) \end{aligned} $$ Compute the partial derivatives: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial PR_i}{\partial X_r} &= -\frac{(X_{si} - X_{r,0})}{R_{i,0}} = -l_i \\ \frac{\partial PR_i}{\partial Y_r} &= -\frac{(Y_{si} - Y_{r,0})}{R_{i,0}} = -m_i \\ \frac{\partial PR_i}{\partial Z_r} &= -\frac{(Z_{si} - Z_{r,0})}{R_{i,0}} = -n_i \\ \frac{\partial PR_i}{\partial \delta t_r} &= c \end{aligned} $$ where $(l_i, m_i, n_i)$ are the **direction cosines** from receiver to satellite $i$. ### Final Linearized Matrix Form $$ \begin{aligned} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta PR_1 \\ \Delta PR_2 \\ \Delta PR_3 \\ \Delta PR_4 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} -l_1 & -m_1 & -n_1 & 1 \\ -l_2 & -m_2 & -n_2 & 1 \\ -l_3 & -m_3 & -n_3 & 1 \\ -l_4 & -m_4 & -n_4 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta X_r \\ \Delta Y_r \\ \Delta Z_r \\ c \cdot \Delta(\delta t_r) \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ Let $\Delta \mathbf{PR} = \mathbf{H} \cdot \Delta \mathbf{X}$. Solve using **least squares** (for more than 4 satellites) or direct inversion (for exactly 4): $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta \mathbf{X} &= \mathbf{H}^{-1} \cdot \Delta \mathbf{PR} \quad \text{(for 4 satellites)} \\ \Delta \mathbf{X} &= (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H})^{-1} \mathbf{H}^T \Delta \mathbf{PR} \quad \text{(for >4 satellites)} \end{aligned} $$ Update the estimate and **iterate** until $\Delta \mathbf{X}$ is negligibly small (convergence). ## 3.3 Derivation of GDOP from the Geometry Matrix The **covariance matrix** of the position-time solution is: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathbf{Q} &= (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H})^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} q_{xx} & q_{xy} & q_{xz} & q_{xt} \\ q_{xy} & q_{yy} & q_{yz} & q_{yt} \\ q_{xz} & q_{yz} & q_{zz} & q_{zt} \\ q_{xt} & q_{yt} & q_{zt} & q_{tt} \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ The various DOPs are defined as: $$ \begin{aligned} GDOP &= \sqrt{q_{xx} + q_{yy} + q_{zz} + q_{tt}} \\ PDOP &= \sqrt{q_{xx} + q_{yy} + q_{zz}} \\ HDOP &= \sqrt{q_{xx} + q_{yy}} \\ VDOP &= \sqrt{q_{zz}} \\ TDOP &= \sqrt{q_{tt}} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A receiver measures pseudoranges from 4 satellites. The pseudoranges are $PR_1 = 22{,}000{,}000$ m, $PR_2 = 21{,}500{,}000$ m, $PR_3 = 22{,}500{,}000$ m, $PR_4 = 23{,}000{,}000$ m. Given the simplified direction cosines below and initial guess of Earth's center, compute the correction. | Satellite | $l_i$ | $m_i$ | $n_i$ | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0.5 | 0.5 | 0.7 | | 2 | -0.4 | 0.6 | 0.7 | | 3 | 0.3 | -0.5 | 0.8 | | 4 | -0.6 | -0.4 | 0.7 | **Initial guess:** $(X_0, Y_0, Z_0) = (0, 0, 0)$ and $\delta t_{r,0} = 0$. Therefore, $R_{i,0} \approx PR_i$. Step 1 — Compute the design matrix $\mathbf{H}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 & -0.5 & -0.7 & 1 \\ 0.4 & -0.6 & -0.7 & 1 \\ -0.3 & 0.5 & -0.8 & 1 \\ 0.6 & 0.4 & -0.7 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ Step 2 — Compute residuals $\Delta PR_i = PR_i - R_{i,0}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta PR_i = 0 \quad \text{(since we used pseudoranges as initial ranges)} \Rightarrow \Delta \mathbf{X} = 0 \end{aligned} $$ Step 3 — In a realistic case, $\Delta PR_i \neq 0$ and one would solve $\Delta \mathbf{X} = \mathbf{H}^{-1} \Delta \mathbf{PR}$. For the sake of demonstration, assume residuals: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta \mathbf{PR} = \begin{bmatrix} 50 \\ -30 \\ 40 \\ 20 \end{bmatrix} \text{ meters} \end{aligned} $$ Step 4 — Solve $\Delta \mathbf{X} = \mathbf{H}^{-1} \Delta \mathbf{PR}$ (matrix inversion, normally done by software): $$ \begin{aligned} \mathbf{H}^{-1} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.80 & 0.60 & -0.20 & -0.50 \\ 0.40 & -0.30 & 0.90 & -0.20 \\ -0.50 & 0.40 & 0.10 & 0.60 \\ 0.30 & 0.50 & -0.40 & 0.20 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta \mathbf{X} &= \mathbf{H}^{-1} \cdot \Delta \mathbf{PR} \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.80 \cdot 50 + 0.60 \cdot (-30) + (-0.20) \cdot 40 + (-0.50) \cdot 20 \\ 0.40 \cdot 50 + (-0.30)(-30) + 0.90 \cdot 40 + (-0.20) \cdot 20 \\ -0.50 \cdot 50 + 0.40 \cdot (-30) + 0.10 \cdot 40 + 0.60 \cdot 20 \\ 0.30 \cdot 50 + 0.50 \cdot (-30) + (-0.40) \cdot 40 + 0.20 \cdot 20 \end{bmatrix} \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} 40 - 18 - 8 - 10 \\ 20 + 9 + 36 - 4 \\ -25 - 12 + 4 + 12 \\ 15 - 15 - 16 + 4 \end{bmatrix} \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 61 \\ -21 \\ -12 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ Step 5 — Interpretation: - $\Delta X_r = 4$ m, $\Delta Y_r = 61$ m, $\Delta Z_r = -21$ m - $c \cdot \Delta(\delta t_r) = -12$ m $\Rightarrow \Delta(\delta t_r) = -12 / (3 \times 10^8) = -40$ ns The new receiver position is updated and the receiver clock corrected accordingly. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix Summary of Key GPS Equations | Concept | Governing Equation | Significance | |---|---|---| | Pseudorange | $PR = c \cdot \Delta t$ | Apparent range with errors | | True range | $R = \sqrt{\Delta X^2 + \Delta Y^2 + \Delta Z^2}$ | Geometric distance | | Position accuracy | $\sigma = \sigma_{URE} \times DOP$ | Accuracy depends on geometry | | Geometry matrix | $\mathbf{H}$ with direction cosines | Used in least-squares solution | | Iono delay (1st order) | $\Delta_{iono} = (40.3 \cdot TEC) / f^2$ | Frequency dependent | | Carrier wavelength | $\lambda = c / f$ | Determines phase resolution | | Range from carrier phase | $R = \lambda(N + \varphi)$ | $N$ = integer ambiguity | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of GPS ```mermaid graph TD A[SPACE SEGMENT
24+ Satellites
6 Orbital Planes
Inclination 55 deg] -->|Radio Signals L1 L2 L5| B[RECEIVER ANTENNA] B --> C[USER SEGMENT
GPS RECEIVER] D[CONTROL SEGMENT
Master Station Colorado
16 Monitoring Stations
11 Ground Antennas] -->|Upload Navigation Data
Clock Correction Ephemeris| A D -->|Telemetry Tracking Command| A C --> E[RF FRONT END
Amplification Filtering] E --> F[CORRELATOR
Code and Carrier Tracking] F --> G[NAVIGATION PROCESSOR
Pseudorange Computation] G --> H[LEAST SQUARES SOLVER
4 Satellite Equations] H --> I[POSITION VELOCITY TIME
X Y Z Vx Vy Vz t] H --> J[DOP COMPUTATION
GDOP PDOP HDOP VDOP] style A fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333 style D fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#333 style C fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333 style I fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.2 GPS Positioning Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Satellite Transmits
Pseudorandom Code and
Navigation Message] --> S2 S2[Step 2: Receiver Captures Signal
at Local Clock Time] --> S3 S3[Step 3: Compute Pseudorange
PR = c times Delta t] --> S4 S4[Step 4: Extract Ephemeris
to Get Satellite Coordinates Xs Ys Zs] --> S5 S5[Step 5: Form 4 or More
Nonlinear Pseudorange Equations] --> S6 S6[Step 6: Linearize Using
Taylor Series Expansion] --> S7 S7[Step 7: Build Design Matrix H
with Direction Cosines] --> S8 S8[Step 8: Solve Least Squares
for Delta Xr Delta Yr Delta Zr Delta dt] --> S9 S9[Step 9: Update Estimate and
Iterate Until Convergence] --> S10 S10[Step 10: Output Final Position
X Y Z in ECEF or LLH] --> S11 S11[Step 11: Compute DOP
from Covariance Matrix] --> S12 S12[Step 12: Apply Atmospheric
and Error Corrections] --> End((Final Fix)) style S1 fill:#ffeb99 style End fill:#ff9999 ``` ## 4.3 Error Sources Hierarchy in GPS ```mermaid graph TD A[GPS PSEUDORANGE ERROR SOURCES] --> B[SATELLITE RELATED] A --> C[PROPAGATION RELATED] A --> D[RECEIVER RELATED] A --> E[GEOMETRY RELATED] B --> B1[Clock Bias 2 m] B --> B2[Orbital Ephemeris 2.5 m] C --> C1[Ionospheric Delay 5 m] C --> C2[Tropospheric Delay 0.5 m] D --> D1[Receiver Clock and Noise 0.3 m] D --> D2[Multipath 1 m] E --> E1[Poor Dilution of Precision
PDOP Greater than 6] style A fill:#ffcc99 style B fill:#ffffcc style C fill:#ffffcc style D fill:#ffffcc style E fill:#ffffcc ``` ## 4.4 GPS Signal Structure (L1 Band — Conceptual) ```mermaid graph LR L1[L1 CARRIER
1575.42 MHz] --> M[MODULATED BY] CA[C/A CODE
1.023 MHz
Civilian] P[P CODE
10.23 MHz
Encrypted] N[NAVIGATION MESSAGE
50 bps
Ephemeris Almanac] M --> CA M --> P M --> N style L1 fill:#cce5ff style CA fill:#ccffcc style P fill:#ffcccc style N fill:#ffffcc ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]** **List the three segments of GPS and state the functions of the control segment.** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** The three segments of GPS are: 1. **Space Segment** — consists of 24+ satellites orbiting the Earth in 6 planes at 20,200 km altitude. 2. **Control Segment (Ground Segment)** — consists of 1 Master Control Station, 16 Monitor Stations, and 11 Ground Antennas. 3. **User Segment** — consists of all GPS receivers (civilian and military) and their operators. **Functions of the Control Segment:** - Tracking the GPS satellites and monitoring their orbital parameters - Computing and uploading **navigation data** (ephemeris, clock corrections, almanac) - Maintaining **synchronization** of satellite atomic clocks with GPS master time - Detecting and correcting satellite malfunctions, and ensuring constellation health - Performing **integrity monitoring** of the system --- ### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** **Why do we need a minimum of four satellites to obtain a 3D position fix in GPS?** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** A 3D position fix requires solving for **three unknowns** (X, Y, Z coordinates of the receiver). However, the receiver's internal clock is not perfectly synchronized with GPS time and has an unknown **clock bias** ($\delta t_r$). This bias translates into a range error of $c \cdot \delta t_r$, which can be as large as several kilometers if not corrected. Therefore, the system has **four unknowns**: $X_r, Y_r, Z_r, \delta t_r$. Each satellite provides one independent pseudorange equation. Hence, **a minimum of four satellites** is required to set up four independent equations and uniquely solve for the four unknowns. Three satellites would leave the clock bias unresolved, leading to large position errors. --- ## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (1 × 14 = 14 Marks) *Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE full question.* --- ### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** Explain the fundamental working principle of GPS with a neat block diagram of the user segment. Discuss how the pseudorange is computed. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** What is Dilution of Precision (DOP)? Explain the various types of DOP and their significance in GPS surveying. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Working Principle:** GPS operates on the principle of **one-way passive ranging** using precise time-of-arrival measurements. The fundamental steps are: 1. Each GPS satellite continuously broadcasts a **spread-spectrum signal** containing a unique PRN code, navigation message (with its own ephemeris and clock corrections), and carrier signal. 2. The GPS receiver at the user's location receives these signals and, by **correlating** the incoming C/A or P code with a locally generated replica, measures the **time shift** between transmission and reception. 3. This time difference, multiplied by the speed of light, gives the **pseudorange** $PR = c \cdot \Delta t$. 4. Using pseudoranges to at least **4 satellites** (with known positions from their broadcast ephemerides), the receiver solves the **trilateration equations** to compute its 3D position and clock bias. **Block Diagram of User Segment:** ``` Antenna → RF Front End (LNA, Filter) → Down-Converter → IF Stage → Sampling → Correlator (Code/Carrier Tracking) → Pseudorange Generator → Navigation Processor (Position, Velocity, Time) ``` **Pseudorange Computation:** $$ \begin{aligned} PR &= c \times (t_{received} - t_{transmitted}) \\ PR &= R_{true} + c \cdot \delta t_r + c \cdot \delta t_s + I + T + M + \varepsilon \end{aligned} $$ where: - $R_{true}$ = geometric range - $c \cdot \delta t_r$ = receiver clock error - $c \cdot \delta t_s$ = satellite clock error - $I$ = ionospheric delay - $T$ = tropospheric delay - $M$ = multipath error - $\varepsilon$ = measurement noise The term *pseudo* (false) range arises because the measured range is contaminated by these errors. For surveying-grade work, errors are corrected using dual-frequency, atmospheric models, and differential techniques. #### Model Solution for (b): **Definition of DOP:** Dilution of Precision (DOP) is a **dimensionless factor** that quantifies the effect of satellite geometry on the accuracy of a GPS position fix. It is the ratio of the positioning accuracy achieved to the ranging accuracy of the measurements: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{position} &= \sigma_{URE} \times DOP \end{aligned} $$ Lower DOP ⇒ better satellite geometry ⇒ higher position accuracy. A DOP of 1 is ideal; values > 8 indicate poor geometry and the fix should be rejected. **Types of DOP:** | Type | Stands for | Affects | |---|---|---| | GDOP | Geometric | Position + time | | PDOP | Position | 3D position | | HDOP | Horizontal | Latitude, Longitude | | VDOP | Vertical | Height | | TDOP | Time | Clock bias | **Significance in GPS Surveying:** - **Survey planning** — masks low-elevation satellites (cutoff angle 10–15°) to avoid multipath and improve DOP - **Mission planning software** (e.g., Trimble Planning) — predicts DOP for a given day/time before fieldwork - **RTK and static surveys** — require PDOP < 6 for reliable cm-level results - **Network design** — when establishing a control network, choose observation windows with low PDOP --- ### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Question]** **(a)** Describe the space segment of GPS in detail. Include satellite orbital characteristics, signal structure, and atomic clocks. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the major sources of error in GPS measurements and explain how each is mitigated in modern receivers. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Space Segment — Detailed Description:** The space segment consists of the **constellation of GPS satellites** that broadcast ranging signals and navigation data to users worldwide. **Constellation Design:** - **Minimum baseline constellation:** 24 satellites - **Current operational:** 31+ satellites - **Orbital planes:** 6 - **Inclination:** 55° to the equatorial plane - **Altitude:** ~20,200 km (Medium Earth Orbit) - **Orbital period:** 11 hours 58 minutes (half a sidereal day, ensuring same sky geometry repeats daily) - **Repeat ground track:** twice per day **Satellite Subsystems:** 1. **Atomic Clocks (4 per satellite)**: - 2 Cesium clocks — long-term stability - 2 Rubidium clocks — short-term stability - Frequency stability: ±1 × 10⁻¹³ - Provide timing accuracy better than ±1 nanosecond 2. **Power Subsystem**: - Solar panels generating ~700 W - Ni-H₂ backup batteries for eclipse periods 3. **TT&C (Telemetry, Tracking & Command)**: - S-band uplink/downlink for control segment communication 4. **L-band Signal Generation**: - Generates L1 (1575.42 MHz), L2 (1227.60 MHz), L5 (1176.45 MHz) carriers - Modulated by C/A code, P(Y) code, and navigation message **Signal Structure:** Each satellite transmits on L1 (and L2/L5) modulated by: - **C/A code** (Coarse/Acquisition) — 1.023 Mbps, 1 ms period, accessible to civilians - **P(Y) code** (Precise, encrypted) — 10.23 Mbps, 267-day period, military only - **Navigation Message** — 50 bps, contains ephemeris, almanac, clock corrections, ionospheric model **Generation of Signal:** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{L1}(t) &= A_P P(t) D(t) \cos(2\pi f_1 t) + A_C C(t) D(t) \sin(2\pi f_1 t) \\ S_{L2}(t) &= B_P P(t) D(t) \cos(2\pi f_2 t) \end{aligned} $$ where $C(t)$ is C/A code, $P(t)$ is P code, $D(t)$ is navigation data, and $f_1, f_2$ are L1, L2 carrier frequencies. #### Model Solution for (b): **Major Sources of GPS Errors:** 1. **Ionospheric Delay (≈ 5 m)** - **Cause:** Free electrons in the ionosphere (60–1000 km altitude) slow the signal — group delay for code, phase advance for carrier. - **Mitigation:** Dual-frequency receivers compute an **ionosphere-free linear combination** $L_C = (f_1^2 L_1 - f_2^2 L_2)/(f_1^2 - f_2^2)$ which removes ~99% of the first-order ionospheric error. Single-frequency users apply the **Klobuchar model** transmitted in the navigation message. 2. **Tropospheric Delay (≈ 0.5 m at zenith, up to 2.4 m at low elevation)** - **Cause:** Neutral atmosphere causes a non-dispersive delay (same for all frequencies). - **Mitigation:** Standard models like **Hopfield** or **Saastamoinen** using surface meteorological data. Apply a mapping function to project zenith delay to slant range. 3. **Multipath (≈ 1 m, can be higher near reflectors)** - **Cause:** Signal reflections from buildings, ground, water, vehicles. - **Mitigation:** Use **choke-ring antennas**, raise elevation cutoff (15°), use **wideband** signals (L5), improved receiver correlator technology (narrow correlator, MEDLL). 4. **Satellite Clock Error (≈ 2 m)** - **Cause:** Atomic clocks still drift. - **Mitigation:** Clock correction parameters in broadcast ephemeris; precise ephemeris products from IGS. 5. **Orbital (Ephemeris) Error (≈ 2.5 m)** - **Cause:** Imperfect knowledge of satellite position. - **Mitigation:** Differential GPS (DGPS), precise ephemeris from IGS, SBAS corrections. 6. **Receiver Noise (≈ 0.3 m)** - **Cause:** Internal thermal noise and quantization. - **Mitigation:** Better electronics, longer observation time, phase smoothing. 7. **Satellite Geometry (DOP)** - **Cause:** Poor angular separation between satellites amplifies range errors. - **Mitigation:** Survey planning software, mask low-elevation satellites, use multi-constellation (GPS + GLONASS + Galileo + IRNSS + BeiDou). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students often **omit the user segment** or **miss the function of monitoring stations** — mention the *Master Control Station* by name (Schriever AFB, Colorado) and the **role of the control segment** in uploading navigation data. Examiners frequently ask for this in **Part A** of the exam. > - In numerical questions, **forget the 4th satellite equation** — always write the 4th equation with clock-bias term to demonstrate the **4 unknowns / 4 equations** logic. > - In DOP questions, students often confuse HDOP with PDOP. Remember: **HDOP** is horizontal only; **PDOP** is 3D; **GDOP** includes time. > - **Selective Availability (SA)** was **DISABLED on 1 May 2000** — never state it is still active. > - Always mention that **atomic clocks** in satellites (Cs and Rb) provide the timing reference, not the receiver's quartz clock. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **GPS** = NAVSTAR, owned by **US DoD**, fully operational from **17 July 1995** - **Three segments:** Space, Control, User — always name all three in any descriptive question - **Constellation:** 24+ satellites, 6 planes, 55° inclination, 20,200 km altitude - **Orbital period:** 11 h 58 min (half sidereal day) - **L1 = 1575.42 MHz, L2 = 1227.60 MHz, L5 = 1176.45 MHz** (frequencies must be exact) - **C/A code = 1.023 MHz** (civilian), **P code = 10.23 MHz** (military) - **Speed of light c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** is used in every pseudorange calculation - **Pseudorange** = apparent range; **true range** = geometric distance; difference is **clock bias + errors** - **4 satellites minimum** for a 3D fix due to 4 unknowns (X, Y, Z, δt) - **Trilateration** (not triangulation) is the correct method - **Master Control Station** is in **Schriever AFB, Colorado, USA** - **DOP** = geometric strength factor; **lower is better**; PDOP < 6 is desirable for surveying - **Ionospheric delay** is dispersive (frequency dependent) — removed by **dual-frequency combination** - **Tropospheric delay** is non-dispersive — removed by **Hopfield / Saastamoinen** models - **Selective Availability (SA)** was turned **off on 1 May 2000** — historical fact often asked - **Horizontal accuracy (standalone):** ~5–10 m; **with DGPS:** ~1–3 m; **RTK:** cm-level - **Multipath mitigation** = choke-ring antenna + elevation mask + site selection - **GDOP² = PDOP² + TDOP²**; **PDOP² = HDOP² + VDOP²** - The **MOST IMPORTANT FORMULA** for KTU derivations is: $\sigma_{position} = \sigma_{URE} \times DOP$ - **Carrier phase ranging** is used in **geodetic / surveying-grade receivers** for mm-cm accuracy - **Code-based ranging** is used in **hand-held / navigation-grade receivers** for m-level accuracy - The **ionosphere-free (LC) combination** is given by: $L_C = (f_1^2 \cdot L_1 - f_2^2 \cdot L_2) / (f_1^2 - f_2^2)$ - **GNSS** (Global Navigation Satellite System) is the generic term — GPS is one of several (GLONASS, Galileo, BeiDou, IRNSS/NavIC, QZSS)

topics.title: Satellite Ranging - Calculating Position
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET304 - SURVEYING AND GEOMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Satellite Ranging - Calculating Position

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Satellite Ranging** is the geodetic technique of determining the distance between a ground-based receiver and an orbiting satellite by precisely measuring the time taken for a signal (electromagnetic wave) to travel from the satellite to the receiver. This measured range, when combined with the known orbital coordinates of multiple satellites, enables the calculation of the receiver's three-dimensional position (X, Y, Z or Latitude, Longitude, Height) on or near the Earth's surface through the geometric principle of **trilateration**.

The fundamental governing equation is:

$$\rho = c \cdot \Delta t$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = Geometric range (slant distance) between satellite and receiver
- $c$ = Speed of light in vacuum ($\mathbf{2.99792458 \times 10^8}$ m/s)
- $\Delta t$ = Signal travel time

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In real-world GNSS operations, the directly measured quantity is the **pseudo-range** (because the receiver and satellite clocks are not perfectly synchronized). True range requires clock-error correction via the **pseudorange equation**:
>
> $$PR = c \cdot (t_R - t^T) + c \cdot \Delta t_{errors}$$
>
> Where $t_R$ is reception time, $t^T$ is transmission time, and $\Delta t_{errors}$ captures ionospheric, tropospheric, and clock biases.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

**The Lighthouse Analogy:**

Imagine you are lost at sea at night. You spot **four lighthouses** on distant cliffs. Each lighthouse flashes a light at a precisely known time. If you know the *exact* moment each flash occurred (because they are perfectly synchronized) and the *exact* moment you saw the flash (with a stopwatch), you can compute how far each lighthouse is from your boat (using speed of light × time = distance). Once you know your distance from four known points, you can draw spheres around each lighthouse — and the unique point where all four spheres intersect is **your boat's exact 3D position**.

A satellite is simply a lighthouse **orbiting at ~20,200 km altitude**, and the "flashes" are coded radio signals. Your GPS receiver is the "boat with a stopwatch."

> [!NOTE]
> **Why four satellites, not three?**
> - 3 satellites would fix your X, Y, Z position mathematically.
> - But because your **receiver clock is cheap and unsynchronized**, the unknown receiver time offset (Δt) becomes a 4th unknown.
> - Therefore, **4 satellites are the minimum** to solve 4 unknowns (X, Y, Z, Δt) simultaneously.
> - This is the **rank principle** of GNSS positioning.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | $2.99792458 \times 10^8$ m/s | Defines conversion of time to range |
| GPS satellite altitude | $h$ | ~20,200 km | Medium Earth Orbit (MEO) |
| GPS orbital period | $T$ | ~11 h 58 min (sidereal day) | Two revolutions per sidereal day |
| L1 carrier frequency | $f_{L1}$ | 1575.42 MHz | Coarse/Acquisition (C/A) code |
| L2 carrier frequency | $f_{L2}$ | 1227.60 MHz | Precision (P) code |
| Number of operational GPS satellites | $N$ | ≥ 31 (nominal constellation) | Ensures global 4-satellite visibility |
| Wavelength of L1 | $\lambda_{L1}$ | ~19.05 cm | Fundamental unit for carrier-phase ranging |
| Earth's equatorial radius | $R_e$ | 6,378.137 km | Reference for geodetic coordinates |
| Earth's flattening | $f$ | 1/298.257223563 | WGS-84 ellipsoid parameter |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown — Step-by-Step Logic

**Stage 1: Signal Generation at the Satellite**
- Each GPS satellite transmits a unique **Pseudo-Random Noise (PRN) code** modulated on **two carrier frequencies (L1 and L2)**.
- The PRN code is a binary sequence known to the receiver — it serves as a "fingerprint" identifying the satellite and enabling time-tagging.

**Stage 2: Signal Propagation**
- The signal travels from satellite to receiver through:
  - **Ionosphere** (60–1000 km altitude) — dispersive medium; introduces frequency-dependent delay.
  - **Troposphere** (0–60 km altitude) — non-dispersive; introduces neutral atmospheric delay.
  - **Line-of-sight vacuum path** — purely geometric.

**Stage 3: Reception and Correlation**
- The receiver generates a replica of every satellite's PRN code.
- It **shifts** its replica in time until correlation peak is found.
- The time shift required = signal travel time $\Delta t$.

**Stage 4: Pseudo-Range Computation**
- Pseudo-range (PR) = $c \times (t_R - t^T)$
- **Critical insight:** "Pseudo" because the time difference includes the receiver clock error $\delta t_R$ and satellite clock error $\delta t^T$.

**Stage 5: Position Solution via Least-Squares**
- With ≥ 4 pseudo-ranges, solve the system:
  $$PR_i = \sqrt{(X_i - x)^2 + (Y_i - y)^2 + (Z_i - z)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_R$$
  where $(X_i, Y_i, Z_i)$ = known satellite coordinates, $(x, y, z)$ = unknown receiver position.

**Stage 6: Iterative Refinement**
- The equations are **non-linear**. Linearize using Taylor series expansion around an initial guess, then iterate (Newton-Raphson / least-squares) until convergence.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU Board Exam High-Yield** — memorize, understand, and practice.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho = c \cdot \Delta t$ | True geometric range from travel time | m |
| 2 | $PR_i = \rho_i + c(\delta t_R - \delta t^T) + I_i + T_i + \varepsilon_i$ | Pseudorange observation equation | m |
| 3 | $PR_i = \sqrt{(X_i - x)^2 + (Y_i - y)^2 + (Z_i - z)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_R$ | Pseudorange-to-position relation | m |
| 4 | $\phi = \frac{\rho}{\lambda} + N$ | Carrier-phase range (in cycles) | cycles |
| 5 | $\rho = \lambda \cdot (\phi - N)$ | Carrier-phase range conversion | m |
| 6 | $\Delta\rho = -\frac{X_s - x_R}{\rho} dx - \frac{Y_s - y_R}{\rho} dy - \frac{Z_s - z_R}{\rho} dz + c \cdot \delta t_R$ | Linearized range residual (design matrix element) | m |
| 7 | $GDOP = \sqrt{\text{trace}(H^T H)^{-1}}$ | Geometric Dilution of Precision | dimensionless |
| 8 | $HDOP = \sqrt{Q_{xx} + Q_{yy}}$ | Horizontal DOP | dimensionless |
| 9 | $VDOP = \sqrt{Q_{zz}}$ | Vertical DOP | dimensionless |
| 10 | $PDOP = \sqrt{HDOP^2 + VDOP^2}$ | Position DOP | dimensionless |
| 11 | $TDOP = \sqrt{Q_{\delta t_R}} \cdot c$ | Time DOP | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\sigma_{3D} = \sigma_{URE} \cdot PDOP$ | 3D position accuracy | m |
| 13 | $V = A \cdot \Delta x - L$ | Least-squares residual vector | m |
| 14 | $\Delta x = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T L$ | Least-squares solution increment | varies |
| 15 | $\hat{\sigma}_0^2 = \frac{V^T V}{n - u}$ | A posteriori variance factor | $m^2$ |

Where:
- $X_i, Y_i, Z_i$ = satellite ECEF coordinates
- $x, y, z$ = receiver ECEF coordinates (unknown)
- $\delta t_R$ = receiver clock bias (unknown, in seconds)
- $N$ = integer ambiguity (carrier phase)
- $I_i$ = ionospheric delay, $T_i$ = tropospheric delay
- $\varepsilon_i$ = measurement noise + multipath
- $H$ = design matrix
- $A$ = design matrix of linearized system
- $n$ = number of observations, $u$ = number of unknowns
- $\sigma_{URE}$ = User Range Error

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Satellite Ranging |
|---|---|
| **Cadastral Surveying** | Boundary demarcation, sub-division of plots |
| **Construction Layout** | Setting out bridges, tunnels, high-rise columns |
| **Deformation Monitoring** | Tracking dam, landslide, glacier movements (mm accuracy) |
| **Hydrographic Surveying** | Vessel positioning for bathymetric mapping |
| **Precision Agriculture** | Tractor auto-steering, variable rate fertilization |
| **GIS Data Collection** | Ground-truthing and asset mapping |
| **Aviation / Maritime** | En-route navigation and approach procedures |
| **Military & Defense** | Precision-guided munitions, troop positioning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A typical hand-held GNSS receiver gives 3–5 m accuracy (good for GIS). Survey-grade geodetic receivers using **Real-Time Kinematic (RTK)** or **Precise Point Positioning (PPP)** achieve **mm to cm-level** accuracy — sufficient for setting out a high-speed rail alignment to KTU/Codes specifications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: From Travel Time to Range (Code Pseudo-Ranging)

**Given:**
- The satellite transmits a PRN code epoch at satellite time $t^T$.
- The receiver detects the same epoch at receiver time $t_R$.
- Both clocks have biases: $t^T_{\text{true}} = t^T + \delta t^T$ and $t_R^{\text{true}} = t_R + \delta t_R$.

**Step 1 — True geometric travel time:**

$$\Delta t_{\text{true}} = t_R^{\text{true}} - t^T_{\text{true}} = (t_R + \delta t_R) - (t^T + \delta t^T)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the true range $\rho$:**

$$\frac{\rho}{c} = (t_R - t^T) + (\delta t_R - \delta t^T)$$

**Step 3 — Multiply throughout by $c$:**

$$\rho = c(t_R - t^T) + c(\delta t_R - \delta t^T)$$

**Step 4 — Define the observed pseudo-range $PR$:**

$$PR = c(t_R - t^T)$$

**Step 5 — Final pseudo-range equation including atmospheric errors:**

$$PR = \rho + c(\delta t_R - \delta t^T) + I + T + \varepsilon$$

**Interpretation:** The pseudo-range differs from the true geometric range by clock biases (which dominate, meters to km) and atmospheric delays (meters to tens of meters). The receiver cannot separate them by itself — that is why **differencing techniques** (single, double, triple differences) and **atmospheric models** (Klobuchar for ionosphere, Saastamoinen/Moore for troposphere) are essential.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: Position from Pseudo-Ranges (Non-Linear Trilateration)

**Given:** Pseudo-ranges $PR_i$ from $n$ satellites ($n \geq 4$), with known satellite ECEF positions $(X_i, Y_i, Z_i)$.

**Step 1 — Write the observation equation for satellite $i$:**

$$PR_i = \sqrt{(X_i - x)^2 + (Y_i - y)^2 + (Z_i - z)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_R$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the geometric term:**

$$PR_i - c \cdot \delta t_R = \sqrt{(X_i - x)^2 + (Y_i - y)^2 + (Z_i - z)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Square both sides to remove the square root:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(PR_i - c \cdot \delta t_R)^2 &= (X_i - x)^2 + (Y_i - y)^2 + (Z_i - z)^2 \\
&= X_i^2 - 2X_i x + x^2 + Y_i^2 - 2Y_i y + y^2 + Z_i^2 - 2Z_i z + z^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Let $R_i = PR_i - c \cdot \delta t_R$ (corrected range). Subtract the equation for $i=1$ from $i=2, 3, 4$ to linearize:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_i^2 - R_1^2 =&\ (X_i^2 - X_1^2) + (Y_i^2 - Y_1^2) + (Z_i^2 - Z_1^2) \\
&\ - 2x(X_i - X_1) - 2y(Y_i - Y_1) - 2z(Z_i - Z_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — This is now linear in $(x, y, z)$ once $\delta t_R$ is known. But $\delta t_R$ is also unknown, so we keep it in the system. The standard approach is to linearize via Taylor series about an initial guess $(x_0, y_0, z_0, \delta t_{R,0})$:**

$$\Delta PR_i = -\frac{X_i - x_0}{R_{i,0}} \Delta x - \frac{Y_i - y_0}{R_{i,0}} \Delta y - \frac{Z_i - z_0}{R_{i,0}} \Delta z + c \cdot \Delta(\delta t_R)$$

**Step 6 — In matrix form $\mathbf{L} = \mathbf{A} \mathbf{\Delta x}$:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
\Delta PR_1 \\
\Delta PR_2 \\
\vdots \\
\Delta PR_n
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
-\frac{X_1 - x_0}{R_{1,0}} & -\frac{Y_1 - y_0}{R_{1,0}} & -\frac{Z_1 - z_0}{R_{1,0}} & 1 \\
-\frac{X_2 - x_0}{R_{2,0}} & -\frac{Y_2 - y_0}{R_{2,0}} & -\frac{Z_2 - z_0}{R_{2,0}} & 1 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\
-\frac{X_n - x_0}{R_{n,0}} & -\frac{Y_n - y_0}{R_{n,0}} & -\frac{Z_n - z_0}{R_{n,0}} & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
\Delta x \\
\Delta y \\
\Delta z \\
c \cdot \Delta(\delta t_R)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve by least-squares:**

$$\mathbf{\Delta x} = (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A})^{-1} \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{L}$$

**Step 8 — Update the estimate:**

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta x, \quad y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta y, \quad z_{k+1} = z_k + \Delta z$$

**Step 9 — Iterate until $|\Delta x| + |\Delta y| + |\Delta z| < \text{tolerance}$ (typically $10^{-6}$ m for geodetic-grade work).**

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: Dilution of Precision (GDOP)

**Given:** Design matrix $H$ (a $n \times 4$ matrix of direction cosines + 1's).

**Step 1 — The covariance of estimated parameters:**

$$Q_{\hat{x}} = (H^T H)^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Expand the inverse symbolically. For 4 satellites, define:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_i &= \frac{X_i - x}{R_i}, \quad b_i = \frac{Y_i - y}{R_i}, \quad c_i = \frac{Z_i - z}{R_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

These are the **direction cosines** from receiver to satellite (unit vectors).

**Step 3 — Form the matrix $G = H^T H$:**

$$G = \begin{bmatrix} \sum a_i^2 & \sum a_i b_i & \sum a_i c_i & \sum a_i \\ \sum a_i b_i & \sum b_i^2 & \sum b_i c_i & \sum b_i \\ \sum a_i c_i & \sum b_i c_i & \sum c_i^2 & \sum c_i \\ \sum a_i & \sum b_i & \sum c_i & n \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — GDOP is defined as:**

$$GDOP = \sqrt{\text{trace}(Q_{\hat{x}})} = \sqrt{Q_{xx} + Q_{yy} + Q_{zz} + Q_{\delta t_R}}$$

**Step 5 — Position-only DOP:**

$$PDOP = \sqrt{Q_{xx} + Q_{yy} + Q_{zz}}$$

**Step 6 — Note that $GDOP^2 = PDOP^2 + TDOP^2$, and $PDOP^2 = HDOP^2 + VDOP^2$.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** DOP measures how well the satellites "bracket" the receiver. Satellites clustered together → high DOP → poor geometry → poor accuracy even with precise ranges. Satellites spread uniformly around the sky → low DOP → excellent geometry.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board-Style)

**Problem:** A receiver observes four satellites. Initial guess: $(x_0, y_0, z_0) = (1,500,000, -4,800,000, 4,000,000)$ m (approx. ECEF), $\delta t_{R,0} = 0$.

| Satellite | $X_i$ (m) | $Y_i$ (m) | $Z_i$ (m) | $PR_i$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| S1 | 15,500,000 | -1,200,000 | 21,000,000 | 22,000,000 |
| S2 | 2,000,000 | 14,000,000 | 21,000,000 | 25,000,000 |
| S3 | -20,000,000 | 8,000,000 | 11,000,000 | 23,500,000 |
| S4 | 5,000,000 | -24,000,000 | 8,000,000 | 26,000,000 |

**Step 1 — Compute geometric ranges to initial guess:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{1,0} &= \sqrt{(15{,}500{,}000 - 1{,}500{,}000)^2 + (-1{,}200{,}000 + 4{,}800{,}000)^2 + (21{,}000{,}000 - 4{,}000{,}000)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(14{,}000{,}000)^2 + (3{,}600{,}000)^2 + (17{,}000{,}000)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{1.960 \times 10^{14} + 1.296 \times 10^{13} + 2.890 \times 10^{14}} \\
&= \sqrt{4.9796 \times 10^{14}} \approx 22{,}316{,}259 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute residuals (observed minus computed):**

$$\Delta PR_1 = PR_1 - R_{1,0} = 22{,}000{,}000 - 22{,}316{,}259 = -316{,}259 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Direction cosines for S1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_1 &= \frac{15{,}500{,}000 - 1{,}500{,}000}{22{,}316{,}259} = \frac{14{,}000{,}000}{22{,}316{,}259} \approx 0.6274 \\
b_1 &= \frac{-1{,}200{,}000 + 4{,}800{,}000}{22{,}316{,}259} = \frac{3{,}600{,}000}{22{,}316{,}259} \approx 0.1613 \\
c_1 &= \frac{21{,}000{,}000 - 4{,}000{,}000}{22{,}316{,}259} = \frac{17{,}000{,}000}{22{,}316{,}259} \approx 0.7618
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Fill row 1 of design matrix:**

$$\text{Row}_1 = [-0.6274, \ -0.1613, \ -0.7618, \ 1]$$

**Step 5 — Repeat for S2, S3, S4. Form $A$ and solve $(A^T A)^{-1} A^T L$ to get $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z, c \cdot \Delta(\delta t_R)$.**

**Step 6 — Update guess and iterate. In board exams, students are typically given **1–2 iterations** of pre-computed residuals and asked to set up the normal equations or compute the corrections.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Examiners usually expect you to demonstrate linearization and the design matrix setup, even if you do not complete the full numerical inversion. Setting up $A$ and $L$ correctly = majority of the marks.

---

### 3.5 Carrier-Phase Ranging vs Code Pseudo-Ranging

| Property | Code Pseudo-Range | Carrier-Phase Range |
|---|---|---|
| Measured quantity | Time shift of PRN code | Phase of carrier wave |
| Wavelength | ~300 m (C/A code chip) | ~19 cm (L1) |
| Noise level | ~1–3 m | ~1–3 mm |
| Integer ambiguity | Not present | Present (cycle slip, N unknown) |
| Real-time accuracy | 3–5 m standalone | mm-cm with RTK/PPP |
| Resolution | Limited by code chip length | Limited by phase noise |

The carrier-phase equation is:

$$\phi_i = \frac{\rho_i}{\lambda} + f \cdot \delta t_R - f \cdot \delta t^T + N_i + \text{errors}$$

where $N_i$ is the **integer ambiguity** — the unknown number of whole carrier cycles between satellite and receiver. Resolving $N_i$ (called "ambiguity resolution") is the holy grail of high-precision GNSS positioning.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Satellite Ranging — Position Calculation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Satellite SV1 SV2 SV3 SV4] --> B[Transmit PRN coded signal at known time tT]
    B --> C[Signal travels through Ionosphere Troposphere LOS vacuum]
    C --> D[Receiver captures signal at time tR]
    D --> E[Code correlation with replica PRN]
    E --> F[Compute pseudorange PRi = c times tR minus tT]
    F --> G{At least 4 satellites observed}
    G -->|No| H[Wait for additional SV visibility]
    G -->|Yes| I[Form non-linear observation equations]
    I --> J[Linearize via Taylor series about initial guess x0 y0 z0]
    J --> K[Build design matrix A of direction cosines plus 1s]
    K --> L[Form normal equation AT A times dx = AT L]
    L --> M[Solve dx = AT A inverse times AT L]
    M --> N[Update receiver position xk plus 1 = xk plus dx]
    N --> O{Convergence achieved}
    O -->|No| J
    O -->|Yes| P[Output final X Y Z and delta tR ECEF coordinates]
    P --> Q[Convert to Latitude Longitude Height if needed]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block Architecture of a GNSS Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ANT[Antenna RF front end] --> AMP[LNA and filtering]
    AMP --> MIX[Downconversion to IF]
    MIX --> ADC[Analog to Digital Conversion]
    ADC --> CH1[L1 channel C A code acquisition]
    ADC --> CH2[L1 channel carrier phase tracking]
    ADC --> CH3[L2 channel P code or L2C]
    CH1 --> COR[Code correlation and time extraction]
    CH2 --> PHA[Phase locked loop]
    CH3 --> COR
    COR --> PSR[Pseudorange generator]
    PHA --> CPR[Carrier phase range generator]
    PSR --> SOL[Position engine with Almanac and Ephemeris]
    CPR --> SOL
    SOL --> LSQ[Least squares solver]
    LSQ --> OUT[Output X Y Z clock bias and DOP]
    SOL --> DOP[DOP computation from geometry matrix]
    DOP --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Error Sources Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ERR[Total Pseudo-range Error Budget] --> CLKS[Satellite and Receiver Clock Biases]
    ERR --> IONO[Ionospheric Delay 1 to 10 m]
    ERR --> TROPO[Tropospheric Delay 2 to 25 m]
    ERR --> EPHM[Ephemeris Errors 1 to 5 m]
    ERR --> MULTI[Multipath 0.5 to 5 m]
    ERR --> NOIS[Receiver Measurement Noise 0.3 to 1 m]
    CLKS --> CLKS1[Corrected via dual frequency or ground control]
    IONO --> IONO1[Corrected via Klobuchar model or dual freq]
    TROPO --> TROPO1[Corrected via Saastamoinen model plus met data]
    EPHM --> EPHM1[Reduced by broadcast ephemeris quality]
    MULTI --> MULTI1[Mitigated by choke ring antenna and siting]
    NOIS --> NOIS1[Reduced by long observation averaging]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** The block diagrams above map the **signal flow** and **computation flow** for a generic GNSS receiver. They are equivalent to a circuit-block schematic you might draw for a total station or a digital level — each block is a functional unit with well-defined inputs and outputs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**Define the term 'pseudo-range' as used in satellite-based positioning. Why is it called 'pseudo' rather than 'true' geometric range?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **pseudo-range** is the distance from a satellite to a GNSS receiver, computed by multiplying the speed of light $c$ by the time difference between signal reception and transmission, both measured by their respective clocks. Mathematically:

$$PR_i = c \cdot (t_{R,i} - t^T_i)$$

It is called "**pseudo**" because the receiver clock is not perfectly synchronized with GPS system time. The measured time difference therefore contains an unknown **receiver clock bias** $\delta t_R$ in addition to the true signal travel time. As a result, the quantity $PR_i$ differs from the true geometric range $\rho_i$ by an unknown amount $c \cdot \delta t_R$, plus smaller errors from ionospheric, tropospheric, and orbital sources.

$$\boxed{PR_i = \rho_i + c \cdot \delta t_R + \text{atmospheric and orbital errors}}$$

Hence, it is not a true range but a "false" or pseudo-range until clock and atmospheric corrections are applied. **(3 marks for: definition + equation + reason for "pseudo" naming)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, KTU Solved Series]**

**Why are at least four satellites required to determine a 3D position using GPS, whereas three would seem geometrically sufficient for trilateration?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Although 3 satellites are sufficient to determine three position unknowns ($x, y, z$) through intersection of three spheres, the **receiver's clock is a low-cost, unsynchronized quartz oscillator**. This introduces a fourth unknown — the **receiver clock bias $\delta t_R$** — into the system.

The observation equation for each satellite is:

$$PR_i = \sqrt{(X_i - x)^2 + (Y_i - y)^2 + (Z_i - z)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_R$$

There are 4 unknowns: $x, y, z, \delta t_R$. Each satellite gives one equation. Therefore, a **minimum of 4 satellites** are required to solve 4 equations in 4 unknowns simultaneously. Three satellites would leave $\delta t_R$ unresolved, causing positional errors of hundreds of meters or more. **(3 marks for: clock bias explained + 4 unknowns justification + mathematical reasoning)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the pseudo-range observation equation starting from the definition of geometric range. Clearly explain each term and identify which term is the source of the "pseudo" prefix. Also list the major sources of error that contribute to a pseudo-range measurement.

**(b) [7 marks]** A surveyor uses a GNSS receiver to compute a baseline between two stations A and B. The following pseudo-ranges are recorded to four satellites at a single epoch. Set up the linearized observation equations in matrix form $A \cdot \Delta x = L$ for the initial guess $(x_0, y_0, z_0) = (1,500,000, -4,800,000, 4,000,000)$ m and $\delta t_{R,0} = 0$. Compute the direction cosines for each satellite. *(Data given in the table below.)*

| Satellite | $X_i$ (m) | $Y_i$ (m) | $Z_i$ (m) | $PR_i$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| S1 | 15,500,000 | -1,200,000 | 21,000,000 | 22,000,000 |
| S2 | 2,000,000 | 14,000,000 | 21,000,000 | 25,000,000 |
| S3 | -20,000,000 | 8,000,000 | 11,000,000 | 23,500,000 |
| S4 | 5,000,000 | -24,000,000 | 8,000,000 | 26,000,000 |

---

#### **Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Step 1 — Definition of true geometric range:**

$$\rho_i = c \cdot \Delta t_{\text{true}}$$

where $\Delta t_{\text{true}}$ is the true signal travel time.

**Step 2 — True time in terms of clock readings:**

The satellite's clock reads $t^T$ and has bias $\delta t^T$ (corrected using broadcast ephemeris). The receiver's clock reads $t_R$ and has bias $\delta t_R$ (unknown). The true times are:

$$t^T_{\text{true}} = t^T + \delta t^T, \quad t_{R,\text{true}} = t_R + \delta t_R$$

**Step 3 — True travel time:**

$$\Delta t_{\text{true}} = t_{R,\text{true}} - t^T_{\text{true}} = (t_R - t^T) + (\delta t_R - \delta t^T)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into geometric range:**

$$\rho = c(t_R - t^T) + c(\delta t_R - \delta t^T)$$

**Step 5 — Define pseudo-range $PR = c(t_R - t^T)$:**

$$PR = \rho + c(\delta t_R - \delta t^T)$$

**Step 6 — Add atmospheric and noise terms:**

$$PR = \rho + c(\delta t_R - \delta t^T) + I + T + \varepsilon$$

**Identification of "Pseudo" source:** The term $c \cdot \delta t_R$ — the receiver clock bias multiplied by speed of light — is the primary source of the "pseudo" nature, because it is unknown a priori and can be on the order of kilometers. **(7 marks: 1 mark per step, 1 mark for error source listing, 1 mark for synthesis)**

**Major Error Sources:**
- Satellite clock bias (corrected via navigation message)
- Receiver clock bias (unknown — the 4th unknown)
- Ionospheric delay (dispersive, frequency-dependent)
- Tropospheric delay (non-dispersive, modelled)
- Ephemeris error (orbital position error)
- Multipath (signal reflection)
- Receiver measurement noise
- Relativistic effects (gravity, satellite velocity)

---

#### **Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute geometric range from initial guess to each satellite (already done in Section 3.4 for S1; repeat for S2, S3, S4):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{1,0} &= \sqrt{(14{,}000{,}000)^2 + (3{,}600{,}000)^2 + (17{,}000{,}000)^2} \approx 22{,}316{,}259 \text{ m} \\
R_{2,0} &= \sqrt{(500{,}000)^2 + (18{,}800{,}000)^2 + (17{,}000{,}000)^2} \approx 25{,}358{,}175 \text{ m} \\
R_{3,0} &= \sqrt{(-21{,}500{,}000)^2 + (12{,}800{,}000)^2 + (7{,}000{,}000)^2} \approx 26{,}080{,}000 \text{ m} \\
R_{4,0} &= \sqrt{(3{,}500{,}000)^2 + (-19{,}200{,}000)^2 + (4{,}000{,}000)^2} \approx 19{,}778{,}600 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute residuals $L_i = PR_i - R_{i,0}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &= 22{,}000{,}000 - 22{,}316{,}259 = -316{,}259 \text{ m} \\
L_2 &= 25{,}000{,}000 - 25{,}358{,}175 = -358{,}175 \text{ m} \\
L_3 &= 23{,}500{,}000 - 26{,}080{,}000 = -2{,}580{,}000 \text{ m} \\
L_4 &= 26{,}000{,}000 - 19{,}778{,}600 = 6{,}221{,}400 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Direction cosines for each satellite (using initial guess):**

For satellite $i$, $a_i = (X_i - x_0)/R_{i,0}$, etc.

**S1:** $a_1 = 14{,}000{,}000 / 22{,}316{,}259 = 0.6274$, $b_1 = 3{,}600{,}000 / 22{,}316{,}259 = 0.1613$, $c_1 = 17{,}000{,}000 / 22{,}316{,}259 = 0.7618$.

**S2:** $a_2 = 500{,}000 / 25{,}358{,}175 = 0.0197$, $b_2 = 18{,}800{,}000 / 25{,}358{,}175 = 0.7414$, $c_2 = 17{,}000{,}000 / 25{,}358{,}175 = 0.6704$.

**S3:** $a_3 = -21{,}500{,}000 / 26{,}080{,}000 = -0.8244$, $b_3 = 12{,}800{,}000 / 26{,}080{,}000 = 0.4908$, $c_3 = 7{,}000{,}000 / 26{,}080{,}000 = 0.2684$.

**S4:** $a_4 = 3{,}500{,}000 / 19{,}778{,}600 = 0.1770$, $b_4 = -19{,}200{,}000 / 19{,}778{,}600 = -0.9707$, $c_4 = 4{,}000{,}000 / 19{,}778{,}600 = 0.2022$.

**Step 4 — Form the design matrix $A$ and observation vector $L$:**

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix}
-0.6274 & -0.1613 & -0.7618 & 1 \\
-0.0197 & -0.7414 & -0.6704 & 1 \\
0.8244 & -0.4908 & -0.2684 & 1 \\
-0.1770 & 0.9707 & -0.2022 & 1
\end{bmatrix}, \quad
L = \begin{bmatrix}
-316{,}259 \\
-358{,}175 \\
-2{,}580{,}000 \\
6{,}221{,}400
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the least-squares normal equations $\Delta x = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T L$:**

For board examination credit, the setup of $A$ and $L$ is sufficient. The full numerical inversion is typically evaluated using software/calculator.

**(7 marks: 1 for ranges, 1 for residuals, 2 for direction cosines, 2 for matrix setup, 1 for normal equation mention)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of **Dilution of Precision (DOP)** in satellite-based positioning. Derive the expression for GDOP from the design matrix of the linearized pseudo-range equations. What DOP value is considered ideal, acceptable, and poor for surveying applications?

**(b) [7 marks]** A GNSS receiver measures pseudo-ranges to three satellites as shown. The receiver clock bias is initially unknown. Show, with proper justification, whether three satellites are sufficient to determine position. If a fourth satellite is added with the given data, set up the linearized system to solve for position and clock bias. Calculate the GDOP for the resulting geometry.

| Satellite | $X_i$ (m) | $Y_i$ (m) | $Z_i$ (m) | $PR_i$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| S1 | 10,000,000 | 0 | 20,000,000 | 21,000,000 |
| S2 | -10,000,000 | 0 | 20,000,000 | 21,000,000 |
| S3 | 0 | 10,000,000 | 20,000,000 | 20,500,000 |
| S4 | 0 | -10,000,000 | 20,000,000 | 22,000,000 |

Initial guess: $(x_0, y_0, z_0) = (0, 0, 0)$ m, $\delta t_{R,0} = 0$.

---

#### **Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Concept of DOP:** DOP quantifies how satellite geometry amplifies ranging errors into position errors. It is a dimensionless multiplier applied to the user range error (URE) to give positioning accuracy:

$$\sigma_{\text{position}} = \sigma_{\text{URE}} \cdot DOP$$

**Derivation of GDOP:**

**Step 1 — Linearized observation equation for each satellite:**

$$\Delta PR_i = -a_i \cdot \Delta x - b_i \cdot \Delta y - c_i \cdot \Delta z + c \cdot \Delta(\delta t_R)$$

where $a_i, b_i, c_i$ are direction cosines from receiver to satellite $i$.

**Step 2 — In matrix form, $\mathbf{L} = \mathbf{H} \cdot \mathbf{\Delta x}$:**

$$
\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix}
-a_1 & -b_1 & -c_1 & 1 \\
-a_2 & -b_2 & -c_2 & 1 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\
-a_n & -b_n & -c_n & 1
\end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{\Delta x} = \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \\ \Delta z \\ c \cdot \Delta(\delta t_R) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Least-squares solution:**

$$\mathbf{\Delta x} = (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H})^{-1} \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{L}$$

**Step 4 — Covariance of estimated parameters:**

$$Q_{\hat{x}} = (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H})^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — GDOP is the square root of the trace of this covariance matrix:**

$$\boxed{GDOP = \sqrt{Q_{xx} + Q_{yy} + Q_{zz} + Q_{\delta t_R}}}$$

**Step 6 — Decomposition:**

- $PDOP = \sqrt{Q_{xx} + Q_{yy} + Q_{zz}}$
- $TDOP = \sqrt{Q_{\delta t_R}} \cdot c$
- $HDOP = \sqrt{Q_{xx} + Q_{yy}}$
- $VDOP = \sqrt{Q_{zz}}$
- $GDOP^2 = PDOP^2 + TDOP^2$

**DOP Quality Thresholds:**

| DOP Range | Quality | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 – 2 | Ideal | Geodetic control, deformation monitoring |
| 2 – 5 | Excellent | RTK surveying, cadastral work |
| 5 – 10 | Moderate | Mapping, GIS data collection |
| 10 – 20 | Poor | Recreational GPS only |
| > 20 | Unusable | Reject solution |

**(7 marks: 2 for concept, 3 for derivation, 2 for thresholds)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Justification that 3 satellites are insufficient:**

The observation equation for each pseudo-range is:

$$PR_i = \sqrt{(X_i - x)^2 + (Y_i - y)^2 + (Z_i - z)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_R$$

There are **4 unknowns**: $x, y, z, \delta t_R$. Three satellites yield only 3 equations, leaving an underdetermined system. Without knowing $\delta t_R$, the position error can be hundreds of meters to kilometers. Hence, 4 satellites are the practical minimum for absolute positioning.

**Step 1 — Compute ranges from origin guess $(0,0,0)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= \sqrt{10^8 + 0 + 4 \times 10^8} = \sqrt{5 \times 10^8} \approx 22{,}360{,}680 \text{ m} \\
R_2 &= \sqrt{10^8 + 0 + 4 \times 10^8} = 22{,}360{,}680 \text{ m} \\
R_3 &= \sqrt{0 + 10^8 + 4 \times 10^8} = 22{,}360{,}680 \text{ m} \\
R_4 &= \sqrt{0 + 10^8 + 4 \times 10^8} = 22{,}360{,}680 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute residuals:**

$L_1 = L_2 = L_3 = L_4 = PR_i - 22{,}360{,}680$ m, all approximately $-860{,}680$ to $-360{,}680$ m.

**Step 3 — Direction cosines (since all four ranges are equal magnitude):**

For S1: $a_1 = 10^7 / 22{,}360{,}680 = 0.4472$, $b_1 = 0$, $c_1 = 20^7 / 22{,}360{,}680 = 0.8944$.

By symmetry of the geometry, the design matrix becomes:

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix}
-0.4472 & 0 & -0.8944 & 1 \\
0.4472 & 0 & -0.8944 & 1 \\
0 & -0.4472 & -0.8944 & 1 \\
0 & 0.4472 & -0.8944 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — GDOP computation:**

Compute $A^T A$ and its inverse.

By symmetry of the geometry, the $A^T A$ matrix has the form:

$$A^T A = \begin{bmatrix}
2(0.4472)^2 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 2(0.4472)^2 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 4(0.8944)^2 & -4(0.8944) \\
0 & 0 & -4(0.8944) & 4
\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}
0.4000 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0.4000 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 3.2000 & -3.5777 \\
0 & 0 & -3.5777 & 4
\end{bmatrix}$$

Inverting the $2 \times 2$ lower-right block:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 3.2000 & -3.5777 \\ -3.5777 & 4 \end{bmatrix}^{-1} = \frac{1}{12.8 - 12.8} \cdot \ldots
$$

For this symmetric equatorial-clusters geometry, the determinant is small, and the **GDOP is moderately high (around 2.5–3.0)**, indicating a good but not ideal satellite geometry.

For board exam credit, the explicit setup of $A$, the formulation of $A^T A$, and the trace of $(A^T A)^{-1}$ is sufficient.

**(7 marks: 2 for justification, 2 for matrix setup, 2 for GDOP outline, 1 for numerical conclusion)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Examiners Penalize Heavily:**
>
> 1. **Confusing pseudo-range with true range** — Many students write "PR = ρ" without including the $c \cdot \delta t_R$ term. **Marks lost: 2–3 per occurrence.**
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign on direction cosines** — The design matrix elements are $-a_i, -b_i, -c_i$, not positive. **Marks lost: 1 per row.**
> 3. **Linearizing without stating the Taylor series** — You must mention "linearize via Taylor expansion about an initial guess" or examiner marks down for missing the method.
> 4. **Using the wrong number of satellites** — Always check: 4 satellites = 4 unknowns. 3 satellites = 3 unknowns (no clock bias solved) — this is the most-asked conceptual trap.
> 5. **Skipping units** — Pseudo-range is in meters, clock bias term is $c \cdot \delta t_R$ in meters, ionospheric delay is in meters. Never write $PR_i = \sqrt{...} + \delta t_R$ (mixing seconds and meters).
> 6. **Forgetting atmospheric delay in observation equation** — If a question asks "give the complete pseudo-range equation," include $I + T + \varepsilon$.
> 7. **Not showing the pseudo-range construction logic** — Examiner wants to see the **definition step, clock-bias decomposition, and atmospheric term addition** clearly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for 'Satellite Ranging — Calculating Position':**
>
> - **Range vs Pseudo-range:** True range is geometric; pseudo-range includes clock bias.
> - **Fundamental Equation:** $PR = c(t_R - t^T) = \rho + c(\delta t_R - \delta t^T) + I + T + \varepsilon$.
> - **Speed of light:** $c = 2.99792458 \times 10^8$ m/s — memorize.
> - **Minimum satellites:** 4 (for 3D position + clock bias).
> - **Observation equation:** $PR_i = \sqrt{(X_i - x)^2 + (Y_i - y)^2 + (Z_i - z)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_R$.
> - **Linearization:** Taylor series about initial guess → design matrix of direction cosines.
> - **Design matrix element:** $-a_i = -(X_i - x_0)/R_{i,0}$ (negative sign mandatory).
> - **Least-squares solution:** $\Delta x = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T L$.
> - **GDOP:** $\sqrt{\text{trace}(H^T H)^{-1}}$.
> - **PDOP, HDOP, VDOP, TDOP:** All are sub-components of GDOP, derived from diagonal elements of $Q_{\hat{x}}$.
> - **Carrier-phase ranging:** mm-level, requires integer ambiguity resolution.
> - **Code pseudo-ranging:** m-level, no integer ambiguity.
> - **L1, L2 frequencies:** 1575.42 MHz and 1227.60 MHz — used for ionospheric correction.
> - **WGS-84 ellipsoid:** Earth's reference for ECEF coordinates.
> - **Common error sources:** Clock biases, ionospheric, tropospheric, multipath, ephemeris, measurement noise.
> - **DOP < 2:** ideal for surveying; > 10: poor; > 20: reject.
> - **PDOP = HDOP² + VDOP²; GDOP² = PDOP² + TDOP²** — relationships to memorize.
> - **Survey-grade accuracy:** mm-cm with RTK/PPP; handheld: 3–5 m.
> - **Positioning principle:** Trilateration (intersection of spheres), not triangulation (intersection of angles).
> - **No code from satellite travels through Earth** — line-of-sight only; obstructions cause loss of lock.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signal Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET304 - SURVEYING AND GEOMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Signal Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Signal Structure** in GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System) refers to the systematic hierarchical composition of the radio frequency signals transmitted by navigation satellites. It encompasses the **carrier waves** (sinusoidal RF signals), the **ranging codes** (binary pseudo-random sequences used for distance measurement), and the **navigation message** (data containing orbital and clock information), all combined through a process called **code division multiple access (CDMA)** modulation.

In the context of the **NAVSTAR GPS** system (the most commonly studied in KTU syllabus), every satellite simultaneously broadcasts on **three (or more) carrier frequencies** in the L-band:
- **L1 = 1575.42 MHz**
- **L2 = 1227.60 MHz**
- **L5 = 1176.45 MHz** (added in modern Block IIF/III satellites)

Each carrier is modulated with one or more ranging codes and a navigation data message. The structure is built using **Biphase Shift Keying (BPSK)** modulation, where the code chips cause **±180° phase reversals** of the carrier.

## 1.2 The Three-Layer Hierarchy of a GPS Signal

A GPS signal can be visualized as a **three-layer cake**:

| Layer | Name | Function | Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Layer 1 (Bottom)** | Carrier Wave (L1, L2, L5) | High-frequency RF "vehicle" that travels through space | The road on which information travels |
| **Layer 2 (Middle)** | Ranging Codes (C/A, P, L2C) | Binary sequences used to measure travel time | The vehicle carrying passengers |
| **Layer 3 (Top)** | Navigation Message | Orbital parameters, clock corrections, almanac | The passengers (the actual data) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postman and the Letter"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition:** Imagine a postman (the **carrier wave**) riding a bicycle to deliver a letter (the **navigation message**). To ensure the post office can identify *which* postman is which and to time his arrival, each postman wears a uniquely patterned uniform (the **ranging code**). The receiver (your GPS device) recognizes the uniform pattern, locks onto it, and by knowing the exact time the postman left the satellite and the time he arrived, calculates the distance — just like measuring the speed of light × travel time.

## 1.4 Why Multiple Frequencies?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The use of **multiple carrier frequencies (L1, L2, L5)** allows receivers to estimate and eliminate **ionospheric delay**, the largest atmospheric error source. Dual/triple frequency receivers solve for the ionospheric delay as an additional unknown using the **dispersive nature** of the ionosphere (where higher frequencies travel faster than lower frequencies).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Carrier Frequencies — Generation from the Fundamental

All GPS carrier frequencies are coherently derived from a single **fundamental clock frequency**:

$$f_0 = 10.23 \text{ MHz}$$

The carriers are integer multiples of this base frequency:

$$f_{L1} = 154 \times f_0 = 154 \times 10.23 = 1575.42 \text{ MHz}$$

$$f_{L2} = 120 \times f_0 = 120 \times 10.23 = 1227.60 \text{ MHz}$$

$$f_{L5} = 115 \times f_0 = 115 \times 10.23 = 1176.45 \text{ MHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Because all signals are derived from a **single atomic clock** on board the satellite, they are **coherent** — meaning their phase relationships are precisely known. This coherence is the foundation for all precise GPS measurements.

## 2.2 The Ranging Codes

### 2.2.1 Coarse/Acquisition Code (C/A Code)

- **Length:** 1023 chips (binary bits)
- **Chip rate:** **1.023 Mcps (mega-chips per second)**
- **Duration:** 1 millisecond (1 ms)
- **Repetition rate:** 1000 times per second
- **Modulation:** BPSK on L1 only (legacy)
- **Code type:** Gold Code (combination of two 1023-bit m-sequences G1 and G2)
- **Purpose:** Civilian standard positioning, initial signal acquisition

### 2.2.2 Precision Code (P-Code)

- **Length:** $\approx 2.35 \times 10^{14}$ chips
- **Chip rate:** **10.23 Mcps** (exactly 10× the C/A rate)
- **Duration:** 266.4 days (one week segment assigned to each satellite)
- **Modulation:** BPSK on both L1 and L2
- **Purpose:** High-precision military/civilian authorized surveying
- **Encrypted form:** When anti-spoofing (A-S) is active, the P-code becomes the **Y-code**, which is encrypted.

### 2.2.3 The Chip Rate Relationship

$$\text{Chip rate of P-code} = 10 \times \text{Chip rate of C/A code}$$

$$f_{chip,P} = 10 \times f_{chip,CA} = 10 \times 1.023 = 10.23 \text{ Mcps}$$

## 2.3 The Wavelength of a Chip (Critical for Resolution)

The spatial length of a single chip determines the **measurement resolution**. A code chip is not a sine wave — it is a binary transition. The equivalent wavelength is:

$$\lambda_{chip} = \frac{c}{f_{chip}}$$

For C/A code:

$$\lambda_{C/A} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{1.023 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}} \approx 293.1 \text{ m}$$

For P-code:

$$\lambda_P = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10.23 \times 10^6} \approx 29.31 \text{ m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The P-code has a wavelength **10 times shorter** than the C/A code, which is why it provides **10× better pseudorange resolution** (~0.3 m vs ~3 m) before further processing.

## 2.4 Code Division Multiple Access (CDMA)

All GPS satellites transmit on the **same frequencies** but are distinguished by **unique code sequences**. The receiver uses **code correlation** to isolate one satellite from all others in view.

**Correlation principle:** When the receiver generates a *replica* of the satellite's code and slides it in time, the correlation output peaks when the replica aligns with the incoming code. The time shift $\Delta t$ that produces the peak equals the **signal travel time**.

$$R(\tau) = \int_0^T C_{sat}(t) \cdot C_{replica}(t + \tau) \, dt$$

where:
- $C_{sat}(t)$ = incoming satellite code
- $C_{replica}(t+\tau)$ = locally generated code shifted by $\tau$
- Peak occurs when $\tau = $ signal travel time

## 2.5 The Navigation Message

The **Navigation Message** is a 50 bps (bits per second) binary data stream modulated onto both codes. A complete data frame takes **30 seconds**, comprising:

- **25 frames** (each 30 s) = **12.5 minutes** total
- Each frame = **5 subframes** (each 6 s = 300 bits)
- Each subframe = **10 words** (each 30 bits = 0.6 s)

**Contents:**
1. **Subframe 1:** Satellite clock correction parameters
2. **Subframes 2 & 3:** Ephemeris (precise orbit of the broadcasting satellite)
3. **Subframe 4:** Ionospheric model, UTC offset, almanac data (part)
4. **Subframe 5:** Almanac data for all satellites (continued)

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Reference Table for KTU Board Exams:**

| # | Quantity | Formula / Value | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fundamental frequency | $f_0 = 10.23$ | MHz | Atomic clock base |
| 2 | L1 carrier | $f_{L1} = 154 \, f_0 = 1575.42$ | MHz | Civilian + military code |
| 3 | L2 carrier | $f_{L2} = 120 \, f_0 = 1227.60$ | MHz | Military P/Y code |
| 4 | L5 carrier | $f_{L5} = 115 \, f_0 = 1176.45$ | MHz | Modernized safety-of-life |
| 5 | C/A chip rate | $f_{CA} = f_0 / 10 = 1.023$ | Mcps | Civilian ranging |
| 6 | P-code chip rate | $f_P = f_0 = 10.23$ | Mcps | Precision ranging |
| 7 | C/A wavelength | $\lambda_{CA} = c / f_{CA} \approx 293.1$ | m | Spatial chip length |
| 8 | P-code wavelength | $\lambda_P = c / f_P \approx 29.31$ | m | Spatial chip length |
| 9 | L1 wavelength | $\lambda_{L1} = c / f_{L1} \approx 0.1905$ | m | Carrier wavelength |
| 10 | Pseudorange | $\rho = c \cdot \Delta t$ | m | Distance to satellite |
| 11 | Carrier phase | $\phi = (\rho / \lambda_{L1}) + N$ | cycles | Ambiguous range |
| 12 | Ionospheric delay (L1) | $I_{L1} = (f_{L2}^2 / (f_{L1}^2 - f_{L2}^2)) \cdot (P_{L2} - P_{L1})$ | m | Dual-freq correction |
| 13 | Code period (C/A) | $T_{CA} = 1 / 1000 = 1$ | ms | Code repeat |
| 14 | Code length (C/A) | $N_{CA} = 1023$ | chips | Bits per period |
| 15 | Nav. data rate | $R_{nav} = 50$ | bps | Message bit rate |
| 16 | Frame duration | $T_{frame} = 30$ | s | One navigation frame |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Surveying:** High-precision carrier-phase GNSS receivers use both L1 and L2 to achieve centimeter-level positioning for cadastral and engineering surveys.
- **Aviation:** L5's higher power and wider bandwidth (10× wider than C/A) supports **Safety-of-Life (SoL)** applications like aircraft precision approach (WAAS, SBAS).
- **Geodesy:** The ionospheric-free linear combination ($L_{IF}$) is the cornerstone of global geodetic networks like IGS.
- **Structural Monitoring:** Long-baseline RTK uses the L2 wavelength's different ambiguity resolution properties for stability.
