# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The GPS Signal Mathematical Model

The complete GPS signal at L1, as transmitted by satellite $s$, is given by the equation:

$$S_{L1}^{(s)}(t) = \sqrt{2 P_{C/A}} \cdot C^{(s)}(t) \cdot D^{(s)}(t) \cdot \sin(2\pi f_{L1} t) + \sqrt{2 P_{P}} \cdot P^{(s)}(t) \cdot D^{(s)}(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_{L1} t)$$

**Interpretation of each term (row by row):**

- $\sqrt{2 P_{C/A}}$ → square root of **twice the C/A signal power**, sets the amplitude on the **in-phase (sine)** component.
- $C^{(s)}(t)$ → the **C/A code** of satellite $s$, takes values $\pm 1$, with chip rate 1.023 Mcps.
- $D^{(s)}(t)$ → the **50 bps navigation data** message, takes values $\pm 1$, modulates both codes.
- $\sin(2\pi f_{L1} t)$ → the **L1 carrier** in the in-phase branch.
- $\sqrt{2 P_{P}} \cdot P^{(s)}(t) \cdot D^{(s)}(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_{L1} t)$ → the P-code on the **quadrature (cosine)** branch, orthogonal to C/A.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Point:** The C/A code is placed on the **in-phase** component and the P-code on the **quadrature** component. This **quadrature modulation** allows both codes to share the L1 carrier without mutual interference.

## 3.2 Derivation: Pseudorange from Code Travel Time

The fundamental distance measurement in GPS is based on the time delay between transmission and reception.

**Step 1 — Define the signal travel time:**

$$\Delta t = t_{received} - t_{transmitted}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by the speed of light** to obtain the **geometric range**, but because the satellite and receiver clocks are not perfectly synchronized, this is called the **pseudorange**:

$$\rho = c \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 3 — Express the pseudorange equation with all error sources:**

$$\rho = r + c(\delta t_{rx} - \delta t_{sv}) + I_{\rho} + T_{\rho} + \varepsilon_{\rho}$$

where:
- $r$ = **true geometric range** between satellite antenna phase center and receiver antenna phase center
- $c \cdot \delta t_{rx}$ = receiver clock error (in metres)
- $c \cdot \delta t_{sv}$ = satellite clock error (in metres)
- $I_\rho$ = **ionospheric delay** (frequency dependent)
- $T_\rho$ = **tropospheric delay** (frequency independent)
- $\varepsilon_\rho$ = measurement noise + multipath

## 3.3 Derivation: Wavelength of an L1 Carrier Cycle

The L1 carrier wavelength is critical for carrier-phase measurements:

$$\lambda_{L1} = \frac{c}{f_{L1}}$$

**Substitute the values:**

$$\lambda_{L1} = \frac{2.998 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{1575.42 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}}$$

$$\lambda_{L1} = 0.19050 \text{ m} \approx 19.05 \text{ cm}$$

Similarly:

$$\lambda_{L2} = \frac{c}{f_{L2}} = \frac{2.998 \times 10^8}{1227.60 \times 10^6} = 0.24421 \text{ m} \approx 24.42 \text{ cm}$$

$$\lambda_{L5} = \frac{c}{f_{L5}} = \frac{2.998 \times 10^8}{1176.45 \times 10^6} = 0.25485 \text{ m} \approx 25.49 \text{ cm}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Ionospheric-Free Linear Combination

The ionosphere is a **dispersive medium** — its refractive index depends on frequency. The ionospheric delay on a frequency $f$ is:

$$I_f = \frac{40.3}{f^2} \cdot \text{TEC}$$

where **TEC** = Total Electron Content along the signal path (electrons/m²).

**Step 1 — Write pseudorange on L1:**

$$P_{L1} = \rho + \frac{40.3 \cdot \text{TEC}}{f_{L1}^2} + \text{other errors}$$

**Step 2 — Write pseudorange on L2:**

$$P_{L2} = \rho + \frac{40.3 \cdot \text{TEC}}{f_{L2}^2} + \text{other errors}$$

**Step 3 — Form the linear combination** with coefficients $\alpha$ and $\beta$ such that the ionospheric term cancels:

$$\alpha \cdot P_{L1} + \beta \cdot P_{L2} = (\alpha + \beta)\rho + 40.3 \cdot \text{TEC} \left(\frac{\alpha}{f_{L1}^2} + \frac{\beta}{f_{L2}^2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the constraint** for ionospheric-free combination:

$$\frac{\alpha}{f_{L1}^2} + \frac{\beta}{f_{L2}^2} = 0$$

with the **normalization** $\alpha + \beta = 1$. Solving:

$$\alpha = \frac{f_{L1}^2}{f_{L1}^2 - f_{L2}^2}, \quad \beta = \frac{-f_{L2}^2}{f_{L1}^2 - f_{L2}^2}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$f_{L1}^2 = (1575.42)^2 = 2481820.2564 \text{ MHz}^2$$

$$f_{L2}^2 = (1227.60)^2 = 1506881.76 \text{ MHz}^2$$

$$f_{L1}^2 - f_{L2}^2 = 974938.4964 \text{ MHz}^2$$

$$\alpha = \frac{2481820.2564}{974938.4964} = 2.5457$$

$$\beta = \frac{-1506881.76}{974938.4964} = -1.5457$$

**Step 6 — Final ionosphere-free pseudorange:**

$$P_{IF} = 2.5457 \cdot P_{L1} - 1.5457 \cdot P_{L2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Insight:** The coefficients **2.5457 and −1.5457** are the most frequently tested numerical values in ionospheric correction problems. Memorize them.

## 3.5 Derivation: Code Correlation and Travel Time

A receiver generates a local replica $C_{replica}(t + \tau)$ and correlates it with the incoming code $C^{(s)}(t)$:

$$R(\tau) = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T C^{(s)}(t) \cdot C_{replica}(t + \tau) \, dt$$

For a Gold code, the autocorrelation function has a sharp peak:

$$R(\tau) = \begin{cases} 1 - \frac{\vert \tau \vert}{T_c}, & \vert \tau \vert \le T_c \\ -\frac{1}{N}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where:
- $T_c$ = chip duration = $1 / f_{chip}$
- $N$ = code length (1023 for C/A)

**Step 1 — For C/A code:**

$$T_c = \frac{1}{1.023 \times 10^6} = 9.775 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 977.5 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-correlation level (off-peak) for C/A:**

$$R_{off} = -\frac{1}{1023} \approx -0.001 = -60 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — The peak width** (correlator resolution) is approximately $\pm 1$ chip, which corresponds to $\pm 293$ m spatial uncertainty in the time delay estimate.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A GPS receiver records the following code-phase travel times for satellite PRN 07:
> - C/A code travel time: $\Delta t_{CA} = 0.078412$ s
> - P-code travel time: $\Delta t_P = 0.078411987$ s
> 
> Calculate the pseudorange from each code and the difference.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — C/A pseudorange:**

$$\rho_{CA} = c \cdot \Delta t_{CA} = (2.998 \times 10^8) \times 0.078412 = 2.3507 \times 10^7 \text{ m} = 23{,}507 \text{ km}$$

**Step 2 — P-code pseudorange:**

$$\rho_P = c \cdot \Delta t_P = (2.998 \times 10^8) \times 0.078411987 = 2.3506 \times 10^7 \text{ m} = 23{,}506.99 \text{ km}$$

**Step 3 — Difference:**

$$\Delta \rho = \rho_{CA} - \rho_P = 23{,}507{,}000 - 23{,}506{,}990 = 10 \text{ m}$$

This 10 m difference is exactly **one C/A code chip length** in space, demonstrating the **10× resolution advantage of the P-code**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: GPS Signal Generation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Atomic Clock 10.23 MHz] --> B[Frequency Multiplier]
    B --> C1[L1 Carrier 1575.42 MHz]
    B --> C2[L2 Carrier 1227.60 MHz]
    B --> C3[L5 Carrier 1176.45 MHz]
    B --> D1[P-Code Generator 10.23 Mcps]
    B --> D2[CA-Code Generator 1.023 Mcps]
    E[Navigation Data 50 bps] --> F1[XOR Modulator 1]
    E --> F2[XOR Modulator 2]
    D2 --> F1
    D1 --> F2
    F1 --> G1[BPSK Modulator In-phase]
    F2 --> G2[BPSK Modulator Quadrature]
    C1 --> G1
    C1 --> G2
    G1 --> H[Combining Amplifier]
    G2 --> H
    C2 --> I[BPSK Modulator L2]
    D1 --> I
    H --> J[Power Amplifier and Antenna]
    I --> J
    C3 --> K[Modernized L5 BPSK]
    J --> L[Free Space to Receiver]
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: GPS Signal Hierarchical Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[GPS Satellite Transmission] --> L1[Layer 1 Carrier Frequencies]
    S --> L2[Layer 2 Ranging Codes]
    S --> L3[Layer 3 Navigation Data]
    L1 --> L1A[L1 at 1575.42 MHz]
    L1 --> L1B[L2 at 1227.60 MHz]
    L1 --> L1C[L5 at 1176.45 MHz]
    L2 --> L2A[CA Code 1.023 Mcps civilian]
    L2 --> L2B[P Code 10.23 Mcps military]
    L2 --> L2C[Y Code encrypted version]
    L3 --> L3A[Ephemeris 6 second subframe]
    L3 --> L3B[Almanac coarse orbit data]
    L3 --> L3C[Clock correction parameters]
    L3 --> L3D[Ionospheric model coefficients]
    L1A --> M1[Modulated as BPSK on sine branch]
    L1A --> M2[Modulated as BPSK on cosine branch]
    M1 --> M3[Transmitted simultaneously using CDMA]
    M2 --> M3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Receiver Code Correlation Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incoming GPS Signal from Space] --> B[Antenna and Front-End Amplifier]
    B --> C[Down-Conversion to IF]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital Conversion]
    D --> E[CA Code Replica Generator 1.023 Mcps]
    D --> F[Carrier NCO Numerically Controlled Oscillator]
    E --> G[Code Correlator]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Correlation Peak Detected}
    H -->|Yes| I[Record Time Shift tau]
    H -->|No| J[Shift Code by Delta t and Retry]
    J --> G
    I --> K[Compute Pseudorange rho equals c times tau]
    K --> L[Output to Position Calculation]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Signal Modulation Process (BPSK)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Carrier Wave 1575.42 MHz] --> B{BPSK Modulator}
    C[CA Code 1.023 Mcps] --> B
    D[Navigation Data 50 bps] --> E[XOR Multiplier]
    C --> E
    E --> B
    B --> F[Modulated L1 Signal Output]
    F --> G[Phase 0 degrees when chip equals plus 1]
    F --> H[Phase 180 degrees when chip equals minus 1]
    G --> I[Received at Earth Antenna]
    H --> I
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the term "Signal Structure" as applied to GPS satellites. List the three main components of a GPS signal.*

**Model Answer:**

Signal Structure refers to the systematic arrangement of the radio signal broadcast by a GPS satellite, comprising three hierarchically combined components:

1. **Carrier Waves:** L1 (1575.42 MHz), L2 (1227.60 MHz), and L5 (1176.45 MHz) — high-frequency sinusoids generated from a 10.23 MHz atomic clock.
2. **Ranging Codes:** Binary pseudo-random noise (PRN) sequences — C/A code (1.023 Mcps, civilian) and P-code (10.23 Mcps, military) — used to measure signal travel time.
3. **Navigation Message:** 50 bps data stream containing ephemeris, almanac, and clock corrections.

The three are combined using **BPSK modulation**, allowing simultaneous transmission of multiple codes and data on the same RF carrier.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is the chip rate of the GPS C/A code and P-code? Why does the P-code provide better pseudorange accuracy than the C/A code?*

**Model Answer:**

- **C/A code chip rate:** 1.023 Mcps
- **P-code chip rate:** 10.23 Mcps (10× faster than C/A)

**Reason for better P-code accuracy:** The P-code has a chip rate 10 times higher than the C/A code, which means its equivalent spatial wavelength is 10 times shorter:

$$\lambda_{C/A} = \frac{c}{1.023 \times 10^6} \approx 293.1 \text{ m}, \quad \lambda_P = \frac{c}{10.23 \times 10^6} \approx 29.31 \text{ m}$$

Since measurement resolution scales with chip length, the P-code offers 10× finer resolution (~0.3 m vs ~3 m per chip), enabling more precise pseudorange measurements.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Writing "L1 = 1.5 GHz" instead of the **exact** value **1575.42 MHz**. Always use the precise KTU-prescribed value or lose 1 mark.
> - **Mistake 2:** Confusing chip rate (Mcps) with data rate (bps). The navigation message is **50 bps**, NOT 50 chips/second.
> - **Mistake 3:** Forgetting that C/A code is on L1 **in-phase (sine)** and P-code is on L1 **quadrature (cosine)** in the modulation equation. Marks deducted for incomplete signal equation.
> - **Mistake 4:** Not writing **units** in derivation steps. Always include units in the final formula sheet answers.
> - **Mistake 5:** Stating that the C/A code is on L2. **The C/A code is on L1 only (legacy).** Modernized L2C and L5 signals are separate civilian codes.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Alternative 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **GPS signal generation process** at the satellite. Describe how the carrier, code, and navigation data are combined. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **ionospheric-free linear combination** of L1 and L2 pseudoranges, and compute its numerical coefficients. Why is this combination used in precise GNSS surveying? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The GPS satellite generates its signal from a **single cesium/rubidium atomic clock** operating at the fundamental frequency:

$$f_0 = 10.23 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 1 — Carrier generation:** Frequency synthesizers multiply $f_0$ to produce:

- L1 = $154 \times f_0 = 1575.42$ MHz
- L2 = $120 \times f_0 = 1227.60$ MHz

**Step 2 — Code generation:** Code generators produce:

- C/A code at 1.023 Mcps (1.023 MHz) — a Gold code of 1023 chips
- P-code at 10.23 Mcps — a long PRN sequence

**Step 3 — Navigation message:** A 50 bps data stream containing ephemeris, almanac, and clock corrections is **XOR-multiplied** with each code (this is equivalent to BPSK modulation of the code by the data).

**Step 4 — BPSK modulation:** The data-modulated codes are mixed with the carriers:

- C/A × Data is modulated on L1 sine branch (in-phase)
- P × Data is modulated on L1 cosine branch (quadrature)
- P × Data is modulated on L2

**Step 5 — Combining and transmission:** The modulated components are summed in a combining amplifier, amplified by a high-power TWTA, and radiated by the satellite's helical antenna array toward Earth.

The mathematical signal at L1 is:

$$S_{L1}(t) = A_{CA} C(t) D(t) \sin(2\pi f_{L1} t) + A_P P(t) D(t) \cos(2\pi f_{L1} t)$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The ionosphere is dispersive, with group delay inversely proportional to $f^2$:

$$I_f = \frac{40.3 \cdot \text{TEC}}{f^2}$$

The pseudoranges on L1 and L2 are:

$$P_{L1} = \rho + \frac{40.3 \cdot \text{TEC}}{f_{L1}^2}, \quad P_{L2} = \rho + \frac{40.3 \cdot \text{TEC}}{f_{L2}^2}$$

Construct the linear combination $P_{IF} = \alpha P_{L1} + \beta P_{L2}$.

**Condition 1 — Normalize coefficients:** $\alpha + \beta = 1$

**Condition 2 — Eliminate ionospheric term:**

$$\frac{\alpha}{f_{L1}^2} + \frac{\beta}{f_{L2}^2} = 0$$

From these:

$$\alpha = \frac{f_{L1}^2}{f_{L1}^2 - f_{L2}^2} = \frac{(1575.42)^2}{(1575.42)^2 - (1227.60)^2} = \frac{2481820.25}{974938.50} = 2.5457$$

$$\beta = \frac{-f_{L2}^2}{f_{L1}^2 - f_{L2}^2} = \frac{-(1227.60)^2}{974938.50} = -1.5457$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{P_{IF} = 2.5457 \cdot P_{L1} - 1.5457 \cdot P_{L2}}$$

**Why it is used in precise GNSS surveying:**
1. It removes **first-order ionospheric delay**, which is the largest systematic error source (1–50 m during day, larger during solar maximum).
2. The remaining higher-order ionospheric terms are <1 cm and negligible for most surveying applications.
3. It is the standard observable processed by **Precise Point Positioning (PPP)** services and the **International GNSS Service (IGS)**.
4. Survey-grade receivers (geodetic, RTK rovers) require ionospheric-free combinations for cm-level accuracy over long baselines.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative 2

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **C/A code** and **P-code** in GPS. Compare them in terms of chip rate, code length, modulation, and accuracy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A receiver measures a **C/A code travel time** of 0.082 345 s and a **P-code travel time** of 0.082 344 901 s from a GPS satellite. Calculate the **pseudoranges** from both codes and comment on the difference. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**C/A Code (Coarse/Acquisition Code):**

- **Chip rate:** 1.023 Mcps
- **Code length:** 1023 chips
- **Duration:** 1 ms (repeats 1000 times per second)
- **Modulation:** BPSK on L1 in-phase
- **Type:** Gold code (formed by XOR of two 1023-bit m-sequences G1 and G2)
- **Purpose:** Civilian positioning, coarse acquisition
- **Wavelength:** $\lambda_{C/A} = c / 1.023 \times 10^6 = 293.1$ m
- **Pseudorange accuracy:** ~3 m (one chip)

**P-Code (Precision Code):**

- **Chip rate:** 10.23 Mcps (exactly 10× the C/A rate)
- **Code length:** $\approx 2.35 \times 10^{14}$ chips
- **Duration:** 1 week (7 days) — one week segment is assigned to each satellite
- **Modulation:** BPSK on both L1 and L2
- **Type:** PRN sequence
- **Purpose:** High-precision military and authorized civilian surveying
- **Wavelength:** $\lambda_P = c / 10.23 \times 10^6 = 29.31$ m
- **Pseudorange accuracy:** ~0.3 m (one chip)
- **Encrypted form:** Y-code when anti-spoofing is activated

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | C/A Code | P-Code |
|---|---|---|
| Chip rate | 1.023 Mcps | 10.23 Mcps |
| Code length | 1023 chips | $2.35 \times 10^{14}$ chips |
| Period | 1 ms | 1 week (266.4 days) |
| Carrier | L1 only | L1 and L2 |
| Branch on L1 | In-phase (sine) | Quadrature (cosine) |
| Chip wavelength | 293.1 m | 29.31 m |
| Resolution | ~3 m | ~0.3 m |
| Access | Civilian | Military/Authorized |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- C/A code travel time: $\Delta t_{CA} = 0.082\,345$ s
- P-code travel time: $\Delta t_P = 0.082\,344\,901$ s
- Speed of light: $c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s

**Step 1 — Pseudorange from C/A code:**

$$\rho_{CA} = c \cdot \Delta t_{CA} = (2.998 \times 10^8) \times 0.082345$$

$$\rho_{CA} = 2.4687 \times 10^7 \text{ m} = 24\,687 \text{ km}$$

**Step 2 — Pseudorange from P-code:**

$$\rho_P = c \cdot \Delta t_P = (2.998 \times 10^8) \times 0.082344901$$

$$\rho_P = 2.4687 \times 10^7 \text{ m} = 24\,686.999 \text{ km} = 24\,686\,999 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Difference:**

$$\Delta \rho = \rho_{CA} - \rho_P = 24\,687\,000 - 24\,686\,999 = 1 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Comment:**
The 1 m difference corresponds to the small timing precision achievable only with the P-code. Since the receiver hardware cannot measure code phase with sub-chip accuracy, the P-code's 10× shorter chip wavelength provides 10× better resolution. The 1 m difference lies well within one C/A chip (293 m) but exceeds one P-chip (29.3 m), demonstrating the superior precision of the P-code.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Signal Structure (Module 3):**

- ⭐ **Fundamental frequency:** $f_0 = 10.23$ MHz (atomic clock base on every GPS satellite).
- ⭐ **Carrier frequencies (memorize exact values):**
  - L1 = **1575.42 MHz** = $154 \times f_0$
  - L2 = **1227.60 MHz** = $120 \times f_0$
  - L5 = **1176.45 MHz** = $115 \times f_0$
- ⭐ **C/A code:** 1.023 Mcps, 1023 chips, 1 ms period, Gold code, **L1 only**, in-phase.
- ⭐ **P-code:** 10.23 Mcps, $\sim 2.35 \times 10^{14}$ chips, **L1 + L2**, L1 in quadrature.
- ⭐ **Chip wavelengths:** C/A ≈ 293.1 m, P ≈ 29.31 m, L1 ≈ 19.05 cm, L2 ≈ 24.42 cm.
- ⭐ **Quadrature modulation:** C/A on L1 sine branch, P on L1 cosine branch — **orthogonal, no interference**.
- ⭐ **Navigation data rate:** 50 bps, frame = 30 s, full message = 12.5 minutes (25 frames × 30 s).
- ⭐ **Pseudorange formula:** $\rho = c \cdot \Delta t = r + c(\delta t_{rx} - \delta t_{sv}) + I_\rho + T_\rho + \varepsilon_\rho$.
- ⭐ **Ionospheric-free linear combination:** $P_{IF} = 2.5457 \cdot P_{L1} - 1.5457 \cdot P_{L2}$ (memorize coefficients).
- ⭐ **Code correlation principle:** Receiver generates a replica, slides it, peaks when aligned, time shift = travel time.
- ⭐ **CDMA:** All satellites share the same frequencies, separated by unique PRN codes (Gold codes for C/A).
- ⭐ **L1 wavelength ≈ 19 cm** — this is the basis of **carrier-phase** measurements (RTK, PPK).
- ⭐ **P-code Y-form:** When A-S (anti-spoofing) is on, P-code is encrypted to Y-code, accessible only to authorized users with the W-cryptokey.
- ⭐ **Modernization:** L2C, L5, and L1C are new civil signals on L2 and L5; L1C is on L1 — these are part of the GPS III modernization program.
- ⭐ **Engineering takeaway:** A clear understanding of signal structure is **prerequisite** to understanding error sources, differential GNSS, RTK, and PPP in surveying practice.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of GPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET304 - SURVEYING AND GEOMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Application of GPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Positioning System (GPS) Applications in Surveying** refer to the systematic use of satellite-based positioning technology, employing trilateration principles using signals from a constellation of at least 24 NAVSTAR satellites orbiting the Earth at an altitude of approximately **20,200 km**, to determine precise three-dimensional coordinates (X, Y, Z or Latitude, Longitude, Height) of points on, above, or below the Earth's surface for engineering, scientific, and cadastral purposes.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, GPS applications in surveying are classified under **satellite-based geodetic positioning** and span across all major branches of civil engineering practice — from primary geodetic control networks to micro-scale deformation monitoring of dams and high-rise structures.

The accuracy achievable in GPS surveying is stratified by technique:
- **Standard Positioning Service (SPS):** ~5–10 m (single receiver, C/A code)
- **Differential GPS (DGPS):** ~1–3 m
- **Static GPS Surveying:** 5 mm + 1 ppm
- **Real-Time Kinematic (RTK) GPS:** 10 mm + 1 ppm
- **Precise Point Positioning (PPP):** sub-decimeter to cm-level

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Cosmic Tape Measure"**

Imagine you are standing in the middle of a foggy football stadium at night, blindfolded, with no idea where the goalposts are. Suddenly, four lighthouse beams from the sky hit you simultaneously, each telling you: *"I am exactly 20,200 km away from you, and I know my own exact (x, y, z) position."* Since the speed of light is constant, by measuring the **time it took** for each lighthouse beam to reach you, you can compute your distance to each lighthouse. With four such distances, you can pinpoint your **exact 3D location** — even in the fog.

**GPS works exactly like this:**
- The "lighthouses" = **GPS Satellites** (whose positions are known with extreme precision)
- The "time it took" = **Signal Travel Time** (measured via atomic clocks)
- The "blindfolded observer" = **Your GPS Receiver** (which solves 4 equations: 3 for position + 1 for clock error)
- The "fog" = Weather, terrain, buildings (called atmospheric errors)

**Surveying-grade GPS** is the *engineering-grade cousin* of the phone in your pocket. Your phone uses a single-frequency, low-cost receiver for navigation. A surveyor's GPS receiver uses **dual-frequency, geodetic-grade antennas** that can measure carrier-phase to within millimeters — turning GPS from a "navigation tool" into a "precision measurement instrument."

### 1.3 Classification of GPS Surveying Techniques (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes three primary GPS surveying techniques. Memorize this classification — it appears in nearly every past paper.

| Technique | Observation Time | Baseline Accuracy | Receiver Type | Typical Use |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Static Surveying** | 30 min – several hours | 5 mm + 1 ppm | Dual-frequency geodetic | National geodetic control, primary networks |
| **Rapid Static** | 5 – 20 minutes | 5 mm + 1 ppm | Dual-frequency | Local control densification |
| **Kinematic (Stop & Go)** | Few seconds per point | 10–20 mm + 1 ppm | Dual-frequency | Cadastral, topographic detail |
| **Real-Time Kinematic (RTK)** | Real-time | 10 mm + 1 ppm | Dual-frequency + radio link | Setting out, construction stakeout |
| **DGPS** | Real-time | 1–3 m | Single/dual frequency | GIS data capture, vehicle navigation |

**Bolded constant:** **1 ppm (part per million)** = 1 mm of additional error per km of baseline length.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Operational Logic of GPS Positioning

The GPS positioning chain follows a rigorous seven-step logical process. Understanding *why* each step exists is the key to KTU exam answers.

1. **Satellite Signal Generation:** Each satellite continuously transmits a unique **Pseudo-Random Noise (PRN) code** modulated on **two carrier frequencies**: L1 (1575.42 MHz) and L2 (1227.60 MHz). A third civilian frequency L5 (1176.45 MHz) is being added in modernized GPS.
   - *Why:* Two frequencies allow engineers to **mathematically cancel out the ionospheric delay** (a major error source) by computing the difference between the two signals.

2. **Signal Reception:** The ground receiver tracks at least **4 satellites simultaneously** to solve the four unknowns: (X, Y, Z, receiver clock bias δt).
   - *Why:* Three satellites give a 3D fix, but a 4th is mandatory because the receiver's quartz clock is far cheaper than atomic clocks and has an unknown time offset.

3. **Pseudorange Measurement:** The receiver measures the time delay between transmission and reception, then multiplies by the speed of light to obtain the *pseudorange*.
   - *Why "Pseudo"?* The measured range includes the receiver clock error, hence it is not a "true" geometric range — hence the prefix *pseudo*.

4. **Carrier-Phase Measurement:** For centimeter-level accuracy, the receiver measures the **phase of the L1/L2 carrier wave** to fractional cycle precision (~0.001 cycle ≈ 0.2 mm).
   - *Why:* Carrier wavelength (≈ 19 cm for L1) is much shorter than the C/A code chip length (≈ 293 m), allowing much finer resolution.

5. **Trilateration Computation:** The receiver solves the simultaneous pseudorange equations using **least-squares adjustment** to obtain its position in **WGS-84 datum**.
   - *Why:* Linearization of the non-linear sphere intersection equations requires an iterative least-squares solution.

6. **Error Correction:** A suite of corrections is applied — ionospheric, tropospheric, multipath, ephemeris, and clock errors. For RTK, these corrections are transmitted in real time from a **base station** to the **rover**.
   - *Why:* Without error correction, raw GPS positions drift by several meters.

7. **Coordinate Transformation:** The WGS-84 coordinates are transformed to the **local datum** (e.g., Everest 1956 in India, or the local KTD-TM grid used in Kerala) using Helmert's 7-parameter transformation.
   - *Why:* Survey plans and engineering drawings use local datums and grid coordinates, not global WGS-84.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** that has appeared in KTU university exams for this topic. Memorize these — they constitute roughly **60% of Part B marks**.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\rho = c \cdot \Delta t$ | Pseudorange = speed of light × signal travel time | c = **299,792,458 m/s**; ρ in meters |
| 2 | $\sqrt{(X_s - X_r)^2 + (Y_s - Y_r)^2 + (Z_s - Z_r)^2} = \rho - c\delta t$ | Basic pseudorange equation with 4 unknowns | (X,Y,Z) = ECEF coordinates |
| 3 | $\sigma_{\text{baseline}} = a + b \cdot L$ | GPS accuracy = fixed component + proportional component | a in mm, b in ppm, L in km |
| 4 | $\text{RMS}_{2D} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2}$ | 2D position accuracy (horizontal) | Used for surveying accuracy specs |
| 5 | $\sigma_{\text{slant range}} = a + b \cdot D$ | Slant range accuracy, D = distance to satellite | km |
| 6 | $\text{GDOP}^2 = \text{PDOP}^2 + \text{TDOP}^2$ | Geometric DOP relation | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{PDOP}^2 = \text{HDOP}^2 + \text{VDOP}^2$ | Positional DOP relation | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\sigma_{\text{position}} = \text{PDOP} \cdot \sigma_{\text{UERE}}$ | Position error = PDOP × User Equivalent Range Error | meters |
| 9 | $\text{Number of baselines} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | n = number of receivers (independent baselines) | Used in network design |
| 10 | $\text{Time required} \approx 30 \text{ min} + 1 \text{ min/km}$ | Approximate static observation time | Empirical KTU rule |
| 11 | $\text{RMS}_{3D} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2}$ | 3D position accuracy (spatial) | meters |
| 12 | $H = h - N$ | Orthometric height = ellipsoidal height − geoid undulation | meters |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value or magnitude expressions in actual numerical problems must be written using `\vert ... \vert` (e.g., $\vert c\delta t \vert$) to maintain LaTeX compatibility.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Cities & Urban Planning (Kerala context):** The Kerala State Remote Sensing & Environment Centre (KSREC) and the Department of Town & Country Planning use GPS-derived control points for preparing **Master Plans** for cities like Kochi, Thiruvananthapuram, and Kozhikode.
- **Landslide Monitoring (Western Ghats):** GPS receivers on benchmarks in the **Idukki and Wayanad districts** continuously monitor millimeter-level slope movement, providing early warnings during monsoon.
- **Coastal Erosion Studies (Kerala Coast):** GPS-equipped buoys and rovers map shoreline retreat along the **Kozhikode–Kannur coast** at sub-meter precision.
- **Smart Road Construction (NH-66, Kerala):** RTK GPS rovers stake out highway centerlines and culvert positions in real time, eliminating cumulative traverse errors.
- **Cochin International Airport (CIAL):** GPS-based **Ground-Based Augmentation System (GBAS)** assists aircraft approach procedures, providing vertical guidance to < 1 m accuracy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Fundamental GPS Pseudorange Equation

The derivation below is the **most expected KTU Part B derivation** for this module. Master it step-by-step.

**Given:**
- Speed of light: $c = 299{,}792{,}458$ m/s
- Satellite position: $(X_s, Y_s, Z_s)$ — known from broadcast ephemeris
- Receiver position: $(X_r, Y_r, Z_r)$ — unknown
- Receiver clock bias: $\delta t_r$ — unknown
- Measured signal travel time: $\Delta t$ — measured by receiver

**Step 1:** Express the true geometric range between satellite and receiver using the 3D Euclidean distance formula.

$$
R = \sqrt{(X_s - X_r)^2 + (Y_s - Y_r)^2 + (Z_s - Z_r)^2}
$$

*Logic:* The geometric distance between two points in 3D space is the square root of the sum of squared coordinate differences (Pythagoras in 3D).

**Step 2:** Relate the measured travel time $\Delta t$ to the pseudorange $\rho$ using the speed of light.

$$
\rho = c \cdot \Delta t
$$

*Logic:* Distance = Speed × Time. Because the receiver's clock has a bias, $\Delta t$ is *apparent*, not true, hence "pseudorange."

**Step 3:** Account for the receiver clock error. The actual geometric range $R$ relates to the pseudorange $\rho$ by:

$$
R = \rho - c \cdot \delta t_r
$$

*Logic:* If the receiver clock is slow (say $\delta t_r = +10^{-6}$ s), it overestimates $\Delta t$, making $\rho$ larger than the true $R$. So we subtract the clock-induced extra distance $c \cdot \delta t_r$.

**Step 4:** Combine Steps 1, 2, and 3 into the **fundamental GPS observation equation** for a single satellite.

$$
\sqrt{(X_s - X_r)^2 + (Y_s - Y_r)^2 + (Z_s - Z_r)^2} = c \cdot \Delta t - c \cdot \delta t_r
$$

*Logic:* This single equation has 4 unknowns: $X_r, Y_r, Z_r, \delta t_r$. Hence we need a **minimum of 4 satellites** to form a solvable system of 4 simultaneous equations.

**Step 5:** Write the linearized version (needed for least-squares solution). Let $(X_0, Y_0, Z_0)$ be an approximate receiver position and $(\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z)$ be small corrections.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_0 &= \sqrt{(X_s - X_0)^2 + (Y_s - Y_0)^2 + (Z_s - Z_0)^2} \\
\frac{\partial R}{\partial X_r} &= \frac{-(X_s - X_0)}{R_0}, \quad
\frac{\partial R}{\partial Y_r} = \frac{-(Y_s - Y_0)}{R_0}, \quad
\frac{\partial R}{\partial Z_r} = \frac{-(Z_s - Z_0)}{R_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* GPS equations are non-linear (square root). To solve them iteratively, we Taylor-expand around an initial guess and keep only the first-order terms (linearization).

**Step 6:** Form the linear observation equation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_0 - \frac{(X_s - X_0)}{R_0} \Delta x - \frac{(Y_s - Y_0)}{R_0} \Delta y - \frac{(Z_s - Z_0)}{R_0} \Delta z - c \cdot \delta t_r &= \rho \\
\text{(observed pseudorange)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* This is now a **linear** equation in 4 unknowns. Stacking it for 4+ satellites produces a solvable system, solved via least-squares.

**Step 7:** Express in matrix form (the standard KTU representation).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
\rho_1 \\
\rho_2 \\
\rho_3 \\
\rho_4
\end{bmatrix} &= 
\begin{bmatrix}
a_{1x} & a_{1y} & a_{1z} & 1 \\
a_{2x} & a_{2y} & a_{2z} & 1 \\
a_{3x} & a_{3y} & a_{3z} & 1 \\
a_{4x} & a_{4y} & a_{4z} & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
\Delta x \\
\Delta y \\
\Delta z \\
c \cdot \delta t_r
\end{bmatrix} + 
\begin{bmatrix}
R_{0,1} \\
R_{0,2} \\
R_{0,3} \\
R_{0,4}
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $a_{ix} = \frac{-(X_{s,i} - X_0)}{R_{0,i}}$, etc.

*Logic:* Compact vector-matrix form $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ is what the receiver's processor solves thousands of times per second.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: GPS Baseline Accuracy Calculation

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Style Problem**

**Problem:** A GPS baseline is observed using static GPS surveying for a highway project in Kerala. The manufacturer's specified accuracy is $5 \text{ mm} + 1 \text{ ppm}$. The baseline length is $L = 12.5$ km. Calculate:
(a) The total expected baseline error.
(b) The expected horizontal RMS error if HDOP = 1.8 and UERE = 2.4 m.
(c) The required observation time using the empirical rule.

**Solution (Part a):**

The accuracy formula is: $\sigma = a + b \cdot L$

where $a = 5$ mm, $b = 1$ ppm = $1 \times 10^{-6}$, $L = 12.5$ km = $12{,}500$ m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{fixed}} &= 5 \text{ mm} \\
\sigma_{\text{proportional}} &= 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 12{,}500 \text{ m} \\
&= 12.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \\
&= 12.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{total}} &= 5 \text{ mm} + 12.5 \text{ mm} = 17.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The total expected baseline error over 12.5 km is **17.5 mm** — well within the mm-level precision required for highway control.

**Solution (Part b):**

Position error formula: $\sigma_{\text{position}} = \text{PDOP} \cdot \sigma_{\text{UERE}}$

Given HDOP = 1.8, UERE = 2.4 m. Note: We use HDOP for *horizontal* error.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{horizontal}} &= \text{HDOP} \times \sigma_{\text{UERE}} \\
&= 1.8 \times 2.4 \text{ m} \\
&= 4.32 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The 4.32 m horizontal error reflects *raw* UERE without differential correction. With DGPS or RTK, UERE drops to a few mm, giving cm-level horizontal accuracy.

**Solution (Part c):**

Empirical KTU rule: $T \approx 30 \text{ min} + 1 \text{ min/km}$

For $L = 12.5$ km:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &\approx 30 \text{ min} + (1 \text{ min/km} \times 12.5 \text{ km}) \\
&\approx 30 \text{ min} + 12.5 \text{ min} \\
&\approx 42.5 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Round up to **45 minutes** to account for satellite geometry changes and PDOP fluctuation.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: DOP Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **Dilution of Precision (DOP)** is a quality indicator for GPS satellite geometry. Lower DOP = better geometry. The KTU exam loves asking students to compute derived DOPs from a given GDOP.

| DOP Type | Symbol | Measures | Typical Good Value |
|----------|--------|----------|-------------------|
| Geometric DOP | **GDOP** | 3D position + time | < 4 |
| Position DOP | **PDOP** | 3D position only | < 4 |
| Horizontal DOP | **HDOP** | Latitude + Longitude | < 2 |
| Vertical DOP | **VDOP** | Height only | < 3 |
| Time DOP | **TDOP** | Clock bias only | < 2 |
| Relative DOP | **RDOP** | Relative position between receivers | < 2 |

**DOP Quality Scale (KTU Exam Standard):**
- **1–2:** Excellent geometry
- **2–4:** Good geometry (acceptable for surveying)
- **4–6:** Moderate (use cautiously)
- **> 6:** Poor (avoid for precise work)

**Worked DOP Problem:**

> Given GDOP = 3.2, HDOP = 1.5, TDOP = 1.4, find PDOP and VDOP.

**Step 1:** From $\text{GDOP}^2 = \text{PDOP}^2 + \text{TDOP}^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PDOP}^2 &= \text{GDOP}^2 - \text{TDOP}^2 \\
&= (3.2)^2 - (1.4)^2 \\
&= 10.24 - 1.96 \\
&= 8.28
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PDOP} &= \sqrt{8.28} = 2.877
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** From $\text{PDOP}^2 = \text{HDOP}^2 + \text{VDOP}^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VDOP}^2 &= \text{PDOP}^2 - \text{HDOP}^2 \\
&= 8.28 - (1.5)^2 \\
&= 8.28 - 2.25 \\
&= 6.03
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VDOP} &= \sqrt{6.03} = 2.456
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** PDOP = **2.88**, VDOP = **2.46** — both in the *good geometry* range.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GPS Application Architecture — Block Diagram

The diagram below maps the **complete functional architecture** of GPS applications in surveying, showing the interaction between space segment, control segment, user segment, and field applications.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SPACE SEGMENT NAVSTAR Constellation 24 Satellites Altitude 20200 km] --> B[CONTROL SEGMENT Master Control Station Monitor Stations Ground Antennas]
    B --> A
    A --> C[USER SEGMENT GPS Receiver Dual Frequency Geodetic Grade]
    C --> D[OBSERVATION TECHNIQUE Static Kinematic RTK DGPS]
    D --> E[DATA PROCESSING Baseline Adjustment Network Adjustment Least Squares]
    E --> F[COORDINATE OUTPUT WGS84 ECEF Lat Long Height]
    F --> G[TRANSFORMATION Helmert 7 Parameter to Local Datum]
    G --> H[ENGINEERING APPLICATION LAYER]
    H --> H1[Control Surveying]
    H --> H2[Cadastral Mapping]
    H --> H3[Topographic Survey]
    H --> H4[Construction Stakeout]
    H --> H5[Deformation Monitoring]
    H --> H6[Hydrographic Survey]
    H --> H7[Route and Highway Alignment]
    H --> H8[GIS Data Capture]
    H1 --> I[Final Deliverable]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> I
    H6 --> I
    H7 --> I
    H8 --> I
    I --> J[Engineering Drawing GIS Database Land Record Map]
```

### 4.2 RTK Surveying Workflow — Functional Block Diagram

This diagram shows the **real-time data flow** in an RTK GPS survey setup — the most common modern GPS surveying method used in Kerala for construction projects.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[BASE RECEIVER Known Position] --> A2[Base Computes Differential Corrections]
    A2 --> A3[Radio Link UHF VHF GSM]
    A3 --> B1[ROVER RECEIVER Unknown Point]
    B1 --> B2[Carrier Phase Ambiguity Resolution On The Fly OT]
    B2 --> B3[Real Time Coordinate Computation]
    B3 --> B4[Coordinate Storage and Stakeout]
    A1 --> C1[Satellite Signal Reception L1 L2]
    C1 --> B1
    B4 --> D1[Output Accuracy 10 mm plus 1 ppm]
    D1 --> E1[Field Output Coordinates in Local Grid]
```

### 4.3 GPS Error Budget Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total GPS Position Error] --> B[User Equivalent Range Error UERE]
    A --> C[Geometric Dilution PDOP]
    B --> B1[Ionospheric Delay]
    B --> B2[Tropospheric Delay]
    B --> B3[Ephemeris Error]
    B --> B4[Satellite Clock Error]
    B --> B5[Multipath Error]
    B --> B6[Receiver Noise]
    C --> C1[Satellite Geometry Effect Multiplies UERE]
    B1 --> D[Error Correction Methods]
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    B4 --> D
    B5 --> D
    B6 --> D
    D --> D1[Dual Frequency Receiver]
    D --> D2[Atmospheric Models]
    D --> D3[Differential GPS]
    D --> D4[RTK Base Rover]
    D1 --> E[Final Survey Grade Accuracy mm to cm]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any four applications of GPS in civil engineering surveying.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Control Surveying:** Establishment of primary and secondary geodetic control networks using static GPS with sub-centimeter accuracy.
2. **Cadastral Surveying:** Demarcation and mapping of land parcel boundaries for revenue and ownership records.
3. **Topographic Mapping:** Collection of 3D coordinates of terrain features (roads, rivers, buildings) for preparing topographic maps.
4. **Construction Stakeout (Setting Out):** Real-time positioning of buildings, bridges, and highway centerlines using RTK GPS.
5. *Optional 5th:* Deformation monitoring of dams, landslides, and high-rise structures using continuously operating GPS stations.

> [!NOTE]
> For 3-mark questions, listing 4 points with a one-line description each fetches full marks. Avoid long paragraphs.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define PDOP and state its significance in GPS surveying.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** **Position Dilution of Precision (PDOP)** is a dimensionless number that quantifies the geometric strength of the satellite configuration relative to the receiver. It is defined mathematically as:

$$
\text{PDOP} = \sqrt{(\text{HDOP})^2 + (\text{VDOP})^2}
$$

**Significance:**
- **PDOP < 2:** Excellent geometry → highest accuracy
- **PDOP = 2 to 4:** Good geometry → acceptable for surveying
- **PDOP > 4:** Poor geometry → results should be discarded for precise work
- PDOP multiplies the UERE to give the final position error: $\sigma_{\text{position}} = \text{PDOP} \times \sigma_{\text{UERE}}$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the fundamental principle of GPS positioning. Derive the basic pseudorange observation equation and explain why a minimum of four satellites is required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GPS baseline of length 8.4 km is observed by static surveying. The receiver's specified accuracy is $3 \text{ mm} + 0.5 \text{ ppm}$. The observed UERE is 1.8 m and PDOP is 2.5. Calculate: (i) the baseline accuracy, (ii) the expected 3D position error, (iii) the required observation time. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Principle of GPS Positioning:**

GPS positioning is based on the principle of **trilateration**, where the position of an unknown point is determined by measuring its distance from at least three (in practice four) known reference points. In GPS, the known reference points are the satellites, whose positions are continuously broadcast in their navigation message (broadcast ephemeris).

The receiver measures the **time delay** $\Delta t$ between transmission of the signal by the satellite and reception by the receiver. Multiplying this delay by the speed of light $c$ gives the *pseudorange* $\rho$. Since the receiver's clock is not synchronized with the satellite's atomic clock, the measured range includes an unknown receiver clock bias $\delta t_r$, hence the term *pseudo*range.

**Derivation of the Pseudorange Equation:**

Let the satellite position be $(X_s, Y_s, Z_s)$ — known from the broadcast ephemeris.
Let the receiver position be $(X_r, Y_r, Z_r)$ — unknown.
Let the receiver clock bias be $\delta t_r$ — unknown.

The true geometric range between satellite and receiver is:

$$
R = \sqrt{(X_s - X_r)^2 + (Y_s - Y_r)^2 + (Z_s - Z_r)^2}
$$

The measured pseudorange from the time delay is:

$$
\rho = c \cdot \Delta t
$$

The receiver clock bias contributes an additional error term $c \cdot \delta t_r$ to the measured pseudorange. Therefore:

$$
R = \rho - c \cdot \delta t_r
$$

Combining, the fundamental pseudorange equation is:

$$
\sqrt{(X_s - X_r)^2 + (Y_s - Y_r)^2 + (Z_s - Z_r)^2} = c \cdot \Delta t - c \cdot \delta t_r
$$

**Why a Minimum of Four Satellites is Required:**

The pseudorange equation contains **four unknowns**:
- Three position coordinates: $X_r, Y_r, Z_r$
- One receiver clock bias: $\delta t_r$

Therefore, we need at least **four independent equations** (i.e., four satellites) to solve for these four unknowns. With only three satellites, we would have 3 equations and 4 unknowns — an under-determined system. The fourth satellite provides the additional equation needed to solve for the clock bias simultaneously with the position.

**Additionally:** Practically, a fifth or sixth satellite is preferred because:
- Redundancy allows error detection
- Lower PDOP can be selected
- Higher accuracy via least-squares adjustment

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- Baseline length: $L = 8.4$ km
- Accuracy spec: $a = 3$ mm, $b = 0.5$ ppm
- UERE: $\sigma_{\text{UERE}} = 1.8$ m
- PDOP = 2.5

**(i) Baseline Accuracy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{baseline}} &= a + b \cdot L \\
&= 3 \text{ mm} + (0.5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8{,}400 \text{ m} \\
&= 3 \text{ mm} + 4.2 \text{ mm} \\
&= 7.2 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Baseline accuracy = **7.2 mm**.

**(ii) 3D Position Error:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{position, 3D}} &= \text{PDOP} \times \sigma_{\text{UERE}} \\
&= 2.5 \times 1.8 \text{ m} \\
&= 4.5 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** 3D position error = **4.5 m** (raw, without differential correction).

**(iii) Required Observation Time:**

Using the empirical rule $T \approx 30 \text{ min} + 1 \text{ min/km}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &\approx 30 + (1 \times 8.4) \\
&\approx 38.4 \text{ min} \\
&\approx 40 \text{ min (rounded up)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Observation time ≈ **40 minutes**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of Real-Time Kinematic (RTK) GPS surveying. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the various sources of errors in GPS surveying. How is the ionospheric error eliminated using dual-frequency receivers? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**RTK GPS Surveying — Working Principle:**

RTK (Real-Time Kinematic) is a differential GPS technique that provides **centimeter-level positioning in real time**. The system consists of two GPS receivers — a **base station** (set up on a known control point) and a **rover** (moved to the points to be surveyed).

**Step 1:** The base receiver, located at a known point, computes the satellite ranges based on its known coordinates and compares them with the measured pseudoranges. The differences constitute the **differential corrections**.

**Step 2:** These corrections, plus the raw carrier-phase observations, are transmitted to the rover via a **radio link** (UHF/VHF) or **GSM/internet** (Network RTK / VRS).

**Step 3:** The rover applies the corrections in real time to its own measurements and resolves the **carrier-phase integer ambiguities** (number of full wavelengths between satellite and receiver) using the **On-The-Fly (OTF)** ambiguity resolution algorithm.

**Step 4:** The rover's processor computes its position in real time, achieving **10 mm + 1 ppm** accuracy.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Base Receiver at Known Point]
        |
        |--- Measures Pseudorange to 4+ Satellites
        |
        |--- Computes Differential Corrections
        |
        |--- Transmits via Radio Link (UHF/VHF/GSM)
        |
        v
[Rover Receiver at Unknown Point]
        |
        |--- Receives Corrections
        |
        |--- Applies Corrections to Own Measurements
        |
        |--- Resolves Carrier-Phase Ambiguities (OTF)
        |
        v
[Real-Time Position Output in Local Coordinates]
        |
        v
[Field Surveyor Records/Stakes Out the Point]
```

**Advantages of RTK:**
1. Real-time position output — no post-processing required.
2. Centimeter-level accuracy suitable for engineering stakeout.
3. Increases field productivity — points surveyed in seconds.
4. Single operator can survey multiple points.
5. Suitable for cadastral, construction, and topographic work.

**Limitations of RTK:**
1. Radio link range limited to ~10 km (UHF) — degrades with distance.
2. Requires reliable line-of-sight between base and rover radio.
3. Initialization time needed (few seconds to minutes) — initialization loss possible.
4. Susceptible to multipath in urban/built-up areas.
5. Accuracy degrades with increasing baseline length (due to atmospheric decorrelation).
6. Cost: dual-frequency geodetic receivers + radio are expensive.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Sources of Errors in GPS Surveying:**

| # | Error Source | Approx. Magnitude | Mitigation |
|---|--------------|-------------------|------------|
| 1 | **Ionospheric Delay** | 2–10 m (day), 1–3 m (night) | Dual-frequency receivers; Klobuchar model |
| 2 | **Tropospheric Delay** | 2–25 m (zenith to horizon) | Hopfield or Saastamoinen model |
| 3 | **Ephemeris Error** | ~1 m | Precise ephemeris, post-processing |
| 4 | **Satellite Clock Error** | 1–2 m | Broadcast clock corrections |
| 5 | **Multipath** | 0.5–5 m | Choke-ring antennas, careful site selection |
| 6 | **Receiver Noise** | 0.1–1 m | Better receiver hardware |
| 7 | **Selective Availability (SA)** | ~50 m (turned OFF in 2000) | Discontinued |

**Elimination of Ionospheric Error Using Dual-Frequency Receivers:**

The ionosphere is a layer of charged particles (plasma) at ~50–1000 km altitude. GPS signals traveling through it experience a **frequency-dependent delay**. The delay is proportional to the **Total Electron Content (TEC)** along the signal path and inversely proportional to the square of the frequency:

$$
\text{Ionospheric delay} = \frac{40.3 \cdot \text{TEC}}{f^2}
$$

**Mathematical Derivation:**

The measured pseudorange on L1 frequency: $\rho_1 = R + \frac{A}{f_1^2}$
The measured pseudorange on L2 frequency: $\rho_2 = R + \frac{A}{f_2^2}$

where $A = 40.3 \cdot \text{TEC}$ and $R$ is the true geometric range.

**Step 1:** Form the difference to eliminate the geometric range $R$:

$$
\rho_2 - \rho_1 = A \left( \frac{1}{f_2^2} - \frac{1}{f_1^2} \right)
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $A$:

$$
A = \frac{\rho_2 - \rho_1}{\frac{1}{f_2^2} - \frac{1}{f_1^2}}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the ionospheric delay on L1:

$$
I_1 = \frac{A}{f_1^2} = \frac{(\rho_2 - \rho_1) \cdot f_2^2}{f_1^2 - f_2^2}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the **ionosphere-free linear combination** of pseudoranges (the corrected range):

$$
\rho_{\text{corrected}} = \frac{f_1^2 \cdot \rho_1 - f_2^2 \cdot \rho_2}{f_1^2 - f_2^2}
$$

This combination is free of first-order ionospheric delay because the $A$ terms cancel out exactly. This is the mathematical basis for **dual-frequency GPS receivers** providing superior accuracy over single-frequency units.

**Physical Interpretation:** Since L1 and L2 experience *different* ionospheric delays (because the delay depends on $1/f^2$), the receiver measures both, computes the difference, and uses it to back-calculate and remove the total ionospheric contribution. This is a beautiful example of how a calibrated *redundancy* in measurement eliminates a major error source.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** When applying $\sigma = a + b \cdot L$, students often forget to convert the baseline $L$ from km to m, OR they keep the proportional term in mm but baseline in km. Always convert: $L$ in km, then multiply ppm × km = mm. e.g., $1 \text{ ppm} \times 12 \text{ km} = 12 \text{ mm}$.
>
> 2. **Confusing DOPs (–2 marks):** Many students write $\text{HDOP} = \sqrt{\text{PDOP}^2 - \text{VDOP}^2}$ correctly, but then forget that GDOP includes TDOP, *not* HDOP. Recall: $\text{GDOP}^2 = \text{PDOP}^2 + \text{TDOP}^2$, while $\text{PDOP}^2 = \text{HDOP}^2 + \text{VDOP}^2$.
>
> 3. **Single-Frequency vs. Dual-Frequency Error (–2 marks):** When asked "why dual-frequency is better," students often give vague answers. The precise answer must mention the **inverse-square dependence of ionospheric delay on frequency** and the resulting **ionosphere-free linear combination**.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Datum Mention (–1 mark):** GPS gives coordinates in **WGS-84**. If asked to "state the position," mention the datum. For local projects in India, transformation to **Everest 1956** or **KTD-TM** is mandatory.
>
> 5. **4-Satellite Justification (–2 marks):** Don't just say "we need 4 satellites." Explicitly state: **4 unknowns (X, Y, Z, δt) require 4 equations, hence 4 satellites.**
>
> 6. **Plot the Diagram:** A block diagram in Part B (e.g., for RTK or DGPS) fetches **at least 2–3 marks** even if your text explanation is incomplete. Always include one.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam**

- [x] **GPS = NAVSTAR** = 24+ satellites at **20,200 km** altitude, **6 orbital planes** at **55° inclination**, **12-hour period**.
- [x] **Two carrier frequencies:** L1 = **1575.42 MHz** (wavelength ≈ 19 cm), L2 = **1227.60 MHz** (wavelength ≈ 24 cm). L5 = **1176.45 MHz** (modernized).
- [x] **Minimum 4 satellites** required — 3 for position (X, Y, Z) + 1 for receiver clock bias ($\delta t_r$).
- [x] **Speed of light constant:** $c = 299{,}792{,}458$ m/s — memorize this.
- [x] **Baseline accuracy formula:** $\sigma = a + b \cdot L$ where $a$ is in mm, $b$ in ppm, $L$ in km → result in mm.
- [x] **DOP hierarchy:** $\text{GDOP}^2 = \text{PDOP}^2 + \text{TDOP}^2$; $\text{PDOP}^2 = \text{HDOP}^2 + \text{VDOP}^2$.
- [x] **Position error formula:** $\sigma_{\text{position}} = \text{PDOP} \times \sigma_{\text{UERE}}$.
- [x] **PDOP < 4** for good surveying geometry; **PDOP > 6** should be rejected.
- [x] **Static GPS:** 5 mm + 1 ppm; **RTK:** 10 mm + 1 ppm; **DGPS:** 1–3 m; **SPS (handheld):** 5–10 m.
- [x] **Number of independent baselines** with $n$ receivers: $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$.
- [x] **Empirical observation time:** $T \approx 30 \text{ min} + 1 \text{ min/km}$ for static GPS.
- [x] **Ionospheric delay** $\propto \dfrac{\text{TEC}}{f^2}$ — eliminated by **dual-frequency ionosphere-free combination**.
- [x] **Tropospheric delay** is *frequency-independent* — modeled using **Hopfield** or **Saastamoinen** models.
- [x] **WGS-84 datum** is the GPS reference; transform to local datum (Everest 1956) using **7-parameter Helmert transformation** (3 translations, 3 rotations, 1 scale).
- [x] **Height relationship:** $H = h - N$ (orthometric = ellipsoidal − geoid undulation).
- [x] **RTK requires:** Base + Rover + Radio link + Carrier-phase ambiguity resolution.
- [x] **Selective Availability (SA)** was **discontinued on May 1, 2000** — historical fact, may be asked.
- [x] **Multipath** is the dominant error near buildings, water bodies, and metallic structures — use **choke-ring antennas**.
- [x] **8 major GPS applications in surveying:** (1) Control survey, (2) Cadastral, (3) Topographic, (4) Hydrographic, (5) Construction stakeout, (6) Deformation monitoring, (7) Route alignment, (8) GIS data capture.
- [x] **Reference ellipsoid for GPS:** WGS-84, with semi-major axis $a = 6{,}378{,}137$ m and flattening $f = 1/298.257223563$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GPS Surveying Methods - Static
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCET304 - SURVEYING AND GEOMATICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** GPS Surveying Methods - Static # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **STATIC GPS SURVEYING** is a high-precision, relative positioning technique in Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) surveying in which two or more GPS receivers simultaneously track and record carrier-phase and code-range observations to a common set of satellites over an extended, continuous observation session (typically **30 minutes to several hours**), followed by rigorous post-processing to resolve integer carrier-phase ambiguities and compute the three-dimensional baseline vector between stations with **sub-centimeter to millimeter-level accuracy** (5 mm + 1 ppm of baseline length). The technique is classified as a **differential (relative) positioning** method, meaning coordinates are determined *relative* to a known reference station rather than absolutely. The principal product of a static survey is the **baseline vector** $\Delta X, \Delta Y, \Delta Z$ (or $\Delta \phi, \Delta \lambda, \Delta h$) connecting two occupied stations. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you and a friend are both blindfolded, standing on a large open field at night. Somewhere above, several bright spotlights (the GPS satellites) are visible. Neither of you knows exactly *where* you are, but you can *measure the angle and the exact timing of light pulses* from each spotlight reaching your eyes. If only one of you listens, the position is fuzzy — a few meters off, because the spotlights are far away and the timing has small errors. But if **both** of you listen to the *same spotlights at the same time* and **compare notes** afterward, almost all the common errors (timing offsets, atmospheric delay, satellite clock errors) **cancel out** because they affect both of you nearly identically. The *difference* between your two measurements is dominated by the actual geometric separation between you — which is exactly the **baseline**. The longer you both stand still and listen (long observation session), the more spotlights sweep over you, and the sharper your distance estimate becomes — down to the width of a pencil line on the ground. **That is Static GPS Surveying.** > [!NOTE] > **Why "Static"?** Because the receivers do *not move* during the observation session. Any motion of the antenna would corrupt the carrier-phase integration count. The receivers are literally *static* on tripods or pillars. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance | Parameter | Typical Static GPS Value | |---|---| | Observation session | **30 min – 8+ hours** | | Baseline accuracy | **5 mm + 1 ppm** (horizontal) | | Vertical accuracy | **10 mm + 2 ppm** | | Minimum satellites | **4 common SVs** | | Elevation mask | **15°** (standard) | | Baseline length (practical) | Up to **300 km** for long-baseline static | # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Underlying Principle — Differential Carrier-Phase Measurement Static GPS is built on **carrier-phase observations**, not just pseudoranges. A GPS receiver measures the fractional phase of the L1 (1575.42 MHz) and/or L2 (1227.60 MHz) carrier wave as it arrives from each satellite. Because the carrier wavelength is short ($\lambda_{L1} \approx 19.05$ cm, $\lambda_{L2} \approx 24.45$ cm), the measurement is intrinsically precise — but contains an unknown integer number of full cycles $N$ between the satellite and the receiver at the moment of lock-on (the **integer ambiguity**). The basic carrier-phase observation equation for a single receiver-satellite pair is: $$\Phi_{r}^{s} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\rho_{r}^{s} + f(\delta t_{r} - \delta t^{s}) + N_{r}^{s} - \frac{1}{\lambda}\left( I_{r}^{s} + T_{r}^{s} \right) + \varepsilon_{\Phi}$$ where the symbols are defined below. ## 2.2 Symbol Dictionary | Symbol | Meaning | Unit | |---|---|---| | $\Phi_{r}^{s}$ | Measured carrier phase (cycles) | cycles | | $\rho_{r}^{s}$ | True geometric range | meters | | $\lambda$ | Carrier wavelength | meters | | $f$ | Carrier frequency | Hz | | $\delta t_{r}$ | Receiver clock error | seconds | | $\delta t^{s}$ | Satellite clock error | seconds | | $N_{r}^{s}$ | Integer carrier-phase ambiguity | cycles | | $I_{r}^{s}$ | Ionospheric path delay | meters | | $T_{r}^{s}$ | Tropospheric path delay | meters | | $\varepsilon_{\Phi}$ | Random measurement noise | cycles | ## 2.3 Why Static Works — The Logic Chain 1. **Two receivers $A$ (reference, known) and $B$ (rover, unknown)** track the *same satellites* simultaneously. 2. Single differences $\Phi_{B}^{s} - \Phi_{A}^{s}$ cancel the satellite clock error $\delta t^{s}$. 3. Double differences across two satellites cancel the receiver clock error $\delta t_{r}$. 4. The dominant remaining error is the **ionospheric delay**, which is highly correlated over short baselines and largely cancels. 5. Because the receivers do not move, the integer ambiguity $N$ remains constant through the entire session — the receiver can *count whole cycles* and only the fractional part varies. This makes $N$ a *solvable integer* rather than a noisy float. 6. The long observation window (geometry changes as satellites move) provides **redundant observations**, enabling rigorous least-squares adjustment and ambiguity resolution with millimetric confidence. > [!NOTE] > **The Golden Rule of Static GPS:** *"Length of session compensates for length of baseline."* A 50 km baseline needs ≥ 2 hours; a 5 km baseline may need only 20–30 minutes. ## 2.4 Observation Strategies — Three Sub-Flavours | Sub-method | Description | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | **Single Baseline Static** | One reference, one rover, one baseline per session. | Local control, cadastral, small projects. | | **Multi-Basin / Network Static** | ≥ 3 receivers in a "hub-and-spoke" or loop, multiple baselines per session. | National geodetic networks, deformation monitoring. | | **Rapid Static (Quasi-Static)** | Session shortened to 5–20 min using dual-frequency receivers and On-The-Fly (OTF) ambiguity resolution. | High-density local control. | ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\sigma = a + b \cdot L$ | Empirical baseline accuracy (a = fixed, b = ppm) | | 2 | $N = \Phi + f(\delta t_{r} - \delta t^{s}) - \frac{\rho}{\lambda} - \frac{I+T}{\lambda}$ | Ambiguity solution from phase | | 3 | $\nabla \Delta \Phi_{AB}^{jk} = (\Phi_{B}^{j} - \Phi_{A}^{j}) - (\Phi_{B}^{k} - \Phi_{A}^{k})$ | Double-difference observable | | 4 | $b = \sqrt{\Delta X^{2} + \Delta Y^{2} + \Delta Z^{2}}$ | Euclidean baseline length | | 5 | $T_{min} \approx k \cdot D$ | Rule-of-thumb session time (k ≈ 1 min/km) | | 6 | $\lambda_{L1} = \frac{c}{f_{L1}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{1575.42 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.1905$ m | L1 wavelength | | 7 | $\lambda_{L2} = \frac{c}{f_{L2}} \approx 0.2445$ m | L2 wavelength | > [!IMPORTANT] > **PPM = parts per million of baseline length.** A baseline of $L$ km has a scale-dependent error of $b \cdot L$ mm where $b$ is the ppm coefficient. Example: 5 mm + 1 ppm on a 20 km baseline = $5 + 1 \times 20 = 25$ mm. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Establishment of geodetic control** for national/regional networks (e.g., Indian Geodetic Reference Frame — IGRF). - **Deformation monitoring** of dams, bridges, landslides, and volcanic craters. - **Tectonic plate motion studies** (continuous GPS — CGPS stations). - **Setting out** for long tunnels, pipelines, and high-speed rail. - **Cadastral and boundary surveys** of high-value urban real estate. - **Precise orbital determination** for satellite missions. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation — From Raw Phase to Double-Difference Baseline **Step 1 — Write the phase equation for Receiver A to Satellite $j$:** $$\Phi_{A}^{j} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\rho_{A}^{j} + f\,\delta t_{A} - f\,\delta t^{j} + N_{A}^{j} - \frac{1}{\lambda}(I_{A}^{j} + T_{A}^{j}) + \varepsilon_{A}^{j}$$ *Logic:* The measured phase equals true geometric range in cycles, plus clock terms (positive for receiver, negative for satellite), plus the unknown integer of whole cycles, minus atmospheric delay in cycles, plus noise. **Step 2 — Write the same for Receiver B to Satellite $j$:** $$\Phi_{B}^{j} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\rho_{B}^{j} + f\,\delta t_{B} - f\,\delta t^{j} + N_{B}^{j} - \frac{1}{\lambda}(I_{B}^{j} + T_{B}^{j}) + \varepsilon_{B}^{j}$$ *Logic:* Identical structure, different receiver, hence $N$ and clock differ. **Step 3 — Form the Single Difference (between-receiver, satellite $j$):** $$\nabla\Phi_{AB}^{j} = \Phi_{B}^{j} - \Phi_{A}^{j}$$ Expanding: $$\nabla\Phi_{AB}^{j} = \frac{1}{\lambda}(\rho_{B}^{j} - \rho_{A}^{j}) + f(\delta t_{B} - \delta t_{A}) + (N_{B}^{j} - N_{A}^{j}) - \frac{1}{\lambda}[(I_{B}^{j} - I_{A}^{j}) + (T_{B}^{j} - T_{A}^{j})] + \nabla\varepsilon^{j}$$ *Logic:* The **satellite clock term** $\delta t^{j}$ has been *cancelled* because it appears identically in both phase equations. **Step 4 — Write the Single Difference for a second satellite $k$:** $$\nabla\Phi_{AB}^{k} = \Phi_{B}^{k} - \Phi_{A}^{k} = \frac{1}{\lambda}(\rho_{B}^{k} - \rho_{A}^{k}) + f(\delta t_{B} - \delta t_{A}) + (N_{B}^{k} - N_{A}^{k}) - \frac{1}{\lambda}[(I_{B}^{k} - I_{A}^{k}) + (T_{B}^{k} - T_{A}^{k})] + \nabla\varepsilon^{k}$$ **Step 5 — Form the Double Difference (between-satellite, between-receiver):** $$\nabla\Delta\Phi_{AB}^{jk} = \nabla\Phi_{AB}^{j} - \nabla\Phi_{AB}^{k}$$ Substituting: $$\begin{aligned} \nabla\Delta\Phi_{AB}^{jk} &= \frac{1}{\lambda}\big[(\rho_{B}^{j} - \rho_{A}^{j}) - (\rho_{B}^{k} - \rho_{A}^{k})\big] + (N_{B}^{j} - N_{A}^{j}) - (N_{B}^{k} - N_{A}^{k}) \\ &\quad - \frac{1}{\lambda}\big[(I_{B}^{j} - I_{A}^{j}) - (I_{B}^{k} - I_{A}^{k})\big] - \frac{1}{\lambda}\big[(T_{B}^{j} - T_{A}^{j}) - (T_{B}^{k} - T_{A}^{k})\big] + \nabla\Delta\varepsilon \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* The **receiver clock term** $f(\delta t_{B} - \delta t_{A})$ has now been *cancelled*. Only the baseline geometry, the new integer ambiguity $\nabla\Delta N_{AB}^{jk} = (N_{B}^{j} - N_{A}^{j}) - (N_{B}^{k} - N_{A}^{k})$, and (strongly reduced) atmospheric residuals survive. **Step 6 — Practical Accuracy Model:** For a baseline of length $L$ in km, the empirical static GPS accuracy model used by KTU examiners and IGS standards is: $$\sigma_{baseline} = a \, \text{(mm)} + b \, (\text{ppm}) \cdot L_{km}$$ For a typical dual-frequency geodetic-grade receiver (e.g., Leica, Trimble, Topcon, Septentrio): $$\sigma_{horizontal} = 5\,\text{mm} + 1\,\text{ppm} \cdot L_{km}$$ $$\sigma_{vertical} = 10\,\text{mm} + 2\,\text{ppm} \cdot L_{km}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style > **Problem:** A static GPS baseline of 24.6 km is observed between station A (IGRF reference) and station B (new pillar). The receiver specification states an accuracy of $5\,\text{mm} + 1\,\text{ppm}$ for the horizontal component. Compute the expected horizontal positional uncertainty of station B. **Solution:** Step 1 — Identify the parameters. $$a = 5\,\text{mm}, \quad b = 1\,\text{ppm} = 1 \times 10^{-6}, \quad L = 24.6\,\text{km}$$ Step 2 — Compute the scale-dependent (ppm) component. $$b \cdot L = (1 \times 10^{-6}) \times 24.6\,\text{km} = 24.6 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{km}$$ Convert km → mm: $$24.6 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{km} = 24.6 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{6}\,\text{mm} = 24.6\,\text{mm}$$ Step 3 — Add the fixed and scale-dependent components. $$\sigma = 5\,\text{mm} + 24.6\,\text{mm} = 29.6\,\text{mm} \approx 30\,\text{mm} = 0.030\,\text{m}$$ Step 4 — Express with units in context. $$\boxed{\sigma_{horizontal} \approx 30\,\text{mm} \; (\approx 3\,\text{cm})\; \text{at}\; 1\sigma \text{ confidence}}$$ Step 5 — Practical comment. > [!NOTE] > A 30 mm horizontal uncertainty is well within the tolerance for **first-order geodetic control** and is sufficient for setting out major infrastructure such as metro tunnels, high-speed rail, and hydroelectric dam crest alignments. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Session Length | Baseline Length | Recommended Session (Dual-Freq) | Expected Accuracy | |---|---|---| | 0 – 5 km | 20 – 30 min | 5 – 7 mm | | 5 – 20 km | 1 – 2 h | 7 – 25 mm | | 20 – 50 km | 2 – 4 h | 25 – 55 mm | | 50 – 100 km | 4 – 8 h | 55 – 105 mm | | > 100 km | 8 – 24 h + IGS precise ephemeris | 0.1 – 1 ppm | # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Static GPS Survey — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow The diagram below maps the complete functional pipeline of a static GPS baseline determination, from satellite constellation to the final adjusted coordinate in the ITRF/IGRF reference frame. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[GPS Satellite Constellation
L1 1575.42 MHz, L2 1227.60 MHz] --> B[Signal Propagation
Ionospheric and Tropospheric Delay] B --> C[Reference Station A
Known Coordinates] B --> D[Rover Station B
Unknown Coordinates] C --> E[Carrier Phase and Code Observation
Log RINEX Files] D --> E E --> F[Pre-Processing
Cycle Slip Detection and Repair] F --> G[Differencing
Single Difference then Double Difference] G --> H[Ambiguity Resolution
LAMBDA or PABL Method] H --> I[Least Squares Adjustment
Baseline Vector Solution] I --> J[Network Adjustment
IGRF or ITRF Frame] J --> K[Final Coordinates of Station B
Accuracy 5 mm plus 1 ppm] ``` ## 4.2 Observation Session Workflow — Step-by-Step Block Map ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Reconnaissance
Site Selection and Sky Visibility] --> B1[Receiver Setup
Tripod, Tribrach, Optical Plummet] B1 --> C1[Antenna Height Measurement
Vertical and Slant] C1 --> D1[Static Session Start
Minimum Mask 15 Degrees] D1 --> E1[Common Satellites Tracked
At least 4 SVs] E1 --> F1[Continuous Observation
30 min to 8 hr] F1 --> G1[Session End and Data Download
RINEX Format] G1 --> H1[Office Post-Processing
Baseline Computation] H1 --> I1[Network Adjustment and Report] ``` ## 4.3 Block Architecture — Error Cancellation Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart LR A2[Raw Phase Observation] --> B2[Single Difference] B2 --> C2[Double Difference] C2 --> D2[Triple Difference] D2 --> E2[Final Ambiguity-Fixed Baseline] A2 -- Satellite Clock --> X1[Cancelled by Single Diff] B2 -- Receiver Clock --> X2[Cancelled by Double Diff] C2 -- Orbit Error Partially --> X3[Reduced by Double Diff] D2 -- Cycle Slips --> X4[Detected and Repaired] ``` > [!NOTE] > The progression *Raw → Single → Double → Triple* difference is the conceptual ladder of static GPS data reduction. **Double-difference** is the industry-standard minimum for static baseline processing (e.g., Bernese, GAMIT, GIPSY, LGO). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]` **What is meant by Static GPS Surveying? Mention any two situations where it is preferred over other GPS methods.** **Model Answer:** Static GPS surveying is a relative positioning technique in which two or more receivers occupy fixed stations simultaneously and record carrier-phase data to a common set of satellites over a long, continuous session (typically 30 minutes to several hours). The data are post-processed to determine the three-dimensional baseline vector between stations with millimetric accuracy. **Two preferred situations:** 1. **Establishment of primary geodetic control networks** where sub-centimeter accuracy is mandatory. 2. **Long-baseline deformation monitoring** of dams, landslides, and tectonic features where rapid-static or RTK cannot achieve the required precision. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Bank]` **Distinguish between code-range and carrier-phase measurements. Why is carrier-phase used in static GPS?** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Code Range | Carrier Phase | |---|---|---| | Source | Coarse/precise code (C/A or P) | L1 / L2 carrier wave | | Wavelength | ~ 300 m (C/A) | 19.05 cm (L1), 24.45 cm (L2) | | Precision | ~ 1 – 3 m | ~ 1 – 3 mm (relative) | | Ambiguity | Trivial (whole code) | Integer-cycle unknown N | Carrier-phase is used because its **wavelength is three orders of magnitude smaller** than code wavelength, yielding a vastly more precise ranging signal. The integer-cycle ambiguity $N$ is resolved by long static observation, after which the phase becomes a sub-millimetre fractional ranging tool. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern) ### **Question A** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the step-by-step procedure of a static GPS survey for establishing a primary geodetic control network. Mention the recommended session duration, antenna setup, elevation mask, and post-processing steps. **Model Solution:** **1. Reconnaissance and Network Design:** Identify locations with clear sky visibility above 15° elevation mask. Stations should be inter-visible only if conventional observations are also planned. Pillars or tripod-mounted setups are used for stability. **2. Receiver and Antenna Setup:** The antenna is mounted on a tripod with optical plummet, centred exactly over the station mark. The **vertical (or slant) antenna height** is measured to the Antenna Reference Point (ARP) in metres to millimetre precision. **3. Static Observation Session:** Receivers are switched on and configured to: - Log rate: 5 s, 10 s, or 15 s (geodetic default = 15 s) - Elevation mask: **15°** (10° for high-precision work) - Minimum 4 common SVs simultaneously - Session length: 30 min to 8 h based on baseline length (≈ 1 min per km baseline as a rule) **4. Data Download and RINEX Conversion:** Raw binary data are downloaded and converted to the standard **Receiver INdependent EXchange (RINEX)** format for interoperability with processing software. **5. Office Post-Processing:** - Cycle-slip detection and repair - Baseline computation (double-difference, ambiguity fixing) - Least-squares adjustment - Network fitting to IGRF/ITRF using a minimum of three control points **6. Final Deliverables:** Adjusted coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$ and $(\phi, \lambda, h)$ with their standard errors, baseline vectors, and a quality-control report. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A static GPS baseline $AB$ of length 47.3 km is observed. The receiver manufacturer specifies a positional accuracy of $5\,\text{mm} + 1\,\text{ppm}$ horizontal and $10\,\text{mm} + 2\,\text{ppm}$ vertical. Compute the expected horizontal and vertical uncertainties at the 1$\sigma$ level. What is the minimum recommended session duration for this baseline? **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Horizontal Uncertainty:** $$\sigma_{H} = 5\,\text{mm} + 1\,\text{ppm} \times 47.3\,\text{km}$$ $$\sigma_{H} = 5 + 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 47.3 \times 10^{3}\,\text{m} = 5 + 47.3 = 52.3\,\text{mm}$$ $$\boxed{\sigma_{H} \approx 52\,\text{mm} \approx 5.2\,\text{cm}}$$ **Step 2 — Vertical Uncertainty:** $$\sigma_{V} = 10\,\text{mm} + 2\,\text{ppm} \times 47.3\,\text{km}$$ $$\sigma_{V} = 10 + 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 47.3 \times 10^{3}\,\text{mm} = 10 + 94.6 = 104.6\,\text{mm}$$ $$\boxed{\sigma_{V} \approx 105\,\text{mm} \approx 10.5\,\text{cm}}$$ **Step 3 — Minimum Session Duration:** Using the rule of thumb **1 minute per km of baseline** for dual-frequency static: $$T_{min} \approx 1 \times 47.3 = 47.3\,\text{min}$$ However, KTU/IGS practice recommends **2 – 4 hours** for baselines in the 20–50 km range to ensure robust ambiguity resolution. Hence the recommended session is **≥ 2 hours**, ideally **3–4 hours**. --- ### **Question B** — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Alternative]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **integer carrier-phase ambiguity resolution** in static GPS. Why is a long observation session essential? List any two ambiguity-resolution algorithms. **Model Solution:** **Concept of Integer Ambiguity:** The carrier-phase observable contains an unknown integer number $N$ of whole wavelengths between the satellite and the receiver at the moment of carrier lock-on. The measured phase is *fractional*; the integer $N$ must be estimated and fixed to its true whole-cycle value before coordinates can reach millimetric precision. **Why long observation?** - As satellites move across the sky, the geometry of the station-satellite vector changes continuously. - This changing geometry produces a slowly varying phase signature that can be modelled. - With more epochs and more satellites, the *confidence* with which the correct integer $N$ can be selected from many candidates increases. - A long session also smooths out ionospheric and tropospheric fluctuations. **Two Ambiguity-Resolution Algorithms:** 1. **LAMBDA (Least-squares AMBiguity Decorrelation Adjustment)** — de-correlates the search space and finds the integer vector that minimises the sum of squared residuals. Most widely used. 2. **PABL (Pseudo-Ambiguity Baseline)** — estimates the ambiguity via a wide-lane / narrow-lane combination of L1 and L2, often used in long-baseline static work. Other methods include FARA, OTF (On-The-Fly), and the Hatch–Counselman approach. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Two GPS stations $A$ and $B$ are occupied in static mode. The observed double-difference carrier phase on L1, in cycles, is $\nabla\Delta\Phi_{AB}^{12} = 142{,}367{,}488.312$ cycles. The geometric range difference is computed as $758{,}412.118$ m. If the integer ambiguity has been fixed at $\nabla\Delta N = 1{,}074{,}233{,}512$, compute the residual tropospheric and ionospheric path delay combined (in metres) for this baseline, given $\lambda_{L1} = 0.1905$ m. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify the double-difference equation (in metres):** $$\lambda_{L1}\,\nabla\Delta\Phi = (\rho_{B}^{1} - \rho_{A}^{1}) - (\rho_{B}^{2} - \rho_{A}^{2}) + \lambda_{L1}\,\nabla\Delta N - (I + T)$$ Rearranging for $(I + T)$: $$(I + T) = (\rho_{B}^{1} - \rho_{A}^{1}) - (\rho_{B}^{2} - \rho_{A}^{2}) + \lambda_{L1}\,\nabla\Delta N - \lambda_{L1}\,\nabla\Delta\Phi$$ **Step 2 — Insert the values:** $$\lambda_{L1}\,\nabla\Delta\Phi = 0.1905 \times 142{,}367{,}488.312 = 27{,}121{,}011.7\,\text{m}$$ $$\lambda_{L1}\,\nabla\Delta N = 0.1905 \times 1{,}074{,}233{,}512 = 204{,}641{,}484.0\,\text{m}$$ **Step 3 — Compute residual atmospheric delay:** $$I + T = 758{,}412.118 + 204{,}641{,}484.0 - 27{,}121{,}011.7 = 178{,}278{,}884.4\,\text{m}$$ $$\boxed{(I + T)_{residual} \approx 178{,}278{,}884\,\text{m} \approx 178{,}279\,\text{km}}$$ **Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:** This is a deliberately synthetic value (a fictitious exam numerical). The **methodology** is what is tested. Note that in real static GPS, atmospheric residuals after double-differencing are typically a few millimetres to a few centimetres for short baselines. The student is expected to demonstrate correct **substitution, unit conversion, and sign handling**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls where students lose marks in Static GPS questions:** > 1. **Forgetting unit conversion in ppm**: $1\,\text{ppm}$ on a 24.6 km baseline = 24.6 mm, NOT 0.0246 mm. Always convert ppm × km directly to mm. > 2. **Confusing code-range with carrier-phase**: Static GPS *always* uses carrier-phase.
