Writing "pseudorange-based" loses the method's identity. > 3. **Session length as a function of baseline length**: writing a single fixed time (e.g., "1 hour") without qualifying it for the baseline length costs 1–2 marks. > 4. **Missing antenna-height measurement** as a mandatory field step is a frequent 1-mark loss. > 5. **Skipping the elevation mask (15°)** in the setup description is penalised. > 6. **Confusing the L1 wavelength** (≈ 19 cm) with the C/A code wavelength (≈ 293 m). > 7. **Failing to mention ambiguity resolution** in the post-processing chain is a recurring 2-mark deduction. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - Static GPS is a **carrier-phase, relative, multi-receiver, long-session** technique. - Accuracy: **5 mm + 1 ppm horizontal**, **10 mm + 2 ppm vertical** (dual-frequency geodetic). - **Session duration** ≈ **1 min per km of baseline** (rule of thumb); use 2–4 h for 20–50 km. - **Minimum 4 common satellites**; **15° elevation mask**; **15 s data rate** (typical). - Data is **post-processed**, never real-time. - Output is the **3-D baseline vector** $\Delta X, \Delta Y, \Delta Z$. - **Antenna height** must be measured (vertical or slant-to-vertical) at every setup. - **RINEX format** is the universal data exchange. - **Double-difference** is the standard processing observable (cancels both clock errors). - **Integer ambiguity $N$** is the *key unknown* and must be resolved for sub-cm accuracy. - **LAMBDA** is the most widely used ambiguity-resolution algorithm. - **Trop + Iono** residuals are the dominant remaining error sources after double-differencing. - **Long baselines** (>50 km) require IGS precise ephemerides and longer sessions. - **Best applications:** geodetic control, deformation monitoring, crustal motion, tunnel alignment. - **Static ≠ RTK ≠ PPK** — Static is the most accurate, RTK/PPK are faster but less precise.

topics.title: Rapid Static
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCET304 - SURVEYING AND GEOMATICS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Rapid Static ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Static GPS Surveying** is a fast, high-precision satellite-based positioning technique that uses **dual-frequency GNSS receivers** to collect carrier-phase measurements over a **short occupation period of approximately 5 to 20 minutes** on relatively short baselines (typically **less than 20 km**). The technique relies on the rapid *On-The-Fly* (OTF) integer ambiguity resolution of the L1 and L2 carrier-phase observations to deliver centimetre-level (3D) positional accuracy of the order of **5 mm + 1 ppm** to **10 mm + 1 ppm** of the baseline length. It is formally classified as a variant of the *Static* family of GPS observation methods, but with significantly reduced observation time achieved through the use of: - **Dual-frequency (L1 + L2) carrier-phase data** - **Precise ephemeris** (post-processed or broadcast) - **Efficient OTF ambiguity resolution algorithms** (e.g., LAMBDA method) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Smart Camera Exposure":** > Imagine taking a photograph of a moving object at night. A *conventional static* GPS survey is like leaving the camera shutter open for **1–2 hours** to gather enough light (data) to form a clear, sharp image of the satellite geometry. > > *Rapid Static* is the **modern mirrorless camera with AI denoising** — it only needs a **5–20 minute "exposure"** because the sensor (dual-frequency receiver) and the processing chip (OTF algorithm) are far more efficient at extracting the same positional information from a much smaller data set. > > The trade-off? You cannot "photograph" a moving subject (no high dynamics), and the camera's effective range is limited (short baselines only). ### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Hardware Requirements | Parameter | Specification | Importance | |---|---|---| | Receiver type | **Dual-frequency (L1 + L2)** | Mandatory for iono-correction | | Minimum satellites | **≥ 5** (preferably 6) | Redundancy for OTF | | Maximum PDOP | **≤ 5** (preferably ≤ 3) | Geometry strength | | Observation time | **5 – 20 min** per station | Depends on baseline length | | Maximum baseline | **< 20 – 30 km** | To limit ionospheric decorrelation | | Achievable accuracy | **5 mm + 1 ppm** (horizontal) | Centimetre-grade | | Ephemeris | **Broadcast / Precise / Rapid** | A posteriori processing | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" Behind Rapid Static The entire logic of Rapid Static rests on **resolving the integer carrier-phase ambiguities $N$** as quickly as possible. Recall that the carrier-phase observable is: $$\phi = \rho + \lambda N - \frac{I}{f^2} + T + \epsilon$$ where $\rho$ is the geometric range, $I$ is the ionospheric delay, $T$ the tropospheric delay, and $N$ the unknown integer cycle ambiguity. - The unknown integer $N$ is the main hurdle to mm-cm accuracy. - In *Conventional Static*, $N$ is resolved by waiting for the satellite geometry to change sufficiently (hours of observation). - In *Rapid Static*, $N$ is resolved **algebraically/statistically in minutes** by exploiting the **wide-lane linear combination** (longer wavelength $\Rightarrow$ easier ambiguity search) and the **ionosphere-free linear combination** (eliminates the dispersive ionospheric term). ### 2.2 Why Dual Frequency Is Mandatory By forming the **ionosphere-free (IF) linear combination** of L1 and L2 phase observations, the dispersive ionospheric delay cancels to first order: $$L_{IF} = \frac{f_1^2 \, L_1 - f_2^2 \, L_2}{f_1^2 - f_2^2}$$ This is what enables short observation times even during high ionospheric activity. A single-frequency receiver cannot do this — hence the **5–10× speed-up** of Rapid Static over Conventional Static demands a **dual-frequency** receiver. ### 2.3 Wide-Lane and Narrow-Lane Trick | Combination | Wavelength $\lambda$ (cm) | Primary Use in Rapid Static | |---|---|---| | $L_1$ | **19.05** | Primary phase observable | | $L_2$ | **24.45** | Iono-correction partner | | **Wide-Lane** $(L_1 - L_2)$ | **86.25** | Rapid coarse ambiguity search | | **Narrow-Lane** $(L_1 + L_2)$ | **10.7** | Final fine ambiguity tightening | | **Iono-Free** | Geometric | Removes $\sim 99.9\%$ iono delay | The 86.25 cm wide-lane wavelength is **4.5× larger** than $L_1$ alone, so a coarse integer search over a few centimetres can be performed in seconds rather than hours. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **must-memorise** formula set for any Rapid Static numerical or theory question in the KTU ESE. | # | Formula / Symbol | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Wavelength $L_1$ | $\lambda_1 = c / f_1$ | $\lambda_1 \approx$ **19.05 cm**; $f_1 = 1575.42$ MHz | | 2 | Wavelength $L_2$ | $\lambda_2 = c / f_2$ | $\lambda_2 \approx$ **24.45 cm**; $f_2 = 1227.60$ MHz | | 3 | Wide-lane wavelength | $\lambda_W = c / (f_1 - f_2)$ | $\lambda_W \approx$ **86.25 cm** | | 4 | Narrow-lane wavelength | $\lambda_N = c / (f_1 + f_2)$ | $\lambda_N \approx$ **10.7 cm** | | 5 | Iono-free phase | $L_{IF} = (f_1^2 L_1 - f_2^2 L_2)/(f_1^2 - f_2^2)$ | Eliminates first-order iono | | 6 | Horizontal accuracy | $\sigma_H = a + b \cdot D$ | $a$ in mm, $b$ in ppm, $D$ in km | | 7 | Vertical accuracy | $\sigma_V \approx 2 \sigma_H$ | Empirical rule | | 8 | DOP categories | GDOP / PDOP / HDOP / VDOP / TDOP | PDOP $\le$ 5 ideal | | 9 | Occupation time $t$ | $t \approx 5 + 0.5 \cdot D_{km}$ (min) | Empirical KTU rule of thumb | | 10 | Baseline length limit | $D \le 20$ km (ideal), $\le 30$ km (operational) | Iono decorrelation limit | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cadastral and property boundary surveys** — sub-cm legal-grade coordinates in a fraction of the time of conventional static. - **Topographic control densification** in urban infrastructure projects (metro, flyovers, smart-city corridors in Kerala). - **Deformation monitoring of dams and bridges** — fast repeat occupations between cycles. - **Control points for UAV/drone photogrammetry and LiDAR** ground control. - **Coastal and inland waterway surveys** of Kerala's backwaters, where short observation windows are dictated by tide/weather. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Wide-Lane Wavelength Starting from the definition of the wide-lane phase observable: $$\phi_W = \phi_1 - \phi_2$$ where $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$ are the L1 and L2 carrier-phase measurements (in cycles). Converting to range via $L = \lambda \phi$: $$L_W = \lambda_1 \phi_1 - \lambda_2 \phi_2$$ But the *effective* wavelength of the wide-lane combination is obtained by treating the difference frequency: $$\lambda_W = \frac{c}{f_1 - f_2}$$ Substituting the GNSS carrier frequencies: $$\begin{aligned} f_1 - f_2 &= 1575.42 \text{ MHz} - 1227.60 \text{ MHz} \\ &= 347.82 \text{ MHz} \end{aligned}$$ Therefore: $$\begin{aligned} \lambda_W &= \frac{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{347.82 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}} \\ &= 0.8625 \text{ m} \\ &= 86.25 \text{ cm} \end{aligned}$$ **Physical meaning:** Because the wide-lane wavelength is **86.25 cm** (more than four times the $L_1$ wavelength), the integer search space for the wide-lane ambiguity $N_W$ is dramatically smaller per unit range uncertainty, which is the *fundamental reason* Rapid Static achieves ambiguity resolution in minutes rather than hours. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Narrow-Lane Wavelength By the same logic, but using the sum of the two frequencies: $$\begin{aligned} f_1 + f_2 &= 1575.42 \text{ MHz} + 1227.60 \text{ MHz} \\ &= 2803.02 \text{ MHz} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \lambda_N &= \frac{c}{f_1 + f_2} \\ &= \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2803.02 \times 10^6} \\ &= 0.1070 \text{ m} \\ &= 10.7 \text{ cm} \end{aligned}$$ The narrow-lane is used in the *second stage* of ambiguity resolution to "tighten" the wide-lane solution from 86.25 cm precision to millimetre precision. ### 3.3 Derivation of the Ionosphere-Free Linear Combination The phase advance due to the ionosphere along the signal path is dispersive and proportional to $1/f^2$: $$\delta\phi_{iono} = -\frac{I}{f}$$ The total phase in metres is therefore $L = \rho - \delta\phi_{iono}\cdot\lambda$, but in *cycles* the ionospheric term enters with sign and frequency dependence. Eliminating it algebraically: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Let } \alpha &= \frac{f_1^2}{f_1^2 - f_2^2} \\ \text{Let } \beta &= \frac{f_2^2}{f_1^2 - f_2^2} \end{aligned}$$ The iono-free combination is formed as: $$L_{IF} = \alpha L_1 - \beta L_2$$ Substituting $L_i = \rho - I/f_i$ (in metres): $$\begin{aligned} L_{IF} &= \alpha\left(\rho - \frac{I}{f_1}\right) - \beta\left(\rho - \frac{I}{f_2}\right) \\ &= (\alpha - \beta)\rho - I\left(\frac{\alpha}{f_1} - \frac{\beta}{f_2}\right) \end{aligned}$$ Now evaluate the ionospheric coefficient: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{\alpha}{f_1} - \frac{\beta}{f_2} &= \frac{f_1}{f_1^2 - f_2^2} - \frac{f_2}{f_1^2 - f_2^2} \\ &= \frac{f_1 - f_2}{f_1^2 - f_2^2} = \frac{1}{f_1 + f_2} \neq 0 \end{aligned}$$ So the **first-order** ionospheric term is eliminated because in the *cycle* form the geometry is: $$L_{IF_{cycles}} = \alpha \phi_1 - \beta \phi_2, \quad \alpha + \beta = 1, \quad \alpha f_1 = \beta f_2$$ ensuring exact cancellation of the $1/f$ dispersive term. This combination is the workhorse of every Rapid Static baseline processor. ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Occupied Time and Accuracy **Problem:** A Rapid Static survey requires establishing a control point at a baseline of $D = 12$ km from the reference station. Using the empirical rule and the standard accuracy formula, compute (a) the recommended occupation time, and (b) the expected horizontal accuracy. **Solution:** **(a) Occupation time** $$\begin{aligned} t &\approx 5 + 0.5 \cdot D_{km} \text{ minutes} \\ t &\approx 5 + 0.5 \times 12 \\ t &\approx 5 + 6 \\ t &= 11 \text{ minutes} \end{aligned}$$ **Adopt $t = 15$ minutes** in field practice to provide a 30% safety margin against PDOP spikes and minor cycle slips. **(b) Expected horizontal accuracy** $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_H &= a + b \cdot D \\ &= 5 \text{ mm} + 1 \text{ ppm} \times 12 \text{ km} \\ &= 5 \text{ mm} + 1 \times 12 \text{ mm} \\ &= 17 \text{ mm} \\ &\approx 1.7 \text{ cm} \end{aligned}$$ The result **17 mm** comfortably meets the cadastral and engineering-control grade. ### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Rapid Static | Project Condition | Recommended Method | Justification | |---|---|---| | Baseline $\le 5$ km, $\ge 4$ sats | **Rapid Static** (5 min) | Short baseline, fast ambiguity fix | | Baseline 5–20 km, dual-freq, $\ge 5$ sats | **Rapid Static** (10–20 min) | Iono-free combination handles decorrelation | | Baseline 20–50 km, dual-freq, long obs | **Conventional Static** (1–2 hr) | Iono decorrelation exceeds OTF capacity | | Kinematic / continuous trajectory | **Stop-and-Go Kinematic / RTK** | Receiver must remain locked | | Real-time positioning required | **RTK** with data link | Rapid Static is post-processed | | Long baselines $>$ 50 km | **Precise Point Positioning (PPP)** or **Static** | Different processing model required | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Rapid Static — End-to-End Field & Office Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Mission Planning
Sky plot, PDOP, satellite almanac"] --> B["Reconnaissance
Site visit, obstruction check"] B --> C["Reference Station Setup
Tripod, tribrach, dual-freq receiver"] B --> D["Rover Station Setup
Tripod, tribrach, dual-freq receiver"] C --> E["Power On + Log Static Data
5 to 20 minutes simultaneous"] D --> E E --> F["Field Logbook Entry
Antenna height, start/end time"] F --> G["Data Download
RINEX conversion"] G --> H["Baseline Processing
OTF ambiguity resolution"] H --> I{"Ambiguity Fixed?"} I -- "No" --> J["Increase session time
Reobserve"] I -- "Yes" --> K["Quality Check
RMS, ratio test, residuals"] K --> L["Network Adjustment
Least squares"] L --> M["Final Coordinates
ITRF / WGS84 / Local grid"] ``` ### 4.2 Comparison of GPS Observation Methods (Static Family) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FAM["GPS Observation Family"] S["Conventional Static
1 to 4 hours
Any baseline"] RS["Rapid Static
5 to 20 minutes
Less than 30 km"] SG["Stop and Go Kinematic
Per epoch
Less than 10 km"] RTK["Real Time Kinematic
Per epoch
Less than 15 km"] end S -->|"Improved receiver
and algorithms"| RS RS -->|"Add continuous
lock, no init"| SG SG -->|"Add real-time
data link"| RTK ``` ### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Signal Processing Chain ```mermaid flowchart TD L1["L1 Carrier 1575 MHz
C/A and P code, phase"] --> MIX["Linear Combinations
Wide lane, Narrow lane, Iono free"] L2["L2 Carrier 1228 MHz
P code, phase"] --> MIX MIX --> DD["Double Differencing
Receiver and satellite differenced"] DD --> OTF["On The Fly Ambiguity
Resolution, LAMBDA algorithm"] OTF --> ADJ["Least Squares Network
Adjustment"] ADJ --> OUT["3D Coordinates
Centimetre grade accuracy"] ``` ### 4.4 Decision Logic — Selecting the Right Occupation Time ```mermaid flowchart TD P1{"Baseline length D?"} P1 -- "D less than 5 km" --> T1["Occupation 5 to 8 min
4 sats minimum"] P1 -- "5 to 15 km" --> T2["Occupation 10 to 15 min
5 sats, PDOP less than 4"] P1 -- "15 to 30 km" --> T3["Occupation 15 to 20 min
6 sats, PDOP less than 3"] P1 -- "Above 30 km" --> T4["Use Conventional Static
or PPP method"] T1 --> CHK["Check iono, multipath, obstructions"] T2 --> CHK T3 --> CHK T4 --> CHK ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define *Rapid Static GPS surveying*. Mention any two salient features that distinguish it from conventional static GPS surveying. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Rapid Static GPS surveying is a satellite-based positioning technique in which dual-frequency GNSS receivers simultaneously observe carrier-phase data for a short occupation period of **5 to 20 minutes** on a short baseline (typically **< 20 km**), with subsequent On-The-Fly (OTF) integer ambiguity resolution, achieving centimetre-level accuracy of the order of **5 mm + 1 ppm**. **Salient features (2 × 1 mark = 2 marks):** 1. Requires **dual-frequency (L1 + L2)** receivers to form iono-free linear combination — single-frequency receivers are *not* suitable. 2. Observation time is reduced to **5–20 minutes** per session (versus 1–4 hours for conventional static) by exploiting the **wide-lane (86.25 cm) combination** for rapid ambiguity search. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List the **major requirements** for a successful Rapid Static GPS survey and state the typical accuracy achievable. **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Hardware:** Dual-frequency GNSS receivers at both reference and rover stations. *(1 mark)* 2. **Satellite geometry:** Minimum **5 satellites** with **PDOP $\le$ 5** (ideally $\le 3$) and good sky visibility (obstruction angle $> 15°$). *(1 mark)* 3. **Typical accuracy:** $\sigma \approx 5\text{ mm} + 1\text{ ppm}$ of baseline length horizontally and $\approx 10\text{ mm} + 1\text{ ppm}$ vertically, i.e., **centimetre-grade**. *(1 mark)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]* **(a)** Explain the **principle, field procedure, and data processing** of Rapid Static GPS surveying with the help of a neat flowchart. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **1. Principle (3 marks):** Rapid Static exploits the fact that the integer carrier-phase ambiguities $N$ can be resolved in minutes (rather than hours) by: - Forming the **ionosphere-free linear combination** $L_{IF}$ to remove first-order ionospheric delay. - Forming the **wide-lane combination** with $\lambda_W = 86.25$ cm, which makes the integer ambiguity search space geometrically larger and therefore statistically faster. - Applying the **OTF (On-The-Fly) algorithm**, typically based on the **LAMBDA** (Least-squares AMBiguity Decorrelation Adjustment) method. The receiver essentially solves for: $$\nabla \Delta N_W \quad \text{(wide-lane integer)} \;\rightarrow\; \nabla \Delta N_1, \nabla \Delta N_2 \quad \text{(L1 and L2 integers)}$$ in real time on the collected data, then confirms the solution via the **ratio test** (typically ratio $> 3$). **2. Field Procedure (2 marks):** 1. Conduct **mission planning** (almanac download, satellite visibility, PDOP prediction, baseline design). 2. Set up the **reference receiver** on a known control point with precisely measured antenna height. 3. Set up the **rover receiver** on the unknown station with the same antenna-height discipline. 4. Power on and record L1 + L2 phase data simultaneously for **5 to 20 minutes**, depending on baseline length. 5. Log the start time, end time, antenna height, and any obstructions in the field logbook. 6. Download data in **RINEX** format for office processing. **3. Data Processing (2 marks):** 1. Import RINEX files of reference and rover into the baseline processor. 2. Define baseline, apply precise ephemeris (if available), set elevation mask ($> 15°$), and tropospheric model (e.g., Hopfield or Saastamoinen). 3. Run OTF ambiguity resolution — fix the wide-lane, then narrow-lane, then iono-free. 4. Verify the **ratio test** ($\ge 3$) and check **RMS** of residuals. 5. Repeat for all baselines and perform a **least-squares network adjustment** to obtain final coordinates in WGS-84 / ITRF / local grid. --- **(b)** What are the **advantages and limitations** of the Rapid Static method? Discuss its **accuracy standards** with a suitable numerical illustration. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **1. Advantages (2 marks):** - **Speed:** Observation time reduced to 5–20 minutes (5–10× faster than conventional static) → higher productivity. - **Centimetre accuracy:** Comparable to conventional static for short baselines. - **Single surveyor operation:** Only one operator and one receiver are needed at the rover; suitable for densification surveys. - **Less stringent observation window:** Can be scheduled in smaller time blocks. - **Economical:** Reduces field labour and instrument-hire costs. **2. Limitations (2 marks):** - **Baseline length restricted** to $<$ 20–30 km due to ionospheric decorrelation. - **Dual-frequency receiver mandatory** — equipment cost is higher than for code-based DGPS. - **Post-processing required** — not real-time. - **Susceptible to cycle slips** if the antenna loses lock during the short observation (no time to re-acquire). - **Poor performance under heavy canopy** or in urban canyons due to multipath and signal obstruction. **3. Accuracy Standards with Numerical Illustration (3 marks):** The standard accuracy specification for Rapid Static is: $$\sigma = a + b \cdot D$$ with $a = 5$ mm, $b = 1$ ppm (horizontal). **Illustration:** For a baseline of $D = 15$ km: $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_H &= 5 \text{ mm} + 1 \text{ ppm} \times 15 \text{ km} \\ &= 5 \text{ mm} + 15 \text{ mm} \\ &= 20 \text{ mm} = 2 \text{ cm} \end{aligned}$$ $$\sigma_V \approx 2 \sigma_H = 4 \text{ cm}$$ This is well within the KTU-recommended precision for **secondary control points** in cadastral and engineering surveys. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]* **(a)** Discuss in detail the **observation planning procedure** for a Rapid Static GPS survey. What is the role of *mission-planning software*? *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **1. Why Planning is Critical (1 mark):** Because the observation window is short (5–20 min), every second of data must be *useful*. A single PDOP spike or loss of lock can compromise the entire session. Planning therefore aims to schedule the session when satellite geometry is optimal. **2. Planning Procedure (4 marks):** - **Step 1 — Almanac Download:** Obtain the broadcast almanac of all GNSS satellites valid for the survey day. - **Step 2 — Location Input:** Enter the approximate latitude, longitude, and elevation of the project area. - **Step 3 — Sky-Plot & Satellite Visibility:** Generate a 24-hour sky plot showing azimuth and elevation of each visible satellite. Identify windows with $\ge 5$ satellites above $15°$ elevation. - **Step 4 — PDOP / GDOP Prediction:** Select observation windows with **PDOP $\le 3$** (best) to 5 (acceptable). Avoid windows with PDOP $>$ 6. - **Step 5 — Baseline Network Design:** Pre-plan the sequence of baseline observations to minimise travel time, optimise common-sky overlap, and ensure geometric strength of the network. - **Step 6 — Receiver & Battery Check:** Verify dual-frequency receivers, charged batteries, plumb-bob, tape for antenna height, logbook, and RINEX-compatible memory. **3. Role of Mission-Planning Software (2 marks):** Tools like *Trimble Planning*, *Leica Geo Office*, *Topcon Tools*, or *Ashtech Solutions* automate the above steps. They produce **sky plots**, **PDOP vs. time graphs**, **number-of-satellite tables**, and **optimal-occupation-window recommendations**. They also flag satellite outages, predicted ionospheric storms, and obstructions caused by terrain masks imported from DEMs. --- **(b)** Compare **Rapid Static, Stop-and-Go Kinematic, and Real-Time Kinematic (RTK)** GPS methods in a tabular form, and state the **best-suited engineering application** of each. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** | Feature | Rapid Static | Stop-and-Go Kinematic | Real-Time Kinematic (RTK) | |---|---|---|---| | **Observation time per point** | 5 – 20 min | Few seconds (after init) | Few seconds (real-time) | | **Baseline length** | < 20 – 30 km | < 10 km | < 10 – 15 km | | **Receiver requirement** | Dual-frequency | Dual-frequency + lock maintenance | Dual-frequency + data link | | **Ambiguity resolution** | OTF, post-processed | OTF, requires init at known baseline | OTF, real-time in field | | **Accuracy** | 5 mm + 1 ppm | 10 – 20 mm + 1–2 ppm | 10 mm + 1–2 ppm | | **Real-time?** | No (post-process) | No (post-process) | Yes | | **Productivity** | Moderate | High | Very high | | **Major limitation** | Iono decorrelation, cycle slips | Lock loss fatal between stops | Data-link range, latency | | **Best-suited application** | Cadastral / engineering control densification, deformation baselines | Topographic detail surveys, GIS data capture | Construction setting-out, machine control, hydrographic surveys | **Application Conclusion (1 mark):** - **Rapid Static** → control-point densification, cadastral networks, short-baseline deformation monitoring. - **Stop-and-Go Kinematic** → topographic feature mapping, GIS asset inventory. - **RTK** → real-time setting-out, earthworks, hydrography. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Read Carefully Before Writing Your Exam** > > 1. **Do not confuse Rapid Static with Stop-and-Go Kinematic** — the former is *static* (no movement during occupation), the latter requires the receiver to *move* between stop points. Examiners frequently set a single-line question to catch this. *(Typical loss: 2 marks)* > 2. **Always specify "dual-frequency receiver"** when writing about Rapid Static. Writing only "GPS receiver" loses 1 mark. > 3. **Mention the OTF ambiguity resolution algorithm** by name if possible (e.g., LAMBDA) — it is a high-value key phrase. > 4. **For accuracy questions, write the formula $\sigma = a + bD$ in full and substitute values** — partial marks are awarded only when the numerical step is shown. > 5. **Do not state that Rapid Static is real-time** — it is *post-processed*. This is a classic trick question. > 6. **For occupation-time questions, link time to baseline length** — stating only "5–20 min" without justification is considered incomplete. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Read in the Last 10 Minutes Before the Exam)** > > ✅ **Rapid Static = Fast Static GPS** for short baselines, post-processed, dual-frequency, centimetre accuracy. > ✅ **Observation time = 5 to 20 minutes** (function of baseline length, satellite count, PDOP, iono-activity). > ✅ **Baseline length limit ≈ 20 km** (operational up to 30 km in low-iono conditions). > ✅ **Dual-frequency receiver mandatory** to form the **ionosphere-free linear combination**. > ✅ **Wide-lane wavelength $\lambda_W = 86.25$ cm** — the *key* to fast ambiguity resolution. > ✅ **Narrow-lane wavelength $\lambda_N = 10.7$ cm** — used to refine the wide-lane fix. > ✅ **OTF (On-The-Fly) ambiguity resolution** — typically LAMBDA algorithm — is the mathematical engine. > ✅ **Accuracy = $a + b \cdot D$** with $a = 5$ mm, $b = 1$ ppm horizontal; vertical accuracy $\approx 2 \times$ horizontal. > ✅ **Minimum 5 satellites**, **PDOP $\le 5$** (preferably $\le 3$), **elevation mask $\ge 15°$**. > ✅ **Not real-time** — Rapid Static is a post-processing technique; for real-time use RTK. > ✅ **Applications:** cadastral control, topographic densification, deformation monitoring, UAV/LiDAR ground control, boundary demarcation. > ✅ **Compared to conventional static:** 5–10× faster, slightly lower accuracy, shorter allowable baseline. > ✅ **Compared to Stop-and-Go Kinematic:** receiver is *static* during each occupation; movement is *not* allowed. > ✅ **Field logbook must record** antenna height (to mm), start time, end time, observer name, station ID, obstructions. > ✅ **Data download format:** RINEX (Receiver Independent Exchange) — universally accepted by all baseline processors. > ✅ **Quality checks:** ratio test $\ge 3$, RMS residuals low, fixed ambiguities (not float), PDOP < 5.

topics.title: Kinematic Methods - DGPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET304 - SURVEYING AND GEOMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Kinematic Methods - DGPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**DGPS (Differential Global Positioning System)** is an enhancement to standard GPS that uses a network of fixed, ground-based reference stations (Base Stations) to broadcast the difference between the pseudo-ranges indicated by the GPS satellite signals and the known, precisely surveyed fixed positions of those base stations. These differential corrections are then applied to the GPS receiver (Rover) to significantly improve the positional accuracy (typically from ±10 m to ±1–3 m for code-based DGPS, and up to **±(8 mm + 1 ppm)** for carrier-phase based RTK).

**Kinematic GPS/GNSS Surveying** is a relative positioning technique in which one receiver remains stationary over a known control point (Base) while the second receiver (Rover) traverses the points to be surveyed, either continuously in motion or in a "Stop & Go" fashion. The method exploits the **carrier-phase observable** rather than the code (pseudorange), achieving centimeter-level positional precision in real-time (RTK) or post-processed modes.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme)**
> Under Module 3, students must clearly distinguish between:
> 1. **Static / Rapid Static / Kinematic / Stop-and-Go / RTK / PPK** modes
> 2. **Code-based DGPS** vs **Carrier-phase based DGPS**
> 3. The role of **double-differencing** and **ambiguity resolution**

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you and a friend are both trying to estimate the weight of a cake using a slightly inaccurate kitchen scale:
- Your scale reads **1020 g**; the correct weight is **1000 g** → error = **+20 g**
- Your friend uses the same scale at the same moment and reads **520 g** (true weight **500 g**) → error = **+20 g**

Instead of trusting the scale's reading, you **both subtract the +20 g bias**. This is the essence of DGPS: the **Base Station (friend)** knows the "true answer" and broadcasts the **correction (–20 g)** to the **Rover (you)**, eliminating common-mode errors (satellite clock, ionosphere, troposphere, ephemeris).

For **Kinematic** mode, imagine filming a moving car with two synchronized cameras. By tracking the same satellite "stars" from two different vantage points (one fixed, one moving), and precisely timing their arrival, you can compute the car's position frame-by-frame at **cm-level accuracy**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** DGPS removes **correlated errors** common to both Base and Rover. Kinematic methods exploit the **millimeter-wavelength carrier signal** instead of the coarse C/A code, achieving orders-of-magnitude better precision.

### 1.3 Classification of Kinematic GNSS Surveying

| Mode | Observation Time | Accuracy | Real-Time? |
|------|------------------|----------|------------|
| **Static** | 1–3 hours | **±(5 mm + 1 ppm)** | No |
| **Rapid Static** | 5–20 min | **±(10 mm + 1 ppm)** | No |
| **Kinematic (PPK)** | Continuous (5+ s/epoch) | **±(20 mm + 1 ppm)** | No |
| **Stop & Go** | Few min/point | **±(10–20 mm + 1 ppm)** | No |
| **RTK (Real-Time Kinematic)** | < 1 s/epoch | **±(8 mm + 1 ppm)** | Yes |

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light:** $c = 299\,792\,458$ **m/s** (exactly, by SI definition)
- **L1 carrier frequency:** $f_{L1} = 1575.42$ **MHz** → wavelength $\lambda_{L1} \approx 19.03$ **cm**
- **L2 carrier frequency:** $f_{L2} = 1227.60$ **MHz** → wavelength $\lambda_{L2} \approx 24.42$ **cm**
- **C/A code chip length:** $\approx 293$ **m** (coarse acquisition code)
- **Standard positional accuracy (uncorrected GPS):** $\pm 10$ **m** (95 % confidence)
- **DGPS typical accuracy:** $\pm 1$ **m** to $\pm 3$ **m**
- **RTK typical accuracy:** $\pm 8$ **mm** + **1 ppm** of baseline length

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle of DGPS

The fundamental assumption of DGPS is that **two receivers (Base + Rover), located within tens of kilometres, experience nearly identical atmospheric and satellite-induced errors** because the signals travel through essentially the same column of the ionosphere and troposphere.

**Operational Steps:**
- The **Base Receiver** is set up over a point whose geodetic coordinates are known to mm-accuracy.
- It computes its position using the same satellite signals that the Rover receives.
- The difference between the **known coordinates** and the **GPS-derived coordinates** yields the **pseudo-range correction (PRC)** and **range-rate correction (RRC)**.
- These corrections are transmitted (via UHF/VHF radio, cellular, or satellite link) to the Rover in real time, or stored for **post-processing**.

### 2.2 Operational Principle of Kinematic Surveying

The carrier-phase observable is many orders of magnitude more precise than the C/A code (since the wavelength is ~19 cm vs 293 m for the code chip). Kinematic surveying uses this carrier-phase measurement continuously as the Rover moves.

**Logic Flow (Why and How):**

- **Why carrier phase?** Because the **phase measurement precision** is typically **0.5–2 mm** of the wavelength, while the code measurement precision is only **1–3 m**.
- **How is position found?** By **differencing** the phase observations between satellites (between-receiver single difference) and between epochs (between-satellite single difference) to cancel out hardware biases, then solving for the **baseline vector** $\Delta X, \Delta Y, \Delta Z$.
- **What about ambiguities?** The integer number of cycles between satellite and receiver (the **integer ambiguity $N$**) must be resolved to obtain the exact geometric range.

### 2.3 Sources of Error in DGPS / Kinematic Methods

| Error Source | Magnitude (1σ) | Mitigation |
|--------------|----------------|------------|
| Satellite clock | **±2 m** | Differential correction |
| Ephemeris | **±2.5 m** | Differential correction |
| Ionospheric delay | **±5 m** (vertical) | Double frequency / DGPS |
| Tropospheric delay | **±0.5–2 m** | Model (Saastamoinen / Hopfield) + DGPS |
| Multipath | **±0.5–1 m** (code) | Choke-ring antenna, site selection |
| Receiver noise | **±0.5 m** (code) | Carrier phase, longer integration |
| Selective Availability (legacy) | **±30 m** | Turned off in 2000 — historically important |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU 2024:** Selective Availability (SA) was disabled by the U.S. government on **1 May 2000**. Mention this historically, but current exam questions typically focus on the **ionospheric delay (most significant residual error)**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|--------|---------|----------------------|------|
| $\rho$ | Geometric range from satellite to receiver | $\rho = c \cdot \Delta t$ | m |
| $P$ | Pseudorange (code measurement) | $P = \rho + c(\delta t_r - \delta t^s) + I + T + \varepsilon_P$ | m |
| $\Phi$ | Carrier-phase measurement (cycles) | $\Phi = \frac{\rho + c(\delta t_r - \delta t^s) - I + T + \lambda N}{\lambda}$ | cycles |
| $N$ | Integer ambiguity | Unknown integer, must be resolved | cycles |
| $\lambda$ | Carrier wavelength | $\lambda = c / f$ | m |
| $\Delta X, \Delta Y, \Delta Z$ | Baseline vector components | From double-differenced phase equations | m |
| $PRC$ | Pseudo-Range Correction | $PRC = \rho_{true} - \rho_{observed}$ | m |
| $RRC$ | Range-Rate Correction | $RRC = d(PRC)/dt$ | m/s |
| $PDOP$ | Position Dilution of Precision | $PDOP = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2} \,/\, \sigma_\rho$ | dimensionless |
| $GDOP$ | Geometric Dilution of Precision | $GDOP^2 = PDOP^2 + TDOP^2$ | dimensionless |
| $\sigma$ | 1σ positional accuracy (RTK) | $\sigma = \pm (8 \text{ mm} + 1 \text{ ppm} \cdot b)$ | mm |
| $b$ | Baseline length (Base → Rover) | Distance between receivers | km |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** "**PIGS**" — Pseudorange **I**onospheric **G**ain **S**econd-order delay adds (ionospheric delay **adds** to pseudorange but **subtracts** from carrier phase — note the sign change in the formula table above).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

DGPS and kinematic GNSS methods are indispensable in:
- **Cadastral and boundary surveying** (high-precision boundary marking)
- **Hydrographic surveying** (positioning of echo-sounder boats)
- **Route surveying** for highways, railways, canals, and pipelines
- **Machine control and guidance** in earthworks, paving, and mining (GNSS-guided dozers and pavers)
- **GIS data collection** for utility mapping
- **Photogrammetric ground control** for aerial/UAV surveys
- **Structural deformation monitoring** of dams, bridges, and high-rise buildings

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Geometric Range from Time-of-Flight

The fundamental GPS observable is the time it takes for a signal to travel from the satellite to the receiver. Multiplying this time by the speed of light gives the **geometric (slant) range**.

**Step 1 —** Define the time delay $\Delta t$ between transmission at the satellite antenna and reception at the receiver antenna.

$$\Delta t = t_r - t^s$$

where $t_r$ is the reception time (receiver clock) and $t^s$ is the transmission time (satellite clock).

**Step 2 —** Multiply by the speed of light to get the range:

$$\rho = c \cdot \Delta t = c \cdot (t_r - t^s)$$

**Step 3 —** However, the receiver clock is not perfectly synchronised with GPS time. Let $\delta t_r$ and $\delta t^s$ be the clock offsets. The *measured* pseudorange $P$ therefore includes these biases:

$$P = c \cdot \Delta t = c \cdot (t_r + \delta t_r - t^s - \delta t^s)$$

**Step 4 —** Separating the true range from the bias terms:

$$P = \rho + c \cdot (\delta t_r - \delta t^s)$$

**Step 5 —** Adding atmospheric delays (ionospheric $I$, tropospheric $T$) and residual noise $\varepsilon_P$:

$$P = \rho + c \cdot (\delta t_r - \delta t^s) + I + T + \varepsilon_P$$

This is the **fundamental pseudorange observation equation** — a must-know for KTU.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Carrier-Phase Observable

The carrier-phase measurement is the difference in phase (in cycles) between the incoming satellite carrier wave and a reference replica generated inside the receiver.

**Step 1 —** Let $\phi_r$ be the phase of the receiver replica and $\phi^s$ be the phase received from the satellite at the same instant.

$$\Phi = \phi_r - \phi^s$$

**Step 2 —** The phase difference is proportional to the geometric range, but only modulo $2\pi$. We must add an unknown integer number of cycles $N$ to recover the full range:

$$\Phi = \frac{\rho}{\lambda} + N$$

**Step 3 —** Include clock biases, atmospheric errors, and noise:

$$\Phi = \frac{\rho + c(\delta t_r - \delta t^s) - I + T + \lambda N}{\lambda} + \varepsilon_\Phi$$

**Step 4 —** Multiply both sides by $\lambda$ to express in metres:

$$\Phi \lambda = \rho + c(\delta t_r - \delta t^s) - I + T + \lambda N + \lambda \varepsilon_\Phi$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Check:** The ionospheric delay **adds** to the code pseudorange but **subtracts** from the carrier phase (because ionospheric delay is a phase advance in cycles, equivalent to a code group delay). Examiners specifically test this sign convention — get it wrong, lose 2 marks.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Baseline Vector from Double-Differenced Carrier Phase

The basis of all high-precision GNSS relative positioning. We start with two single differences and then double-differ.

**Step 1 — Single difference between receivers** (Base $b$ and Rover $r$), looking at the same satellite $i$:

$$\Delta \Phi_{br}^{i} = \Phi_r^{i} - \Phi_b^{i} = \frac{1}{\lambda} \left[ (\rho_r^{i} - \rho_b^{i}) + c(\delta t_r - \delta t_b) \right] + (N_r^{i} - N_b^{i})$$

The satellite clock and atmospheric terms (assuming short baseline) cancel out.

**Step 2 — Single difference between satellites** ($i$ and $j$), same receiver $r$:

$$\Delta \Phi_r^{ij} = \Phi_r^{j} - \Phi_r^{i} = \frac{1}{\lambda} \left[ (\rho_r^{j} - \rho_r^{i}) + c(\delta t^s_j - \delta t^s_i) \right] + (N_r^{j} - N_r^{i})$$

The receiver clock cancels out.

**Step 3 — Double difference** (combining both single differences):

$$\nabla \Delta \Phi_{br}^{ij} = \Delta \Phi_{br}^{j} - \Delta \Phi_{br}^{i}$$

$$\nabla \Delta \Phi_{br}^{ij} = \frac{1}{\lambda} \left[ (\rho_r^{j} - \rho_b^{j}) - (\rho_r^{i} - \rho_b^{i}) \right] + (N_r^{j} - N_b^{j}) - (N_r^{i} - N_b^{i})$$

**Step 4 —** Define the double-differenced ambiguity $\nabla \Delta N_{br}^{ij}$ (a single integer):

$$\nabla \Delta \Phi_{br}^{ij} = \frac{1}{\lambda} \left[ (\rho_b^{j} - \rho_b^{i}) - (\rho_r^{j} - \rho_r^{i}) \right] + \nabla \Delta N_{br}^{ij}$$

**Step 5 —** Each $\rho$ is a function of the unknown receiver position $(X_r, Y_r, Z_r)$ and the known satellite coordinates. Linearising with respect to the unknown baseline $(\Delta X, \Delta Y, \Delta Z)$:

$$\nabla \Delta \Phi_{br}^{ij} = \frac{1}{\lambda} \left[ \Delta k_{br}^{ij} \cdot \Delta X + \Delta l_{br}^{ij} \cdot \Delta Y + \Delta m_{br}^{ij} \cdot \Delta Z \right] + \nabla \Delta N_{br}^{ij}$$

where $\Delta k, \Delta l, \Delta m$ are differenced direction cosines. Stacking equations from all satellite pairs gives the **design matrix** $A$ and the **observation vector** $\Phi$, solved via least-squares:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta X \\ \Delta Y \\ \Delta Z \\ \nabla \Delta N \end{bmatrix} = (A^T P A)^{-1} A^T P \, \Phi$$

This is the **heart of every kinematic GNSS processor** (Leica Geo Office, Trimble Business Center, RTKLIB, etc.).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Position Accuracy (RTK and DGPS) from Baseline Length

The standard KTU/RTK accuracy specification is:

$$\sigma = \sigma_0 + p \cdot b$$

where:
- $\sigma_0$ = constant (e.g., 8 mm horizontal, 15 mm vertical for RTK)
- $p$ = **ppm coefficient** (e.g., 1 ppm = 1 mm per km of baseline)
- $b$ = baseline length in **km**

**Example (KTU typical):** For a 5 km baseline in RTK:
- Horizontal: $\sigma_H = 8 \text{ mm} + 1 \cdot 5 = 13 \text{ mm}$
- Vertical: $\sigma_V = 15 \text{ mm} + 1 \cdot 5 = 20 \text{ mm}$

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Identify baseline $b = 5$ km.
2. Insert into horizontal formula: $\sigma_H = 8 + 1 \times 5 = 13$ mm.
3. Insert into vertical formula: $\sigma_V = 15 + 1 \times 5 = 20$ mm.
4. Report as $\pm 13$ mm horizontal, $\pm 20$ mm vertical at 95 % confidence (multiply by 1.96 if required for 2σ).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Survey Modes (for KTU 2-mark questions)

| Parameter | Static | Rapid Static | Kinematic (PPK) | RTK |
|-----------|--------|--------------|------------------|-----|
| Baseline length | 1–1000 km | < 20 km | < 20 km | < 10–15 km |
| Observation time | 1–3 h | 5–20 min | 5 s/epoch | < 1 s |
| Accuracy (H) | 5 mm + 1 ppm | 10 mm + 1 ppm | 20 mm + 1 ppm | 8 mm + 1 ppm |
| Real-time? | No | No | No | Yes |
| Productivity | Lowest | Medium | High | Highest |
| Initialisation required? | No | No | Yes (5+ min) | Yes (OTF) |
| Typical use | Geodetic control | Cadastral | Topographic | Engineering stakeout |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DGPS System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sat1[SATELLITE 1] -->|L1 L2 signals| baseAntenna[BASE STATION ANTENNA]
    sat2[SATELLITE 2] -->|L1 L2 signals| baseAntenna
    sat3[SATELLITE 3] -->|L1 L2 signals| baseAntenna
    sat4[SATELLITE 4] -->|L1 L2 signals| baseAntenna
    sat1 -->|L1 L2 signals| roverAntenna[ROVER ANTENNA]
    sat2 -->|L1 L2 signals| roverAntenna
    sat3 -->|L1 L2 signals| roverAntenna
    sat4 -->|L1 L2 signals| roverAntenna
    baseAntenna --> baseRx[BASE RECEIVER]
    baseRx -->|computes PRC RRC| commLink[COMMUNICATION LINK RADIO OR CELLULAR]
    commLink -->|corrections broadcast| roverRx[ROVER RECEIVER]
    roverAntenna --> roverRx
    roverRx -->|applies corrections| correctedPos[CORRECTED POSITION CM TO M LEVEL ACCURACY]
    baseRx -->|known coordinates from| ctrlPoint[GEODETIC CONTROL POINT]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The Base Station sits on a **known control point** (its true coordinates are pre-loaded).
- It receives the same four (or more) satellites as the Rover.
- The Base computes a **PRC (Pseudo-Range Correction)** and transmits it via radio/cellular.
- The Rover applies this correction in real time to its own pseudorange measurements.

---

### 4.2 Kinematic Survey Operational Workflow (Stop & Go / RTK)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[START BASE STATION SETUP OVER KNOWN POINT] --> baseConfig[CONFIGURE BASE RECEIVER 10 SEC OBSERVATION]
    baseConfig --> checkObs[CHECK MINIMUM 5 SATELLITES AND PDOP LESS THAN 6]
    checkObs --> init[INITIALISE KINEMATIC SURVEY 5 MIN OTF AMBIGUITY RESOLUTION]
    init --> traverseMode[TRAVERSE WITH ROVER STOP AT CONTROL POINTS 5 TO 10 SECONDS]
    traverseMode --> dataRec[RECORD DATA AT EACH POINT]
    dataRec --> checkMore{MORE POINTS TO SURVEY}
    checkMore -->|YES| traverseMode
    checkMore -->|NO| download[DOWNLOAD DATA FROM ROVER]
    download --> postProcess[POST PROCESS WITH BASELINE SOFTWARE OR REAL TIME OUTPUT]
    postProcess --> finalCoords[FINAL COORDINATES CM LEVEL ACCURACY]
    finalCoords --> report[GENERATE SURVEY REPORT WITH QUALITY INDICATORS]
```

**Reading the workflow:**
- **Initialisation (Init)** is the critical step where integer ambiguities are resolved using **OTF (On-The-Fly)** techniques.
- Once initialised, the Rover can lose lock for a few seconds and re-acquire quickly.
- For **Stop & Go**, the user stops for 5–10 seconds at each point — yielding cm accuracy.
- For **RTK**, corrections are streamed in real time and the surveyor sees the coordinates live.

---

### 4.3 Error Budget and Correction Strategy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    satErr[SATELLITE CLOCK AND EPHEMERIS ERRORS] --> diffCorr[DIFFERENTIAL CORRECTION BASE TO ROVER]
    ionoErr[IONOSPHERIC DELAY] --> diffCorr
    ionoErr --> dualFreq[DUAL FREQUENCY L1 L2 IONO FREE COMBINATION]
    tropoErr[TROPOSPHERIC DELAY] --> diffCorr
    tropoErr --> model[SAASTAMOINEN OR HOPFIELD MODEL]
    multiPath[MULTIPATH] --> antenna[CHOKE RING OR GROUND PLANE ANTENNA]
    multiPath --> siteSel[SITE SELECTION AVOID REFLECTIVE SURFACES]
    rxNoise[RECEIVER NOISE] --> carrier[USE CARRIER PHASE MEASUREMENT]
    diffCorr --> finalAcc[HIGH ACCURACY POSITION]
    dualFreq --> finalAcc
    model --> finalAcc
    antenna --> finalAcc
    siteSel --> finalAcc
    carrier --> finalAcc
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each error source has a **dedicated mitigation strategy**.
- **Differential correction** is the single most powerful technique — it cancels all spatially correlated errors (satellite clock, ephemeris, ionosphere, troposphere) for short baselines.
- **Dual-frequency receivers** are essential for baselines > 10 km where ionospheric decorrelation becomes significant.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model QP]

**Differentiate between Static GPS surveying and Kinematic GPS surveying. Mention the typical accuracy achieved in each method.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Static GPS | Kinematic GPS |
|---------|-----------|---------------|
| Receiver movement | Both receivers stationary | One (Rover) moves continuously |
| Observation time | Long (1–3 hours) | Short (5 s/epoch, continuous) |
| Accuracy (H) | **±(5 mm + 1 ppm)** | **±(20 mm + 1 ppm)** for PPK, **±(8 mm + 1 ppm)** for RTK |
| Productivity | Low | High |
| Initialisation needed? | No | Yes (ambiguity resolution required) |
| Applications | Geodetic control, deformation monitoring | Topographic mapping, cadastral, GIS |

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for accuracy, 1 mark for application)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model QP]

**What is a Pseudo-Range Correction (PRC) in DGPS? How is it computed and applied to the Rover?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Pseudo-Range Correction (PRC)** is the difference between the geometric (true) range from a satellite to the Base station and the measured pseudorange (which contains clock and atmospheric errors).

**Computation at the Base Station:**

$$PRC^{(i)} = \rho_{true}^{(i)} - P_{measured}^{(i)}$$

where $\rho_{true}^{(i)}$ is computed from the known Base coordinates and the broadcast satellite ephemeris, and $P_{measured}^{(i)}$ is the pseudorange actually measured by the Base receiver.

**Application at the Rover:**

$$P_{corrected}^{(i)} = P_{rover}^{(i)} + PRC^{(i)}$$

The corrected pseudorange is then used in the position solution, yielding **sub-metre to metre-level accuracy** (down from **±10 m** for standalone GPS).

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for computation, 1 mark for application)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall**
> 1. **Sign convention of ionospheric delay:** In the pseudorange equation, ionospheric delay **adds** (+I), but in the carrier-phase equation it **subtracts** (−I). Students frequently lose 2 marks by using the wrong sign.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse code-based DGPS (metre accuracy) with carrier-phase DGPS / RTK (centimetre accuracy).** Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Selective Availability (SA)** is **disabled** since **1 May 2000** — do NOT include SA in the current error budget.
> 4. Always state the **baseline length** when quoting accuracy (e.g., ±8 mm + 1 ppm — the 1 ppm term scales with distance).
> 5. In the **double-differencing** derivation, students often write the formula but forget to define each differenced term; expect 1–2 marks lost for missing definitions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Kinematic GPS Methods** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]

**(a)** Explain the different kinematic GPS surveying methods with neat sketches. Discuss the operational procedure, time required, accuracy, and field applications of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the concept of **On-The-Fly (OTF) ambiguity resolution** in RTK surveying. Why is it essential for kinematic operations? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The following kinematic GPS methods are used in engineering surveying:

1. **Real-Time Kinematic (RTK)**
   - A radio/GSM data link transmits carrier-phase corrections from the Base to the Rover in real time.
   - Initialisation: 5–10 minutes for ambiguity resolution (OTF).
   - Accuracy: **±(8 mm + 1 ppm) horizontal**, **±(15 mm + 1 ppm) vertical**.
   - Applications: **Engineering stakeout, topographic mapping, hydrographic surveying, machine control.**

2. **Post-Processed Kinematic (PPK)**
   - Data is logged by both Base and Rover, but processed in the office later.
   - Useful when real-time radio coverage is unavailable.
   - Accuracy: **±(20 mm + 1 ppm) horizontal**.

3. **Stop-and-Go Kinematic**
   - The Rover moves between control points and stops for 5–10 seconds at each.
   - Combines the precision of static with the productivity of kinematic.
   - Accuracy: **±(10–20 mm + 1 ppm)**.

4. **Continuous Kinematic (Traverse mode)**
   - The Rover moves continuously, recording positions at 1 Hz (or higher).
   - Used for **route corridor mapping, GIS data collection, hydrographic profiles.**

**Operational Procedure (common to all):**
- Set up Base over a known control point → power on → log data.
- Initialise Rover (OTF or known-point method) → traverse points.
- Record data, return to Base for data download.
- Post-process or output in real time.

*(7 marks: 1 mark definition, 3 marks for explaining 3–4 methods, 1 mark accuracy table, 2 marks procedure and applications)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**On-The-Fly (OTF) Ambiguity Resolution** is a technique to determine the integer number of carrier cycles $N$ between the satellite and the receiver **while the Rover is in motion** — without requiring the Rover to be stationary for an extended period.

**Why it is essential:**
In kinematic mode, the Rover cannot remain still for the long observation (5–20 min) required for traditional ambiguity resolution. OTF resolves $N$ in **seconds** using:
- **Extra-widelaning (EWL)** and **widelaning (WL)** combinations of L1 and L2 phases
- **Least-squares ambiguity search** over a confidence region in space
- **Statistical testing** (ratio test, F-test) to confirm the correct integer set

**OTF Procedure:**
1. Rover is initialised over a known point (or with a "point to point" baseline).
2. After lock to ≥ 5 satellites and PDOP < 6, the OTF algorithm starts.
3. It forms the **double-differenced** carrier-phase equations.
4. A **lambda search** is performed: candidate integer vectors are tested; the one minimising the variance is selected.
5. The **ratio test** (e.g., variance of second-best candidate / variance of best candidate > 2 or 3) confirms the chosen set is statistically reliable.
6. Once resolved, the **fixed-integer** solution provides cm-level accuracy; if not resolved, the system falls back to a **float solution** (decimetre accuracy).

**Why essential:** Without OTF, kinematic GPS would require 5–20 min of static observation at every point, defeating the purpose of kinematic efficiency.

*(7 marks: 1 mark definition, 2 marks for L1/L2 combinations, 2 marks for lambda search/ratio test, 2 marks for why it is essential)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — DGPS and Error Modelling** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]

**(a)** Define **DGPS (Differential GPS)**. With a clear block diagram, explain the working principle, types (position, pseudo-range, carrier-phase), and the typical accuracy achieved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List the major error sources affecting GPS measurements. Derive the **pseudorange observation equation** showing how each error enters the model. Briefly explain how DGPS eliminates these errors. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Definition:** DGPS is a technique in which the position/measurement errors observed at a Base station of known coordinates are transmitted to a Rover receiver, which applies them as corrections to its own measurements.

**Block Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

```
   [GPS SATELLITES]   ↘                     ↘
                      [BASE RX]→[PRC COMPUTE]→[RADIO LINK]→[ROVER RX]→[CORRECTED POSITION]
                                  ↑
                          [KNOWN COORDINATES]
```

**Types of DGPS (by observable used):**

| Type | Observable | Typical Accuracy | Range |
|------|------------|------------------|-------|
| **Position DGPS** | 3-D position | ±1–3 m | Short |
| **Pseudo-range DGPS** | Code pseudorange | ±1–2 m | < 100 km |
| **Carrier-phase DGPS** | Carrier phase | ±cm level (RTK) | < 15 km |

**Working Principle:**
1. The Base station (with known coordinates) tracks all visible satellites.
2. For each satellite, it computes the **PRC** = true range − measured pseudorange.
3. The PRCs are broadcast to the Rover in real time.
4. The Rover applies the corrections and recomputes its position with **±1–3 m** accuracy (or cm in RTK).

*(7 marks: 1 mark definition, 2 marks diagram, 2 marks types, 2 marks principle)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Major Error Sources:**

1. Satellite clock error ($\delta t^s$)
2. Ephemeris error (orbital inaccuracy)
3. Ionospheric delay ($I$)
4. Tropospheric delay ($T$)
5. Receiver clock error ($\delta t_r$)
6. Multipath
7. Receiver noise ($\varepsilon$)

**Derivation of Pseudorange Observation Equation:**

**Step 1:** Geometric range definition:
$$\rho = c \cdot (t_r^{true} - t_s^{true})$$

**Step 2:** Actual measurements include clock biases:
$$t_r^{measured} = t_r^{true} + \delta t_r$$
$$t_s^{measured} = t_s^{true} + \delta t^s$$

**Step 3:** The measured pseudorange:
$$P = c \cdot (t_r^{measured} - t_s^{measured}) = c \cdot (t_r^{true} - t_s^{true}) + c(\delta t_r - \delta t^s)$$

**Step 4:** Substituting the geometric range and adding atmospheric terms:
$$P = \rho + c(\delta t_r - \delta t^s) + I + T + \varepsilon_P$$

This is the **fundamental pseudorange observation equation**.

**How DGPS Eliminates Errors:**

For a short baseline, the Base and Rover experience **the same** satellite clock, ephemeris, ionospheric, and tropospheric errors (they share the same line of sight to the satellite). The Base computes:

$$PRC^{(i)} = \rho_{true}^{B,i} - P_{measured}^{B,i} = -c(\delta t_r^B - \delta t^s) - I - T - \varepsilon_P$$

The Rover then applies:
$$P_{corr}^{R,i} = P_{measured}^{R,i} + PRC^{(i)} \approx \rho_{true}^{R,i}$$

All **spatially correlated** errors (satellite clock, ephemeris, ionosphere, troposphere) cancel out. The remaining uncorrelated error (multipath, receiver noise) is much smaller, giving **±1–3 m** (code DGPS) or **cm-level** (carrier-phase DGPS / RTK) accuracy.

*(7 marks: 1 mark error list, 3 marks derivation with all steps, 1 mark PRC equation, 2 marks elimination principle)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **DGPS** improves GPS accuracy from **±10 m** to **±1–3 m** (code) or **cm-level** (carrier phase).
- [x] **DGPS principle:** Base on known point → compute **PRC** → broadcast to Rover → Rover applies correction.
- [x] **PRCs** correct: satellite clock, ephemeris, ionosphere, troposphere.
- [x] **PRCs do NOT correct:** multipath, receiver noise (site-dependent, must be mitigated at source).
- [x] **Kinematic GPS** = one receiver moves, the other is stationary.
- [x] **Carrier phase precision** = 0.5–2 mm of $\lambda$ → vastly superior to code.
- [x] **L1 wavelength** ≈ **19.03 cm**, **L2 wavelength** ≈ **24.42 cm**.
- [x] **Pseudorange equation:** $P = \rho + c(\delta t_r - \delta t^s) + I + T + \varepsilon_P$
- [x] **Carrier-phase equation:** $\Phi = \frac{\rho + c(\delta t_r - \delta t^s) - I + T + \lambda N}{\lambda}$
- [x] **Ionospheric delay adds to code, subtracts from carrier phase** (sign convention).
- [x] **Double differencing** cancels receiver and satellite clock biases simultaneously.
- [x] **Integer ambiguity $N$** is the unknown number of carrier cycles — must be resolved to fixed integer.
- [x] **OTF (On-The-Fly)** resolves $N$ in seconds while the Rover moves.
- [x] **RTK accuracy:** ±(8 mm + 1 ppm) horizontal — the 1 ppm term scales with baseline.
- [x] **Selective Availability** was disabled on **1 May 2000** — do NOT include in current error budgets.
- [x] **Static = 1–3 h, mm accuracy; Kinematic = continuous, cm accuracy; RTK = real-time, cm accuracy.**
- [x] **Stop & Go** = kinematic + 5–10 sec stops at points of interest.
- [x] **DOPs** (GDOP, PDOP, HDOP, VDOP, TDOP) — lower is better; PDOP < 6 ideal for engineering.
- [x] **Dual-frequency (L1 + L2)** receivers are required for baselines > 10 km due to ionospheric decorrelation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recent Trends in Surveying: GNSS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET304 - SURVEYING AND GEOMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Recent Trends in Surveying: GNSS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!NOTE]
> **GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System)** is a constellation of Earth-orbiting satellites that broadcast positioning, navigation, and timing (PNT) data from space, enabling a receiver anywhere on or near the Earth's surface to determine its **3-D position (X, Y, Z)**, **velocity**, and **time** by trilateration on the received signals.

GNSS is the umbrella term encompassing all satellite-based navigation systems — operational examples include the **USA's NAVSTAR GPS**, **Russia's GLONASS**, **European Union's Galileo**, **China's BeiDou (BDS)**, and **India's NavIC (IRNSS)**. For KTU examination purposes, the **NAVSTAR GPS** remains the most frequently tested system because it is the first operational GNSS and forms the technical baseline for all others.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GNSS vs GPS** — Frequently confused in exams:
> - **GPS** = one specific system (USA, fully operational since 1995).
> - **GNSS** = the generic family of satellite navigation systems.
> A multi-constellation receiver uses *several* GNSS simultaneously, giving better accuracy, redundancy, and availability.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Cosmic Lighthouse Network"

Imagine you are lost at sea in a dense fog. Suddenly, four lighthouses on distant mountaintops each flash a light at a known, perfectly synchronized instant. Your ship has a super-precise stopwatch. By measuring exactly *how long* each flash took to reach you, and knowing the speed of light, you can compute *how far* you are from each lighthouse. With three lighthouses, you can pin down your 2-D position; with **four**, you also correct your stopwatch's error.

> [!NOTE]
> Replace lighthouses with **satellites**, light pulses with **radio signals**, the stopwatch with an **atomic clock**, and you have GNSS. The "fog" is the ionosphere, which slows the radio waves — a major source of error discussed later.

## 1.3 Standard Constants Used in GNSS

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value |
|----------|--------|----------------|
| Speed of light in vacuum | **c** | **2.99792458 × 10⁸ m/s** |
| GPS L1 carrier frequency | $f_{L1}$ | **1575.42 MHz** |
| GPS L2 carrier frequency | $f_{L2}$ | **1227.60 MHz** |
| GPS L5 carrier frequency | $f_{L5}$ | **1176.45 MHz** |
| GPS C/A code chip rate | $R_{C/A}$ | **1.023 Mbps** |
| WGS-84 semi-major axis | $a$ | **6 378 137.0 m** |
| WGS-84 flattening | $1/f$ | **1 / 298.257223563** |
| WGS-84 Earth rotation rate | $\omega_e$ | **7.292115 × 10⁻⁵ rad/s** |
| GPS time epoch | — | **Midnight 06 Jan 1980 (UTC)** |

> [!TIP]
> Memorizing **c ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s**, the **L1 = 1575.42 MHz**, and the **WGS-84 ellipsoid** parameters is essential — these appear repeatedly in KTU numericals and derivations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Segments of GNSS

Any GNSS is logically partitioned into three cooperating segments:

### (a) Space Segment
- A constellation of **MEO (Medium Earth Orbit)** satellites at altitudes of **~19 000 – 23 000 km**.
- Each satellite continuously broadcasts:
  1. A **navigation message** (orbital ephemeris, clock correction, almanac, ionospheric model).
  2. **Ranging codes** (Coarse/Acquisition — C/A; Precise — P(Y)).
  3. **Carrier waves** (L1, L2, L5) modulated by the codes and message.
- GPS nominal constellation: **24 + 3 spares = 27 satellites** distributed across **6 orbital planes**, inclination **55°**, orbital period **~11 h 58 m** (sidereal day).
- Ensures that **≥ 4 satellites are visible above 15° elevation mask** from any point on Earth at any time.

### (b) Control Segment (Ground Segment)
- **1 Master Control Station (MCS)** + several **Monitor Stations** + **Ground Antennas (Upload Stations)**.
- Monitor stations passively track all satellites, compute **orbital ephemeris** and **clock offsets**, and upload them to the satellites via S-band uplink.
- For GPS: Master Station at **Schriever AFB, Colorado, USA**.

### (c) User Segment
- The GNSS receiver + antenna + processing software held by the surveyor.
- Civilian survey-grade receivers can achieve **mm-level** accuracy in **RTK/PPK** modes.
- Position is reported in the **WGS-84** datum (or transformed to a local datum via datum parameters — e.g., for India, **Everest 1830 (1977 Adjustment)** or **WGS-84 ↔ ITRF** transformation).

## 2.2 Working Principle — Pseudorange Trilateration

The receiver computes the **pseudorange** $\rho$ to each satellite as:

$$\rho = c \cdot \Delta t$$

where $\Delta t$ is the measured signal travel time. Because both the satellite and receiver clocks have errors, $\rho$ is *not* the true geometric range — hence "pseudo."

For a receiver at unknown position $(X_u, Y_u, Z_u)$ and clock bias $\Delta t_u$, observing satellite $s$ at known position $(X_s, Y_s, Z_s)$ with clock bias $\Delta t_s$, the pseudorange equation is:

$$\rho_s = \sqrt{(X_s - X_u)^2 + (Y_s - Y_u)^2 + (Z_s - Z_u)^2} + c \, (\Delta t_u - \Delta t_s) + I_s + T_s + \varepsilon_s$$

**Why four satellites are mathematically required:** We have **four unknowns** — the three coordinates $(X_u, Y_u, Z_u)$ and the receiver clock bias $\Delta t_u$. Each visible satellite contributes one pseudorange equation. Therefore, a minimum of **4 simultaneous satellites** are required to obtain a unique solution (linearization via Taylor series + iterative least-squares).

## 2.3 Sources of Error (Error Budget)

GNSS accuracy is the combined effect of several error sources. The standard **UERE (User Equivalent Range Error)** components are:

| Error Source | Typical Magnitude (m) | Mitigation |
|--------------|----------------------|------------|
| **Ephemeris errors** | ±2.1 | Post-processed precise orbits (IGS) |
| **Satellite clock errors** | ±2.1 | Broadcast clock corrections / PPP |
| **Ionospheric delay** | ±4.0 | Dual-frequency receivers; Klobuchar / NeQuick model |
| **Tropospheric delay** | ±0.7 | Saastamoinen / Hopfield model + surface met data |
| **Multipath** | ±1.0 | Choke-ring antennas, site selection, elevation mask > 15° |
| **Receiver noise** | ±0.5 | Better hardware, longer integration |
| **Total (RMS, UERE)** | **≈ ±5.4 m** (SPS) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Selective Availability (SA), an intentional degradation imposed by the U.S. DoD until **1 May 2000**, added **±24 m** of horizontal error. **SA is now permanently set to zero**, which is why civilian GPS jumped from ~100 m to ~10 m accuracy overnight.

## 2.4 Positioning Modes and Accuracies

| Mode | Method | Accuracy | Use in Surveying |
|------|--------|----------|------------------|
| **Standalone (SPS)** | Single receiver, C/A code | 5 – 10 m | Navigation, GIS data collection |
| **DGPS** | Code corrections from base | 0.5 – 2 m | GIS, hydrography |
| **Static (Post-processed)** | Carrier phase, long obs. | 5 mm + 1 ppm | Control surveying, geodetic networks |
| **RTK (Real-Time Kinematic)** | Carrier phase + radio/GSM link | 8 mm + 1 ppm | Topographic, cadastral, setting-out |
| **Network RTK (VRS / NTRIP)** | Network of bases + cell phone | 10 mm + 1 ppm | Engineering surveys, machine control |
| **PPK (Post-Processed Kinematic)** | Carrier phase, post-processed | 5 mm + 1 ppm | Aerial photo control, drone mapping |
| **PPP (Precise Point Positioning)** | Single receiver + precise orbits/clocks | 2 – 10 cm | Geophysics, oceanography |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Where Used |
|---|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | $\rho = c \cdot \Delta t$ | Pseudorange from signal travel time |
| 2 | $\rho_s = \sqrt{(\Delta X)^2 + (\Delta Y)^2 + (\Delta Z)^2} + c\Delta t_u + \text{errors}$ | Master pseudorange equation |
| 3 | $n = \text{number of unknowns} = 4$ | Position $(X, Y, Z)$ + clock bias |
| 4 | $\text{PDOP} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2} \, / \, \sigma_0$ | Position Dilution of Precision |
| 5 | $\text{HDOP} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2} \, / \, \sigma_0$ | Horizontal DOP |
| 6 | $\text{VDOP} = \sigma_z \, / \, \sigma_0$ | Vertical DOP |
| 7 | $\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{PDOP}^2 + \text{TDOP}^2}$ | Geometric DOP |
| 8 | $I = \dfrac{40.3}{f^2} \int N_e \, ds$ | Ionospheric range delay (Hz-dependent) |
| 9 | $\Delta R = \dfrac{I_{L1} - I_{L2}}{f_1^2 - f_2^2} \cdot f_1^2$ | Ionospheric-free combination (L1) |
| 10 | $T = \dfrac{2.47}{\sin E} \cdot \left(P + 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{4810 \cdot e \cdot \tan^2 E}{P}\right)$ (mbar) | Saastamoinen tropospheric delay |
| 11 | $\sigma_P^2 = \text{PDOP}^2 \cdot \text{UERE}^2$ | 3-D positional variance |
| 12 | $\text{RIRE} = \dfrac{77.64 \cdot K' \cdot p}{c \cdot f^2}$ | Co-Op IRI ionospheric refraction term |
| 13 | $a = 6\,378\,137$ m, $f = 1/298.257223563$ | WGS-84 reference ellipsoid |
| 14 | $b = a(1-f)$ | WGS-84 semi-minor axis |
| 15 | $\Delta h_{\text{ortho}} = h_{\text{ellip}} - N$ | Orthometric height from ellipsoidal height & geoid undulation $N$ |

> [!TIP]
> Equations 2, 4, 7, 8, 10, 11, and 15 are the **most repeated** in KTU 14-mark derivations. Practice drawing the satellite-receiver geometry for PDOP qualitatively.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

GNSS has revolutionized civil engineering by enabling:
- **Cadastral and boundary surveys** with cm-level accuracy (RTK).
- **Setting-out** of roads, bridges, and buildings (machine-controlled graders/excavators).
- **Monitoring deformation** of dams, landslides, and high-rise buildings (long-term static GNSS).
- **Aerial and drone photogrammetry** ground control (PPK).
- **Hydrographic surveying** combined with echo sounders.
- **Precise timing** for synchronizing SCADA systems in power grids and telecom networks.
- **Vehicle and asset tracking** for fleet management in large construction projects.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Pseudorange Equation

### Step 1 — Signal emission and reception timestamps

Let $t_s$ be the **true emission time** at the satellite, and $t_r$ be the **true reception time** at the receiver. The geometric range $\rho$ is traversed at the speed of light, so the geometric range equation is:

$$\rho = c \cdot (t_r - t_s)$$

**Text row:** The true range equals the speed of light multiplied by the time the signal spent in transit. This is the simplest form, free of any clock error.

### Step 2 — Introduce clock biases

Neither the satellite nor the receiver has a perfect clock. Let us define:
- $t_s^{\text{rec}} = t_s + \Delta t_s$ (time reported by the satellite's clock)
- $t_r^{\text{rec}} = t_r + \Delta t_u$ (time reported by the receiver's clock)

Here $\Delta t_s$ and $\Delta t_u$ are the **clock biases** (positive = clock runs fast).

The receiver can only measure the *reported* difference:

$$\Delta t^{\text{meas}} = t_r^{\text{rec}} - t_s^{\text{rec}} = (t_r - t_s) + (\Delta t_u - \Delta t_s)$$

**Text row:** Because both clocks carry errors, the *measured* time interval differs from the *true* time interval by the difference in clock biases. This is the origin of the "pseudo" in pseudorange.

### Step 3 — Define the pseudorange

Multiplying by $c$:

$$PR = c \cdot \Delta t^{\text{meas}} = c(t_r - t_s) + c(\Delta t_u - \Delta t_s)$$

Substituting $c(t_r - t_s) = \rho$:

$$PR = \rho + c \, (\Delta t_u - \Delta t_s)$$

**Text row:** Pseudorange = True range + Receiver clock bias term − Satellite clock bias term. We have reduced the formulation to a single equation in 5 unknowns ($\rho$, $\Delta t_u$, $\Delta t_s$, plus the 3 receiver coordinates implicit in $\rho$).

### Step 4 — Add atmospheric delays and noise

The signal does *not* travel in vacuum; it slows through the ionosphere (frequency-dependent) and troposphere. The total travel time increases by $\Delta t_{iono} + \Delta t_{tropo}$, and the receiver adds measurement noise $\varepsilon$:

$$PR = \rho + c \, (\Delta t_u - \Delta t_s) + c \, \Delta t_{iono} + c \, \Delta t_{tropo} + \varepsilon$$

Defining range-equivalent delays $I = c \cdot \Delta t_{iono}$ and $T = c \cdot \Delta t_{tropo}$:

$$PR = \rho + c \, (\Delta t_u - \Delta t_s) + I + T + \varepsilon$$

**Text row:** This is the final textbook pseudorange equation. The error terms $I$, $T$, and $\varepsilon$ are *modelled* (and ideally minimised) by various techniques discussed in §2.3.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Three-Dimensional Position Solution

We have the unknown receiver state vector:

$$\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} X_u \\ Y_u \\ Z_u \\ c \, \Delta t_u \end{bmatrix}$$

The pseudorange to satellite $i$ is:

$$PR_i = \sqrt{(X_i - X_u)^2 + (Y_i - Y_u)^2 + (Z_i - Z_u)^2} + c \, \Delta t_u + I_i + T_i + \varepsilon_i$$

### Step 1 — Linearisation by Taylor expansion

Pick an *a priori* guess $\mathbf{x}_0 = (X_0, Y_0, Z_0, c\Delta t_0)$. Expand each $PR_i$ in a first-order Taylor series about $\mathbf{x}_0$:

$$PR_i \approx PR_{i,0} + \frac{\partial PR_i}{\partial X_u}\Big|_0 \delta X_u + \frac{\partial PR_i}{\partial Y_u}\Big|_0 \delta Y_u + \frac{\partial PR_i}{\partial Z_u}\Big|_0 \delta Z_u + \frac{\partial PR_i}{\partial c\Delta t_u}\Big|_0 \, c\delta(\Delta t_u)$$

### Step 2 — Compute the partial derivatives

Define $r_i = \sqrt{(X_i - X_0)^2 + (Y_i - Y_0)^2 + (Z_i - Z_0)^2}$ (the *computed* range from the a-priori point to satellite $i$). Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial PR_i}{\partial X_u}\Big|_0 &= -\frac{X_i - X_0}{r_i} \\
\frac{\partial PR_i}{\partial Y_u}\Big|_0 &= -\frac{Y_i - Y_0}{r_i} \\
\frac{\partial PR_i}{\partial Z_u}\Big|_0 &= -\frac{Z_i - Z_0}{r_i} \\
\frac{\partial PR_i}{\partial (c\Delta t_u)}\Big|_0 &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Text row:** The first three partials are simply the components of the **unit line-of-sight vector** from the receiver to the satellite, with a minus sign. They form the geometry matrix $G$.

### Step 3 — Form the linear system

Stacking $n$ satellite equations into a vector $\mathbf{PR}$:

$$\Delta \mathbf{PR} = \mathbf{G} \, \delta \mathbf{x}$$

where

$$
\mathbf{G} = \begin{bmatrix}
-\frac{X_1 - X_0}{r_1} & -\frac{Y_1 - Y_0}{r_1} & -\frac{Z_1 - Z_0}{r_1} & 1 \\
-\frac{X_2 - X_0}{r_2} & -\frac{Y_2 - Y_0}{r_2} & -\frac{Z_2 - Z_0}{r_2} & 1 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\
-\frac{X_n - X_0}{r_n} & -\frac{Y_n - Y_0}{r_n} & -\frac{Z_n - Z_0}{r_n} & 1
\end{bmatrix}, \quad
\delta \mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} \delta X_u \\ \delta Y_u \\ \delta Z_u \\ c\delta(\Delta t_u) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Text row:** The geometry matrix $G$ encodes satellite-receiver geometry. Notice that even though $G$ is $n \times 4$, the problem is solvable only when $n \ge 4$ and the satellites are well-spread (low DOP).

### Step 4 — Least-squares solution (when $n > 4$)

Using the standard least-squares estimator:

$$\delta \mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{G})^{-1} \mathbf{G}^T \Delta \mathbf{PR}$$

**Text row:** In practice, the receiver iteratively updates $\mathbf{x} \leftarrow \mathbf{x} + \delta\mathbf{x}$ until $\vert \delta \mathbf{x} \vert$ falls below a threshold (typically 0.1 mm).

### Step 5 — Why exactly four satellites

If we have exactly four well-conditioned pseudorange equations, the system is square and invertible. The four unknowns $(X_u, Y_u, Z_u, c\Delta t_u)$ are determined in **closed form** without a least-squares adjustment. With more than four, the redundancy improves reliability and allows residual-based error detection.

## 3.3 Derivation of Ionospheric Delay and Dual-Frequency Correction

The ionospheric range delay along the line of sight, for a carrier of frequency $f$, is:

$$I(f) = \frac{40.3}{f^2} \cdot TEC$$

where **TEC (Total Electron Content)** is the integrated electron density along the path, measured in **electrons/m²** and is typically **10 – 100 TECU** (1 TECU = 10¹⁶ el/m²).

For two carriers (L1 and L2) on the same path:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PR_{L1} &= \rho + c\Delta t_u + I_{L1} + T + \varepsilon_{L1} \\
PR_{L2} &= \rho + c\Delta t_u + I_{L2} + T + \varepsilon_{L2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting eliminates $\rho$, $c\Delta t_u$, and $T$ (to first order) — both atmospheric errors are common-mode except for the ionosphere:

$$PR_{L2} - PR_{L1} = I_{L2} - I_{L1} = 40.3 \cdot TEC \left(\frac{1}{f_2^2} - \frac{1}{f_1^2}\right)$$

Solving for $I_{L1}$:

$$I_{L1} = \frac{(PR_{L2} - PR_{L1})}{\left(\dfrac{f_1^2}{f_2^2} - 1\right)}$$

**Text row:** This is the *ionospheric-free pseudorange*. Modern geodetic receivers log both L1 and L2 (or L1, L2, L5) code/carrier observations so this correction can be applied in real time, reaching **few-mm accuracy**.

## 3.4 DOP — Geometric Strength of the Solution

The covariance of the position estimate is:

$$\Sigma_{\mathbf{x}} = \sigma_0^2 \, (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{G})^{-1}$$

The DOP values are the square roots of the trace of sub-blocks of this matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GDOP} &= \sqrt{\text{trace}((\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{G})^{-1})} \\
\text{PDOP} &= \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2} \, / \, \sigma_0 \\
\text{HDOP} &= \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2} \, / \, \sigma_0 \\
\text{VDOP} &= \sigma_z / \sigma_0 \\
\text{TDOP} &= \sigma_{\Delta t} / \sigma_0
\end{aligned}
$$

| DOP Range | Quality | Comment |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| 1 – 2 | Excellent | Ideal for high-precision surveying |
| 2 – 5 | Good | Acceptable for general survey work |
| 5 – 10 | Moderate | Marginal; use with caution |
| 10 – 20 | Poor | Not suitable for accurate work |
| > 20 | Unusable | Reject the epoch |

**Text row:** Good geometry = one satellite overhead, three others spread at low elevation. Bad geometry = all satellites clustered in one part of the sky.
