# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reminder (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - Part A: Short answer (3 marks each) — tests **Remember / Understand** (Bloom L1–L2).
> - Part B: Long answer with internal choice (14 marks total, usually **7 + 7**) — tests **Apply / Analyse** (Bloom L3–L4).

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`
**Define Geographic Coordinate System. Differentiate it from a Projected Coordinate System.**

**Model Answer (≈ 120 words):**

A **Geographic Coordinate System (GCS)** is a 3D angular reference frame that locates points on the Earth's curved surface using **latitude (φ)** and **longitude (λ)**, referenced to a chosen **horizontal datum** (e.g., WGS 84). Its units are angular — degrees, minutes, seconds. A **Projected Coordinate System (PCS)** is a 2D Cartesian system (Easting, Northing) in linear units (metres/feet), obtained by mathematically projecting the GCS onto a flat surface using a *map projection*. The GCS preserves the curved 3D geometry of the Earth; the PCS converts it to a flat plane for measurement, mapping, and engineering design — but at the cost of inevitable distortion in shape, area, distance, or direction.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`
**State any four properties used to classify map projections and give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (≈ 100 words):**

Map projections are classified by the **geometric property they preserve**:

1. **Conformal** — preserves shape (angles) locally. *Example: Mercator, Transverse Mercator / UTM.*
2. **Equal-Area (Equivalent)** — preserves area. *Example: Albers Equal-Area Conic, Lambert Azimuthal Equal-Area.*
3. **Equidistant** — preserves distance along specific lines. *Example: Plate Carrée (equirectangular), Azimuthal Equidistant.*
4. **Azimuthal (True-Direction)** — preserves direction from a central point. *Example: Gnomonic, Stereographic.*

A fifth classification is by **developable surface** (cylindrical, conic, planar).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Format

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model | Module 4 | CO4 | Bloom L3]`

**(a)** Define the following terms with suitable sketches: (i) Latitude, (ii) Longitude, (iii) Geodetic datum, (iv) Convergence of meridians. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A station **P** is located at latitude **φ = 10° 15' N** and longitude **λ = 76° 30' E** in WGS 84. Determine:
  (i) The UTM zone number and central meridian of the station.
  (ii) The meridian convergence γ at this station, in degrees.
  (iii) Whether the station falls in the **'K'** or **'L'** latitude band of UTM. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Definitions & Sketches:**

- **Latitude (φ):** The angle subtended at the centre of the Earth between the **equatorial plane** and a line normal to the ellipsoid at the given point. Range: **0° to 90° N/S**. (Note: geodetic latitude, not geocentric.)

- **Longitude (λ):** The dihedral angle between the plane of the **prime meridian** (Greenwich) and the meridian plane through the point, measured along the equatorial plane. Range: **0° to 180° E/W**.

- **Geodetic Datum:** A reference framework that defines the size, shape, position, and orientation of an ellipsoid relative to the Earth. Example: **WGS 84** — semi-major axis **a = 6378137 m**, flattening **1/f = 298.257223563**, centred at the Earth's centre of mass.

- **Convergence of Meridians (γ):** The angle at a point between **true north** (geodetic meridian) and **grid north** (projection meridian). It arises because meridians on the curved Earth are great circles converging at the poles, but become parallel straight lines on a flat projection — so the angle between them changes with longitude and latitude.

For all four, the student should draw a simple labelled diagram (Earth + ellipsoid + axes + meridians/parallels).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) UTM Zone and Central Meridian:**

Zone number:

$$
n = \left\lfloor \dfrac{76.5 + 180}{6} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \dfrac{256.5}{6} \right\rfloor + 1 = 42 + 1 = \mathbf{43}
$$

Central meridian of Zone 43:

$$
\lambda_{0} = 6(43) - 183 = 258 - 183 = \mathbf{75^{\circ}\ E}
$$

**(ii) Convergence of meridians:**

$$
l = \lambda - \lambda_{0} = 76.5^{\circ} - 75^{\circ} = 1.5^{\circ} = 0.02618\ \text{rad}
$$

$$
\gamma \approx l \cdot \sin\varphi = 0.02618 \times \sin(10.25^{\circ})
$$

$$
\sin(10.25^{\circ}) = 0.17794
$$

$$
\gamma = 0.02618 \times 0.17794 = 0.004659\ \text{rad}
$$

Convert to degrees:

$$
\gamma = 0.004659 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} = \mathbf{0.267^{\circ}}
$$

**(iii) Latitude band:**

The **'K' band** covers **32° N to 40° N**.
The **'L' band** covers **40° N to 48° N**.

Our station is at **10° 15' N**, which is far below the 'K' band. It lies in the **'J' band** (16° S to 8° N) — wait, 10° 15' N is in the **'J' band** (0° to 8° N) is incorrect; 10.25° N is between 8° N and 16° N, which is the **'K'** band? No — let us recheck:

- **'J' band** = 0° to 8° N
- **'K' band** = 8° N to 16° N

So 10° 15' N = 10.25° N falls in the **'K' band** ✓.

**Answer:** The station is in **UTM Zone 43, latitude band 'K'**, and the convergence is **0.267°**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model | Module 4 | CO4 | Bloom L3–L4]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction of a **Lambert Conformal Conic (LCC)** projection. State the two standard parallels and the formula for the scale factor along a parallel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two stations **A** and **B** are given:
- **A:** φ = 20° N, λ = 78° E
- **B:** φ = 20° N, λ = 79° E

Compute (i) the **geodesic distance** between A and B (along the parallel), and (ii) the **convergence of meridians** at A, in degrees, if the projection's central meridian is **λ₀ = 75° E**. Radius of Earth R = 6371 km. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Lambert Conformal Conic (LCC) Construction:**

- A **cone** is placed over the Earth such that it **secant**s the ellipsoid along **two standard parallels** φ₁ and φ₂.
- Distances along the standard parallels are preserved exactly; elsewhere the scale factor varies between **k = 1 at φ₁ and φ = 1 at φ₂**, and is less than 1 (or slightly greater) between them.
- Meridians appear as **straight lines radiating** from the apex of the cone; parallels appear as **concentric circular arcs**.
- The projection is **conformal** (preserves shape locally).

**Two standard parallels** (commonly chosen as **1/6 and 5/6** of the latitude range to be mapped, e.g., for India φ₁ = 12° N, φ₂ = 36° N).

**Scale factor along a parallel** at latitude φ (between φ₁ and φ₂):

$$
k = \dfrac{\cos\phi_{1}}{\cos\phi} \cdot \dfrac{\tan\!\left[\dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2} - \phi_{2}\right)\right]^{n}}{\tan\!\left[\dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2} - \phi\right)\right]^{n}}
$$

where the cone constant:

$$
n = \dfrac{\ln(\cos\phi_{1}) - \ln(\cos\phi_{2})}{\ln\!\left[\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4} + \phi_{2}/2\right)\right] - \ln\!\left[\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4} + \phi_{1}/2\right)\right]}
$$

For practical purposes, students should write the working equations and the **standard sketch** of a cone intersecting the Earth at φ₁ and φ₂.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Geodesic distance along the parallel between A and B:**

Along a parallel, distance = **R · cos(φ) · Δλ** (in radians):

$$
\Delta\lambda = 79^{\circ} - 78^{\circ} = 1^{\circ} = 0.017453\ \text{rad}
$$

$$
\text{Distance} = 6371 \times \cos(20^{\circ}) \times 0.017453
$$

$$
\cos(20^{\circ}) = 0.93969
$$

$$
6371 \times 0.93969 = 5986.3
$$

$$
5986.3 \times 0.017453 = \mathbf{104.47\ km}
$$

**(ii) Convergence of meridians at A:**

$$
l = \lambda_{A} - \lambda_{0} = 78^{\circ} - 75^{\circ} = 3^{\circ} = 0.05236\ \text{rad}
$$

$$
\gamma \approx l \cdot \sin\varphi = 0.05236 \times \sin(20^{\circ})
$$

$$
\sin(20^{\circ}) = 0.34202
$$

$$
\gamma = 0.05236 \times 0.34202 = 0.01791\ \text{rad}
$$

Convert to degrees:

$$
\gamma = 0.01791 \times (180/\pi) = \mathbf{1.026^{\circ}}
$$

So at station A, **true north is rotated 1.026° clockwise from grid north**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the +500 000 m false easting** in UTM Easting — losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up geodetic and geocentric latitude** — geodetic latitude is what GPS gives; geocentric latitude is different (smaller) because the normal is not the radius vector.
> 3. **Computing convergence in degrees and forgetting to convert** the final answer from radians to degrees (or vice versa) — losing 1 mark.
> 4. **Choosing the wrong zone number** (off-by-one error: e.g., computing n = 42 instead of 43) because the student forgot the +1 in the floor formula.
> 5. **Mixing up WGS 84 and Kalianpur 1975 datum coordinates** without applying the transformation — datum change can shift coordinates by **200–500 m** in India.
> 6. **Not labelling the sketch** (lat, long, prime meridian, equator) in part (a) — losing up to 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **GCS = 3D angular** (lat, long in degrees); **PCS = 2D linear** (Easting, Northing in metres).
- [ ] WGS 84: **a = 6378137 m**, **1/f = 298.257223563**, **e² ≈ 0.00669438**.
- [ ] UTM has **60 zones × 6°** each; **central meridian λ₀ = 6n − 183°**.
- [ ] UTM **false easting = 500 000 m**; **false northing = 0 (N)** / **10 000 000 m (S)**.
- [ ] UTM **scale factor at CM: k₀ = 0.9996** (less than 1 — "deflation trick").
- [ ] Convergence: **γ ≈ (λ − λ₀) · sin φ** (radians → convert to degrees).
- [ ] Distance along a parallel: **D = R · cos(φ) · Δλ**.
- [ ] **Conformal** ⇒ preserves shape (Meridional scale = Parallel scale, h = k).
- [ ] **Equal-Area** ⇒ preserves area (Albers, LCC equal-area variant).
- [ ] **Memory aid:** Cylindrical = equator; Conic = mid-latitudes; Azimuthal = poles.
- [ ] **Memory aid:** False easting 500 000 m ensures easting is always positive (the UTM "zero trick").
- [ ] **Datum shifts** (WGS84 ↔ Kalianpur 1975) can cause **hundreds of metres** of error in India — never mix datums silently in a survey.
- [ ] **Kerala falls in UTM Zone 43 N, latitude band 'K'** (most of south Kerala is in 'J'–'K').
- [ ] Always state **units** in the final answer (degrees / metres / km) — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.
- [ ] Always **convert DMS → decimal degrees** before computing (e.g., 10° 15' = 10 + 15/60 = 10.25°).
- [ ] For KTU 14-mark answers, **show all intermediate steps**; jumping to the final answer loses 3–5 marks in valuation.
- [ ] For a **boundary survey / GPS layout**, the project planner must specify: **Datum, PCS, UTM zone, false easting/northing, scale factor** — without all five, the survey is legally ambiguous.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET304 - SURVEYING AND GEOMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Types - Spatial and Attribute Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 : DATA TYPES - SPATIAL AND ATTRIBUTE DATA

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Spatial Data** is the geospatial information that describes the **location, geometry, and topological relationships** of real-world features on, above, or below the Earth's surface. It is represented through coordinate values (e.g., x, y, z) within a defined Coordinate Reference System (CRS).

> [!NOTE]
> **Attribute Data** (also called *non-spatial* or *descriptive data*) is the **qualitative or quantitative information** that describes the characteristics, properties, and features of spatial entities. It answers the question *"What is it?"* rather than *"Where is it?"*

The two primary data categories that form the backbone of any Geographic Information System (GIS) are:

1. **Spatial Data** → Geometry / Shape / Location
2. **Attribute Data** → Description / Property / Identity

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy : The Google Maps Pin**
>
> Imagine you are searching for the **Tech Park** in your city on a digital map:
>
> - **Spatial Data** = the **pin's position** on the map. It tells you *WHERE* the Tech Park is located (Latitude: 10.0261° N, Longitude: 76.3125° E). It is the geometry that locates the object in space.
>
> - **Attribute Data** = the **pop-up information card** when you click the pin. It tells you *WHAT* the place is, its *NAME* (Technopark), its *AREA* (300 acres), its *FLOOR COUNT* (10 floors), and its *ESTABLISHMENT YEAR* (1990). It is the descriptive metadata attached to the object.
>
> Together, the pin (spatial) + the info card (attribute) = a complete, useful geospatial record.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Units

| Parameter | Standard Unit | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Geographic Coordinates | **Decimal Degrees (DD)** | Latitude, Longitude on WGS-84 / EPSG:4326 |
| Projected Coordinates | **Meters / Feet** | Northing (Y), Easting (X) in UTM / State Plane |
| Raster Pixel Resolution | **Meters per pixel (m/px)** | Ground Sampling Distance (GSD) |
| Attribute Data Storage | **Bytes / Kilobytes** | Per record, dependent on data type |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Spatial Data

Spatial data is broadly classified into two contrasting representation models:

### 2.1.1 Vector Data Model
The vector model represents real-world features as **discrete geometric primitives** defined by coordinate pairs.

| Primitive | Geometry | Real-World Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Point** | A single (x, y) coordinate pair | A survey benchmark, a well, a tree |
| **Line / Polyline** | A connected series of (x, y) vertices | A road, river, railway track, pipeline |
| **Polygon** | A closed series of (x, y) vertices enclosing an area | A land parcel, a lake, a building footprint |

> [!NOTE]
> **Topology** is the spatial relationship logic in vector data. Key topological rules include *connectivity* (lines meeting at nodes), *containment* (points inside polygons), and *adjacency* (shared polygon boundaries).

### 2.1.2 Raster Data Model
The raster model represents space as a **continuous matrix of square cells (pixels or grid cells)**, where each cell holds a single value representing a property of that location.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Common raster datasets include satellite imagery (e.g., Sentinel-2, Landsat-9), Digital Elevation Models (DEMs), and scanned topo-sheets.

### 2.2 Classification of Attribute Data

Attribute data is stored in **relational database tables** linked to spatial features via a unique identifier (**Feature ID / FID**).

| Attribute Type | Storage Format | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Qualitative (Nominal)** | Text / Categorical Code | Land use class: "Residential" / "Commercial" |
| **Ordinal** | Integer (with rank) | Soil fertility: 1 (Low), 2 (Medium), 3 (High) |
| **Quantitative (Interval)** | Integer / Float | Temperature in °C, Elevation in m |
| **Quantitative (Ratio)** | Float with absolute zero | Population count, Area in m² |
| **Boolean** | True / False (1 / 0) | Flood-affected: Yes / No |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are essential for KTU University Exam calculations on raster and vector data.

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{File Size} = R \times C \times \frac{B}{8}$ | Raster file size estimation | Bytes |
| 2 | $\text{Ground Resolution} = \text{IFOV} \times H$ | Pixel size on ground (sensor) | Meters |
| 3 | $A_{poly} = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \right\vert$ | Polygon area (Shoelace formula) | m² |
| 4 | $L_{line} = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sqrt{(x_{i+1} - x_i)^2 + (y_{i+1} - y_i)^2}$ | Polyline total length | m |
| 5 | $D = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$ | Euclidean distance between two points | m |
| 6 | $N_{rows} = \frac{\text{Extent Height}}{R_{pixel}}$ | Number of raster rows | Count |
| 7 | $N_{cols} = \frac{\text{Extent Width}}{R_{pixel}}$ | Number of raster columns | Count |
| 8 | $\text{Compression Ratio} = \frac{S_{uncompressed}}{S_{compressed}}$ | Data compression efficiency | Ratio |
| 9 | $B_{range} = B_{max} - B_{min} + 1$ | Bit-depth gray-value range | Levels |

> Where: $R$ = rows, $C$ = columns, $B$ = bit-depth (bits per pixel), IFOV = Instantaneous Field of View (radians), $H$ = sensor flying height (m).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **civil engineering projects** (highways, irrigation, smart-cities), spatial data drives alignment design, while attribute data stores layer-thickness, material, and year-of-construction. In **disaster management**, raster data feeds flood simulation; attribute data populates population-at-risk registers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Raster File Size Calculation

A raster image is a regular grid of $R$ rows and $C$ columns. Each cell stores a value using $B$ bits of binary data.

$$
\text{Step 1:} \quad \text{Total cells} = R \times C
$$

This is because the raster is a rectangular matrix of dimensions $R$ (vertical) by $C$ (horizontal).

$$
\text{Step 2:} \quad \text{Total bits} = (R \times C) \times B
$$

Since each of the $R \times C$ cells occupies $B$ bits, the total raw storage in bits is the product.

$$
\text{Step 3:} \quad \text{Total bytes} = \frac{(R \times C) \times B}{8}
$$

Computer storage is typically measured in bytes. Since 1 byte = 8 bits, we divide by 8.

$$
\boxed{\text{File Size (Bytes)} = R \times C \times \frac{B}{8}}
$$

**Worked Example:**
A satellite image covers a study area of $10 \text{ km} \times 10 \text{ km}$ at a ground resolution of $R_{pixel} = 10 \text{ m}$, with 8-bit panchromatic pixels. Compute the file size.

$$
N_{rows} = \frac{10{,}000 \text{ m}}{10 \text{ m}} = 1{,}000 \text{ rows}
$$

$$
N_{cols} = \frac{10{,}000 \text{ m}}{10 \text{ m}} = 1{,}000 \text{ columns}
$$

$$
\text{File Size} = 1{,}000 \times 1{,}000 \times \frac{8}{8} = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes} = 1 \text{ MB}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Polygon Area via the Shoelace Formula

For a polygon with $n$ vertices $(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)$ listed in order, the area is given by the signed Shoelace sum.

$$
\text{Step 1:} \quad S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i)
$$

This is the cross-multiplication sum where the index wraps around (i.e., $x_{n+1} = x_1$ and $y_{n+1} = y_1$).

$$
\text{Step 2:} \quad A_{poly} = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert S \right\vert
$$

The absolute value ensures the area is non-negative regardless of vertex traversal direction (clockwise or counter-clockwise).

**Worked Example (Square Plot):**
A square land parcel has vertices (0,0), (50,0), (50,50), (50,50) ... actually corrected: (0,0), (50,0), (50,50), (0,50).

$$
S = (0 \cdot 0 - 50 \cdot 0) + (50 \cdot 50 - 50 \cdot 0) + (50 \cdot 50 - 0 \cdot 50) + (0 \cdot 0 - 0 \cdot 50)
$$

$$
S = 0 + 2500 + 2500 + 0 = 5000
$$

$$
A = \frac{1}{2} \vert 5000 \vert = 2500 \text{ m}^2
$$

This matches the geometric area of a 50 m × 50 m square.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Vector vs Raster

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam table**. Memorize the key contrasts for Part A 3-mark questions.

| Parameter | Vector Data | Raster Data |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Data Structure** | Points, Lines, Polygons | Grid of pixels (cells) |
| **Storage Format** | Shapefile (.shp), GeoJSON, KML | GeoTIFF (.tif), IMG, NetCDF |
| **Coordinate System** | Explicit (x, y) vertices | Implicit (row, column index) |
| **Best Suited For** | Discrete features (roads, parcels) | Continuous phenomena (elevation, temperature) |
| **Topology** | Yes (defined by rules) | No explicit topology |
| **Resolution** | Infinite (coordinate-precise) | Finite (limited by cell size) |
| **Data Volume** | Compact for sparse features | Large for high-resolution imagery |
| **Overlay Analysis** | Complex, slow | Fast, cell-by-cell operation |
| **Attribute Linking** | Direct via Feature ID | Per-cell value or attribute table |
| **Examples** | Utility network, cadastral map | DEM, satellite image, land-use grid |

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Spatial vs Attribute Data

| Parameter | Spatial Data | Attribute Data |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Question Answered** | Where is it? What shape? | What is it? How much? |
| **Data Type** | Geometric (numeric coordinates) | Alphanumeric (text, numbers) |
| **Stored As** | Geometry field (Shape) | Database table columns / fields |
| **Visualization** | Map layers, symbols, labels | Charts, queries, reports |
| **Update Frequency** | Slower (survey-grade updates) | Frequent (administrative changes) |
| **CRS Dependency** | Yes (must declare CRS) | No (CRS-independent) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GIS Data Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real World Feature e.g. Building] --> B[Data Acquisition GPS, Total Station, Satellite]
    B --> C[GIS Database Core]
    C --> D[Spatial Data Geometry Layer]
    C --> E[Attribute Data Descriptive Table]
    D --> D1[Vector Points Lines Polygons]
    D --> D2[Raster Pixels Grids DEMs]
    D --> D3[Topology Network Rules]
    E --> E1[Qualitative Nominal Categorical]
    E --> E2[Quantitative Interval Ratio]
    E --> E3[Metadata CRS Units Source]
    D <-->|Linked by Feature ID FID| E
    D --> F[Spatial Analysis Overlay Buffer]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Output Map Chart Decision Support]
```

## 4.2 Spatial Data Type Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Spatial Data] --> V[Vector Model]
    SP --> R[Raster Model]
    V --> V1[Point 0D]
    V --> V2[Line 1D Polyline]
    V --> V3[Polygon 2D Area]
    R --> R1[Grid Cell Matrix]
    R --> R2[Image Pixel]
    R --> R3[DEM Digital Elevation Model]
    V1 --> EX1[Survey Benchmark Tree]
    V2 --> EX2[Road River Pipeline]
    V3 --> EX3[Land Parcel Lake Building]
    R1 --> EX4[Thematic Map]
    R2 --> EX5[Satellite Image Aerial Photo]
    R3 --> EX6[Slope Aspect Hillshade]
```

## 4.3 Attribute Table Schema (Linked to Spatial Features)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FID[Feature ID Primary Key] --> F1[Polygon Feature Parcel 101]
    FID --> F2[Polygon Feature Parcel 102]
    FID --> F3[Point Feature BM 01]
    F1 --> A1[Owner Name : TEXT]
    F1 --> A2[Area Sqm : FLOAT]
    F1 --> A3[Land Use : CATEGORY]
    F1 --> A4[Tax Paid : BOOLEAN]
    F2 --> B1[Owner Name : TEXT]
    F2 --> B2[Area Sqm : FLOAT]
    F2 --> B3[Land Use : CATEGORY]
    F3 --> C1[Easting m : FLOAT]
    F3 --> C2[Northing m : FLOAT]
    F3 --> C3[Elevation m : FLOAT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how the **Feature ID (FID)** acts as the **bridge key** that connects every spatial geometry to one or more rows of descriptive attribute records. This relational linkage is the foundation of all GIS software (ArcGIS, QGIS, GRASS).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the term **Attribute Data** in GIS. List any two types of attribute data with suitable examples.

**Model Answer:**

> **Attribute data** is the non-spatial, descriptive information that characterizes a geographic feature, stored as rows and columns in a relational database table linked to the spatial layer by a unique Feature ID.
>
> *Types:*
> 1. **Qualitative (Nominal)** — e.g., Land-use class: *Residential* / *Commercial* / *Industrial* / *Agricultural*.
> 2. **Quantitative (Ratio)** — e.g., Population of a district = 1,250,000 persons; Area of a parcel = 450 m².

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between **Vector Data** and **Raster Data** representations in GIS. Mention one limitation of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Vector Data | Raster Data |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Representation | Points, lines, polygons defined by (x, y) coordinates | Regular grid of square cells (pixels) |
| Suitability | Discrete features (roads, buildings) | Continuous phenomena (elevation, imagery) |
| Limitation | Slow for large-area overlays | Storage-heavy; pixel-based precision loss |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **two main types of spatial data models** used in GIS, with neat sketches and two real-world examples for each primitive. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A **satellite image** covers a study area of $30 \text{ km} \times 20 \text{ km}$ at a ground resolution of $15 \text{ m}$ per pixel, using 16-bit radiometric resolution. Calculate the **uncompressed file size in Megabytes (MB)**. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to (a):

The two main spatial data models are:

**(i) Vector Data Model:** Stores features as discrete geometric primitives defined by explicit coordinate pairs.

- **Point (0-Dimensional):** A single (x, y) pair.
  *Examples:* A survey benchmark, a single tree location, a bus stop.
- **Line / Polyline (1-Dimensional):** An ordered series of (x, y) vertices connected by straight segments.
  *Examples:* A road centreline, a river, a power transmission line.
- **Polygon (2-Dimensional):** A closed loop of (x, y) vertices enclosing an area.
  *Examples:* A land parcel, a lake boundary, a building footprint.

> Vector data is topology-aware, supports precise boundary representation, and is the preferred format for cadastral and utility mapping.

**(ii) Raster Data Model:** Represents space as a regular matrix of square cells (pixels), each cell holding a single numeric value.

- **Grid Cell (Thematic):** Used for land-use, soil-type, or geomorphology maps.
- **Image Pixel (Imagery):** Used for satellite photos, aerial orthophotos, scanned topo-sheets.
- **DEM Pixel (Elevation):** Used for Digital Elevation Models, slope, aspect, hillshade derivatives.

> Raster data is resolution-dependent, best suited for continuous geospatial phenomena, and supports fast cell-by-cell analytical operations.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- Extent = 30 km × 20 km = 30,000 m × 20,000 m
- Pixel size $R_{pixel} = 15 \text{ m}$
- Bit depth $B = 16 \text{ bits}$

**Step 1 — Number of rows and columns:**

$$
N_{rows} = \frac{\text{Extent Height}}{R_{pixel}} = \frac{20{,}000 \text{ m}}{15 \text{ m}} = 1{,}333.33 \approx 1{,}334 \text{ rows}
$$

$$
N_{cols} = \frac{\text{Extent Width}}{R_{pixel}} = \frac{30{,}000 \text{ m}}{15 \text{ m}} = 2{,}000 \text{ columns}
$$

**Step 2 — Total file size in bytes:**

$$
\text{File Size} = R \times C \times \frac{B}{8} = 1{,}334 \times 2{,}000 \times \frac{16}{8}
$$

$$
\text{File Size} = 1{,}334 \times 2{,}000 \times 2 = 5{,}336{,}000 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to MB (1 MB = 1,048,576 bytes in binary OR 1,000,000 bytes in SI):**

$$
\text{File Size (SI)} = \frac{5{,}336{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 5.336 \text{ MB}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{File Size} \approx 5.34 \text{ MB (SI)}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the different **types of attribute data** with examples. Explain how attribute data is linked to spatial features in a GIS database. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A **land parcel** has the following corner coordinates measured in a local grid (in meters):
$P_1(0,0),\ P_2(40,0),\ P_3(40,30),\ P_4(20,50),\ P_5(0,30)$. Using the **Shoelace formula**, calculate the area of the parcel in square meters. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Attribute Data Types:**

1. **Nominal (Qualitative):** Categorical labels with no inherent order. *Example:* Road type = "Highway" / "Street" / "Lane".
2. **Ordinal:** Ordered categories with rank, but unequal intervals. *Example:* Soil fertility = 1 (Low) < 2 (Medium) < 3 (High).
3. **Interval (Quantitative):** Numeric values on a scale with no true zero. *Example:* Temperature in °C, year of construction.
4. **Ratio (Quantitative):** Numeric values with an absolute zero. *Example:* Population count, area in m², length in m.
5. **Boolean:** Binary True/False. *Example:* "Is this parcel litigated?" = True.

**Linkage Mechanism in GIS:**

In any GIS database, the spatial layer (geometry table) and the attribute table are joined through a **unique key field** called the **Feature ID (FID)** or **Object ID (OID)**. Each polygon, line, or point geometry carries one FID; that same FID appears as a row in the attribute table. This is a classic **one-to-one relational join**, allowing the GIS to instantly retrieve the descriptive properties of any feature clicked on the map.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given vertices in order:**
$P_1(0, 0),\ P_2(40, 0),\ P_3(40, 30),\ P_4(20, 50),\ P_5(0, 30)$

**Shoelace formula:**

$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i)
$$

**Term-by-term expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= x_1 y_2 - x_2 y_1 = (0)(0) - (40)(0) = 0 \\
T_2 &= x_2 y_3 - x_3 y_2 = (40)(30) - (40)(0) = 1{,}200 \\
T_3 &= x_3 y_4 - x_4 y_3 = (40)(50) - (20)(30) = 2{,}000 - 600 = 1{,}400 \\
T_4 &= x_4 y_5 - x_5 y_4 = (20)(30) - (0)(50) = 600 \\
T_5 &= x_5 y_1 - x_1 y_5 = (0)(0) - (0)(30) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summation:**

$$
S = 0 + 1{,}200 + 1{,}400 + 600 + 0 = 3{,}200
$$

**Final Area:**

$$
A = \frac{1}{2} \vert S \vert = \frac{1}{2} \times 3{,}200 = 1{,}600 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{A = 1{,}600 \text{ m}^2}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Triggers:**
> 1. **Confusing spatial and attribute data** — Students often describe coordinate values as "attribute data." Coordinates are spatial; attributes are descriptive.
> 2. **Forgetting to divide by 8** in raster file-size questions — bit-to-byte conversion is the most-skipped step.
> 3. **Shoelace wrap-around** — Failing to close the polygon (i.e., omitting the last term $x_n y_1 - x_1 y_n$) yields an incorrect area.
> 4. **Not declaring CRS** — Spatial data without a Coordinate Reference System is considered **non-spatial** by KTU examiners.
> 5. **Mixing units** — km vs m, DD vs DMS conversions must be done carefully for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE:**

- ☐ **Spatial Data** = geometry (point / line / polygon / pixel). Answers **"Where?"**
- ☐ **Attribute Data** = descriptive (nominal / ordinal / interval / ratio / boolean). Answers **"What?"**
- ☐ **Vector model** is **topology-aware**, **coordinate-precise**, best for **discrete** features.
- ☐ **Raster model** is **cell-based**, **resolution-limited**, best for **continuous** phenomena.
- ☐ The **Feature ID (FID)** is the **primary key** linking spatial geometry to attribute table rows.
- ☐ **Raster file size formula:** $\text{Bytes} = R \times C \times \dfrac{B}{8}$
- ☐ **Shoelace formula** for polygon area requires **ordered, closed-loop** vertex traversal.
- ☐ **Euclidean distance** $D = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$ is the base for line-length summation.
- ☐ A **CRS (Coordinate Reference System)** is **mandatory metadata** for any valid spatial dataset.
- ☐ **Metadata** = "data about data" — describes source, accuracy, units, CRS, and date of capture.
- ☐ Remember the **SI vs binary** conversion: 1 MB = 1,000,000 bytes (SI) vs 1,048,576 bytes (binary).
- ☐ **Topology rules** (connectivity, containment, adjacency) only apply to **vector** data, never raster.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Raster and Vector Data Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET304 - SURVEYING AND GEOMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Raster and Vector Data Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fundamental Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Geographic Information Systems (GIS) and Geomatics, spatial data describing real-world features can be encoded digitally in two principal structural paradigms: the **Raster Data Model** and the **Vector Data Model**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Raster Data Model:** A spatial data representation scheme in which the geographic space is divided into a regular grid of equally-sized cells (pixels), and each cell stores a single attribute value representing a specific feature characteristic at that location. The geometric position is implied by the row and column indices of the cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Vector Data Model:** A spatial data representation scheme in which geographic features are represented as discrete geometric primitives — **points**, **lines (arcs)**, and **polygons** — defined by explicit coordinate pairs (x, y or x, y, z) stored in a structured, non-uniform geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TIN (Triangulated Irregular Network):** A vector-based topological data structure used specifically in surveying for representing terrain surfaces as a network of non-overlapping triangles whose vertices correspond to measured spot elevations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are asked to draw a map of your college campus on two different types of paper:

- **Raster Analogy — "The Mosaic Wall Painting":** Take a sheet of graph paper (with small squares). Color each square with a single shade (green for grass, grey for roads, red for buildings). The campus is described square-by-square, like a pixelated digital photo. You cannot draw a smooth curve — only a staircase approximation. Resolution depends on **how small the squares are**. This is the **Raster Model**.

- **Vector Analogy — "The Architectural CAD Drawing":** Take a drafting sheet. Use a compass to plot exact points with precise x, y coordinates, connect them with mathematically perfect lines, and enclose polygons for buildings. Every line is a vector equation. You can zoom in infinitely without losing sharpness. This is the **Vector Model**.

> [!TIP]
> **Field-based features** (elevation, temperature, rainfall, soil type, land cover) are most naturally represented as **rasters**, while **discrete object features** (roads, cadastral boundaries, utility lines, building footprints) are most naturally represented as **vectors**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Terminology

| Term | Definition | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Pixel / Cell** | Smallest unit of raster data | 0.5 m to 30 m ground resolution |
| **Resolution** | Ground distance represented by one cell | 1 m, 10 m, 30 m |
| **Bit Depth** | Number of bits used to store each cell value | 1, 8, 16, 24, 32 bits |
| **Bands / Layers** | Number of attribute matrices stacked in raster | 1 (DEM), 3 (RGB), 7 (Landsat) |
| **Vertex** | A coordinate pair defining a point in a vector feature | (x, y) or (x, y, z) |
| **Node** | Topological junction (start/end of arc) | Integer ID |
| **Arc / Edge** | Directed linear sequence connecting two nodes | Ordered vertex list |
| **Topology** | Spatial relationships (connectivity, adjacency, containment) | Arc-Node, Polygon topology |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Raster Data Representation — The Internal Architecture

The raster model discretizes continuous geographic space into a tessellation. The two principal tessellation schemes are:

1. **Regular Square Grid (most common)** — Used in satellite imagery (Landsat, Sentinel), DEMs, scanned maps.
2. **Regular Hexagonal Grid** — Equal-area cells, six neighbours, used in some ecological modelling.
3. **Triangular Irregular Network (TIN — hybrid)** — Used for terrain modelling (covered in 2.3).

**Key Structural Parameters of a Raster:**

- **Cell Size (Δx, Δy):** The ground distance each pixel represents. A 10 m × 10 m cell means each pixel covers 100 m² on the ground.
- **Number of Rows (R) and Columns (C):** Determined by the bounding box and resolution.
- **Origin:** Usually the **upper-left corner** (row 1, column 1) in screen coordinates.
- **Cell Value (Z):** A single numeric value (integer, floating, or categorical) representing the attribute.
- **Coordinate System:** Implicit — cell (r, c) maps to ground coordinates via an affine transform.

**Why "Why" and "How" Logic:**

- **Why squares?** Square cells simplify coordinate transformation and allow efficient array-based storage.
- **Why raster for imagery?** Sensors (CCD arrays) physically produce a grid; raster is the *native* format of remote sensing data.
- **How is value assigned?** Through four conventions:
  - Cell **contains** the feature (rare, used for very large features).
  - Cell **centroid** contains the feature (most common in GIS).
  - Cell is assigned the **majority** value (used in classification).
  - Cell is assigned the **closest** feature (used in some proximity analyses).

**Compression Schemes in Raster Data:**

- **Run-Length Encoding (RLE):** Stores sequences of identical values as (value, count) pairs. Excellent for thematic maps (e.g., land use) with large homogeneous regions.
- **Quad-Tree Decomposition:** Recursively subdivides the raster into four quadrants until each quadrant is homogeneous. Used in GIS packages like ArcGIS and SPANS.
- **LZW / LERC:** Dictionary-based compression used in GeoTIFF, MrSID, ECW formats.

### 2.2 Vector Data Representation — Points, Lines, Polygons

The vector model stores geometry as **explicit coordinates** with optional topology.

**A. Point Features** — A zero-dimensional primitive defined by a single coordinate pair.
- Examples: Wells, benchmark monuments, spot heights, trees, sampling stations.

**B. Line / Arc Features** — A one-dimensional primitive defined by an ordered list of vertices.
- Examples: Rivers, roads, contour lines, power lines, fault lines.
- **Simple line:** Straight segments between consecutive vertices.
- **Smooth curve:** Vertices define a spline or Bézier control points.

**C. Polygon Features** — A two-dimensional primitive defined by a closed chain of arcs enclosing an area.
- Examples: Parcels, lakes, building footprints, administrative boundaries.

**Vector Topological Relationships (Three Classical Tests — Egenhofer 1989):**

| Test | Description |
|---|---|
| **Disjoint** | Two features share no points |
| **Meet** | Features touch at a boundary |
| **Overlap** | Features share interior points |
| **Equal** | Geometries coincide |
| **Contains / Within** | One feature encloses the other |
| **Covers / Covered By** | One feature covers the boundary of the other |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Topological Data Model:** In a full topological structure (used by Arc/Info, ArcGIS, PostGIS), three explicit tables are maintained: **PAT (Polygon Attribute Table)**, **AAT (Arc Attribute Table)**, and a separate **Node topology file**. This enables efficient adjacency, connectivity, and containment queries.

### 2.3 TIN — Triangulated Irregular Network (Surveying-Specific)

A TIN is a vector model specialized for terrain surface representation:

- **Vertices (nodes):** Spot elevations from field survey, LiDAR, or photogrammetry.
- **Edges:** Connecting edges between vertices, ensuring no edge crosses another.
- **Faces:** Triangular planar facets; each face has a constant slope and aspect.

**TIN Generation Algorithm (Delaunay Triangulation):**
The most common method. The Delaunay criterion states: *a triangulation is Delaunay if and only if no point lies inside the circumcircle of any triangle.* This maximizes the minimum angle, producing "well-shaped" triangles.

**Advantages of TIN over Grid DEM:**
- Adaptive density — more vertices in rugged terrain, fewer in flat areas.
- Preserves terrain break-lines (ridges, valleys, cliffs).
- No data redundancy in flat regions.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Raster File Size | $\text{Size} = R \times C \times B / 8$ | Bytes | R = rows, C = columns, B = bit depth |
| 2 | Number of Cells in Raster | $N = R \times C$ | cells | Equals pixel count |
| 3 | Ground Area per Pixel | $A_{px} = \Delta x \times \Delta y$ | m² | Δx, Δy = cell size |
| 4 | Total Area Covered | $A_{total} = N \times \Delta x \times \Delta y$ | m² | For square cells Δx = Δy |
| 5 | Ground Sampling Distance (GSD) | $\text{GSD} = \frac{H \times p}{f}$ | m | H = flying height, p = pixel pitch, f = focal length |
| 6 | Number of Triangles in TIN (max) | $T_{max} = 2V - k - 2$ | triangles | V = vertices, k = convex hull boundary points |
| 7 | Number of Edges in TIN (max) | $E_{max} = 3V - k - 3$ | edges | Delaunay triangulation |
| 8 | Polygon Area (Shoelace Formula) | $A = \frac{1}{2}\left\vert\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i)\right\vert$ | m² | Closed polygon with n vertices |
| 9 | Arc Length | $L = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1}\sqrt{(x_{i+1}-x_i)^2 + (y_{i+1}-y_i)^2}$ | m | Sum of Euclidean segment lengths |
| 10 | Compression Ratio | $\text{CR} = \frac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | dimensionless | Higher is better |
| 11 | RMSE of TIN vs Survey Points | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(Z_{i}^{TIN} - Z_i^{true})^2}{n}}$ | m | Quality metric of TIN surface |
| 12 | Sliver Triangle Threshold | $A_{sliver} < 0.05 \times \overline{A}_{triangle}$ | m² | Triangles below this are removed |

> [!TIP]
> **Vector Data Formula — Shoelace formula for polygon area** is the single most important formula for Part-B numerical questions in KTU 2024 ESE for this module. Memorize and practice it for at least 5-vertex polygons.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cadastral Mapping:** Vector data is mandatory for legal land parcel boundaries.
- **Hydrological Modelling:** Raster DEMs feed ArcSWAT, HEC-RAS for watershed delineation.
- **Urban Planning:** Vector building footprints + raster land-use grids are integrated for zoning.
- **Mine Surveying:** TIN models drive volumetric calculations in open-pit mines.
- **Transportation Engineering:** Vector route alignment + raster slope/curvature maps support highway design.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Raster File Size and Storage Calculation

**Problem Setup:** A satellite captures a 4-band multispectral scene covering a 60 km × 60 km area on the ground. The Ground Sampling Distance (GSD) is 15 m in each band, and each pixel is quantized to 8 bits.

**Step 1: Compute the number of rows and columns of the raster.**

$$R = \frac{\text{Length of strip in ground direction}}{\text{GSD}}$$
$$R = \frac{60{,}000 \text{ m}}{15 \text{ m}} = 4000 \text{ rows}$$

$$C = \frac{\text{Width of strip in ground direction}}{\text{GSD}}$$
$$C = \frac{60{,}000 \text{ m}}{15 \text{ m}} = 4000 \text{ columns}$$

**Step 2: Compute the total number of pixels (cells).**

$$N = R \times C$$
$$N = 4000 \times 4000 = 16{,}000{,}000 \text{ pixels per band}$$

**Step 3: Compute bytes per band.**

$$\text{Bytes per band} = N \times \frac{B}{8} = 16 \times 10^6 \times \frac{8}{8} = 16 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 4: Compute total bytes for all 4 bands.**

$$\text{Total Size} = 4 \times 16 \times 10^6 = 64 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ MB}$$

**Conversion to gigabytes:**

$$\text{Size in GB} = \frac{64 \times 10^6}{1024^3} \approx 0.0596 \text{ GB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A single 60 km × 60 km 4-band scene at 15 m GSD requires approximately **64 MB** of uncompressed storage. The Landsat-8 OLI archive contains millions of such scenes, demanding **terabyte-scale** data centres.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: GSD from Sensor Parameters

**Problem Setup:** A drone-mounted camera has a pixel pitch of **3.76 µm**, a focal length of **8.8 mm**, and flies at a height of **120 m** above the ground. Compute the Ground Sampling Distance (GSD).

**Step 1: Convert all units to a consistent system (metres).**

$$p = 3.76 \text{ µm} = 3.76 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$
$$f = 8.8 \text{ mm} = 8.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$
$$H = 120 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Apply the GSD formula derived from similar triangles.**

$$\text{GSD} = \frac{H \times p}{f}$$
$$\text{GSD} = \frac{120 \times 3.76 \times 10^{-6}}{8.8 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 3: Simplify numerator and denominator.**

$$\text{GSD} = \frac{4.512 \times 10^{-4}}{8.8 \times 10^{-3}} \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Perform division.**

$$\text{GSD} = 5.127 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} \approx 0.0513 \text{ m} = 51.3 \text{ mm}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** To halve the GSD (double the spatial resolution), you must either halve the flying height OR halve the pixel pitch OR double the focal length. Drone surveyors exploit this by flying lower for higher detail.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Polygon Area Using the Shoelace Formula (Vector Model)

**Problem Setup:** A cadastral parcel has vertices given in sequence as: A(10, 20), B(40, 30), C(50, 60), D(20, 50). Compute the area of the polygon ABCD using the vector shoelace formula.

**Step 1: Write down the shoelace formula.**

$$A = \frac{1}{2}\left\vert \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i \cdot y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} \cdot y_i) \right\vert$$

**Step 2: Tabulate the (x, y) pairs cyclically, closing back to A.**

| i | Vertex | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_{i+1}$ | $y_{i+1}$ | $x_i y_{i+1}$ | $x_{i+1} y_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A | 10 | 20 | 40 | 30 | 300 | 800 |
| 2 | B | 40 | 30 | 50 | 60 | 2400 | 1500 |
| 3 | C | 50 | 60 | 20 | 50 | 2500 | 1200 |
| 4 | D | 20 | 50 | 10 | 20 | 400 | 200 |

**Step 3: Sum the columns.**

$$\sum x_i y_{i+1} = 300 + 2400 + 2500 + 400 = 5600$$

$$\sum x_{i+1} y_i = 800 + 1500 + 1200 + 200 = 3700$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the shoelace formula.**

$$A = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert 5600 - 3700 \right\vert = \frac{1}{2} \times 1900 = 950 \text{ unit}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The cadastral parcel ABCD has an area of **950 square units** (square metres if coordinates are in metres). The absolute value ensures positivity regardless of vertex orientation (clockwise or counter-clockwise).

---

### 3.4 Derivation: TIN Topological Counts (Euler's Formula Applied)

**Problem Setup:** A TIN has **V = 250 vertices**, of which **k = 12** lie on the convex hull boundary. Compute the maximum number of edges and triangles.

**Step 1: Recall Euler's formula for planar graphs applied to a TIN.**

$$V - E + F = 1 + C$$

For a single connected TIN covering a simply-connected region, the number of connected components C = 1 and the outer face counts as 1 of the F faces. For Delaunay triangulations:

$$T_{max} = 2V - k - 2$$
$$E_{max} = 3V - k - 3$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$T_{max} = 2(250) - 12 - 2 = 500 - 14 = 486 \text{ triangles}$$
$$E_{max} = 3(250) - 12 - 3 = 750 - 15 = 735 \text{ edges}$$

**Step 3: Verify with Euler's formula.**

$$F = T_{max} + 1 = 486 + 1 = 487 \text{ faces (including outer)}$$

$$V - E + F = 250 - 735 + 487 = 2 \checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** Each triangle in a TIN stores 3 elevation values (the Z-coordinates of its three vertices) and one slope-aspect pair. A TIN with 486 triangles stores approximately 486 × 4 = **1944 numerical attributes** for terrain representation.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Raster vs. Vector Comparative Analysis

| Parameter | Raster Model | Vector Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Structure** | Regular grid of cells | Points, lines, polygons with coordinates |
| **Storage Format** | GeoTIFF, IMG, ECW, MrSID, NetCDF | Shapefile (.shp), GeoJSON, KML, DWG, GDB |
| **Coordinate Storage** | Implicit (by cell index) | Explicit (x, y, z) |
| **Topology** | Implied by adjacency | Explicit (nodes, arcs, polygons) |
| **Best for** | Continuous fields (elevation, temperature) | Discrete features (parcels, roads) |
| **Overlay Operation Speed** | Fast (array algebra) | Slower (geometric intersection) |
| **Network Analysis** | Difficult | Excellent (graph-based) |
| **Cartographic Output** | Pixelated at high zoom | Smooth at any zoom |
| **Data Volume** | High (especially high resolution) | Low to moderate |
| **Attribute Handling** | One value per cell; multi-band | Multiple attributes per feature |
| **Geometric Accuracy** | Limited by cell size | Limited by coordinate precision |
| **Coordinate System** | Must define origin + cell size + skew | Standard projections (UTM, etc.) |
| **Conversion to other model** | Rasterization | Vectorization / Tracing |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Differentiate between Raster and Vector data models in GIS. List any four advantages of the Vector data model over the Raster data model.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Raster Model | Vector Model |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Grid of cells (pixels) | Points, lines, polygons |
| Storage | Implicit (by cell index) | Explicit (x, y coordinates) |
| Topology | Implied | Explicitly stored |
| Resolution | Fixed by cell size | Infinite (coordinate-based) |

**Four advantages of Vector over Raster:**

1. **Precise geometry** — Coordinates define features with sub-millimetre accuracy, ideal for cadastral and engineering surveys.
2. **Compact storage for sparse data** — A river boundary stored as a polyline takes far less memory than a band of pixels of the same river.
3. **Excellent topology** — Explicit arc-node structure supports efficient network tracing (water supply, electrical grids).
4. **Smooth cartographic output** — Lines and polygons do not pixelate at any zoom level, producing publication-quality maps.

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Explain the concept of a Triangulated Irregular Network (TIN) in terrain modelling. State any two advantages of TIN over a Grid DEM.**

**Model Answer:**

A **TIN** is a vector-based surface model representing terrain as a network of non-overlapping triangular facets whose vertices are surveyed spot elevations. The triangles are typically generated using the **Delaunay triangulation algorithm**, which ensures that no point lies inside the circumcircle of any triangle, producing well-shaped (equiangular) triangles.

**Two advantages of TIN over Grid DEM:**

1. **Adaptive spatial density** — Vertices can be dense in rugged terrain (mountain ridges) and sparse in flat areas (plains), making TIN storage-efficient.
2. **Preservation of break-lines** — Natural and man-made features like ridges, valleys, and embankments can be enforced as triangle edges, giving high-fidelity representation of terrain discontinuities.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PBCET304]** With neat sketches, explain the Raster and Vector data models of GIS. Compare them in terms of (a) data structure, (b) storage efficiency, (c) suitability for spatial analysis, and (d) cartographic output. Illustrate with at least one engineering surveying application for each model.

**Model Solution Structure (Mapped to KTU Mark Distribution):**

**(i) Raster Data Model — Explanation + Sketch (4 marks)**

The Raster model represents geographic space as a **regular grid of equal-sized square cells** (pixels), each storing a single attribute value. The position of any cell is determined by its **row and column indices**, and the ground location is computed via an affine transform. Sketches must show: a geographic area subdivided into pixels with assigned values (e.g., 1 = forest, 2 = water, 3 = urban). The four conventions for value assignment (cell-contains, centroid, majority, closest) should be mentioned.

**(ii) Vector Data Model — Explanation + Sketch (4 marks)**

The Vector model represents features as **discrete geometric primitives**:
- **Points:** Defined by a single (x, y) coordinate — example: survey benchmarks.
- **Lines/Arcs:** Ordered series of vertices connected by straight segments or smooth curves.
- **Polygons:** Closed chains of arcs forming a bounded area.

Sketches must illustrate a cadastral parcel with explicit vertex coordinates, a river polyline, and a polygon with internal labels.

**(iii) Comparative Analysis (4 marks — 1 mark × 4 sub-parts)**

(a) **Data Structure:** Raster uses a matrix/array of values; Vector uses coordinate lists and topological tables.
(b) **Storage Efficiency:** Raster is inefficient for sparse data; Vector is inefficient for dense imagery.
(c) **Suitability for Spatial Analysis:** Raster is best for continuous fields (DEM, slope); Vector is best for network analysis and cadastral work.
(d) **Cartographic Output:** Vector produces sharp, smooth output at any zoom; Raster pixelates at high zoom.

**(iv) Engineering Surveying Applications (2 marks)**

- **Raster:** DEM generation from LiDAR for highway vertical alignment design and cut-fill volume calculation.
- **Vector:** Cadastral boundary survey using total station / GNSS; transmission line corridor mapping.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PBCET304, Module 4 Alternative]** A satellite image of a study area covers 30 km × 30 km on the ground. The image has 4 spectral bands with a Ground Sampling Distance (GSD) of 10 m and 8-bit radiometric resolution.

**(a)** Compute the total uncompressed file size of the image in megabytes (MB). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the same image is re-sampled to 30 m GSD and compressed using Run-Length Encoding (RLE) achieving a compression ratio of 4.5:1, determine (i) the new file size in MB and (ii) the new total area represented by one pixel. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Uncompressed File Size (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Compute number of rows and columns.

$$R = \frac{30{,}000 \text{ m}}{10 \text{ m}} = 3000 \text{ rows}$$
$$C = \frac{30{,}000 \text{ m}}{10 \text{ m}} = 3000 \text{ columns}$$

**Step 2:** Compute total number of pixels per band.

$$N = R \times C = 3000 \times 3000 = 9 \times 10^6 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 3:** Compute bytes per band (8-bit = 1 byte).

$$\text{Bytes per band} = 9 \times 10^6 \times 1 = 9 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by the number of bands (4).

$$\text{Total Size} = 4 \times 9 \times 10^6 = 36 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes} = 36 \text{ MB}$$

**Part (b) — Re-sampled and Compressed (7 marks)**

**(i) New file size after re-sampling to 30 m and RLE compression (3.5 marks):**

Number of rows and columns at 30 m GSD:

$$R' = \frac{30{,}000}{30} = 1000 \text{ rows}$$
$$C' = \frac{30{,}000}{30} = 1000 \text{ columns}$$

New pixels per band:

$$N' = 1000 \times 1000 = 1 \times 10^6$$

Uncompressed size per band:

$$1 \times 10^6 \times 1 \text{ byte} = 1 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes per band}$$

Total uncompressed (4 bands):

$$4 \times 1 \times 10^6 = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ MB}$$

Compressed size with CR = 4.5:

$$\text{Compressed Size} = \frac{4 \text{ MB}}{4.5} \approx 0.889 \text{ MB}$$

**(ii) New ground area per pixel (3.5 marks):**

$$A_{px} = \Delta x \times \Delta y = 30 \text{ m} \times 30 \text{ m} = 900 \text{ m}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit consistency trap:** Many students forget to convert km to m, yielding wrong row/column counts. Always write units explicitly in the numerator and denominator.
> 2. **Band count omission:** In multi-band problems, students often compute for **one band only** and forget to multiply by the number of bands. Read the question twice — note "4 spectral bands."
> 3. **Compression ratio inversion:** A compression ratio of 4.5:1 means the compressed file is **4.5 times smaller** than the original; hence the compressed size = original ÷ 4.5, NOT original × 4.5.
> 4. **GSD unit mismatch:** GSD is in metres on the ground; pixel pitch from the sensor is in micrometres. Do not confuse the two.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE (Module 4):**

- **Two fundamental data models:** Raster (grid) and Vector (point/line/polygon). Remember: Raster is best for **fields**, Vector is best for **objects**.
- **Raster cell conventions:** Cell-contains, centroid, majority, and closest — know when to use each.
- **Vector primitives:** Points (0-D), Lines (1-D), Polygons (2-D), Surfaces/TIN (2.5-D).
- **Topology pillars:** Connectivity (which arcs meet at a node), Adjacency (which polygons share an arc), Containment (which polygon contains another). These enable network tracing and overlay.
- **TIN Delaunay rule:** No point inside the circumcircle of any triangle — guarantees well-shaped facets.
- **TIN counts:** $T_{max} = 2V - k - 2$ and $E_{max} = 3V - k - 3$ — must be memorised cold.
- **GSD formula:** $\text{GSD} = \frac{H \cdot p}{f}$ — focal length (mm), pixel pitch (µm), flying height (m). Practice unit conversions.
- **Shoelace formula:** For vector polygon area; the single most likely 7-mark numerical in Part B. Always tabulate the cyclic cross-products.
- **File size formula:** $\text{Size} = R \times C \times B / 8$ — remember the /8 to convert bits to bytes.
- **Compression types for raster:** RLE, Quad-Tree, LZW — know which is used in which format (GeoTIFF, ECW, MrSID).
- **Common formats to memorise:** Raster — GeoTIFF, IMG, ECW, MrSID, NetCDF; Vector — Shapefile, GeoJSON, KML, DWG, File Geodatabase.
- **Conversion:** Rasterization (sampling), Vectorization (edge detection + tracing) — both have inherent information loss.
- **Surveying-specific applications:** TIN for terrain, Vector for cadastral and utilities, Raster DEM for hydrology and cut-fill.
- **Examiner hot-spots:** Comparison tables, file size numerics, TIN topology counts, GSD derivation — these four areas carry 70% of the marks for this module.
